Crosscurrents: Week of May 18-24, 2026

Cover decoding (clockwise from top-left): the scale of justice weighted by a money-bag against thin legal documents — the Trump $1.776B “Anti-Weaponization Fund” and the four-orientation legal-architecture cluster around it; the torn autopsy fragment — the DNC’s released 2024 report and the five-writer convergence on party dysfunction; the round summit table with two figures and an empty third chair — the May 15 Trump-Xi Beijing summit and Europe’s structural absence; the tortoise atop a utility pole — the censored Douyin meme of Xi-as-tortoise that surfaced this week and the non-Anglophone reading publics’ view of him; the data-center silhouette with fiber-optic cables — the AI infrastructure / build-out theme and the GOP-voter backlash; the drone over the Baltic shoreline — the Baltic-states airspace incursions and Europe’s frontline civil-defense gap.

    • One More Time: The Average American K-12 Student is Doing Fine Relative to the International Baseline
    • You Can’t Defend a Policy By Getting Angry at the Suggestion That It’s Benefitted People
    • Ideas are still big but books got small

Notable Quotes

“The superrich have every visible advantage — Supreme Court, money, press ownership, captured Republicans, meek Democrats, corporate control — but one critical weakness: they don’t just want to be rich, they demand to be loved.” — Matt Stoller, BIG (“The Rage of the Billionaires Is Coming”)

“Pointing out that Congress could repair the damage acknowledges the damage.” — The Crosscurrents synthesis, paraphrasing the McCarthy / Puri / Rubenfeld convergence on the Anti-Weaponization Fund

“There is no Red Phone. There are no codified rules of the game. There is no SALT, no START. The Cold War’s balance of terror has been replaced by the terror of imbalance.” — Hassan Aourid (حسن أوريد), Al Jazeera Arabic

“Trump ‘started small to test what he could get away with’ — $5M to Ashli Babbitt’s family, $1.25M to Michael Flynn, $1M to Carter Page — and scaled up once each smaller payment passed without resistance.” — John R. Puri, National Review (“Trump’s Egregious Abuse of an Egregious Delegation”)

“Severing the rhetorical from the operational is precisely the move the mechanism denies.” — Crosscurrents rebuttal essay, 2026-05-17, recurring this week as the diagnostic frame on counter-terrorism designation

Themes

The Trump Anti-Weaponization Fund and the institutional power calculus

Synthesis

The Trump DOJ announced a $1.776 billion “Anti-Weaponization Fund” this week, structured as the resolution of Trump’s own January lawsuit against the IRS over the 2018-2020 tax-return leaks. The mechanics are unusual enough to warrant restating: Trump, who controls the IRS as defendant, settled with Trump as plaintiff; acting Attorney General Todd Blanche (formerly Trump’s personal lawyer) additionally issued a blanket civil-and-criminal release covering Trump, his sons, and the Trump Organization on any pending or potential federal matter; the fund will be administered by five officials Trump can fire at will; recipient names and amounts may be kept secret; and Judge Kathleen Williams closed the case without entering the settlement on the record. The money will be paid out of the Judgment Fund, a 1956 permanent appropriation that requires no further congressional action.

What makes the cluster load-bearing is not the fund itself but the response. Four writers spanning four genuinely different political coordinates — institutional-conservative legal, separation-of-powers conservative, centrist constitutional scholar, and left investigative — independently described the same institutional pattern. Two additional pieces show the same one-way-state-discretion architecture operating in unrelated regulatory and national-security venues.

  • The fund may be lawful and is still the scandal. McCarthy (NR, institutional-conservative legal; former SDNY prosecutor) argues collusive intra-executive litigation has enough jurisprudential ambiguity that Judge Williams could have ruled either way, and points to Biden-era settlements with Andrew McCabe, Peter Strzok, and Lisa Page as evidence that the pattern is bipartisan in kind even if Trump’s version is “far more ostentatious.” Rubenfeld (FP, constitutional-law scholar) reaches the same conclusion through a standing analysis — no one obvious is positioned to sue — and concedes a non-trivial point: some Biden-era lawfare targets (Trump-allied figures who paid large legal fees, deplatformed individuals) may genuinely deserve compensation. Both writers insist that doctrinal survival is not the same as institutional legitimacy, and that opacity, at-will administrators, and plaintiff-controls-defendant are the structural defects that matter.

  • The release covers civil liability, which is where future accountability would happen. Rubenfeld singles out Blanche’s blanket release as “better than a pardon” — pardons reach only criminal liability, but the release also forecloses civil claims, which is the actual vehicle a future administration would use to recover money or impose accountability. The Obama-era $680M discrimination fund Blanche cites as precedent had judicial disbursement, public process, and no plaintiff-defendant identity; it does not actually cover the structure on offer here. The release is voidable by a future Democratic AG in principle, but voiding it would itself look like the weaponization the fund nominally compensates — which is the design feature.

  • The deeper failure is congressional, not prosecutorial. Puri (NR, separation-of-powers conservative) reframes the diagnosis: until the mid-20th century, Congress approved every monetary judgment against the United States through annual appropriations under Article I’s “no money shall be drawn from the Treasury, but in Consequence of Appropriations made by Law” clause. The 1956 Judgment Fund was a permanent appropriation for judgments under $100,000; in the 1970s Congress expanded it to cover negotiated “compromise settlements” and removed the cap entirely, leaving an unlimited pool that “spends billions per year, all without a whit of involvement by the one branch empowered to spend public money.” Trump’s fund is an abuse of legislative power Congress gave away decades ago — a pattern that escalated through $5M to the Babbitt family, $1.25M to Michael Flynn, $1M to Carter Page before reaching $1.776B. Puri proposes non-delegation litigation against the fund’s structure itself; McCarthy’s parallel remedy is impeachment-and-oversight via his 2014 Faithless Execution framework — both authors concede the political path to their preferred remedy does not currently exist, particularly after Trump v. United States (2024) made most presidential abuses unprosecutable.

  • The same one-way-discretion pattern shows up in unrelated venues. McLaughlin (NR) describes the SEC’s rescission of its 54-year-old Rule 202.5(e), which had barred civil-settlement defendants from publicly contesting SEC charges even when they never admitted them. The agency dropped the rule because enforcing it would have invited a strict-scrutiny First Amendment challenge it expected to lose — the rule survived only because it was never tested. Stoller (BIG, anti-monopoly mechanism-first) reads the $67-120B NextEra-Dominion utility merger as the same gold-plating mechanism in rate-setting: regulated utilities earn a guaranteed 9-11% return on equity (against roughly 4% on market savings), which converts them into “banks with a guaranteed return” that maximize capital deployment regardless of efficiency. NextEra’s merger documents explicitly project “11% regulatory capital employed growth” across “constructive regulatory environments,” which Stoller translates as captured regulators. The U.S. has built 375 miles of ultra-high-voltage transmission over the 2020s; China has built 8,200 miles.

  • The administration’s national-security metrics show the same pattern of unfalsifiable state claims. Klippenstein (left investigative) examines the “860 jihadis killed” figure that counterterrorism czar Sebastian Gorka has been promoting, and the Trump Truth Social announcement that Abu-Bilal al-Minuki — described as “second in command of ISIS globally” — was killed in Nigeria. Al-Minuki was a regional Boko Haram veteran whose elevation to “global second-in-command” is sold to a U.S. domestic audience as decapitation of a worldwide threat; the strike was executed by Nigerian forces with U.S. providing only ISR; Nigerian officials had already claimed al-Minuki killed in February 2024. The 860 figure is unverifiable by design. The mechanism is the same as the fund: an official metric whose flagship instance does not survive reconstruction, with no institutional venue available where scrutiny would bind.

Read together, the cluster does a second piece of work the four writers do not name individually: every prescribed remedy is one the prescriber simultaneously concedes will not be deployed. McCarthy’s impeachment-and-oversight remedy is paired with the acknowledgment that the criminal law was never built for this and that after Trump v. United States most such abuses are unprosecutable anyway — and that Congress will not use the impeachment mechanism he proposes. Puri’s non-delegation litigation would strike down a 70-year-old appropriations mechanism against a doctrine the Supreme Court has not seriously deployed in nearly a century, with no plaintiff whose standing he can identify. Rubenfeld’s future-Democratic-AG rescission of Blanche’s blanket release is paired with the observation that rescission would itself be read as the weaponization the fund nominally exists to compensate, making the path politically prohibitive. Klippenstein offers no institutional venue at all. The architectural diagnosis therefore also functions as a closing argument — that the remedies the institutional-conservative-legal tradition has built for exactly this kind of executive self-dealing no longer have credible deployment paths, and that the four-orientation convergence is on something stronger than “this is bad,” it is on the harder claim that institutional constraint has run out of mechanisms across four independent frames. The convergence on remedy-exhaustion is sharper than the convergence on diagnosis, and points at a gap none of the four writers presses on: the constituency that would have to mobilize for any of their preferred remedies to bind is unnamed in every piece. The architectural-diagnosis frame may be crowding out the political-coalition frame the moment requires, and the convergence’s authority may be making that crowding-out easier to miss.

↑ Back to TOC

The DNC autopsy and the Democratic coalition crisis

Synthesis

The Democratic National Committee released its long-delayed 193-page autopsy of the 2024 election on Thursday, under CNN-leak pressure rather than on its own timetable. Chair Ken Martin had commissioned it from Democratic consultant Paul Rivera on a part-time volunteer basis, sat on it for over a year, attached a personal note saying he did not endorse it, and held a Thursday-morning DNC-members call at which he read a prepared statement and took no questions. The document arrived with an opening disclaimer that the DNC could not verify its own assertions, hostile marginal annotations contesting its claims throughout, no conclusion section, no references to “Israel,” “Gaza,” “inflation,” “immigration,” or Biden’s age, and platitudes (“It’s imperative that Democrats meet the moment”) where causal analysis should be.

Four writers across the project’s full orientation range converged within hours on the same reading: the document is evidence of party dysfunction more than coherent finding. Read as competing causal stories, their accounts would let each writer’s ideological prior pick its preferred upstream cause without arguing against the others. Read together, they don’t compete — they compound, describing different links in a single causal chain in which each step makes the next possible.

  • The autopsy fails as an institutional product, and the artifact itself is the evidence. Goldberg (NYT centrist columnist), Barker (Free Press, former Democratic fundraiser who voted Trump in 2024), Lowry (National Review editor, syndicated), and Rothman (National Review columnist) all treat the disclaimer-from-the-chair, contradicting annotations, missing conclusion, and topical omissions as the story. Lowry calls it “a thoroughly unimpressive, unfinished document that says more about the low state of the Democratic Party than any of its analysis does.” Goldberg notes that a 192-page autopsy commissioned from a part-time volunteer is the behavior of a leadership that does not want a real autopsy.

  • Rob Flaherty’s parallel autopsy outside the DNC is the tell. Goldberg names this most directly: Flaherty (Harris campaign deputy manager) published his own post-mortem in The Bulwark arguing Democrats poll-test themselves into “mealy-mouthed ads about prescription drugs” while Republicans drive viral narratives — the opposite of the official autopsy’s claim that the Harris campaign suffered from insufficient polling-driven messaging. A serious post-mortem now exists outside the DNC because the DNC could not produce one inside.

  • The Gaza-cost-Harris narrative is empirically refuted by within-state down-ballot comparison. This is Rothman’s distinctive empirical move and the strongest single move in the cluster. The narrative was sourced in February to the IMEU Policy Project (Institute for Middle East Understanding) via an Axios report alleging Martin was suppressing findings that Netanyahu’s conduct of the war had cost Harris winnable states. When the autopsy appeared without those findings, far-left figures (David Hogg of the DNC vice chair role, Abdul El-Sayed who won the Michigan Senate primary, Graham Platner who won the Maine Senate primary, RootsAction’s Norman Solomon) pivoted from citing the alleged findings to attacking the DNC for not including them — converting falsification into further evidence for the claim. Against this, Rothman assembles the within-pair test: Democratic senators Slotkin (Michigan), Gallego (Arizona), Rosen (Nevada), Baldwin (Wisconsin), and gubernatorial winner Stein (North Carolina) all outperformed Harris in their states while running on identical Israel positions, and Third Way’s Matt Bennett (Kessler) supplies the parallel Shapiro-vs-Fetterman within-state comparison. If the Israel-position variable were operative, this pattern would not exist.

  • The party’s internal honesty is constrained by what the activist base will permit. Barker’s contribution — from inside the former-operative perspective — is that Martin suppressed the report because confronting its findings would require a conversation the progressive base will not permit. The findings she flags as politically radioactive: Harris lost Black male voters because she “assumed identity politics will hold male voters of color” rather than addressing economic concerns; Josh Stein won North Carolina by focusing “less on abstract issues and identity politics”; Trump’s attack ads on Harris’s 2019 pledge to fund transition surgeries for federal inmates were “very effective” and nothing would have worked because she did not change her position; the 2024 strategist bet on demographic destiny in Texas and the Sun Belt failed in both regions. Barker reads the El-Sayed and Platner primary wins as evidence the progressive base is winning the internal argument anyway.

  • Party autopsies are historically useless on their own terms. Lowry’s structural frame: the 2012 Republican autopsy, which recommended immigration moderation and outreach to non-white voters, was famously inverted by Trump in 2016 — and he won. Midterms turn on incumbent approval rather than out-party renovation, and presidential comebacks come from charismatic candidates assembling new coalitions (Obama 2008, Trump 2016) rather than from strategist-authored retrospectives. The autopsy’s content doesn’t matter; the autopsy’s incompetence matters as a leading indicator of organizational decay.

  • The cross-coalitional parallel: donor-class enforcement is happening on both sides. Barkan’s UnHerd piece on Massie — in the Class, housing, work, and the Massie coalitional event cluster below — reads the parallel donor-class-enforcement event from the right side of the same coalitional moment, where AIPAC-aligned money has organized a $20M primary challenge against Thomas Massie (R-KY) for his Iran-war dissent.

Read in sequence, the four writers’ mechanisms form a causal chain rather than competing diagnoses. Lowry’s organizational decay is the precondition — a party with a functioning research apparatus does not commission a 193-page autopsy from a part-time volunteer in the first place. Given that decay, Goldberg’s Martin-specific incompetence (the perks-not-vision chairmanship, the year of suppression, the disclaimer-from-the-chair) is what determines that the decayed apparatus produces this particular document at this particular moment rather than some less embarrassing artifact. Given Martin’s procedural failures, Barker’s progressive-base veto is what determines the topical omissions — no “Israel,” no “Gaza,” no reckoning with the Black-male-voter loss as anything other than identity-politics-failure, no engagement with the cultural-issue findings she enumerates. And given the omissions, Rothman’s activist-left coercion is what determines that even the falsification of the Gaza-cost-Harris narrative gets converted into further evidence for it — the IMEU-sourced February claim survives the autopsy’s silence by pivoting to attack the silence itself.

Each step is necessary; none is sufficient. A competent chair atop a decayed apparatus might have produced a shorter, more defensible document. A capable apparatus under Martin might have routed around him. A party without the progressive-base veto might have written the racial-politics and cultural-issue findings into a conclusion. An activist left operating through normal persuasion rather than narrative-laundering might have lost the post-hoc framing battle when the autopsy contradicted its predictions. The chain-reading predicts which reform attempts will fail: replacing Martin (the operational-incompetence fix the centrist Blue Dog faction and Amanda Litman are aligned on) addresses one link but leaves the apparatus, the topical vetoes, and the narrative-laundering intact, which is why the next autopsy under a more competent chair will likely fail in a different but structurally similar way.

The Republican parallel Lowry invokes upgrades the diagnosis to class-wide. The 2012 GOP autopsy famously inverted by Trump in 2016 is the same failure mode operating at a different link: the GOP’s research apparatus produced a coherent document and the base nullified it at the next presidential primary, where the Democratic chain fails at the production step rather than the adoption step. Both parties’ analytical apparatuses are now captured by their respective bases’ vetoes on internal honesty — the autopsy-as-institutional-product is structurally extinct, not Democratic-specific, and the four mechanisms isolated this week are the taxonomy of how that capacity gets lost rather than whose it is.

↑ Back to TOC

After the Iran war — strategic reordering and Beijing

Synthesis

The Beijing summit of May 14 and the post-Khamenei Iran environment together pose three sub-questions to the project’s standing assumptions: whether the Iranian regime is a transitional object on the way to a politically usable successor, whether Trump’s actual China policy advances or surrenders the strategic-competition position it claims, and what the same week looks like read from outside the Atlantic frame.

The findings cross-cut the sub-questions. Ten pieces this week converge on five claims rather than ten:

  • The post-strike Iranian regime is constitutionally replacement-proof. Peter Frankopan arrives at this from two centuries of foreign-concession history (the 1872 Reuter mineral and infrastructure monopoly, the 1890 tobacco concession, the 1914 secret British majority stake in Anglo-Persian Oil, the 1925 Pahlavi installation, the 1953 CIA-backed coup against Mosaddegh) — Iran’s revealed-preference posture of asymmetric warfare, sanctions-resistant trade, and nuclear ambiguity is grounded in a pattern the regime weaponizes, and the response to vulnerability is deepening rather than concession. Saeid Golkar arrives at the same place from regime-structural analysis of the leaked Trump-Israel plan to install former president Ahmadinejad: the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps answered only to Khamenei even when Ahmadinejad was president, sided with the Supreme Leader during their second-term break, and arrested Ahmadinejad’s loyalist network — so the Guard, not any single face, is the regime, and “nobody can rule without first controlling the security-theocratic structure.” Different rhetorical paths, same load-bearing finding: there is no politically usable successor.

  • Trump’s China policy surrenders the strategic-competition position it claims to advance — and the evidence comes from opposite political poles. Matthew Yglesias builds the case from a center-left abundance-liberal frame: Trump operates on naive mercantilism (any bilateral deficit is bad) which contaminates the genuine geopolitical-competition case, exports commodities (soybeans, energy) Xi was happy to buy anyway, gives Nvidia favorable chip-export rulings, kills wind and solar permits while China adds hundreds of gigawatts of solar per year and builds reactors on 56-month timelines. His sharpest single example: Trump touted as a “win” getting China to agree the Strait of Hormuz should be demilitarized and untolled long-term — “something no prior president would even have considered asking for because it went without saying.” Yglesias’s companion piece with Jerusalem Demsas concedes the Ricardian free-trade economics in full and the actual-Trump-policy-is-protectionism point — but holds the strategic-competition line on Chinese EVs because car assembly is the largest battery end-market, and the U.S. needs that depth in a competition window Hong Kong and Xinjiang have locked in. Sumantra Maitra, writing in his mechanism-first realist register from a venue aligned with Orbán-coalition strategic-autonomy advocacy, arrives at the same diagnostic from the opposite political coordinate: the summit revealed the arrival of a US-China duopoly with no European seat, China’s near-monopoly on rare-earth processing (~90% of separation capacity) lets Beijing extract concessions from any Western actor that needs the inputs, and the EU AI Act is all formal legal authority and no material capacity to enforce against US or Chinese firms whose chips and models the Act nominally constrains. When a center-left abundance-liberal and a right-realist arrive independently at “current Trump China policy is incoherent protectionism that surrenders leverage,” through incompatible analytical vocabularies, the operational diagnostic is more credible than either framework alone. This is the week’s strongest cross-orientation signal.

  • The Anglophone analytic apparatus is misfiring on multipolar dynamics. Aurelien, in his canonical long-form public-service-essay register, traces Western Escalation Theory — codified in Herman Kahn’s 1965 On Escalation with its 44-rung ladder, built on John von Neumann’s 1940s Game Theory, carried into US defense policy by Robert McNamara’s Pentagon and Thomas Schelling’s signalling work — back to mathematical economics. The simplifying assumptions (“all governments think alike and will correctly interpret signals as sent”) are merely misleading in economics but “extremely dangerous” in international relations. By Aurelien’s two-part test (an escalatory action requires both a defined purpose tied to a specific end-state and the actual capacity to execute it), much “escalatory” Western behavior is theatre for domestic audiences. Another US strike on Iran would be “de-escalatory in effect” because it would be weaker than the first — missile stocks substantially exhausted, no new levers. The framework, not the events, is what needs replacing.

  • Fukuyama inverts the Taiwan-timing question. Francis Fukuyama reads the Beijing summit’s staged optics (no airport reception, a shorter chair making Trump look smaller than Xi, sycophantic Trump praise met with cold Xi reciprocation) and Trump’s newly explicit signaling that Taiwan is a tradeable asset, then offers the cluster’s one clean falsifiable prediction: Xi will not move on Taiwan while a self-immolating Trump is doing the work for him, but a reversing successor (any Democrat, any non-Trump Republican attempting to repair alliances and rebuild advanced-weapons stocks) forces Xi to act on a faster timeline than the demographic-trajectory measure implies. The operative variable shifts from Chinese demographics to US trajectory. The window resolves between 2028 and 2032 — late enough to be patient, soon enough that the project can come back to it.

  • The non-Anglophone trio is one finding: a civilizational-gap tell. Hassan Aourid, writing in Arabic for Al Jazeera, names the missing Cold-War stabilizing infrastructure between Washington and Beijing — no equivalent of the 1963 Moscow-Washington hotline (established after the Cuban Missile Crisis to prevent miscalculation), no SALT/START/INF/ABM-class arms-control architecture, no codified rules — and reaches for the Chinese strategist Yan Xuetong on moral-realist leadership rather than for Kennedy or Kissinger to do the analytical work. Sun Liping walks Chinese readers through Robert Kagan’s 2024 thesis that an anti-liberal current has run through American politics since the founding (the three-fifths compromise, the 1924 Immigration Act and government-backed eugenic sterilization, the recurrent “ordinary whites as victims of a Wall Street/Jewish/cosmopolitan elite” conspiracy register) — Trump and MAGA as the surfacing of a 250-year structural feature rather than as an electoral anomaly. The CDT Douyin-tortoise comment thread is the smallest but most striking data point: a censored-but-surviving register reading Xi as a placeholder elevated by Party elders (“the Immortals”), with one sub-thread invoking foreign strikes by name (B2, F35, “bald eagle”) and citing the Khamenei airstrike and Venezuela as named precedents. The three pieces do not constitute a fourth substantive position competing with the others — they function jointly as evidence that the Chinese, Moroccan, and PRC-internet reading publics are operating on frames the Anglophone discourse cannot model from inside. The gap is civilizational and architectural at the same time.

Read across the three sub-questions, the cluster’s load-bearing finding is harder than any of the writers names individually: the absence of an identifiable actor that the standard “who is gaining, who is losing” frame can attach to. In Iran, Frankopan and Golkar converge on a regime that is constitutionally replacement-proof — the IRGC is the regime, the Guard outlasted Ahmadinejad even when he was president, and so the leaked Trump-Israel plan to install him failed not because the wrong successor was chosen but because the category “successor” does not apply. There is no politically usable face for any post-Khamenei deal to be negotiated with. In US-China, the Yglesias–Maitra convergence on “Trump’s policy is incoherent protectionism that surrenders leverage” sharpens into something stronger: the doves’ position (free trade, friend-shoring, Ricardian rationality) and the hawks’ position (industrial-base depth, allied coordination, decoupling on chips and EVs) are both unrepresented in the administration’s actual decisions. The China-policy frame produces fluent answers but the answers don’t map onto identifiable coalition players inside the government conducting the policy. In Europe-as-third-pole, Maitra’s auctoritas without potestas on the AI Act and the rare-earth dependency that lets Beijing extract concessions is the most direct version of the pattern, and Aourid supplies the structural complement from outside the Atlantic frame: the missing Moscow-Washington-hotline-equivalent and the missing arms-control architecture are not coordination failures but the absence of an institutional actor to coordinate with. The Beijing summit had two principals and an empty third chair.

The week-to-week roundup is organized around tracking which actor is gaining position — Iran or the US in the post-strike phase, Trump’s hawks or doves, Europe as autonomous pole or US satellite. Those framings each presume a definite actor on each side of the question. When the load-bearing finding in three regions simultaneously is the non-existence of one of the actors, the analytical frame is producing fluent readings of vacuums. Fukuyama is the partial exception that reinforces rather than contradicts the pattern — his Taiwan-timing inversion works precisely because it names a future actor (a reversing successor administration attempting to repair alliances and rebuild advanced-weapons stocks) the present configuration lacks, and locates the operative variable in whether that actor materializes. The prediction is falsifiable because it asserts an identity the present configuration does not have.

Carry-forward: the iran-time-direction open disagreement — does time favor the US or Iran in the post-strike sanctions-and-negotiations phase? — keeps sharpening toward what the project has been calling the third resolution: not “time favors the US” and not “time favors Iran” but the question is wrong. Both sides’ institutional incentives produce identical “we are winning” rhetoric while the costs migrate to third parties (Britain, Europe, the global poor); the configuration is stable rather than transitional. Frankopan supplies 200-year historical depth for the regime-continuity that read predicts; Golkar confirms no politically usable successor exists; Aurelien adds the meta-critique that Anglophone Escalation Theory cannot describe non-Western deliberation. The Yglesias-Maitra convergence adds direct evidence for the industrial-and-AI-deployment measure on the china-power-axis-disagreement (which side is gaining structural advantage); Fukuyama’s Taiwan inversion is a falsifiable 2028-2032 claim the project can return to.

↑ Back to TOC

Europe: collapse, choice, hardening

Synthesis

The cluster’s load-bearing finding is a three-orientation convergence on the same diagnostic: Europe — and Germany specifically — is structurally incapable of executing the security posture its political class is now pledging. The writers occupy different positions (Constantini on the post-liberal Commonplace right; Sternberg as WSJ centrist transatlantic political-economy specialist; Maitra as Chronicles right-realist) and arrive at the description through different priors. None cites the others. The political-consequence layer (Hallam) and the European elite-reaction layer (the two Euro|topics curations) supply what the diagnostic implies but does not on its own deliver.

  • Three-orientation diagnostic convergence on Europe’s structural incapacity. Constantini supplies the operational case: von der Leyen’s €800B EU defense pledge is really €150B with a third earmarked for already-rearmed Poland; Merz’s 3.5%-of-GDP commitment was quietly trimmed to 3.05% with every meaningful deadline set after Trump leaves office; German electricity runs at 39.6¢/kWh against the US 15¢; the industrial base shed 160,000 jobs in 2025 with Volkswagen closing its first-ever home-country factory; Merz polls at 19% while the AfD sits at 29% and the cordon sanitaire (the cross-party convention barring coalitions with the AfD) leaves only SPD/Greens/Linke as available partners, none of whom will accept the welfare cuts rearmament requires. Sternberg, writing a direct follow-up to his 2026-05-02 “Europeans find out how poor they are” column, treats the European pushback his prior piece drew (a Krugman-adjacent Substack debunking, then a more rigorous Garicano response) as itself evidence: disproportionate emotional heat tracks the embarrassment of those who built careers on the now-falsified promise of simultaneously have-able and eatable cake (the welfare-state-as-equal-prosperity thesis European politicians sold to voters for decades). Maitra endorses the Pentagon’s cancellation of the 2nd ABCT (Armored Brigade Combat Team) Poland rotation, the German troop reduction, and the Norfolk NATO base closure as overdue burden-shift consistent with the Eisenhower 1956 design.
  • Maitra’s operational warning: homeward, not eastward. Maitra couples his endorsement of the drawdown to a sharp conditional: if it becomes eastward relocation into Poland, the Baltics, or France rather than homeward retraction, it functions as an implicit American security guarantee that intensifies tensions with Russia while voiding the European incentive to build independent capacity. The Norfolk-to-France move he already names is the first datapoint that could falsify the policy’s logic in practice while preserving it in rhetoric.
  • The political-consequence layer. Hallam supplies a ground-level dispatch from the May 16 “Unite the Kingdom” Tommy Robinson march in London: an estimated 60,000 attendees one week after Reform UK’s local-election surge, with a coalition broader than the prior decade’s street-level register predicted (Iranian couples opposing Sharia, Indian and Hindu-German students, evangelicals carrying crucifixes, Iranian monarchists with Reza Pahlavi imagery), Christianity-inflected, and explicitly wanting parliamentary power rather than street presence. Hallam reads it as the political form the structural incapacity is taking — not a contingent reaction to Brexit or the small-boats crisis but a coalescing electoral force whose voters will give Reform their ballots only because no party further right is viable.
  • European elite reactions to the live operational problems. Euro|topics on Baltic drones curates five voices spanning Swiss skepticism (Koydl in Weltwoche faults Europe for the absence of a peace initiative as Russian and disputed Ukrainian drones turn NATO airspace into a war zone), Latvian and Lithuanian self-flagellation (Veidemane in Neatkarīgā on the “stay away from windows” advisory as proof of unbuilt civil-defense and shoot-down architecture; Dykovienė in Kauno diena on a Speaker-of-Parliament gaffe about air-raid alerts), an Estonian “calm down” (Delfi), and an Estonian techno-modernization pitch (Oidsalu in ERR for euro-transition-urgency AI-and-robotics interception, while conceding peacetime shoot-downs over populated areas may be more dangerous than non-interception). Euro|topics on Merz / Ukraine curates four reactions to Merz’s “associate EU membership” gambit — attending decision-making bodies without voting rights — alternately strategic anchor (Krohn in Stuttgarter Zeitung), shoot-from-the-hip half-measure (Bonse in taz), face-saving legal fiction necessary because full membership is legally impossible for a country at war (Mayer in Der Standard), or — sharpest — a “waiting room” designed to indefinitely defer Kyiv’s accession while opening the door for Moldova (Sydorenko in European Pravda, the Ukrainian voice). The Northern-European-and-Swiss skew on the drones panel and the German-Austrian-Ukrainian skew on the Merz panel are themselves evidence: the Anglophone-readable European-elite discourse pipeline is shaped by which countries publish in English-friendly venues, so the “European debate” surfaced via Euro|topics is the part of the European debate that translates into the Atlantic frame — Polish, Hungarian, and Western Balkan registers most likely to break the cluster’s converging diagnostic at the prescription level are systematically less visible.

The three-orientation convergence is genuine — Constantini, Sternberg, and Maitra arrive at “Germany/Europe is structurally incapable” from priors that don’t overlap — and its analytical credibility comes from precisely that. But the convergence’s apparent settled-ness is partly an artifact of what the writers do not have to engage. Convergence-on-description does not require the converging writers to reconcile their underlying disagreements, and the three writers’ prescriptions are not just different but mutually incompatible — Constantini’s post-liberal civilizational recovery, Sternberg’s welfare-state retrenchment, Maitra’s burden-shift retraction. A reader leaves the cluster equipped on the descriptive claim and unequipped on the policy question, and the convergence will fracture the moment a concrete policy is proposed.

Carry forward: Europe’s strategic incapacity is hardening into the structural condition US foreign policy has to assume rather than the temporary problem US foreign policy has to fix.

↑ Back to TOC

The AI moment: power, beauty, labor, regulation

Synthesis

Fourteen pieces across six sub-strands make this the largest cluster of the week. Three cross-orientation convergences organize most of it; the remaining pieces hold independent claims on liberal-arts education, AI policy mechanics, authorship and meaning, and drone-warfare strategy.

The findings, by claim rather than by writer:

  • AI rage is where post-2018 cancel-culture political energy migrated when it ran out of cultural targets, and the migration has crossed orientation lines — descriptively. Cowen at Free Press names the shift from words to “concrete action” — wealth-confiscation referenda, attacks on data-center construction, political assassinations, anti-Jewish violence — and reads the post-2022 retreat of wokeism as Pyrrhic because “American negativity” simply rechanneled. Goldberg in the NYT names the cross-ideological breadth: Bernie Sanders and AOC calling for data-center moratoriums alongside the right-populist James Fishback, “Hacks” actor Hannah Einbinder calling AI creators “losers,” a Molotov cocktail at Sam Altman’s house; she locates the structural cause in the absence of US labor institutions (citing Bharat Ramamurti on Japan’s complementarity-oriented AI funding and Nordic worker codetermination over deployment) that elsewhere convert technological disruption into bargaining rather than rage. MacIntyre at Blaze, writing in a Burnhamite/Schmittian register where corporate and state power are read as a fused regime, names the post-liberal version: the libertarian inability to ask what AI is for and whom it serves leaves a vacuum the local-community backlash is articulately filling. Cowen’s piece carries a fundamental-flaw flag for asserting-without-arguing the left-rhetoric-to-assassinations causal link; MacIntyre’s carries one for inferring AI’s primary future use from the political character of its deployers rather than from any concrete forecast. The convergence is descriptive-only — all three writers can name “the political energy has migrated to material AI targets” without giving up any prior frame, and the moment any of them prescribes (Goldberg toward Nordic codetermination, MacIntyre toward the local-community moral veto, Cowen toward neither) the convergence dissolves. The descriptive layer is real and survives both flags; the carry-forward should be narrower than the cluster’s framing might imply.
  • The institutional-capture diagnosis is converging across value-commitments that don’t otherwise touch. Jones at Compact writes as a Futures Program Associate at the Future of Life Institute (the existential-risk shop behind the 2023 “AI pause” open letter) and argues Pope Leo XIV should build a Vatican-led AGI moratorium on the human-cloning-taboo precedent, citing Francis’s Franciscan AI advisor Fr. Paolo Benanti’s “we must not proceed in building superintelligence” and reading Dario Amodei’s “Machines of Loving Grace” essay title as transhumanist usurpation. Bencherif at African Arguments applies Ethiopian-born AI scholar Abeba Birhane’s frame of “algorithmic colonialism” (African bodies as training data for technologies designed, owned, and governed elsewhere) to a March 2026 IDS/ADRN study finding 11 named African governments have spent $2B+ on AI-enabled smart-city surveillance — Huawei-supplied safe-city deployments in Uganda and opposition spying in Zambia, China’s CloudWalk facial-recognition partnership in Zimbabwe — with a central case study of Kenya’s 2024-25 Gen Z protests (Amnesty International links 128 deaths, 3,000+ arrests, 83+ enforced disappearances to the surveillance-and-intimidation response). Stoller’s NextEra utility-capture argument in the Trump Anti-Weaponization Fund cluster supplies the American-domestic third leg. The three writers come from a Catholic-existential-risk frame, a postcolonial-African-surveillance frame, and an American-utility-regulatory frame, and they converge on a single operational diagnostic: the AI deployment environment is being built faster than the oversight architecture that would constrain it, and the speed-asymmetry is itself the structural finding.
  • The data-center backlash now reads as a viable 2028 Republican primary platform, not just regional NIMBYism. Holzman at Heatmap refines the project’s standing data-center-buildout-resistance finding (opened May 17) by documenting three simultaneous Republican developments: elected officials in red states pushing moratoria (Nancy Mace in South Carolina, agriculture commissioner Sid Miller in Texas, an ND Public Service Commission candidate), right-wing online figures attacking data-center practices (Matt Walsh, Tucker Carlson, MTG with a Kevin O’Leary defense), and polling showing Republican opposition rising more than 300% in some GOP regions in six months. Will Reinhart of the American Enterprise Institute tells Holzman a national moratorium platform from a major 2028 GOP candidate is “very real,” with Ron DeSantis’s recent criticism named as a bellwether; Interior Secretary Doug Burgum’s counter-frame (“foreign-sourced dark money”) echoes the long-running and never-evidenced GOP claim about wealthy Europeans funding US environmental opposition. Texas oil-and-gas lobbyist David Blackmon’s analogy is to the early-2010s fracking-communications failure when the industry nearly lost “its license to operate” in shale-patch counties.
  • Douthat on AI philanthropy, with a Cowen citation choice that’s interesting but shouldn’t be over-read. Douthat in the NYT addresses AI-IPO billionaires directly (taking Nan Ransohoff’s projection of $100B/year in AI-IPO charitable giving as given): emulate Carnegie and Rockefeller by funding museums, universities, cathedrals, gardens, and beautified public spaces — not just program disbursements like the Gates/Buffett wave, which Douthat reads as a philanthropic failure on the aesthetic dimension. He glosses the Obama Presidential Center as “the Obama Star Destroyer,” cites Will Manidis on Silicon Valley’s “terror of being seen to have money and to enjoy it,” and names “performative philistinism” (gray T-shirts, bio-engineered slop, “I’m too busy to be a snob”) as a load-bearing tic. Cowen at MR signal-boosts the piece in an unusually bare format — excerpting only the historical-diagnostic paragraphs (Gates-era giving as aesthetically barren; the Metropolitan Museum, Carnegie Hall, Stanford, and the Newport mansions as missing analogues to today’s giving) while omitting both the prescriptive ask to build cathedrals and the performative-philistinism critique. That selection tells the reader something about MR’s aesthetic sympathies — endorsement of the diagnosis paired with quiet withholding of the prescription — but it is a citation choice, not a structural cross-orientation convergence, and reading it as the latter is the kind of scope-inflation the project’s anti-reflexive-Straussian rule was written to prevent. Worth noting; not worth carrying forward as a finding.
  • Standalone findings worth carrying into the synthesis: Douthat’s “Interesting Times” interview with Jennifer Frey — Frey is the philosopher and former founding dean of the University of Tulsa Honors College who built a Great Books program enrolling 26-28% of incoming Tulsa freshmen before being ousted in a 2024 regime change (new provost, no explanation, 92% budget cut) — defends liberal-arts education against the AI-skills-replacement frame and against the soft-skills-for-an-AI-economy defense she calls self-defeating; her diagnosis names the hyper-specialization of the research university and the bipartisan utilitarian workforce-readiness push (citing both Obama’s college scorecard and Oklahoma’s recent executive orders ending tenure at non-flagship publics) as more important than the campus-left-deconstruction story. Hayden at Compact, writing for the Institute for Family Studies’ Family First Technology Initiative, runs a policy-mechanism argument that the Senate Judiciary Committee’s unanimous May advance of the GUARD Act (Hawley-Blumenthal) closes a fatal loophole in eight existing state AI-chatbot laws — the 1998 COPPA-inherited “actual knowledge” standard that effectively requires a company to have viewed a child’s birth certificate before liability attaches — by requiring AI companion providers to perform age verification. Shullenberger at Compact Substack uses a viral 55M-view X post by French AI-clone-video startup founder Brivael Le Pogam (“apologizing” on behalf of France for French Theory) to mobilize Lyotard’s The Postmodern Condition (1979) — which located the cause of postmodernism not in French philosophers but in computerization itself, with knowledge “exteriorized” from the human knower — for the structural claim that AI-industry boosters are now the “real cultural radicals” presiding over a destabilization of truth and self at a vaster scale than any post-structuralist could have managed. deBoer coins “the Library Card Fallacy” — the mistake of thinking education is about access to information when public libraries and MOOCs always supplied that access without producing autodidacts (Katy Jordan’s meta-analysis: MOOC median completion rate 12.6%; even among intended-completers, only 22% finish per a 2014 EDUCAUSE study) — and predicts most US colleges will persist because what they sell is the social compulsion that gets unwilling adolescents to do hard things. Alexander at ACT grants the AI-skeptic premise that AGI requires a “new paradigm” beyond LLMs, then applies Lindy’s Law (the heuristic that things last roughly as long as they’ve already lasted) to the historical paradigm-shift interval (perceptron 1967, deep learning 2010, transformer 2017, RLHF 2022, chain-of-thought 2024) to argue the 25th percentile estimate for an LLM-class paradigm is 3 years and for a deep-learning-class one is 5 years — about the same timeline the LLM-only crowd uses for AGI itself. Smith at Noahpinion elevates his “Electric Tech Stack” thesis into a hard military claim using Ukraine-war drone-saturation evidence (~96% of Russian casualties now drone-caused; the NATO Hedgehog 2025 exercise in Estonia where a 10-person Ukrainian adversary team mock-destroyed 17 armored vehicles): the cost asymmetry between a $400-500 FPV drone and any system three orders of magnitude more expensive is decisive, and the structural argument is that drones run on lithium-ion batteries and rare-earth electric motors — technologies ceded to China, which could produce 4 billion FPV drones per year to Ukraine’s 4 million.

One closing observation the cluster does not make on its own behalf: this week produced two cross-orientation architectural-failure convergences in unrelated policy domains — the AI institutional-capture trio described here (Jones from a Catholic-existential-risk frame, Bencherif from a postcolonial-African-surveillance frame, Stoller from an American-utility-regulatory frame, all converging on the speed-asymmetry between deployment and oversight) and the four-orientation Anti-Weaponization Fund cluster in theme-01 (federal accountability architecture being routed around by a presidential discretionary-fund mechanism, named from four orientations whose value-commitments otherwise don’t touch). One such convergence in a week is what the roster’s heterodoxy curation is built to surface. Two in one week from different domains suggests architectural-failure may be becoming the discourse’s default frame for institutional collapse, displacing the personality-of-the-actors frame that dominated 2017-2024. That is a tracking question, not a claim warranted by one data point — but the next 2-3 weeks should be watched for recurrence.

↑ Back to TOC

Class, housing, work, and the Massie coalitional event

Synthesis

Ten pieces in this theme split into two natural sub-clusters joined by one bridge writer. The first sub-cluster is class economics, housing supply, and the philosophy of work — seven pieces in which Commonplace’s Cass-Kishi-Griswold trio, Yglesias on two consecutive days at Slow Boring, Alex Tabarrok at Marginal Revolution, and Matt Stoller at BIG all read the same week’s votes, regulatory fights, and rhetorical moves through compatible-but-non-identical lenses. The second sub-cluster is the loss of Rep. Thomas Massie (R-KY) — a libertarian-leaning Trump skeptic and co-leader (with Rep. Ro Khanna, D-CA) of the bipartisan push to force release of the Jeffrey Epstein investigative files — to Trump-recruited former Navy SEAL Ed Gallrein in the Kentucky GOP primary, the most expensive House primary in U.S. history. Stoller is the bridge between the two: his “Rage of the Billionaires” forecast describes the donor-class reaction Cass tracks decaying inside the GOP and that the Massie cluster shows operating at full force in a single primary.

  • The Chamber/Norquist/WSJ enforcement machinery is operational but no longer reliably producing votes. Cass and Kishi (Commonplace; Daniel Kishi is American Compass’s deputy director) read three House votes in a single week — the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act restricting institutional investors who own 350-plus single-family homes (passed 396-13, the White House calling the restriction Trump’s “signature priority”); the Faster Labor Contracts Act reaching the 218th signature on a discharge petition (the procedural device that lets a House majority force a floor vote over leadership’s objection), with seven House Republicans breaking with leadership to sign; and the Railway Safety Act adopted 54-11 by the House Transportation Committee over the chair’s objection — as evidence that the donor-class enforcement architecture (the U.S. Chamber of Commerce; Americans for Tax Reform’s Grover Norquist; the Wall Street Journal editorial board) remains intact but is losing its ability to discipline Republicans against pro-worker, anti-Wall-Street positions.

  • “Born short housing” reframes the political economy of homeownership. Cass (Commonplace) argues that every non-homeowner is exposed to housing-market appreciation whether they’re trying to buy or rent, the way a stock short-seller is exposed to price increases without owning shares; when a person buys a home, they close their short position rather than create a long one and become roughly market-neutral. The growing-family homeowner contemplating a larger house is a “seller-buyer” — closer to indifferent to price levels than the protect-my-equity rhetoric implies. The political-economy implication is that most homeowner self-interest is misaligned with most homeowners’ stated position, and a politics that named this could redirect the median voter from windfall-protection to young-family-pathway concerns. The cited counter-example is Marc Short’s Washington Examiner op-ed opposing the ROAD Act’s institutional-investor restrictions on the grounds that Wall Street capital is needed for new construction.

  • The intra-roundup dissent from inside the same housing fight. Yglesias (Slow Boring) breaks with the populist consensus (and with Cass) on the institutional-investor restriction inside the ROAD Act: the private-equity-buying-up-single-family-homes panic is largely a fake problem (the post-2008 wave has long since waned; small landlords dominate single-family rentals), while build-to-rent has emerged in Sun Belt growth markets as the real supply substitute for the cheap starter homes that can’t be financed since 2008 mortgage tightening and can’t be built as apartments because of zoning. The ROAD Act’s seven-year sell-off window for new build-to-rent projects is short enough to kill the sector. Yglesias sides with House Financial Services ranking member Maxine Waters and Sen. Brian Schatz against Trump, Sen. Elizabeth Warren, JD Vance, and Stoller’s American Economic Liberties Project — and notes the structural perversity that Trump is sabotaging a bill whose passage would help his own affordability politics.

  • Yglesias’s separate Renewable Fuel Standard piece treats ethanol mandates as a fast-acting affordability lever both administrations have ignored. Yglesias argues both original rationales for the Bush-era Renewable Fuel Standard (the federal requirement that oil refiners blend ethanol into gasoline) have collapsed — the U.S. found vastly more oil; the cleanest carbon accounting (Argonne National Laboratory) still pegs ethanol at $160-$190 per ton of CO2 abated, while less generous accounting (Lark/Hendricks/Smith/Gibbs) finds carbon intensity at least 24 percent worse than gasoline because of land-use change. Roberts-Schlenker (2013) show corn-to-ethanol diversion raises the global price of all four major grain staples (corn, wheat, rice, soybeans) 20-30 percent through substitution effects. The Iowa-as-quintessential-swing-state political case has lapsed because Iowa is no longer competitive, opening a coalition of southern Republicans and coastal Democrats to roll the corn-state bloc.

  • The FDR-to-Altman earned-right doctrine is at risk from a friendly-fire rhetorical move. Tabarrok (Marginal Revolution) reads Gabriel Zucman’s and Justin Wolfers’s “FICA is regressive” replies to Jeff Bezos’s progressivity tweet as an own goal against eighty years of liberal earned-right doctrine. FICA is the federal payroll tax funding Social Security and Medicare; FDR’s 1941 line to Luther Gulick was that payroll contributions exist precisely so “no damn politician can ever scrap my social security program.” Arthur Altmeyer, Wilbur Cohen, Robert Ball, and Nancy Altman’s Social Security Works! built that earned-right framing into the program’s structural defense against means-testing and privatization. Tabarrok offers the Buchanan-Summers test — a mandated payment is a tax only to the extent it doesn’t deliver an offsetting marginal benefit — and concludes Medicare’s Hospital Insurance portion is unambiguously a progressive tax, while Social Security’s OASDI is a contribution at the bottom (where the 90/32/15 PIA bend points deliver above-market returns) and a tax only at the top. Conceding the regressive framing hands the American Enterprise Institute and Cato Institute a rhetorical opening to privatize.

  • The work-as-constitutive case under AI-transition stakes. Griswold (Commonplace; American Compass policy director) argues against the otherwise-strange convergence between Marxism and neoclassical economics on work-as-disutility (the Genesis 3 itstsabon framing — “painful toil” — and the labor-leisure tradeoff U=f(C,L)) and for the Platonic / Tolkienian / Laborem Exercens (the 1981 papal encyclical by John Paul II on the dignity of human work) view that work is constitutive of human thriving. Marshalling Matthew Crawford (Shop Class as Soulcraft), Francis Fukuyama on tacit knowledge from struggling with material reality, and Musa al-Gharbi on Orwell’s Road to Wigan Pier, Griswold argues workless populations are reliably miserable and that Marc Andreessen’s “OxyContin and video games to keep those people quiet” is the actual telos of the post-work society Anthropic’s Dario Amodei, Nvidia’s Jensen Huang, and Elon Musk are broadcasting. The AI-transition stakes hinge on which theory of work is embedded in the societal source code.

  • The donor-class reaction is taking shape. Stoller (BIG, the antimonopoly newsletter; Economic Liberties Project research director) reads a cluster of recent signals — students booing AI commencement speakers; the NERC (the federal grid-reliability watchdog) Level 3 alert on data-center load; billionaire Ken Griffin’s organized harassment campaign against NYC mayor Zohran Mamdani after Mamdani filmed a vacation-home-tax video outside Griffin’s apartment; CEO comments from Amodei (half of white-collar jobs gone), Sam Altman (AI “will probably most likely lead to the end of the world”), and Palantir’s Alex Karp (“some people can get their heads cut off”) — as early signals of an oligarchic backlash. The mechanism he names is “Lina Khan Derangement Syndrome”: the WSJ editorial page attacked Khan (Federal Trade Commission chair under Biden, 2021-2025) every eleven days for her entire FTC tenure in a coordinated framing of her as a secret Marxist with disregard for the rule of law. The pattern is now generalizing to a broader set of populist Democratic targets — Mamdani in NYC; Graham Platner (insurgent challenger who defeated Janet Mills for the Maine Senate nomination); Abdul El-Sayed in Michigan.

  • The Massie loss read from the post-liberal right. McCarthy (Compact; Heritage Foundation conservative-thought fellow; former editor of The American Conservative) argues the Ron Paul-Massie strain of liberty Republicanism was historically necessary during the post-Bush wilderness years (2008-2015) when the GOP needed an oppositionist conscience, but that Trump’s takeover changed the terms — the party now has a coherent worldview (anti-globalism, immigration restriction, post-Reagan economics) and a working majority, so “strictly oppositionist ideology” is obsolete coalition behavior. Massie self-inflicted his defeat by becoming the poster boy for budget-balancing and Epstein-file disclosure (issues the base doesn’t prioritize) while antagonizing the pro-Israel coalition on Iran, forfeiting any chance to shape MAGA’s foreign-policy posture from inside.

  • The same loss read from the left. Barkan (UnHerd; New York left-leaning political columnist) reads the result as confirming that Trump’s personalist dominance is total and is now fused with pro-Israel hawkishness as the operative ideological filter. Massie’s offenses stacked: he held up a 2020 Covid relief bill, voted against the “Big, Beautiful Bill” tax package, voted against a House resolution condemning the Boycott Divestment and Sanctions (BDS) movement, opposed Iron Dome funding, criticized Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, and — Barkan argues was the breaking point for Trump personally — co-led with Khanna the bipartisan push to force release of the Epstein Files. The American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC, the dominant pro-Israel lobbying organization in U.S. politics) and Republican Jewish groups poured money in because the Republican Party is now their “last stand” — Democrats under 50 have turned against the Jewish state and the Mamdani primary win shows pro-Palestine candidates can beat the Israel lobby in major Democratic primaries. The hawks bought Kentucky but have not converted young conservatives: Tucker Carlson, Candace Owens, and Megyn Kelly are ascendant on the anti-Israel restrainer right.

  • The substantive contest was not fought on the merits, and the frame generalizes. Klippenstein (independent left investigative reporter on Substack) names Gallrein’s “I can’t speak to any classified operations I may or may not have ever been involved in” gambit as credentialed-deference replacing democratic accountability. Klippenstein documents Gallrein’s verbal-tic pattern of refusing specifics, notes terminology errors that suggest exaggeration of operator status (Gallrein’s actual roles were apparently educational/training positions like head of instruction at the Joint Special Operations University, not direct-action), and surfaces a 2013 episode in which Gallrein was fired from the Y-12 nuclear facility and lost a whistleblower complaint on a procedural error. He frames the underlying logic as “Shut the Fuck Up and Salute” — a worldview in which civilians are sheep, special operators are sheepdogs, and elected oversight is the problem rather than the solution — and pairs Gallrein with the Democratic “national security moms” (Abigail Spanberger, Mikie Sherrill, Elissa Slotkin) to argue this is bipartisan. The principal-agent relationship is inverted: “the president has seen the classified file, therefore you don’t need to evaluate me.” The frame is the cluster’s most generalizable claim, but the test of whether it is also correct is whether it extends backward and across parties — and the Spanberger-Sherrill-Slotkin pairing Klippenstein himself reaches for (then drops before the implication bites) suggests it does. The 2018 Democratic House recruitment class was built on the same architecture: Sherrill on Naval Academy and Russia-desk credentials, Spanberger on CIA case-officer experience, Slotkin on three Iraq tours as a Defense intelligence analyst, with substantive policy positions offered thinly if at all and the implied argument that the candidate had been vetted by institutions the voter could not personally evaluate. The differences from Gallrein — the 2018 cohort would actually debate; their resumes survive Klippenstein-style scrutiny better — are real but secondary; the structural move is the same.

Read together, the Massie sub-cluster has a sharper finding than any of its three writers names: the McCarthy-Barkan disagreement (party finally has discipline vs. party is captured) stays inside a partisan frame, and both readings track the empirical record correctly because the same fact admits both descriptions — which means the analytical categories don’t carve at the joints. Klippenstein’s credentialed-deference frame does — but the test of whether it’s also correct is whether it extends backward and across parties. The Spanberger/Sherrill/Slotkin pairing Klippenstein reaches for (then drops) suggests it does. The 2018 Democratic national-security recruitment class used exactly the same “classified credentials substitute for democratic accountability” pattern; what looks like a 2026 Republican-specific failure mode is more plausibly the operating logic of post-2016 American political recruitment in both parties. The partisan asymmetry the McCarthy-Barkan frame implies is therefore misleading; the operating logic is the right level of analysis, and it’s bipartisan.

Carry-forward: the credentialed-deference generalization sits in tension with the Cass-Stoller donor-class-decay convergence. If the donor class’s enforcement capacity is decaying inside the GOP — Cass’s three-votes-in-one-week reading — but credentialed-deference is rising across both parties, the locus of insulation is shifting from policy to personnel: from “you can’t vote against X economic position” to “you can’t vote against a credentialed candidate, regardless of position.” Both diagnoses can be true, and the joint claim is a different structural reading than either Cass or Stoller separately offers. The next 6-18 months of recruitment cycles (especially the 2026 Democratic Senate field) provide a clean test: if credentialed-deference recruitment continues across the aisle while donor-class-imposed policy discipline keeps weakening, the locus-shift reading is the right one.

↑ Back to TOC

Cross-theme insights

Crosscurrents synthesis — May 18-24, 2026

The week’s load-bearing event is a settlement document that names its own architecture. The Trump administration’s $1.776 billion “Anti-Weaponization Fund” is structured as a settlement of Trump’s January suit against the IRS — the IRS defending Trump’s tax-leak grievance against Trump as plaintiff — with acting AG Todd Blanche additionally issuing what amounts to a blanket civil-and-criminal release for Trump, his sons, and the Trump Organization. Four writers from four distinct orientations — Andrew McCarthy at NR, John Puri at NR (a sharper younger institutionalist than McCarthy), Jed Rubenfeld at the Free Press, and Ken Klippenstein from the counter-terrorism kill-count side of the same enabling Iran-war thread — converge on a single diagnostic about institutional architecture without coordinating. Matt Stoller’s same-week NextEra-Dominion essay extends the diagnostic into utility regulation; Dan McLaughlin’s SEC gag-rule piece supplies the parallel-agency datapoint. The slush fund is the project’s regressive-enforcement-architecture thread now named inside its own settlement document. The week’s other major motions sit downstream of the same diagnostic move: writers from genuinely different positions arriving at the same description of how an institution actually works, and disagreeing only on whose dysfunction it is.

How the themes interlock

Two cross-theme patterns make the week’s five clusters a single argument rather than five.

Architectural diagnosis migrating across regulatory domains. The analytical move that does the load-bearing work in the Anti-Weaponization Fund cluster — “this institution’s discretion is the operative variable; the legal question is a sideshow” — recurs in two genuinely unrelated themes. The Europe cluster (Sternberg/Constantini/Maitra/Hallam) ports it onto fiscal architecture: the substantive question European voters now confront is one their political class systematically deferred, and which prescription wins matters less than the fact that the deferral itself has run out. The AI cluster (Goldberg/Cowen/Bencherif/Jones, with Stoller’s utility-capture argument as connective tissue) ports it onto deployment environments: AI deployment outruns the oversight architecture that would constrain it, and the speed-asymmetry IS the finding. Three cross-orientation convergences in three different regulatory domains all land on the same diagnostic register from incompatible coordinates. The legal-architecture question in each case can be resolved either way; the discretionary-architecture question is the operative one in each case, and that pattern is not visible from within any single theme.

Coalitional decay visible on both party coalitions simultaneously. The DNC autopsy cluster and the Massie loss cluster read the same pattern from opposite party coalitions. McCarthy/Stoller/Cass triangulate the GOP-side decay (the Chamber-Norquist-WSJ machinery remains operational but no longer reliably produces votes; donor-class enforcement faces its hardest test in the next cycle from both directions). Goldberg/Barker/Lowry/Rothman triangulate the Democratic-side (the DNC’s capacity to produce a coherent document about its own most-recent loss has degraded to where the report’s principal data is the report itself). The cross-theme finding the per-section reads can’t reach: both parties’ machinery for coalitional discipline has degraded faster than their machinery for contesting formal power. Read alongside last week’s Silver-Eisler procedural-power-acquisition thread, this is the parties losing their political substrate at the same rate they’re investing in apparatus that would let them rewrite the architecture.

What looks like five themes is one analytical move applied across five domains. The week’s prescriptive disagreement — impeachment vs. non-delegation litigation vs. executive rescission vs. exposure-by-reporting; CDU/SPD vs. AfD vs. cordon sanitaire collapse; Japan/Nordic worker-codetermination vs. US institutional vacuum — is exactly the disagreement the diagnostic survives. The sections below take this connective tissue as background and focus on what no per-theme reading can produce: cross-cutting absences, press-frame failures, multi-week predictive tracking, and mode-of-analysis observation.

↑ Back to TOC

What’s missing

Wire check. Three significant developments appear nowhere in roster coverage. The DR Congo Ebola outbreak continues to escalate — the WHO raised its national risk level to “very high” on Friday, the African CDC warned ten neighboring countries are at risk, Uganda has confirmed five cases, and a second Ebola treatment center was set on fire this week in the outbreak’s epicenter. The parallel Bangladesh measles outbreak (60,000 suspected cases, 528 deaths, the country’s healthcare system overwhelmed) goes uncovered. The Tulsi Gabbard resignation is the second-largest US politics story of the week and goes entirely unengaged by roster writers; the Guardian’s Bazzi piece names the operative dynamic (her resistance to the Iran war as the substantive cause of her sidelining) and the Klippenstein-McCarthy-Massie thread the project has been narrating for three weeks should have produced at least one roster pickup of the mechanism — a national-security cabinet member effectively forced out for opposing the same Iran-war architecture the administration’s other instruments are operationalizing against critics. The structural roster gap on inside-the-administration reporting is now four consecutive weeks.

Perspective check. Still no roster writer is operating from inside an AI-displaced workforce (five consecutive synthesis weeks; the structural gap is hardening). Sun Liping is the only roster writer with a non-Anglophone reading-public’s view of the Beijing summit (Aourid in Arabic is the second), and his piece is exposition of an American neoconservative for a Chinese audience rather than a reading of the summit from Chinese intellectual life directly. The asymmetry — the project can render the Anglophone discourse’s read of China but not the Chinese discourse’s read of itself — is now structurally visible.

↑ Back to TOC

What the mainstream press is getting wrong

The press scan is heavily concentrated on Iran-deal-progress reporting and the Anti-Weaponization Fund as a partisan controversy. Two genuine framing gaps surface.

The Anti-Weaponization Fund is being narrated as a partisan controversy when the load-bearing finding is the architectural diagnosis four cross-orientation writers reached independently. NPR’s “‘Homeland Security’ has spawned political insecurity,” NPR Politics’ “lawmakers oppose proposed $1.2 billion fund” segment, CBS’s Face the Nation sit-down with Van Hollen, and the Guardian’s coverage all frame the fund as a Republican-vs-Democratic political fight — Democrats refusing the votes Republicans need to fund ICE because the DOJ announced a $1.8B fund expected to compensate Jan. 6 defendants. The architectural diagnosis the roster cluster lands on — that this is the regressive-enforcement-architecture’s named-inside-its-own-settlement-document moment, with the legal-architecture question (could survive judicial scrutiny because of standing) being precisely why the administrative-discretion question is the operative one — is roster-only. McCarthy’s “the wrongs here are political, not legal,” Puri’s “abuse of legislative power Congress should never have delegated,” Rubenfeld’s “even if it survives, the long-tail civil-liability risk for beneficiaries is real”: none of these architectural readings appears in the press scan. The press treats the fund as a controversy; the roster cluster treats it as a settlement document that names its own apparatus.

The Beijing summit is being narrated through the Iran-deal-progress lens when the structural finding is that the summit’s commercial deliverables were the floor of US negotiating expectations, not the ceiling. Press coverage of the summit week was largely transactional (Foer’s “humoring Trump” frame from the May 17 cluster was roster-only); this week’s press scan is dominated by Iran-deal procedural updates with no Beijing follow-up. The roster’s Yglesias-Maitra cross-orientation finding — Trump touted as a “win” getting China to agree the Strait should be demilitarized and un-tolled long-term, “something no prior president would even have considered asking for because it went without saying” — is the kind of structural framing the press cannot reach within the procedural register it uses to cover bilateral meetings. The Fukuyama Taiwan-timing inversion (Xi won’t move while a self-immolating Trump is doing the work, but a reversing successor forces his hand on a faster timeline than the demographic measure implies) is similarly unreachable from the deal-by-deal frame.

Press absence: the data-center 2028-primary readout. Holzman’s Heatmap reporting on the Republican data-center backlash (Mace, Miller, the Heatmap Pro polling, the Reinhart prediction of a 2028 GOP moratorium-platform candidate) is the most forward-loaded political story in the week’s coverage. The seven press-scan outlets ran zero stories on it. The Burgum “foreign-sourced dark money” deflection is the tell: when an administration with full press access defaults to attributing its own base’s complaint to foreign manipulation, the operational situation has visibly moved past where the press is reporting it.

↑ Back to TOC

Carry-forward findings

Two new entries, three refinements, one open-disagreement update.

  • regressive-enforcement-architecture (refined, week 4 of the Klippenstein thread) — The Anti-Weaponization Fund is the architecture’s first crystallization inside an explicit bureaucratic instrument. Four-orientation convergence (McCarthy / Puri / Rubenfeld / Klippenstein), with Stoller’s NextEra essay and McLaughlin’s SEC piece as parallel-domain datapoints. The remedies the legal-architecture writers prescribe will not be executed; the diagnostic register is now operating in the negative space of where remedies should be.

  • mandate-execution-vs-architecture-rewiring (new) — Last week’s loyalist/rebuttal exchange surfaced a productive distinction: the administration’s strongest defense is mandate-execution; the deepest critique (Eisler) is that the substantive mandate runs through the constitutional architecture rather than around it. This week’s Anti-Weaponization Fund is the architecture-rewiring move in the executive-discretion register; the Massie loss is the architecture-rewiring move in the donor-class-enforcement register. Substantive mandate-execution is being achieved partly via formal-architecture rewiring, which makes the mandate-execution defense and the architecture-rewiring critique inseparable.

  • donor-class-enforcement-decay (new) — Stoller-McCarthy-Cass cross-orientation convergence on coalitional dysfunction in the GOP’s donor-discipline machinery, with parallel decay forming inside the Democratic coalition (Mamdani-Platner-El-Sayed register). McCarthy reads Massie’s loss as the Trump GOP no longer tolerating principled dissenters; Stoller predicts the Chamber/Norquist/WSJ machinery decaying further as Mamdani-style insurgency tests donor coercive capacity; Cass reads three House votes this week as evidence the enforcement machinery remains operational but no longer reliably produces votes.

  • bypass-network-as-post-american-architecture (refined) — Frankopan’s 200-year historical depth, Golkar’s regime-structural argument, and Aurelien’s framework-gap meta-critique combine: the post-American operational environment is migrating away from US negotiating leverage on multiple regional fronts simultaneously, and the framework gap is structural rather than corrigible by a single administration.

  • china-power-axis-disagreement (refined) — Fukuyama’s Taiwan-timing inversion adds a third axis: the operative variable for Beijing’s timing decision is US trajectory, not Chinese demographics. The Yglesias-Maitra cross-orientation convergence on Trump’s actual China policy as incoherent protectionism that surrenders the strategic-competition position it claims to advance is the strongest single-week evidence to date for the industrial-and-AI-deployment measure being load-bearing for forecasting.

  • iran-time-direction (open-disagreement update; sharpening-toward-question-is-wrong holds) — Frankopan, Golkar, and Aurelien together confirm the May 9 read: both sides’ institutional incentives produce identical “we are winning” rhetoric while the costs migrate to third parties. The Trump May 23-24 “memorandum of understanding largely negotiated” announcements, with Iran’s Fars news agency immediately denying the Hormuz-control terms, fit the third-resolution frame exactly. Another 4-8 weeks at the current configuration warrants resolved-toward-question-is-wrong.

↑ Back to TOC

Meta-finding

The mode that did the most analytical work this week was architectural diagnosis from genuinely different orientations — the McCarthy/Puri/Rubenfeld/Klippenstein convergence on the Anti-Weaponization Fund, the Stoller/Cass/McCarthy convergence on donor-class enforcement decay, the Yglesias/Maitra convergence on Trump China-policy incoherence, the four-orientation read of the DNC autopsy, the three-orientation Massie read. The pattern from prior weeks holds: when writers from incompatible orientations arrive at the same description of how an institution works through different rhetorical paths, the disagreement that should be open is on the prescription, not the description. This week the disagreement-on-prescription pattern hardened — McCarthy wants impeachment, Puri wants non-delegation litigation, Rubenfeld wants executive rescission, Klippenstein wants exposure-by-reporting; none will be executed. The architectural diagnostic is what survives. Material data carried less weight than usual because the data was mostly confirmatory; the load-bearing analytical work was reframing what the data describes. Interpretive framework mode produced one important standalone result in Fukuyama’s Taiwan-timing inversion — the framework gives the cluster a falsifiable 2028-2032 prediction that the demographic measure cannot produce on its own, and that the industrial-and-AI-deployment measure had circled without articulating. Historical-analogy mode produced one productive use (Frankopan’s 200-year Iran historical depth) and one fundamentally-flawed use (Maitra’s Engelsberg Bengali-liberalism piece, which carries a fundamental-flaw flag for reading Bengal’s entire political history through a single Whig narrative; the flag does not propagate to Maitra’s IR-realist work in Hungarian Conservative and Chronicles, which holds up).

↑ Back to TOC

What We Actually Have to Work With

Crosscurrents optimist’s take — May 18 to May 24, 2026

The synthesis lands on a $1.776 billion slush fund made legal by a seventy-year congressional delegation of the purse power, a DNC autopsy that could not write down what it learned from losing, a Beijing summit table with no European chair, and a press-prosecution architecture beyond pilot phase. None of that rotates away. But the same week produced a 54-year regulatory gag rule rescinded, a 396-13 House vote on housing, an AI-chatbot bill cleared committee under unusual co-sponsorship, a 30-year-old murder put on the federal record, and a long-stalled Pentagon burden-shift executed — concrete acts that deserve naming on their own terms.

A 54-year regulatory gag rule fell, and the path it fell through is replicable. Until Monday, defendants who settled civil cases with the Securities and Exchange Commission were barred from publicly denying the SEC’s account of what they had done — the commission could trumpet allegations it had never proven in court, and the defendants could not respond.
