The architecture survived since 1972 because the SEC never tried to enforce it; under strict-scrutiny free-speech review it knew it would lose. The New Civil Liberties Alliance litigated the issue for eight years and had a cert petition pending in Powell v. SEC when the commission rescinded the rule rather than test it. What broke it was the prospect of losing in front of the Supreme Court; the institution that constructed that prospect was a small nonprofit law firm working from a doctrine that does not require which-party-holds-the-agency to function. The same asymmetry between regulator speech and regulated-party speech exists across the regulatory state, sustained by policies that survive because they have never been litigated. The SEC case is the existence proof that those asymmetries are now in play.

A named regulatory architecture for AI is being assembled in venues the discourse has not been watching. The Senate Judiciary Committee unanimously advanced the GUARD Act — co-sponsored by Josh Hawley and Richard Blumenthal, two senators whose ideological distance is among the largest in the chamber — closing the loophole that has made eight states’ AI-chatbot laws unenforceable. Every state law inherited the 1998 Children’s Online Privacy Protection Act’s “actual knowledge” standard requiring a company to have effectively viewed a child’s birth certificate before liability attaches; the GUARD Act requires age verification on AI companions instead. It is the first federal AI law with both populist-right and progressive-left sponsorship that targets a concrete harm — lawsuits over teen suicides allegedly encouraged by companion chatbots — with a verifiable enforcement mechanism. The Vatican is building its end of the same architecture: the International Theological Commission’s “Quo Vadis, Humanitas?” is the first Magisterial document to address artificial general intelligence, on the model of the human-cloning case, which built a durable two-decade taboo via religious authority, domestic laws, and non-binding multilateral statements. AI policy is not waiting for a constituency before the first laws get written.

The data-center backlash crossing into 2028 primary politics is democratic feedback functioning on infrastructure decisions at the scale where they actually get made. Republican opposition to data centers rose more than 300 percent in some GOP regions over six months. A South Carolina gubernatorial primary candidate, a Texas agriculture commissioner, and a North Dakota Public Service Commission candidate are running on moratoria; an AEI fellow told Heatmap a national moratorium platform from a major 2028 Republican presidential candidate is “very real.” Interior Secretary Doug Burgum’s “foreign-sourced dark money” attribution is the tell: when an administration explains its own base’s opposition as foreign manipulation, the opposition is broad enough to need explaining away. The McCormick “covenant” framing — data centers as net energy contributors to the grids they draw from, closed-loop water systems, commitments to local tax base and local workers — is the operational template emerging from the same pressure, and hyperscale projects will negotiate against it going forward.

A 30-year-old murder is now in the federal legal record. In February 1996, Cuban MiGs ambushed two unarmed Brothers to the Rescue Cessnas over international waters off Cuba and killed four men: Carlos Costa, Armando Alejandre Jr., Mario de la Peña, and Pablo Morales. The 2003 indictment stopped at the operational officers. This week’s superseding filing reaches up the chain of command to Raúl Castro himself and five co-defendants. Castro is 94 and will not be extradited. The point is the durable, citable, public account — cockpit recordings of pilots celebrating, the ICAO’s confirmation that the planes were over international waters, and the corrected record against the 2020 Netflix Wasp Network fictionalization of the spies as patriotic underdogs and the dead men as a subplot. The political timing is not coincidental; that is independent of what the document accomplishes as a record that will outlast its instrumental use.

A 396-13 housing vote and a discharge petition on labor-contract rules are evidence the donor-class veto no longer reliably produces Republican votes. The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act restricting institutional investors who own 350 or more single-family homes passed the House 396-13 against an explicit Chamber of Commerce position. The Faster Labor Contracts Act reached 218 discharge-petition signatures with seven House Republicans breaking leadership. The Railway Safety Act passed the House Transportation Committee 54-11 over the chair’s objection. The bills face specific critiques — the build-to-rent provision risks freezing a 7-percent-of-new-construction segment without a clear replacement supply — but the structural fact, that three pieces of legislation the donor-class enforcers had long held off all moved in a single week with cross-coalition margins, is the political-economy update.

The European force-posture drawdown finally happened. The Pentagon cancelled the 2nd Armored Brigade Combat Team’s Poland rotation, reduced troop presence in Germany, and shifted elements from Norfolk to France — the first material burden-shift since the late 1990s, when U.S. European troop strength fell from 300,000 to 60,000. Germany’s 45th Panzer Brigade in Lithuania is the corresponding European forward-deployment, and Merz’s “associate EU membership” proposal for Ukraine signals to Kyiv and Moscow that European integration is not waiting for the war’s end. The burden-shift consensus has finally produced operational reorientations.

What the synthesis has right, and the path. The slush-fund finding is real, the Beijing summit was as bad as the synthesis says, the press-prosecution architecture is operating, and the DNC autopsy is the evidence of party dysfunction the synthesis names. The optimist read does not refute any of it. What the same evidence supports is that the institutional pathways still working — free-speech challenges to legacy regulatory asymmetries, cross-coalition Senate sponsorship on AI harms, voter-pressure feedback into infrastructure templates, 30-year prosecutorial follow-through, House cross-coalition majorities, and allied-and-American burden-shift converging at the same moment — are doing what they are supposed to do in venues the synthesis’s diagnoses do not reach. The path: doctrine challenges propagate, the GUARD-Act template gets used for the next AI harm before the displacement crisis arrives, the covenant template becomes the price of entry for hyperscale projects, cross-coalition majorities form again the next time a donor-class veto weakens, and the European burden-shift compounds rather than reverses. Each is observable within two to four years; none requires the synthesis to be wrong about the pressures it has named.

↑ Back to TOC

What the Administration Would Say

Crosscurrents loyalist’s take — May 18 to May 24, 2026

This week’s synthesis reads as a chain of converging structural indictments — the Anti-Weaponization Fund as institutional capture, the DNC autopsy as system-wide breakdown, Massie’s defeat as classified-credentials displacing democratic accountability, the Beijing summit as strategic-retreat optics, the data-center backlash as administration failure. Re-anchored to the strategic logic an electorate ratified in November 2024, the same evidence describes a presidency executing a mandate the previous bipartisan consensus refused to take seriously. The work this week is to articulate what survives the strongest synthesis-side critiques.

Start with the Anti-Weaponization Fund, where the synthesis lands hardest and where the first move is to concede the load-bearing problem. Acting AG Todd Blanche’s blanket civil-and-criminal release for the Trump plaintiffs is structurally indefensible — pardons cover criminal liability and require political-cost commitments; this release covers both criminal and civil, requires no commitment, and runs through manufactured plaintiff-defendant identity. Defenders should not paper over it. What remains, after the concession, is the architecture McCarthy and Puri are actually describing: the Judgment Fund as a 1956 unbounded delegation, the post-1976 expansion to compromise settlements, the Biden-era McCabe/Strzok/Page settlements as the established bilateral pattern within the same architecture, and Letitia James’s New York fraud judgment as the cross-party demonstration that politically-targeted settlements are the bipartisan default rather than a Trump invention. Rubenfeld’s own concession — the fund may technically survive judicial scrutiny because no one obvious has standing — is the load-bearing legal claim three writers from three orientations converge on. The convergence is more precisely on this is procedurally legitimate and structurally awful, and that distinction is the loyalist’s opening: the wrong is congressional and bipartisan, not unilateral and Trump-specific. The remedies McCarthy prescribes (impeachment and oversight) and Puri prescribes (non-delegation litigation against the Judgment Fund itself) are both about Congress, the branch the synthesis would prefer to read as a victim. Pointing out that Congress could repair the damage acknowledges the damage — and acknowledges where institutional responsibility for repair actually sits.

The Beijing summit deliverables are where the loyalist position is sharpest. A $14B Boeing aircraft order, the partial GE engine reentry, the rare-earth export-license easing the previous administration could not extract across four years — Xi did not hold a summit of this form for Biden at all. The Yglesias frame reads the goods as “commodities Xi was happy to buy anyway”; that is the wrong description of airframes and engines, which are precisely the sectors where China has a domestic-substitution program (C919) and where commercial leverage costs Beijing real industrial-policy capital. The Maitra-Yglesias convergence on “Trump’s actual China policy is incoherent” is the synthesis’s strongest move here, and decoupling-by-tariff is genuinely worse strategic competition than decoupling-by-industrial-policy. But “the implementation could be better” is not “the prior administration’s posture was better.” The transactional-vs-strategic framing that assigns no value to extracted concessions is itself the abstract-IR-theory move Aurelien attacks in this same roundup — smuggling Game Theory’s simplifying assumptions into IR analysis. Revealed preference: Xi flew Trump in, sat for the table, made the concessions. The transactional read explains that behavior; the “Xi humoring Trump” frame does not explain why Xi paid the price.

The Massie loss is a case where the synthesis’s coalitional-dysfunction frame applies more cleanly to the opposition party than to the governing one. McCarthy at Compact reads it as the GOP finally having coherent worldview discipline after sixteen years of oppositionist drift; the synthesis reads $34M in pro-Israel outside spending as donor-class coercion and Gallrein’s classified-credentials gambit as the new format for legitimacy. The credentials-as-warrant pattern Klippenstein names is a real problem defenders should not dismiss — when special-operator status replaces substantive policy debate, the principal-agent relationship the executive owes voters inverts. But two facts cut against the synthesis. First, the $34M had to win an actual primary, and the voters who turned out chose Trump-aligned national-security commitments over the Epstein-files-and-budget-balancing package Massie offered. The “the seat was bought” frame elides what voters voted for. Second, Barkan’s own frame (“AIPAC poured money in because the Republican Party is now their last stand”) concedes the demographic political reality the administration’s pro-Israel posture is responding to. A “donor-class enforcement” frame would predict primary upsets when the donors miscalculate; this primary was the donors calculating correctly. That is what working political coalitions look like.

The DNC autopsy fiasco is the synthesis cluster the loyalist can leave largely intact, with one redirect. The Goldberg-Barker-Lowry-Rothman convergence on “this report is malpractice” describes coalitional dysfunction in the opposition party, not the political system the administration governs. A 193-page report containing zero references to “Israel” or “Gaza,” a self-attached disclaimer that the party cannot verify its own assertions, hostile in-document annotations, no conclusion section, and a chair who suppressed the document for five months — these are the load-bearing facts. Rothman’s documentation that activist figures pivoted from citing the alleged Gaza findings as proof to attacking the DNC for not including them is the cleanest evidence that one of the two parties has lost the capacity to interpret its own electoral data. The administration is governing while the opposition runs the autopsy on itself in public.

Europe is the synthesis cluster where the loyalist’s case is structurally strongest. Constantini’s three-leg structural case (energy cost, social demilitarization, political cordon sanitaire) and Sternberg’s collapse-of-the-welfare-state-prosperity-theory diagnosis arrive from different priors at “Germany cannot rearm itself,” and that is precisely the diagnostic Pentagon’s force drawdown is responding to. Maitra’s “if the drawdown becomes mere eastward relocation it functions as an implicit guarantee” warning is exactly the correct restrainer-doctrine refinement — the burden-shift only works as burden-shift, not as US-presence-rearranged-within-NATO. The bipartisan complaint at every NATO summit since Obama was that European underwriting of European deterrence was inadequate. The previous administration produced rhetorical pressure without operational change. This one is producing operational change with rhetoric the European political class finds intolerable. The synthesis’s reading frames this as administration weakness; three writers from three different priors read it as the correction the bipartisan consensus never had the nerve to make.

The data-center backlash is where the loyalist owes the honest concession. The Burgum “foreign dark money” deflection is the tell — when the administration first attributes its own base’s complaint to foreign manipulation, it has lost the internal argument. Holzman documents Republican-led moratorium proposals in red states and 300%-rise GOP-region polling against data centers; that is a coalitional cost the administration is choosing to absorb to win the AI infrastructure race against China. The strategic logic is correct. The local-coalitional implementation is clumsy, and the right policy response is the McCormick-style “covenant” framing the same roundup reports: bring more energy than you use, recycle water in closed-loop systems, commit to local tax base and local workers. That is what the administration should be doing where it is currently deflecting.

The week’s costs are real, and they are the costs of executing a mandate the prior consensus declined to take seriously. Where the synthesis cornered the administration honestly — the Blanche release, the Burgum deflection — concede and trade. Where the synthesis substituted the prior bipartisan consensus for the November 2024 mandate as the baseline — Beijing deliverables, Massie’s voters, European burden-shift — the substitution is the synthesis’s bias, not the administration’s failure.

↑ Back to TOC

Why the Loyalist Doesn’t Land

Crosscurrents rebuttal — May 18 to May 24, 2026

The loyalist made four moves the synthesis under-engaged with, and they have to be conceded up front. The DNC autopsy cluster really does describe coalitional dysfunction inside the opposition party rather than the political system the administration governs — a 193-page document whose chair won’t endorse it and whose drafters can’t verify their own assertions is a story about who is not currently capable of governing, not about who is currently governing badly. The European cluster really does converge from three orientations on “Germany/Europe is structurally incapable,” and if Constantini, Sternberg, and Maitra are all right that the welfare-state-prosperity model has collapsed and the political class will not cut welfare to fund rearmament, then “rhetoric the European political class finds intolerable” is not by itself an indictment of the administration’s posture. The data-center backlash concession — that the Burgum “foreign dark money” deflection is the tell — is the loyalist owning a real failure honestly. And the underlying mandate-execution frame — that the November 2024 mandate is the relevant baseline, not the prior bipartisan consensus — is the move last week’s exchange already established as load-bearing; the synthesis owes the loyalist explicit acknowledgment that mandate-execution-as-baseline is a live question, not a settled rebuttal.

The loyalist’s case still fails on four of its defenses, on its own terms.

The Anti-Weaponization Fund defense conflates procedural legitimacy with bipartisan equivalence, and they are not the same claim. The architecture argument — that the 1956 Judgment Fund is an unbounded congressional delegation, that compromise settlements have been a bipartisan instrument since the 1970s, that McCabe/Strzok/Page and Letitia James establish a cross-party pattern — is the strongest part of the loyalist’s case on the cluster. But the move from “the architecture is procedurally legitimate” to “the wrong is congressional and bipartisan, not unilateral and Trump-specific” requires the McCabe/Strzok/Page settlements to be the same kind of instrument as the Anti-Weaponization Fund, and they are not. The McCabe settlement was a wrongful-termination claim resolved on standard FLSA-and-pension-restoration grounds; Strzok and Page were Privacy Act suits against text-message leaks the DOJ Inspector General had already documented. The Anti-Weaponization Fund is Trump-controlled-IRS settling with Trump-as-plaintiff for $1.776 billion, with at-will-fireable administrators, secret recipients, and a blanket civil-and-criminal release the loyalist itself concedes is structurally indefensible. Treating these as instances of the same architecture requires collapsing the plaintiff-defendant-identity question into the broader compromise-settlement category — the move Puri’s piece refuses to make. The loyalist’s own concession that the Blanche release is structurally indefensible is the admission that the prior-settlements axis and the Anti-Weaponization Fund axis are not the same one.

The Beijing-summit revealed-preference defense is the loyalist’s sharpest move on the cluster, and it answers the wrong question. The revealed-preference reading — Xi flew Trump in, sat for the table, made concessions in sectors (airframes, engines) where China has a C919 domestic-substitution program, therefore Xi paid the price — is correct on the question it asks. The question Yglesias and Maitra converge on is not whether the deliverables had value to the U.S.; it is whether the floor of U.S. negotiating expectations moved against the U.S. position, and the Hormuz example is the test case the loyalist’s framework needs to engage but does not. Trump touted as a “win” getting China to agree the Strait should be demilitarized and un-tolled long-term — “something no prior president would even have considered asking for because it went without saying.” Revealed preference is satisfied on the airframe order and on Xi getting the demilitarization-of-Hormuz concession written into a deliverables document as a Chinese concession rather than as the silent default. Extracted concessions have some value; that framework does not survive when the extracted concession is the formal recognition of a status that was previously the un-negotiated default. The “abstract-IR-theory” charge against Yglesias-Maitra would land harder if the convergence were on a theoretical claim. It is not. Yglesias is naming a specific deliverable; Maitra is naming a specific institutional architecture (the G2 framework formalized without European representation). The convergence-on-mechanism is what the “implementation could be better” reframing does not reach.

The Massie-loss defense — that $34M had to win an actual primary, that Barkan’s “AIPAC last stand” frame concedes the demographic political reality the administration is responding to, that “the seat was bought” elides what voters voted for — assumes a fact the defense’s own framework needs to engage but doesn’t: that the operative variable in the primary was the substance of the contest, not the format. Klippenstein’s third dimension is what the loyalist sidesteps. Gallrein’s classified-credentials gambit, the principal-agent inversion (“the president has seen the classified file, therefore you don’t need to evaluate me”), Gallrein’s actual military roles being apparently educational positions at JSOU rather than direct-action, his firing from the Y-12 nuclear facility in 2013 — these are not substantive policy debate voters chose between. They are the format of the contest the McCarthy/Barkan frames argue about. The loyalist’s “voters chose national-security commitments over Massie’s Epstein-and-budget package” reframing requires the contest to have been substantively about national-security commitments; the credentials-as-warrant format is the substrate that ate the substantive contest. “Working political coalitions” is the right diagnostic for what happened; what this coalition was enforcing was credentialed-deference replacing democratic accountability, the cost the working-coalition framing concedes without naming.

The European-cluster defense is structurally the loyalist’s strongest, and it exposes a synthesis weakness the rebuttal should acknowledge: the synthesis frames the drawdown as administration weakness when three writers from three different priors read it as the correction the bipartisan consensus never had the nerve to make. That part of the loyalist’s case holds. But the defense becomes incoherent on its own terms when it endorses Maitra’s “if the drawdown is mere eastward relocation it functions as an implicit guarantee” warning and defends the operational drawdown as burden-shift in the same paragraph. Maitra’s warning is conditional: the drawdown is correct burden-shift only if it is homeward retraction, not eastward relocation. The loyalist endorses the framework and then defends the operational instance without supplying the evidence that the drawdown is homeward rather than eastward. The 2nd Armored Brigade Combat Team rotation cancellation, the Germany troop reduction, and the Norfolk NATO training base closure are consistent with both readings; Maitra’s warning is precisely that the empirical question is unresolved and the wrong resolution turns the burden-shift into the opposite of what the loyalist claims it is. The defense imports the conditional and drops the condition.

The week’s evidence supports the loyalist on the DNC autopsy as opposition-party dysfunction rather than system-wide breakdown, on the European-cluster diagnostic, and on the data-center deflection being the loyalist’s honest concession. It breaks against the loyalist on the Anti-Weaponization Fund’s procedural-vs-bipartisan-equivalence conflation, on the Beijing-summit reframing of the wrong question, on the Massie-loss substrate-vs-format sidestep, and on the European-drawdown defense importing the warning’s framework while dropping its condition. The mandate-execution distinction last week opened still holds: where the synthesis substituted the prior bipartisan consensus for the November 2024 mandate as the baseline, the loyalist is right that the substitution is the synthesis’s bias. Where the substantive mandate is being executed via formal-architecture rewiring — the Anti-Weaponization Fund as executive-discretion instance, the Gallrein credentials-as-warrant format as donor-class-enforcement instance — the mandate defense and the architecture-rewiring critique remain inseparable. This week’s exchange is a sharpening, not a resolution.

↑ Back to TOC

Access Issues & Limitations

See access-issues.md for the full structured report. 72 articles saved, 1 byline-mismatch flag (fp-trumps-endgame-in-cuba.md, since corrected to Martin Gurri).

Notable Access Notes

  • Chartbook (Tooze): 8 articles enumerated, 0 saved — all 8 were “Top Links” roundup posts. The user is not on Tooze’s paid tier, but the filter rule (skip Top Links) would have eliminated them anyway. No substantive Tooze original essay this week.
  • WOTR (War on the Rocks): 16 articles enumerated, 0 saved — all standalone-looking pieces tested came back under 300 words (members-only beyond intro). The WOTR free-content tier appears to have tightened; flagged for next week’s handoff.
  • Eurotopics body extraction: the fetch_and_save path returns only the debate intro (~50-60 words); custom BeautifulSoup extraction of panel/blockquote blocks was applied inline this week. If Euro|topics becomes a regular pick, the news_mcp.py body extractor for that domain should be patched.
  • Dalio: published a LinkedIn commencement speech on 5/18, judged out of scope for the macro-strategic brief.
  • NYT (Tufekci): 0 articles in window. The May 12/13 hantavirus piece was indexed last week.
  • WSJ Sternberg via ProQuest U.S. Newsstream: 2 indexed (Online + Eastern Edition print), 1 saved, 1 duplicate skipped. ProQuest auth carried over from the NYT agent’s session.

Notes for Future Runs

  • Per-article analysis pass + parallel fragment-writer pattern continues to scale: 72 articles + 7 themes + ~15 minutes parallel pass + ~5 minutes assembly.
  • The Massie / DNC autopsy / Anti-Weaponization Fund convergence pattern this week (3-5 writers per cluster across 3-4 orientations each) is a strong demonstration of the cross-orientation triangulation finding format — recommend a vocabulary entry or carry-forward to lock the analytical move in.
  • Add a one-line note to weekly_roundup.md under “Parallelization Strategy”: for shared files (notes.md, oneliners.md), prefer cat >> file <<EOF over Edit, which fails on concurrent modification.

Article Summaries

The Trump Slush Fund Is an Abuse of Political Power, Not a Legal Wrong

Andrew C. McCarthy · 2026-05-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Trump DOJ-approved settlement of the Trumps’ collusive lawsuit against Trump-controlled executive agencies, which routes $1.776 billion in Judgment Fund money to a “Anti-Weaponization Fund” payable to the president’s political allies, plus an unrelated provision (slipped in by acting AG Todd Blanche, Trump’s former personal lawyer) granting Trump tax-audit immunity. McCarthy concedes the editorial board’s outrage is correct on the merits but insists the wrongs here are political, not legal: collusive intra-executive litigation has enough jurisprudential wiggle room that Judge Kathleen Williams could have ruled either way, and the parallel Biden-era settlements (McCabe, Strzok, Page) plus Letitia James’s outsized New York fraud judgment show the pattern is bipartisan in kind even if Trump is “doing what Democrats did, just far more ostentatiously.” Drawing on his 2014 Faithless Execution framework and Federalist 65, McCarthy reframes impeachable conduct as a category of public wrongs (Hamilton’s all-caps “POLITICAL”) that need not be criminal — and notes that after Trump v. United States (2024), presidential immunity makes most such abuses unprosecutable even when criminal. The implication: Congress must use its appropriations, confirmation, oversight, and impeachment powers, because the courts cannot and the criminal law was never built to. The remedy he prescribes is therefore the one he knows will not happen — making the piece, despite its juridical voice, a counsel of institutional despair.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

McCarthy is doing two things at once, and the second is more interesting than the first. The surface argument — that the slush fund is “an abuse of political power, not a legal wrong” — is a doctrinally orthodox restatement of the Trump v. United States (2024) immunity regime plus the Framers’ impeachment design. The Federalist 65 / Faithless Execution through-line is genuine McCarthy: he has been writing this argument for over a decade, and the consistency is to his credit. Within the four corners of the doctrine he describes, the analysis holds. Collusive intra-executive litigation is in fact a gray zone in Article III standing jurisprudence; the Judgment Fund, as Puri’s companion piece in NR this week makes clear, was structured by Congress precisely to remove substantive limits; and post-Trump v. United States, even a clear-cut bribery prosecution of a sitting president is now functionally unavailable.

But the prescriptive layer of the piece is where the institutional pessimism quietly does most of the work. McCarthy’s remedy is congressional: deny funding, deny confirmations, hold hearings, impeach if needed. He knows — and the piece’s closing sentences come close to saying — that the current Republican-controlled Congress will do none of those things, and that the next Democratic-controlled Congress will face the same Trump-immunity doctrine plus a precedent-baseline that has now normalized $1.776B political payouts as Article II prerogatives. The doctrinal modesty (“this isn’t a legal wrong”) and the political maximalism (“Congress must check this”) sit in tension that McCarthy doesn’t resolve. Reading the column charitably, this is the point — he is showing that the constitutional design depends on a Congress that no longer exists, and the criminal-law fallback the public reaches for is a category error. Reading it less charitably, the legal-institutionalist frame supplies a sophisticated permission slip for inaction: “appalling, but not illegal” lands the same place as “regrettable, but lawful.”

The piece pairs naturally with John R. Puri’s same-day NR column on the Judgment Fund’s structural unconstitutionality (excessive non-delegation under Article I), and the two together represent NR’s house position this week: the wrong is real, the wrong is bipartisan in lineage, and the remedy is the legislative branch reasserting its appropriations power. Puri offers a mechanism (the courts striking down the Judgment Fund’s structure under the non-delegation doctrine); McCarthy offers a counsel (impeachment). Both authors share the assumption that the criminal-law frame is the wrong frame. That convergence is itself a significant move within the conservative-legal commentariat — it preempts the “lock him up” reflex from the right that will eventually arrive when a Democratic administration uses the same Judgment Fund mechanism. As legal-establishment positioning, it’s coherent. As a guide to what would actually constrain executive abuses of this kind in 2026, it leaves the reader on doctrinally solid ground that is also, in McCarthy’s own telling, politically inert.

↑ Back to TOC

Trump’s Egregious Abuse of an Egregious Delegation

John R. Puri · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the same Trump DOJ “Anti-Weaponization Fund” McCarthy addresses — the $1.776 billion settlement structured by acting AG Todd Blanche to pay out to Trump’s political allies, drawing on the Judgment Fund — but reframed as a separation-of-powers diagnosis rather than an impeachment-and-immunity one. Puri’s mechanism is structural: until the mid-20th century, Congress approved every monetary judgment against the United States through annual appropriations under Article I’s “No Money shall be drawn from the Treasury, but in Consequence of Appropriations made by Law” clause. The 1956 Judgment Fund was created as a permanent indefinite appropriation for judgments under $100,000; over the next two decades, Congress expanded it to cover “compromise settlements” (i.e., negotiated payouts that bypass judicial scrutiny) and removed the $100,000 cap entirely, leaving an unlimited slush fund that “spends billions per year, all without a whit of involvement by the one branch empowered to spend public money.” Trump’s settlement is therefore not (in Puri’s strict reading) an abuse of presidential power but an abuse of legislative power — exploiting a delegation Congress should never have made and now lacks the votes or courage to revoke. The constitutional remedy he proposes is that the courts strike down the Judgment Fund’s structure under the non-delegation doctrine (the principle that Congress cannot delegate appropriations authority “unbounded by any intelligible principle”), citing the Supreme Court’s historical willingness to do so. The piece’s escalation thesis: Trump “started small to test what he could get away with” — $5M to Ashli Babbitt’s family, $1.25M to Michael Flynn, $1M to Carter Page — and scaled up once each smaller payment passed without resistance.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Puri is doing what NR’s younger legal-institutionalist writers do well: locating a specific Trump-era abuse in a deeper structural failure that long predates Trump, then arguing the fix has to operate at the structural level rather than the personality level. The 1956-and-after history of the Judgment Fund’s expansion is correct and underexplored in mainstream coverage. Congress did in fact surrender granular control over federal-government settlements as a labor-saving measure, and the Judgment Fund did in fact become a vehicle for resolving large compromise settlements without appropriations oversight (the Keepseagle precedent Blanche cites for the current slush fund is itself an example of the mechanism’s prior use under Obama). The non-delegation argument is doctrinally serious — the Court’s 2024 Loper Bright and 2025 Jarkesy lines have revived non-delegation reasoning as a live constraint, and a challenge to the Judgment Fund’s structure on intelligible-principle grounds would have more purchase now than at any time since the New Deal.

The piece’s principal limitation is the same as McCarthy’s companion column: the prescribed remedy depends on actors who will not act. Puri’s path requires either (a) Congress to amend the statute (he concedes “the president would veto their reprisal” and there is no veto-override coalition) or (b) the Supreme Court to invalidate a 70-year-old appropriations mechanism on non-delegation grounds — which would not just stop the slush fund but invalidate decades of routine settlement payments, an outcome the Court is unlikely to risk for the political fallout. The escalation-trajectory observation (Babbitt $5M → Flynn $1.25M → Page $1M → slush fund $1.776B) is sharp and worth flagging as a carry-forward warrant: if the pattern holds, the next iteration will be larger still, and the Judgment Fund’s unbounded structure provides no ceiling.

This piece pairs tightly with Andrew McCarthy’s same-day NR column on the same slush fund. McCarthy locates the wrong in political abuse and prescribes impeachment-and-oversight; Puri locates the wrong in structural abuse of delegated legislative power and prescribes non-delegation-doctrine litigation. The two analyses are complementary, not redundant — they describe different layers of the same failure mode. The cross-reference matters: a Crosscurrents reader who takes only McCarthy’s frame gets the “post-Trump v. United States impeachment is the only remedy” pessimism; one who takes only Puri’s frame gets the “Court could fix this with a non-delegation ruling” optimism. The honest synthesis is that both remedies have to operate for the Judgment Fund’s slush-fund vulnerability to close, and neither is on a near-term track. As legal-institutionalist NR positioning this is sophisticated work — neither author is defending the slush fund, both are reframing why the obvious legal-prosecution response is unavailable, and the combined argument lands the conservative reader on “Congress and the Court are the answer, and they are both failing” without quite saying so out loud.

↑ Back to TOC

Trump’s Victims Fund Is a Scandal. But It May Be Legal.

Jed Rubenfeld · 2026-05-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the DOJ’s announcement of a $1.8 billion “Anti-Weaponization Fund,” created via a settlement of Trump’s January suit against the IRS over the 2018-2020 tax-return leaks, in which the Justice Department also issued what amounts to a blanket civil-and-criminal release for Trump, his sons, and the Trump Organization on any pending or potential federal matter. Rubenfeld, a constitutional-law scholar, walks through the mechanics: Trump (in charge of the IRS as defendant) settled with Trump (as plaintiff); the presiding judge had flagged the “a man cannot sue himself” problem and signaled a hearing, but the settlement preempted it and Judge Williams then formally closed the case noting the settlement was not on the record. The fund will be administered by five officials Trump can fire at will, recipients and amounts may be kept secret, and Acting AG Blanche additionally declared the United States permanently barred from bringing any future civil or criminal action against the Trump plaintiffs — “better than a pardon,” Rubenfeld notes, since pardons cover only criminal liability. He concedes a non-trivial point: some Biden-era lawfare victims (Trump-allied targets who paid huge legal fees, people deplatformed under government pressure) may actually deserve compensation, and Trump’s own $25M Meta settlement for deplatforming is the existence proof. But the fund is the wrong vehicle for two reasons: (1) opacity and at-will administrators guarantee at minimum the appearance of corrupt self-dealing, and (2) the administration’s own lawfare record (the unconstitutional executive orders against law firms) means the fund will systematically exclude administration-disfavored victims, making it lawfare-by-other-means. The Obama-era $680M discrimination fund Blanche cites as precedent is structurally disanalogous (no plaintiff-defendant identity, judicial disbursement, public process). Rubenfeld closes by noting that a future Democratic administration could plausibly void Blanche’s release and claw back the money — meaning the fund’s beneficiaries face real long-tail legal risk even if it survives immediate challenge. The load-bearing move is the title’s “but” — the piece concedes the fund may technically survive judicial scrutiny because nobody obvious has standing, but argues this is precisely why the administration’s discretion to rescind matters.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is the strongest legal-architecture take on the IRS-settlement story this week, and the value is in what Rubenfeld is willing to concede. Unlike commentators who treat the fund as straightforwardly criminal, he walks through the technical reasons it may survive — no obvious party with standing, the judge already closed the case, the Anti-Deficiency Act and other appropriations-law angles aren’t a clean kill — and lands on the harder claim that the legality question is the wrong question. The relevant question is whether a settlement between a man and himself, administered in secret by people he can fire, is a thing the American legal system is supposed to permit even when no specific rule prohibits it. That’s a more sophisticated argument than the “this is obviously illegal” line and is, on the merits, harder to rebut.

The concession that some Biden-era lawfare victims may deserve compensation is doing real work in the piece. It immunizes Rubenfeld against the “you’re just defending Democrats” rejoinder, and it lets him isolate the specific structural defects (opacity, at-will administrators, plaintiff-controls-defendant) as the actual problem rather than the underlying premise. The Meta-$25M-deplatforming-settlement reference is the cleanest case for the “yes, some people really were illegitimately targeted” point. This is steel-manning of the kind the project values.

Two weaknesses worth naming. First, Rubenfeld treats the “future Democratic administration claws this back” scenario as a serious deterrent, but the structural lesson of the Trump-2-era enforcement environment is that retroactive enforcement against politically-protected beneficiaries is exactly what the lawfare-discourse has discredited. If the next Democratic AG goes after Anti-Weaponization Fund recipients, that AG will be accused of (and arguably will be engaged in) the very weaponization the fund nominally compensates. The piece doesn’t reckon with that self-reinforcing dynamic. Second, the executive-orders-against-law-firms comparison is the right one, but Rubenfeld stops short of the synthesis-level claim that this is the same move in a different register — the administration both punishes its enemies via state power and compensates its friends for the punishments it claims to oppose. The Klippenstein piece this week (separate roundup item) does that connecting work more explicitly.

What Rubenfeld brings that other coverage doesn’t: the credentialed legal-architecture read on why this is probably going to survive courts and why that’s the wrong reason to be relieved. The piece should be read as the institutional-rule-of-law conservative position — distinct from but compatible with the McCarthy/Puri/Klippenstein takes — and is the strongest “this isn’t just bad optics, it’s structurally corrupt” argument this week.

↑ Back to TOC

The End of the SEC Gag Rule

Dan McLaughlin · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the SEC’s Monday announcement, under Chairman Paul Atkins, rescinding Rule 202.5(e) — the 1972 “no admit/no deny” policy under which civil-settlement defendants were barred from publicly contesting the SEC’s charges even though they never had to admit them. McLaughlin frames the move as both a regulatory housekeeping win (delivered by the New Civil Liberties Alliance’s eight-year litigation campaign, which had a cert petition pending in Powell v. SEC with the SEC’s response due May 26) and a structural correction: the gag rule existed because it let the SEC publicly trumpet allegations it had not proven in court while preventing defendants from defending themselves outside court, a one-way ratchet that “insulates the commission from public criticism” rather than protecting investors. He distinguishes courtroom allocutions (legitimate) from out-of-court speech bans (constitutionally suspect), and notes the SEC’s own admission that it had never tried to enforce the rule because doing so would have invited the strict-scrutiny ruling that, on a 5-4 / 6-3 First Amendment court, it would likely have lost. Concession to the defense bar: settling parties lose the “we can’t legally comment” shield; concession to the plaintiffs’ bar: nothing changes about private damages suits. The net is that the SEC will have to make its case in charging documents rather than in settlements designed to extract reputational damage without proof.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is McLaughlin in his most characteristic register — a tightly argued regulatory-law explainer that uses a single agency action to surface the broader pattern of “rule by settlement.” The mechanism he describes is real and well-documented: agencies that face resource constraints and uncertain legal theories prefer to settle cases against parties who have business reasons (pending mergers, IPOs) to avoid extended fights, then use the settlement as quasi-precedent. The gag rule was the keystone that let the SEC have it both ways — accusation without proof, plus enforced silence from the accused. NCLA’s persistent litigation strategy (build the as-applied challenge after losing the facial one in the Second and Ninth Circuits, then keep escalating until the Supreme Court’s evident First Amendment maximalism makes the SEC’s risk calculus untenable) is a genuinely effective administrative-law campaign and McLaughlin gives them appropriate credit.

The piece’s blind spot, characteristic of NR’s regulatory coverage this cycle, is what happens to the deterrent function of SEC enforcement now that the gag rule is gone. McLaughlin acknowledges (briefly) that “the SEC staff may feel compelled to work harder to make their case in charging documents” — but this elides the more likely outcome, which is that settlement volume drops, agency resources stretch thinner, and the universe of unprosecuted securities-law violations expands. The Atkins-led SEC of 2026 is also rolling back enforcement broadly (the commission has shifted from the Gensler-era posture of aggressive crypto and ESG-disclosure cases); the gag-rule rescission is one move in a coordinated repositioning toward investor-protection-light, and a piece that focused only on the speech-rights win obscures the dual-purpose nature of the policy change. The Mark Cuban and Elon Musk closing reference is the tell: the principals McLaughlin names as beneficiaries are Trump-adjacent billionaires who have been the SEC’s most visible adversaries, and the celebration of their newfound speech rights does the political work the doctrinal argument is meant to obscure.

That said, the underlying free-speech argument is sound and would have prevailed on the merits had Powell v. SEC gone to the Court. The Second Circuit’s SEC v. Romeril (2021) defense of the rule under Cohen-style waiver doctrine has aged poorly against the Roberts Court’s recent unanimous speech jurisprudence (NRA v. Vullo, Counterman v. Colorado, the Murthy standing punt notwithstanding). McLaughlin’s prediction that this rescission will be harder for a future Democratic administration to reverse than the original policy was to maintain is plausible: agency inertia favored the status quo ante; agency inertia now favors the rescinded status quo, and any reversal would have to defend the rule rather than just letting it persist. As regulatory analysis the column is strong on doctrine, light on second-order political-economy effects, and partial on whose interests the win serves.

↑ Back to TOC

Wires to Nowhere: The Waste and Costs of American Utilities

Matt Stoller · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: NextEra’s announcement on Monday May 19, 2026 of a $67-120 billion acquisition of Dominion Energy (the largest utility merger ever), set against (a) NERC’s recent Level 3 grid-reliability alert “focused on large load challenges” from data centers, (b) 70 million U.S. adults reportedly foregoing food and medicine to pay 2023 utility bills, (c) electricity prices rising at 1.5x general inflation, and (d) Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro publicly questioning the basic investor-owned utility model.

Stoller (the BIG newsletter author and Economic Liberties Project research director; the project’s anti-monopoly mechanism-first specialist) builds a multi-layered structural argument around expert witness Mark Ellis’s chart showing that financial investment in U.S. utilities has soared while actual grid infrastructure investment has not — i.e., capital deployment is driving price hikes, not vice versa. The historical pattern: U.S. electricity behaved like a high-tech product for eighty years (constant innovation, falling prices), then in the 1970s entered a period Stoller calls political capture; the dynamic worsened after George W. Bush’s 2005 deregulation. Despite a 5x increase in transmission spending over twenty years and Obama-era stimulus, the U.S. grid is unreliable now, with consistent blackout risk in MISO territory across the midwest and south. The “where is the money going” answer: a 2024 Mind the Regulatory Gap report found a 26-fold increase in mid-Atlantic spending on “supplementals” (small, local, often-unnecessary projects that don’t require regulatory approval), while the U.S. has built only 375 miles of ultra-high-voltage transmission over the 2020s versus China’s 8,200 miles — China built 80x more high-voltage transmission than the U.S. in the second half of the 2010s.

The mechanism: regulated utilities don’t profit on retail markups; they profit on a guaranteed return on equity (9-11% for most investor-owned utilities, against a current ~4% market savings rate, i.e., a 5-7-point subsidy). This converts utilities into “banks with a guaranteed return” who maximize capital deployment regardless of efficiency, while high-voltage interstate transmission lines — which have to clear regional planning scrutiny they don’t want — go unbuilt. Stoller reads NextEra’s Dominion takeover as the textbook case: Dominion projected 5-7% EPS growth alone; NextEra said it can squeeze 9% out of the same assets, with merger documents explicitly bragging about “11% regulatory capital employed growth” across “constructive regulatory environments” — Stoller’s translation of the latter euphemism is “captured regulators.” The solution he proposes is straightforward: require utilities to finance investments at market rates and recover only what they actually pay, removing the over-investment incentive. The closing structural frame compares utilities to healthcare: rising premiums, closing hospitals, degrading systems, with “sticky-fingered middlemen” extracting rent through waste rather than profit. The political stake: the “rebellion in America right now from normal people” against utility costs is real and growing, and elites who continue to buy the “more investment needed” narrative are missing the point.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Stoller in his strongest analytical register — the structural-mechanism-first piece (rather than the more polemical Monopoly Round-Up format), with the Ellis chart and the Mind the Regulatory Gap “supplementals” 26-fold finding doing real load-bearing work. The argument has a clean causal structure: regulatory regime sets guaranteed returns above market → utilities maximize capital deployment regardless of usefulness → the cheapest-to-build but most-socially-valuable infrastructure (interstate high-voltage transmission) goes unbuilt because it doesn’t generate rate-base growth, while the most-profitable-but-least-needed projects (local “supplementals”) get built repeatedly. The U.S.-China transmission comparison (80x more in the late 2010s, 22x more in the 2020s) is the single most damning data point in the piece, and it is consistent with Tooze’s “Chinamaxxing” frame on Chinese state-capacity buildouts that the project has tracked over multiple weeks.

Where Stoller is doing his most interesting analytical work is in connecting the structural-utility critique to the political-coalition reading he advanced in his Monopoly Round-Up piece this same week. The “Wires to Nowhere” piece’s closing — “rebellion in America right now from normal people” against utility costs — is the same populist-coalition thesis the Monopoly Round-Up advances, applied to a specific concrete case. The two Stoller pieces should be read as a single argument: the structural regulatory failure produces the visible cost-of-living crisis, which generates the political opening for the Mamdani/Platner/Al-Sayed populist Democrats Stoller is championing.

The piece is also doing meaningful structural-power-of-elites work that pairs with this week’s NR / FP coverage of the Trump victims fund slush operation and Klippenstein’s IRS-as-tax-haven piece. Stoller’s “constructive regulatory environments = captured regulators” gloss is the same diagnosis applied to a different sector — the project’s “structural power of elites” cluster is converging this week from multiple angles (Stoller on utilities, Klippenstein on IRS captures, McCarthy at NR on the Trump-slush-fund mechanism, Mills at NR on the SEC gag rule, Puri at NR on egregious delegation). That convergence is one of the more striking cross-orientation alignments the synthesis composer should surface.

Where the piece is weakest is on what to do about it. Stoller’s policy fix — require utilities to recover only their actual cost of capital — is straightforward and probably right on the merits, but it does nothing to address the underlying state-utility-commission capture problem. If state commissioners are setting 9-11% returns when the market clearing rate is 4%, the political economy that produces that capture has to be addressed alongside the rate-setting rule itself; otherwise the new rule will be captured the same way the old one was. Stoller’s gestures at this in the closing (the rebellion will eventually force the change) but doesn’t engage seriously with what a politically-durable utility-regulatory reform looks like beyond invoking populist energy. A piece that walked through the specific FERC-state-PUC division-of-authority machinery and what a populist administration could change at the federal level without state-by-state warfare would be more directly actionable. Still, as structural diagnosis the piece is in the project’s first tier.

↑ Back to TOC

Terrorist “Body Count” Captivates the White House

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Trump administration’s claim, voiced by counterterrorism czar Sebastian Gorka, of having killed 860 “jihadis” in 15 months, and to Trump’s Truth Social announcement that Abu-Bilal al-Minuki — described as “second in command of ISIS globally” — was killed in Nigeria. Klippenstein argues the 860 figure is unverifiable by design and that the al-Minuki characterization is a stretch: al-Minuki was a Nigerian Boko Haram veteran whose elevation to head a single ISIS directorate is sold to a US domestic audience as a global-leadership decapitation, the operation was executed by Nigerian forces (US providing only ISR), and Nigerian officials had already claimed al-Minuki killed in February 2024 — a falsehood now attributed to “fog of counterinsurgency.” He situates this in a pattern: Boko Haram leader Abubakar Shekau and ISIS caliph Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi were each “killed” multiple times before they actually were. The deeper diagnosis names Gorka’s “cloud of red mist” and “stacked like cordwood” boasterism, the NSC “We Will Find You And We Will Kill You” lanyards, as “information warfare” that a Pentagon source dismisses as ranting without deterrence value. The structural critique: the same administration decrying the Mangione/Kirk “assassination culture” is now making numbers-driven assassination an explicit doctrine of statecraft. The piece’s fundamental move is to puncture an official metric by reconstructing how its flagship instance was generated.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is a tighter, more conventional Klippenstein piece than the Gallrein column — short, well-sourced, and built around a single deflation. The HumAngle citation (Nigerian investigative outlet) is load-bearing and gives the piece genuine reporting weight: it shows the “second in command of ISIS globally” label as a regional-promotion claim pitched to a US audience, not a global-decapitation event. The structural argument — that kill-count branding is replacing strategic analysis as the administration’s counterterrorism doctrine — is supported by the contrast between Gorka’s bluster and the actual threat landscape (ISIS Sahel Province growing from 425 to 2,000–3,000 fighters since 2018, six of the world’s ten most terrorism-affected countries in sub-Saharan Africa).

The analysis section’s central move — the assassination-culture inconsistency (Mangione/Kirk decried but kill-count celebrated) — is the rhetorical hook that gives the piece a closer than a wonkish counterterrorism column would have, but it’s also the weakest leg of the argument: the administration has internally-consistent reasons to distinguish state-sanctioned strikes on designated terrorists from private political violence in the homeland, and Klippenstein doesn’t actually engage with that distinction. The point still lands as a moral observation about rhetorical consistency, but as analytic critique it’s thin.

The piece pairs naturally with the cluster of articles this week (and prior weeks) about how the administration converts symbolic-political needs into operational reality — the Maduro-Cartel-of-the-Suns/Venezuela framing, the Venezuelan boat strikes, the IRS-Klippenstein piece if relevant in this window. Read as a structural-mode arc, Klippenstein is consistently exposing the gap between official designation and underlying fact (the “second in command of ISIS globally” label, the “narco-terror” labels for the boat strikes, the “classified credentials” of the Gallrein candidate in his other piece this week). All three are instances of the same move: a stated official metric or designation that, when decomposed, doesn’t survive contact with the underlying facts.

↑ Back to TOC

The Agony Around the Democrats’ Mysterious, Ridiculous Autopsy

Michelle Goldberg · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The May 22 release of the Democratic National Committee’s long-delayed and previously suppressed autopsy of the 2024 election, commissioned by DNC chair Ken Martin from Democratic consultant Paul Rivera on a part-time volunteer basis, and the surrounding internal-party fight over Martin’s leadership.

Goldberg treats the autopsy as significant less for what it says than for how badly Martin handled its release. The 192-page report does not contain the words “Israel” or “Gaza” once, offers no insight into the loss of Black and Latino male voters, and consists largely of platitudes (“It’s imperative that Democrats meet the moment”). Its substantive claims are weak: it attributes Josh Stein’s 2024 North Carolina gubernatorial outperformance of Harris to Harris being a bad candidate rather than to Stein’s opponent being Mark Robinson (the “self-described Nazi” former lieutenant governor and porn-message-board habitué), and it claims Harris’s campaign suffered from insufficient polling-driven messaging — the opposite of Rob Flaherty’s own Bulwark autopsy, which argued Democrats poll-test themselves into “mealy-mouthed ads about prescription drugs” while Republicans drive viral narratives. Martin hid the report from December onward, letting suspicion build (donors and progressives variously thought he was protecting Harris’s 2028 prospects, suppressing a Gaza-blames-Harris finding, or covering up that no real report existed) — Goldberg’s verdict is that he turned a screw-up into a crisis. The party is reportedly approaching near-insolvency under his leadership, with donors withholding contributions and operatives already approaching Run for Something’s Amanda Litman about replacing him. Goldberg’s conclusion: Martin “is insular and thin-skinned,” won his seat by promising perks to voting members rather than articulating a vision, and the growing displeasure with him “isn’t ideological. It’s operational” — he should go.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Goldberg doing internal-Democratic accountability journalism — the kind where the evidence is on-record sourcing (Litman, Phil Gardner of Blue Dog Action, Flaherty’s Bulwark piece) plus close reading of a public document — and on those terms the piece is well-built. The evidence chain is concrete: she quotes the autopsy’s actual platitudes, names the missing words, identifies the specific analytic failure (the Stein-vs-Harris comparison ignoring Robinson), and stacks operational-incompetence sources who are not from one faction. The Litman quote — “We cannot trust the institution is doing its job well” — comes from a progressive-aligned operative, the Gardner quote from a centrist Blue Dog founder, and the convergence is itself the argument: this isn’t a left-flank coup attempt against a moderate chair, it’s a cross-ideological operational complaint.

The piece’s structural reading is calibrated to its evidence. Goldberg doesn’t claim Martin is the cause of Democratic 2024 losses; she claims he’s mishandled the post-loss analysis in ways that compound the underlying problem. That’s a more defensible claim than the bigger one she could have reached for. The “Martin let his screw-up fester until it looked like a cover-up” framing is the correct diagnostic move — it identifies the meta-failure (transparency mismanagement) as separable from the underlying failure (a bad report) — and the column gains credibility by not collapsing them into one indictment.

Where the piece could push harder is on the underlying question of why the DNC produced this autopsy in the way it did, which Goldberg gestures at but doesn’t develop. Commissioning a 192-page election autopsy from a part-time volunteer is not normal party behavior — it’s the behavior of a leadership that doesn’t want a real autopsy, or that doesn’t believe a real autopsy would help. The “it’s now clear, kept under wraps not because it was impolitic, but because it’s a disaster” framing is the piece’s strongest sentence and the one that opens onto the bigger question, but the column then pivots to “replace Martin” rather than asking whether the party’s incentive structure produces this kind of cover-the-bases nullity by design. The Flaherty parallel autopsy — which Goldberg cites approvingly — exists outside the DNC because the DNC could not produce one inside; that institutional fact is the bigger story, and the Martin-must-go conclusion, while probably correct on the merits, is the smaller and easier one.

The “isn’t ideological, it’s operational” framing is also doing some implicit work that’s worth flagging. In a post-2024 environment where the Sanders-AOC left wing, the centrist Blue Dog operatives, and the Harris-campaign veterans are all unhappy with the DNC for somewhat different reasons, “operational” is a useful banner under which an otherwise-disparate coalition can converge. Goldberg surfaces the convergence without quite naming the framing-choice that makes it possible. That’s fine for a column with a stated thesis (“Martin should go”) but worth noting in the synthesis: when “operational” becomes the unifying language, the substantive arguments about what Democrats should do differently (Gaza, immigration, working-class outreach, the labor question) get parked. The autopsy itself dodges those questions; Goldberg’s column, on its chosen ground, does the same.

↑ Back to TOC

Evan Barker: Has the Party I Quit Learned from Its Mistakes?

Evan Barker · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the leaked release of the Democratic National Committee’s (DNC’s) long-suppressed 193-page autopsy of the 2024 election loss, which new chair Ken Martin had commissioned but sat on for over a year and only published when CNN obtained a leak. Barker, a former Democratic fundraiser who publicly voted Trump in 2024, parses the document as a former insider. The report’s findings: Harris lost Black male voters because she didn’t address economic concerns and “assumed identity politics will hold male voters of color”; Josh Stein in North Carolina won by focusing “less on abstract issues and identity politics”; working-class voters support Democratic economic policies (Medicaid expansion, minimum wage) but won’t vote for Democratic candidates and Democratic campaigns have stopped trying; Trump’s attack ads on Harris’s 2019 pledge to fund transition surgeries for federal inmates were “very effective” and nothing would have worked because she did not change her position. Barker confirms from her own donor-side experience that 2024 strategists believed demographic destiny in Texas and the Sun Belt obviated the need for rust-belt white working-class outreach — a bet that failed in both regions. The report urges retaining urban-suburban focus while contesting rural votes, but Barker observes the post-loss argument is already being won by progressives (El-Sayed in Michigan, Platner in Maine winning Senate primaries), and Martin attached a disclaimer to the report stating its views do not reflect the DNC’s — the party distancing itself from its own autopsy. Her diagnosis: Martin suppressed the report because confronting racial-politics findings would require a conversation the progressive base will not permit, and the base “has become so self-radicalized that it can’t see how unpopular progressive ideology, especially when it comes to cultural issues, is to the median American voter.”

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Barker’s positional credibility — fundraiser who quit and went public — is doing real work here, and the piece earns it on the reportorial side. The “donors believed changing demographics in Texas would eliminate the need for white working-class outreach” detail is the kind of inside-the-machine specificity that distinguishes her from the standard FP “Democrats lost touch” essay. The transition-surgery-attack-ad findings she cites from the report are independently confirmable and worth amplifying — the autopsy’s own language that “nothing would have worked” if Harris didn’t change her position is unusually direct for a partisan after-action document, and the fact that Martin sat on it for a year is itself the story.

That said, two structural moves are doing more work than the evidence supports. First, the “progressives are winning the post-loss argument” claim rests on two primary-success data points (El-Sayed, Platner) in the May 2026 election cycle — a thin base from which to declare the intra-party direction settled. The Virginia/New Jersey “affordability” message Barker mentions in passing actually cuts the other way; if affordability is the winning frame in moderate states, the progressive-takeover narrative is less load-bearing than she makes it. The piece would be sharper if it acknowledged this tension instead of waving it past.

Second, Barker’s framing of the autopsy’s reception — Martin’s disclaimer, the no-questions call — as evidence of base capture rather than ordinary CYA political behavior is plausible but underargued. The same data is consistent with: Martin not wanting to own findings he himself didn’t conduct, the DNC chair’s structural position vis-à-vis state parties and candidates, and the report’s own admission (which Barker quotes!) that it is “confusing and disorganized” and “missing key sources throughout.” A 193-page document with that quality profile is exactly the kind of thing a chair attaches a disclaimer to for ass-covering reasons that have nothing to do with progressive-base pressure. Barker’s interpretation may still be the right one, but it should be argued rather than asserted.

What’s distinctive about her piece versus the other DNC-autopsy coverage this week: the insider-experience layer (the demographic-destiny bet in Texas, the donor mentality, the “stopped appealing to these voters altogether” observation) is hers in a way the NR or NYT versions aren’t. The autopsy’s transgender-care finding is the news, and Barker is the writer most willing to call it the news rather than burying it in a both-sides paragraph. That’s worth foregrounding in the roundup composition.

↑ Back to TOC

An Autopsy as Malpractice

Rich Lowry · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Democratic National Committee’s reluctant release of its long-delayed 2024 post-election autopsy, completed under DNC chairman Ken Martin, which arrived with an internal disclaimer that the DNC could not verify many of its own assertions, hostile annotations contesting its claims, and no conclusion section. Lowry’s critique is structural-then-pivot: the document’s drafting failures (no verification of underlying sourcing, contradicting annotations, missing conclusion) make it “a thoroughly unimpressive, unfinished document that says more about the low state of the Democratic Party than any of its analysis does.” He concedes the report’s honest hits — Trump’s they/them ad against Harris, Harris’s failure to separate from Biden, Democratic dependence on Republican candidate self-destruction — but flags the strategic evasions: no engagement with inflation or immigration (the two largest issue drivers), no reckoning with Biden’s age and the catastrophic decision to run for reelection. Lowry’s frame is that party autopsies are historically useless anyway (the GOP 2012 autopsy was famously ignored by Trump in 2016 and won), midterms turn on incumbent approval rather than out-party renovation, and presidential comebacks come from charismatic candidates with new coalitions (Obama 2008, Trump 2016) rather than from strategist-authored retrospectives. The implicit verdict: the autopsy doesn’t matter, but its incompetence does — as a leading indicator of organizational decay.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Lowry is writing a syndicated King Features column here (the byline at the bottom of the piece marks the format), which constrains him to a tighter argumentative arc than a long-form NR essay. Within that constraint he delivers what NR readers want: a “the Democrats are incompetent and rudderless” frame anchored to a concrete document. The critique is partially motivated — Lowry’s identification of Biden-running-for-reelection and Harris-as-charmless-nonentity as the load-bearing facts skips lightly past the fact that the autopsy does engage Biden’s reelection decision and Harris’s failure to differentiate (Lowry himself acknowledges the latter two pages earlier in his own column). The structural critique (“no conclusion, hostile annotations, unverified sourcing”) is real and damning; the substantive critique (“doesn’t grapple with inflation and immigration”) imports Lowry’s preferred causal frame and treats omission of his frame as malpractice rather than as a different theory of the loss.

Where the column genuinely works is the meta-historical move: party autopsies as a literary genre have a poor track record, and Lowry is correct that Trump’s 2016 victory came from doing the opposite of the GOP 2012 autopsy’s recommendations (immigration moderation, Latino outreach, big-tent inclusivity). This is a useful corrective to the implicit assumption — held by some on the Democratic side — that a better-drafted autopsy would have produced a better-positioned party. The deeper point Lowry doesn’t quite state but gestures at: organizations that need an autopsy to know why they lost have already revealed the cognitive deficit that produced the loss. The empirical claim that midterms turn on incumbent approval (here meaning Trump’s approval rating in November 2026) rather than out-party renovation is standard political science and provides a genuine cushion to the Democrats’ organizational dysfunction.

The piece pairs with Noah Rothman’s same-day NR column on the same autopsy, which attacks the activist-left Gaza-excuse narrative directly. Together they form NR’s house position this week: the Democrats are not just losing strategically but losing the ability to think strategically, and the activist-left intimidation campaign Rothman documents is one mechanism by which that thinking is paralyzed. Lowry’s piece is the lighter, syndicated-column register; Rothman’s is the heavier indictment. Both are oriented around the same operative belief: that 2024 was an overdetermined Democratic loss and any party-rescue narrative that depends on single-cause explanations (Gaza, Biden’s debate, racism, sexism) is denial. As critique on the merits this is a partially-shared frame with quite a few Democratic voices (Klein, Bouie, even the autopsy itself) — Lowry’s distinctive contribution is the institutional-incompetence angle, which is real but lighter than Rothman’s structural argument about activist capture.

↑ Back to TOC

DNC Autopsy Exposes the Left’s ‘Gaza’ Excuse as Nonsense

Noah Rothman · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the DNC’s draft 2024 autopsy, which contains zero references to “Gaza,” “Israel,” “genocide,” or related terms — directly contradicting a February Axios report (Holly Otterbein) that had alleged DNC chairman Ken Martin was delaying release specifically to suppress findings that Netanyahu’s conduct of the Gaza war had cost Kamala Harris winnable states. The Axios story relied on sourcing from the IMEU Policy Project (Institute for Middle East Understanding), which had claimed early access to the DNC data. Rothman documents how, once the actual autopsy appeared without the predicted findings, far-left figures (David Hogg, Abdul El-Sayed, Graham Platner, RootsAction’s Norman Solomon) pivoted from citing the alleged findings as proof to attacking the DNC for failing to include the findings — converting falsification of the conspiracy into further evidence for it. He then assembles the evidentiary case against the Gaza-cost-Harris narrative: Harris underperformed in states where down-ballot Democrats (Slotkin in Michigan, Gallego in Arizona, Rosen in Nevada, Baldwin in Wisconsin, Stein in North Carolina) won despite identical Israel positions; the “uncommitted” Muslim-Michigan vote was statistically too small to swing winnable states; pre-election polling consistently showed Gaza was low-salience for general election voters; and the loss was multiply overdetermined by Biden’s infirmities, inflation, immigration, and Harris’s own political-talent deficit. The deeper indictment: the activist-left campaign was never about persuading the party of an empirical claim, it was a power play to “bully the DNC into conceding that they commanded more political authority than was empirically observable” — and the Democratic Party’s reluctance to push back is itself evidence of how that intimidation works. But the analysis flags a fundamental flaw: the column’s empirical core (Slotkin/Gallego/Rosen/Baldwin/Stein outperforming Harris under identical Israel positions) is rigorous, but Rothman’s pivot from “Gaza was not the marginal cause” to a sweeping claim that the activist left is rising “through intimidation, menace, and the selective application of force” is asserted not argued — the cited activist figures (Hogg, El-Sayed, Platner) operate through normal politics, and the “force” frame imports the thesis of Rothman’s forthcoming book Blood and Progress without doing the work in this column.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Rothman writing within his book-promotion window (Blood and Progress: A Century of Left-Wing Violence publishes in May 2026), and the framing inherits from that argument — the activist-left as intimidation-rather-than-persuasion machine is a thesis he is selling beyond this column. The empirical core of the piece is, however, genuinely strong. The down-ballot-Democrats-with-identical-Israel-positions-won-anyway evidence (Slotkin, Gallego, Rosen, Baldwin, Stein) is the strongest available falsification of the Gaza-cost-Harris claim: if Harris’s Israel stance were the operative variable, the senators running on the same stance in the same states would not have outperformed her by the margins they did. The Third Way / Jim Kessler observation that Pennsylvania’s Josh Shapiro maintains majority Democratic support while John Fetterman has shed it — both Israel hawks, the discriminator is Trump-hostility — is the cleanest possible within-pair test. As empirical refutation of one specific causal claim about 2024, this is among the more rigorous arguments NR has run this cycle.

The piece’s analytical move that bears closer scrutiny is the leap from “Gaza was not the marginal cause of Harris’s loss” to “the activist left is rising to primacy within the Democratic coalition through intimidation, menace, and the selective application of force.” The first claim is well-evidenced; the second is asserted rather than argued, and is doing book-promotion work. The selective-force claim in particular is unsupported by the column — the activist-left figures Rothman cites (Hogg, El-Sayed, Platner) are operating through normal politics (statements, primary challenges, public criticism), and characterizing this as “intimidation, menace, and the selective application of force” without naming specific instances of force imports the Blood and Progress thesis without doing the work in this column. The closing line — “all in the name of ‘anti-fascism’” — is the tell that the analytical register has shifted from political analysis to political polemic.

The piece pairs with Rich Lowry’s same-day NR column on the DNC autopsy, which attacks the document on competence/structure grounds rather than activist-capture grounds. Rothman supplies the structural argument Lowry gestures at — the DNC’s incompetence is partly a function of being unable to push back on activist demands without being destroyed — and Lowry supplies the historical context (party autopsies are generically useless) that lets Rothman’s argument breathe. Together they represent NR’s mid-May position on the Democratic Party: not just losing strategically, but losing the institutional capacity to correct losses, in part because the activist-left veto on internal honesty has become structural. The unexpected-aligned-poles observation here is that some of Rothman’s empirical analysis (the multiply-overdetermined loss, the activist-left’s empirical impotence, the falsifiability problem) is functionally identical to recent Yglesias and Klein writing on the same subject from the Democratic side — they share the diagnosis even as they disagree about the prescription. That convergence is the genuine cross-orientation signal in this NR pair, and it is worth tracking.

↑ Back to TOC

Why Iran is right to be paranoid

Peter Frankopan · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the post-strike Iran environment after the US-Israeli airstrike that killed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, including Trump’s statements that he would have “taken Iran’s oil” but for “some stupid people,” US negotiator Steve Witkoff wondering aloud why Tehran hadn’t “capitulated,” and the recent seizure of an oil tanker in the Gulf that Trump celebrated (“We’re like pirates”).

Peter Frankopan, Oxford historian of empires and the Silk Roads, argues that Iranian fear of foreign manipulation is grounded in two centuries of documentable foreign interference — and that this history, more than any specific clerical doctrine, drives the Islamic Republic’s current strategic posture. The piece tours the historical record: the Russo-Persian Wars of 1804–13 and 1826–28 ending in the humiliating Treaties of Gulistan and Turkmenchay (Persia lost the Caucasus — Georgia, Armenia, Azerbaijan); the nineteenth-century “Great Game” with British and Russian agents installed in Tehran; the 1872 Reuter concession granting an Anglo-German entrepreneur monopoly rights over Persia’s mines, roads, and infrastructure (Lord Curzon called it “the most complete and extraordinary surrender of the entire industrial resources of a kingdom into foreign hands” in history); the 1890 tobacco concession to a British army officer that triggered protests and a buy-back; the 1908 discovery of oil and the secret 1914 British government majority stake in Anglo-Persian Oil; British installation of preferred prime ministers in 1915, the Pahlavi dynasty in 1925, and Mohammad Reza in 1941; and the 1953 CIA-backed coup against Mohammad Mosaddegh after he nationalized Persian oil. Frankopan’s argument is that this pattern — foreign powers extracting concessions from a domestic elite unwilling to build sustainable taxation, who then use the foreign-threat narrative to justify centralization — has reproduced itself across Qajar, Pahlavi, and Islamic Republican Iran.
