The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) controls between a fifth and a third of Iran’s economy today, replicating the wealth-concentration pattern Frankopan attributes to all three regimes. His forward-looking claim: Iran will respond to the current pressure not by becoming conciliatory but by deepening asymmetric warfare, rebuilding proxy networks (Hamas, Hezbollah), boosting missile programs, investing in cyber operations, maintaining strategic ambiguity on nuclear enrichment, and building sanctions-resistant trade with China and Russia — because the historical lens tells the regime that vulnerability invites exploitation.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Frankopan is an Oxford historian of the Silk Roads and Eurasian empires, and this piece plays to his strengths: a competent, evidence-backed historical synthesis of Iran’s two-century relationship with foreign powers, organized around the specific extractive concessions (tobacco, Reuter, Anglo-Persian Oil) that became foundational grievances. As historical exposition, it is among the strongest pieces in this week’s discovery batch — the Reuter and tobacco concessions are real, the Curzon quote is real, the Pahlavi installation sequence is real, the 1953 Mosaddegh coup is well-documented (declassified CIA records confirm the operation), and the structural pattern of foreign powers exploiting domestic elite weakness for short-term loans-and-concessions in exchange for long-term influence is a genuine through-line in Iranian historiography. A reader who comes to the piece without a baseline understanding of Iranian-foreign relations will leave with a much better one.

The analytical leap that requires more scrutiny is the connection from history to the current regime’s behavior. Frankopan’s argument has three steps: (1) Iran has been genuinely exploited by foreign powers for two centuries; (2) this experience produces a national disposition that reads current pressure through the historical lens; (3) therefore Iran will not respond to current pressure by capitulating but by deepening the asymmetric-warfare and proxy-network strategies it has used to preserve sovereignty. Steps (1) and (3) are well-supported; step (2) is the load-bearing inferential move and it is doing more work than the evidence quite licenses. The Islamic Republic is not a transparent vessel for two centuries of national memory; it is a specific clerical regime that has both inherited grievances and constructed political uses for them. Frankopan’s own piece acknowledges this in passing — noting that the regime has used the foreign-threat narrative to attack domestic protesters as “Zionist agents” and to justify IRGC capture of a fifth to a third of the economy — but doesn’t quite reconcile the two claims. The historical grievances are real AND the regime weaponizes them; both can be true, but the piece sometimes elides them in service of the “Iran is right to be paranoid” frame.

The piece also under-engages the agency-and-counterfactual question. Frankopan documents two centuries of foreign exploitation but doesn’t sit with the counterfactual: which Iranian governments, when, declined particular concessions and what happened to them? The 1906 Constitutional Revolution that produced an elected parliament is mentioned in one sentence; the 1951 Mosaddegh nationalization is the structural event of the piece but the question of whether a less confrontational nationalization could have avoided the 1953 coup gets no engagement. The implicit narrative — Iran tries to assert sovereignty, foreign powers crush it — is closer to Iranian-revolutionary self-mythology than to a balanced historical reading. A more rigorous version would acknowledge that Iran’s relationship with foreign powers has been genuinely exploitative AND that Iranian regimes have made specific strategic and tactical choices that worsened their negotiating positions (the 1979 hostage crisis being the most obvious example, which Frankopan does not mention).

The forward-looking claims — Iran will deepen asymmetric warfare, build sanctions-resistant trade with China and Russia, maintain nuclear-enrichment ambiguity — are the part of the piece most exposed to the next news cycle. These predictions are reasonable extrapolations of pre-strike Iranian behavior, but they assume the post-Khamenei regime has roughly the same strategic coherence the late-Khamenei regime had. Whether the successor regime (Frankopan’s earlier UnHerd piece, linked in the suggested-reading rail, treats Mojtaba Khamenei as the IRGC’s preferred candidate) actually has the institutional capacity to execute these strategies under sanctions and military pressure is an open empirical question the piece treats as settled. The current iran-time-direction open disagreement in the project — whether Iranian state capacity is recovering or degrading after the Khamenei strike — would benefit from a piece that took the regime-coherence question more seriously than Frankopan’s “Tehran will probably double down” framing allows.

↑ Back to TOC

Ahmadinejad Leading Iran? Color Me Skeptical

Saeid Golkar · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the May 19, 2026 New York Times report that the United States and Israel, in the early days of the Iran war, considered installing former Iranian president Mahmoud Ahmadinejad (2005-2013) as a post-Khamenei replacement and attempted to free him from house arrest on the war’s first day in February — a plan that fell apart when Ahmadinejad was injured in the strikes, became disillusioned, and disappeared from view.

Golkar (a University of Tennessee-Chattanooga political-science associate professor who has written on the Basij paramilitary and the structure of Iran’s coercive apparatus) takes the report at face value and uses it to diagnose the conceptual error behind Trump-administration regime-change planning. The shorter form of his argument is that Washington appears to have generalized the January Venezuela playbook — depose Maduro, install insider-with-name-recognition Delcy Rodríguez — onto Iran, which is not a leader-centered regime but a “theocratic-security state” where power resides in an alliance of clerical institutions with the IRGC (Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps) and Basij paramilitary. Golkar walks through the Ahmadinejad-Khamenei history to show why the choice was incoherent on its own terms: although Ahmadinejad opened ministries and state companies to IRGC veterans during his first term and the Guard crushed the 2009 Green Movement to keep him in office, the relationship was transactional — the IRGC answered only to Khamenei, sided with the Supreme Leader when Ahmadinejad clashed with him in his second term, arrested his loyalist network (the “deviant current”), and ultimately placed Ahmadinejad under house arrest. If Ahmadinejad could not command the IRGC when he was president, Golkar asks, why would he command it after a foreign-backed war and the killing of Khamenei? The IRGC is an ideological force created in 1979 to defend the Islamic Revolution and has spent four decades preparing for coups, infiltration, elite defection, and foreign attack — “nobody, not even Ahmadinejad, can rule without first controlling the security-theocratic structure.” Golkar’s structural worry is that if Washington misread the Iranian regime at the start of the war, it is likely to misread what kind of settlement is possible at the end.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Golkar is doing exactly what an area-expert piece should do: taking a reported decision and explaining why it was structurally impossible to succeed on the regime’s own terms. The Ahmadinejad-IRGC history he walks through is correct in its broad strokes and not widely understood outside Iran-specialist circles — the “deviant current” episode, the second-term break with Khamenei, the asymmetry between Ahmadinejad’s nominal authority and the Guard’s actual loyalty all support the central claim that Ahmadinejad never had a coercive base that could survive Khamenei. The “theocratic-security state” framing is also a useful corrective to the dominant Western frame of “Supreme Leader Khamenei rules Iran” — by emphasizing the institutional alliance rather than the man, Golkar makes the post-decapitation succession problem visible. The piece pairs well with the Iran-policy coverage in this week’s roundup (Klippenstein on the war’s domestic distraction effect; the UnHerd “why Iran is right to be paranoid” piece) by showing that even an apparent decapitation victory would not yield the political objective.

The piece’s main weakness is that it treats the Times report’s premise as established and works backward from it without engaging hard with whether the report itself is accurate. Golkar’s hedge (“if the report is true”) appears twice but the substantive analysis is conducted as if the plan were definitely real, when an alternative read is that the leak is partly disinformation — either Israeli pressure on the Trump administration to legitimize the war retroactively, or an Iranian-regime-friendly source planting a story that makes the U.S. look foolish. A short paragraph weighing whether the reporting is credible would strengthen the analytical scaffolding. There’s also a missing counterfactual: if Ahmadinejad was the wrong insider, who would have been the right one? Golkar’s structural argument seems to imply that no insider replacement would work (because the IRGC-clerical alliance is the regime, not any specific face), but the piece doesn’t quite state that thesis explicitly, leaving open the read that a better-chosen successor might have succeeded. The stronger version of his argument — that the regime is constitutively replacement-proof — would have made for a more durable analytical contribution. Still, as Iran-area-expert commentary the piece does load-bearing work the rest of the roster cannot.

↑ Back to TOC

Upstairs, Downstairs.

Aurelien · 2026-05-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the prolonged Western strategic puzzlement over the Ukraine war (nearly four years old), the ongoing US-Israel-Iran air war (described as “stuck in first gear” with much loose talk about “negotiations”), and the wider Cold-War-2-style framework that has dominated pundit discourse since 2022. Aurelien — former European diplomat, here in his canonical long-form public-service-essay register — sets aside event-level commentary to mount a structural critique of the analytic apparatus Western strategists use. The central historical argument: Escalation Theory — codified by Herman Kahn’s 1965 On Escalation with its 44-rung “escalation ladder,” and built on John von Neumann and Oskar Morgenstern’s 1940s Game Theory — descended from mathematical economics and was carried into US defense policy by Robert McNamara’s Pentagon technocracy and theorists like Thomas Schelling (signalling theory). Aurelien’s claim is that this entire apparatus shares Game Theory’s foundational defect when ported into international politics: the “simplifying assumptions” required to mathematize behavior (rational actors, perfect knowledge, context-independence) are merely “misleading” in economics but “extremely dangerous” in IR, because they assume all governments think alike and will correctly interpret signals as sent. He offers a working definition of Escalation: an action must have (a) a defined purpose tied to a specific desired end-state, and (b) lie within the state’s actual capacity to perform and be plausibly connected to that end-state. By that test, much “escalatory” Western behavior in Ukraine and Iran is just “thrashing around aggressively” or “theatre” for domestic audiences — neither the US nor Israel has anything left to escalate Iran with (missile stocks substantially exhausted, no new commercial or political levers), and Ukraine’s “escalation” devolved into ineffective gestures once it became clear neither sanctions nor weapons would collapse Russia. The historical-civic-military-relations digression (originally Samuel Huntington’s The Soldier and the State) traces a parallel pathology: US fear of military coups (Eisenhower’s farewell address, Seven Days in May) produced a fragmented Pentagon and a “luxuriant forest of Institutes, think-tanks and Foundations” whose career incentives reward generic abstract theorizing over country-specific expertise, leaving Washington structurally unable to model how Moscow, Tehran, or Beijing actually deliberate. The “Continental Tendency” vs. Anglo-Saxon distinction (security as subset of land-warfare defense vs. security as subset of foreign policy) is offered as one example of an analytic frame the abstract-models school cannot accommodate. The Kosovo 1998-99 anecdote — NATO sliding from “we ought to do something about Milošević” to “Oh Shit, how do we get out of this war without destroying NATO” through a series of sideways shuffles — anchors the closing claim that you almost never wind up where you expected, and quite often can’t reconstruct how you got there.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is a more disciplined essay than Aurelien’s average — he has a specific structural target (Escalation Theory and its descendants) and stays close to it. The historical genealogy is largely correct: von Neumann and Morgenstern’s 1944 Theory of Games and Economic Behavior did seed the field, Kahn’s On Escalation and Schelling’s signalling work are the canonical texts, and the parallel between McNamara’s Whiz-Kid Pentagon and the rise of civilian “civil-military relations” theory (Huntington’s The Soldier and the State was 1957) is a real intellectual through-line. The point about Anatol Rapoport — psychologist/biologist, Ukrainian-Viennese background, author of the 1964 Strategy and Conscience — as the school’s most articulate dissenter is the kind of detail Aurelien is uniquely positioned to recover; this is the value-add of having a long-form former-diplomat on the roster.

The piece’s strongest move is its working definition of Escalation. The two-part test — defined purpose tied to end-state, plus actual capacity to execute the step — does real analytic work and survives application to both wars under discussion. The observation that another US strike on Iran would be “weaker and less successful than the first” and therefore “de-escalatory in effect, if not in intent” is the kind of structural inversion most US commentary misses because it confuses verbal escalation (rhetoric, threats) with material escalation (actual change in the cost-benefit calculus of the adversary). Similarly the point that western combat forces in Ukraine would be “targeted and quickly destroyed” and the resulting loss would force Western accommodation — making the move de-escalatory in net effect — is uncomfortable but logically clean.

The piece is weakest where Aurelien lets his civilizational-comparative frame run ahead of his evidence. The “Anglo-Saxon vs. Continental tendency” distinction (security as foreign policy vs. security as land-warfare defense) is a real and useful pattern but he uses it to make stronger claims than the pattern supports — for example, the implicit suggestion that Soviet/Russian strategic culture is fundamentally more grounded because of its land-warfare lineage understates how much Soviet strategic thinking was itself ideologically driven and frequently miscalibrated (the Cuban missile crisis, the Afghanistan invasion, the decision to deploy SS-20s that triggered the INF buildup he himself cites). The closing characterization of Russian Defense Ministry behavior in the post-Cold-War arms control treaty — re-subordinating divisions to the Navy to hide tanks, then breezily explaining it as a “military-technical correction” — undercuts the implicit case that the Continental Tendency produces clearer strategic thinking; it actually shows the same problem (technical-bureaucratic logic detached from political consequence) in mirror form.

The deeper weakness is what the piece declines to take responsibility for predicting. Aurelien’s general framework — Western strategists trapped in obsolete models, conflicts not behaving as expected, “you almost never wind up where you expect to” — is robustly true as historical observation but does not generate forward guidance. The reader closes the essay knowing more about why Western frameworks fail and less about which non-Western frameworks would do better. If the implicit answer is “country-specific expertise grounded in language, history, and lived experience,” that’s defensible but it’s an analytic stance, not a predictive claim, and Aurelien lets the absence of forward claims pass without acknowledgment.

The Kosovo anecdote is the piece’s best concrete contribution because it’s a specific case Aurelien watched first-hand, and the slide from “stern warnings” to “Oh Shit, how do we get out of this war without destroying NATO” is the kind of structural observation about how crisis-management actually unfolds that the abstract-model school is institutionally unable to absorb.

For the composition phase: pair with any Mearsheimer-adjacent or restrainer-realist piece in the week’s batch (Maitra’s Chronicles force-posture and Hungarian Conservative G2 pieces are the natural companions in this batch, though they’re operating mechanism-first IR rather than meta-critique of mechanism-first IR — the distinction is worth surfacing in a synthesis paragraph if both lines survive composition). Aurelien’s claim that Escalation Theory descends from economics-mathematized rational-actor models is also an interesting test case to pair with any deBoer/Klein piece on quantification-as-pseudo-rigor in policy domains.

↑ Back to TOC

Trump’s China policy is a disaster

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Jamie Dimon’s recurring “Trump was right about China” talking point, the Trump-Xi Beijing summit of May 14, and Trump’s concurrent Iran quagmire.

Yglesias separates three distinct critiques of free trade — labor-protectionist (save specific unionized industries), geopolitical-competitive (don’t depend on a hostile power for primary inputs), and naive-mercantilist (any bilateral deficit is per se bad) — and argues Trump operates entirely on the third, which is “total nonsense” and contaminates the first two. The geopolitical case for restricting Chinese imports, he says, is real but turns on retaining U.S. capacity to make complicated goods and stay at the technology frontier; Trump instead exports commodities (soybeans, energy) Xi was happy to buy anyway and hands Nvidia favorable chip-export rulings while bringing Jensen Huang on the Beijing trip. The piece’s secondary thread is that Trump is squandering America’s coalition advantage by picking diplomatic fights with Canada and Denmark, deploying force in Venezuela, and falling into a quagmire in Iran while making allies “even madder” than the U.S. domestic electorate. On energy, Yglesias notes China is adding hundreds of gigawatts of solar per year and rolling out CAP1000 reactors (a Chinese derivative of the U.S. AP1000 design) on a 56-month build timeline while Vogtle 3/4 took 15 years, and that the administration’s response is to cheerlead coal and use every tool to kill wind and solar projects, including blocking a permitting deal that would have unlocked both renewables and gas. The unifying frame: Trump’s brand as a tough dealmaker is doing the political work that ought to be done by actual debate over the substance of China policy, and the lack of scrutiny lets the surrender continue.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This piece is Yglesias in his most characteristic mode — empirically-grounded policy taxonomist using the three-buckets move (labor / geopolitical / mercantilist) to argue that the real China policy debate is being hijacked by a fourth thing that doesn’t even cohere as an argument. The taxonomy itself is the load-bearing analytical move and it works: most of the noise about “China policy” really does conflate the three, and disentangling them makes the actual disagreements (which industries, which partners, what tools) tractable in a way bilateral-deficit rhetoric doesn’t. The specific operational evidence — soybean-and-energy deals as the deliverable, Huang on the trip, the Strait of Hormuz demilitarization concession (“something no prior president would even have considered asking for because it went without saying”) — is the right kind of evidence for the claim, and the Hormuz example in particular is sharp because it shows Trump trading away a default norm rather than a negotiated position.

What’s softer is Yglesias’s handling of the Obama and Biden alternatives. He grants the Trans-Pacific Partnership era as the moment when the old engagement consensus died and a Pelosi-style skepticism plus an Obama-style coalition strategy was on offer, but he doesn’t seriously engage why TPP collapsed politically — including under Hillary Clinton in 2016 and under Biden in office. If the “coalition of free nations” approach is structurally unavailable to any U.S. president because the underlying labor and constituency politics don’t support it, then Trump’s incoherence is downstream of a real constraint, not just a personal-vice story. The piece treats the right answer as known and undefended, with the only question being whether the current operator has the wit to execute it. That’s a recurring pattern in Yglesias’s China writing and worth flagging — the “abundance liberalism” prior reads coalition-trade policy as obviously available if only the politicians weren’t dumb about it.

The energy section is the strongest because it ties the strategic argument to a concrete measurable: chips-plus-electricity is the AI race, China’s grid is scaling and ours isn’t, and the administration is actively making the gap worse by killing renewables under a “we love coal” cover while Burgum, who knows better from his Bismarck days, recites talking points he doesn’t himself believe. The contrast between AP1000 (15-year U.S. build) and CAP1000 (56-month Chinese build) is a real data point and worth holding onto. The weakest closer is the rhetorical-symmetry list (“intermittency is a good reason not to ban fossil fuels but a terrible reason to ban renewables; competition is a valid reason to restrict some imports but…”); it’s elegant but does the work of naming the problem with Trump’s policy rather than explaining why a coalition that elected him would tolerate it. Pair this piece in synthesis with the longer strategic-EVs case (same author, same week, same operative frame) — together they’re a coordinated argument that the Trump-China-hawk reputation is the inverse of the substance.

↑ Back to TOC

The strategic case against Chinese EVs

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Jerusalem Demsas’s argument on The Argument podcast (Yglesias and Demsas co-host) that the de facto U.S. ban on Chinese EVs is rank protectionism dressed as strategic competition, with reference to BYD’s vertical integration and a recent Rhodium Group report on Chinese EV cost structure.

Yglesias concedes the Ricardian free-trade economics in full, concedes that the actual Trump policy is closer to rank protectionism than serious strategic competition, and concedes Demsas’s specific point that subsidies (especially the IRA’s 45X production tax credit for batteries) would do more of the industrial-policy work than tariffs. But he holds the line that some block on Chinese EVs is warranted because automobile assembly is the largest end-product market for batteries, and a battery industry without an end-product industry will not develop the depth the U.S. needs in a strategic-competition scenario with China (a scenario he treats as locked in after Hong Kong, Xinjiang, and the Xi-era turn toward personalistic dictatorship). He spends substantial time on Demsas’s framing of BYD — that BYD started as a battery company that learned to make cars and that vertical integration (80% Tier 1 component in-house, 36% Tier 2, versus Tesla’s 37% Tier 1) explains its cost advantage far more than state subsidies (which the Rhodium report attributes only 5% of BYD’s per-vehicle cost edge to). Demsas pushes the Dale Copeland future-trade-expectations argument — that signaling decoupling intent itself raises war risk — and invokes the 1980s “Japan-as-rival” panic that turned out wrong; Yglesias counters that the Hong Kong crackdown and the Xinjiang repression closed the cooperative-equilibrium window in a way the 1980s Japan case never did, and that the Thucydides Trap (Graham Allison’s 2017 book on rising-power transitions) downward-spiral risk has been overtaken by events. The piece’s most interesting move is Yglesias’s repeated reframing of the U.S. auto industry as bifurcated — a unionized Big Three (Ford/GM/Stellantis) in Michigan plus a non-union “transplant” complex (Hyundai, Kia, Tesla, Toyota, Volvo) in the right-to-work South — and the argument that what tariffs are actually protecting is the transplants, not Detroit, because BYD’s product line doesn’t compete with the F-150 but does compete with the Model 3 and the Ioniq. He concedes that the right policy mix would include a much higher gas tax, dramatic friend-shoring to Europe/Japan/Korea/Latin America (he names Mexico, Colombia, Venezuela, Jamaica, Spain), and Chinese auto investment via U.S. assembly plants (his Volvo XC60 Recharge, owned by China’s Geely, is his half-jokingly-cited model). The piece closes on a smaller second segment (“Peer Review”) discussing Allcott et al.’s national lockable-pouch phone-ban study, where Yglesias is mildly disappointed by the null academic-outcome finding but holds the policy on subjective-well-being grounds.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The structural interest of this piece is that it’s a podcast transcript between two cobelligerents who agree on roughly 70 percent of the framework and disagree on 30 percent of the policy conclusion — and most of the content lives in the working-out of where exactly the line is. That format produces a richer argument than either could write solo, because Demsas is functionally steel-manning the abundance-liberal free-trade position Yglesias normally writes, and Yglesias is steel-manning a strategic-competition position he doesn’t fully inhabit. The two strongest pieces of empirical content come from Demsas, not Yglesias: the Rhodium Group decomposition of BYD’s cost advantage (5% subsidies, the rest vertical integration and decades of battery-industry depth) and the BYD/Tesla Tier 1 component share comparison (80% versus 37%). Both undercut the casual “China cheats” framing that the strategic-competition case usually rides on — and Yglesias, to his credit, does not contest the data. He instead retreats to a terminal industrial-base argument: even if BYD’s edge is legitimate, the U.S. cannot let its auto-and-battery assembly capacity disappear because that capacity is what gets converted in a wartime scenario.

The piece’s strongest move — the bifurcated-auto-industry observation that Big Three Detroit and Southern transplants are distinct political-economic objects, and that Chinese EV tariffs primarily protect the transplants because BYD doesn’t make pickup trucks — is genuinely new framing relative to the standard “save Michigan” rhetoric. It also implies, though Yglesias doesn’t draw the implication explicitly, that the labor-protectionist coalition currently riding strategic-competition rhetoric is largely not the beneficiary of the policy that rhetoric produces. That’s a Yglesias-coded observation (he is congenitally skeptical of UAW-centric industrial policy) and it would land harder if he had named the implication and held it.

The weak spot is Yglesias’s handling of Demsas’s Dale Copeland argument about future-trade-expectations and the self-fulfilling-prophecy risk. His response — “events between 2005 and 2020 just made me think we avoided the opportunity to have a cooperative relationship” with reference to Hong Kong and Xinjiang — is real but does the work of closing the debate rather than engaging the Copeland mechanism on its own terms. Copeland’s point is not that war is avoidable through trade but that the expected trajectory of trade relations shapes incentives; the question Yglesias dodges is whether U.S. signaling about decoupling is itself a major input into Chinese expectations about that trajectory, in which case “we already lost the cooperative equilibrium” can become a partly self-fulfilling claim. The 1980s-Japan analogy Demsas raises is the live counterfactual: contemporaries were equally convinced Japan was a structural rival, and that conviction looks ridiculous in retrospect. Yglesias’s response that Japan was always a U.S. treaty ally is true but evades the question of whether elite consensus in 2026 will look equally provincial in 2046.

Reading this piece against last week’s companion column (“Trump’s China policy is a disaster,” same Slow Boring, three days earlier), the two together form a coordinated Yglesias position: the substantive geopolitical-competition case for some China decoupling is real and Demsas’s Ricardian objection doesn’t override it, AND the actual Trump implementation is the worst of all worlds because it surrenders the chip and ally advantages strategic competition requires while picking up only the bilateral-deficit theater. Yglesias is doing what he often does — granting the establishment-center its strongest version of the case (here, the Biden-administration industrial-policy framework with friend-shoring added) and arguing the right critique of the current administration is from within that center, not from the libertarian-Ricardian side Demsas is pushing or from the labor-protectionist side Trump pretends to represent. That’s a coherent position but it depends on the proposition that an actually-competent friend-shoring-plus-industrial-policy alternative is politically available, which TPP’s collapse and Biden’s IRA-protectionism drift both call into question. The piece would be stronger if it engaged that political-availability problem rather than treating the center-coalition policy as obviously the answer if only the operators were smarter.

The phone-ban segment is essentially filler relative to the EV argument and produces no fundamental update — Yglesias remains a soft policy-supporter on subjective-well-being grounds while genuinely surprised by the null academic-outcome finding, which is honest if not analytically interesting.

↑ Back to TOC

Europe in the Shadow of Beijing: What the Trump–Xi Summit Means for the European Union

Sumantra Maitra · 2026-05-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the 15 May 2026 Trump-Xi summit in Beijing, which produced a US-China “strategic stability” framework for the next three years without any European representation, against the backdrop of US tariffs on European goods, Article 5 ambiguity, the Iran war and its energy consequences, and China’s rare-earth processing leverage demonstrated in the 2025 tariff confrontation. Maitra — writing in his mechanism-first IR-realist register, here from a venue (Hungarian Conservative) aligned with Orbán-coalition strategic-autonomy advocacy — argues that the summit revealed the structural arrival of the G2 scenario European foreign policy thinkers have long feared: a bilateral Washington-Beijing order in which European interests are not represented because Europe lacks the unified scale to compel representation. He invokes a dense referent cluster — Carl Schmitt’s nomos (the spatial-political ordering of power) and großräume (great-space empires); Fernand Braudel on long-cycle economic dominance; Thucydides on the “brute grammar” that great states act in their interest and the rest accommodate; Kant’s perpetual-peace federation of free states (as the Atlantic order’s dismantled aspiration) replaced by Hobbesian dependence-as-leverage logic. The piece’s analytical spine: Europe is simultaneously exposed in three materials-intensive domains (rearmament, energy transition, digital infrastructure) where Beijing controls processing dominance, while America provides no friend-shoring relief (it’s straightforward industrial competition), and the AI Act represents auctoritas without potestas — regulatory authority without enforcement capability. The closing reversal — that Trump’s hostility may unintentionally produce the Hamiltonian/Tocquevillean federalist consolidation his administration most opposes — is the piece’s most striking move, treating the second Trump term as the most effective catalyst for European unity since the Marshall Plan, with Mario Draghi’s call for collective European borrowing as the institutional vehicle.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is the most ambitious of Maitra’s three pieces this week and shows him operating at the venue level he’s likely best suited to: a serious European conservative outlet where Schmitt and Braudel can be invoked without explanation and the reader is already inside the strategic-autonomy debate. The structural-G2-versus-Kantian-order frame is genuinely useful — it names a phenomenon (the empty European chair) that the standard American foreign-policy commentary either misses or treats as Trump idiosyncrasy rather than as the surface expression of European weakness. Maitra correctly identifies that China-and-America-arriving-at-similar-outcomes-via-different-logics (China’s institutional patience vs. America’s transactional impatience) is the analytical move European capitals need to internalize, because it removes the false hope that a different American administration would restore the previous order.

The piece’s strongest section is the rare-earths / industrial-capacity argument. The observation that Europe’s three simultaneous transitions (defense rebuild, climate, digital) all depend on materials China processes, that the 2025 tariff confrontation showed who controls the negotiation when materials are weaponized, and that “friend-shoring” rhetoric from Washington is itself straightforward industrial competition — this is mechanism-first IR doing what it’s supposed to do, naming a specific dependency and tracing its political consequences. The auctoritas without potestas framing of the AI Act is also a sharper formulation than most pro-AI-Act European commentary will accept: regulation of technologies produced entirely elsewhere is borrowed sovereignty, and the only escape route is indigenous capability built at scale only feasible through collective European borrowing.

The piece is most vulnerable on its closing optimism. The claim that external shocks reliably produce European integration — that the second Trump administration may end up as the most effective catalyst for European unity since the Marshall Plan — is a hopeful structural prediction that ignores the actual record of European response to shock: defense spending is indeed rising, the European Defence Union has moved from rhetoric to design, and Draghi’s borrowing proposal is on the table, but each of those advances remains hostage to the same fragmentation Maitra elsewhere correctly identifies as Europe’s structural problem. The Tocquevillean comparison to the American founding underweights the asymmetry: the Articles-of-Confederation states shared a common language, a common political tradition, and the same recent founding war; the EU-27 share none of those. The piece would be stronger if it engaged what the failure mode looks like — what Europe becomes if the integration push stalls and the Beijing-Washington bilateral order solidifies anyway — rather than letting the Hamiltonian-moment frame carry the closing.

For the composition phase: this pairs naturally with any Yglesias / Tooze / Klein piece this week on the Trump-Xi summit and the EU position; it pairs with Maitra’s own Chronicles force-posture piece as the demand-side companion (Chronicles tells Americans to stop subsidizing European defense; Hungarian Conservative tells Europeans the bill has come due). The synthesis composer should note that Maitra is one of the few roster writers who consistently treats Europe as a strategic agent with its own structural problem rather than as an extension of American policy disputes — that vantage point is what makes him distinctive in this slot.

↑ Back to TOC

What Xi Knows That Trump Doesn’t

Francis Fukuyama · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Donald Trump’s May 15, 2026 Beijing summit with Xi Jinping, at which Trump left with two limited market-opening agreements, an unconfirmed $17 billion Chinese agricultural purchase pledge, a Boeing order of 200 aircraft, and no help on Iran — but also a humiliating set of staged optics (no airport reception, a chair on the podium that made Trump look smaller than Xi, public effusiveness from Trump met with cold reciprocation from Xi).

Fukuyama (the Stanford political theorist whose recent register has shifted from end-of-history triumphalism to chronicling democratic backsliding) reads the summit as the moment America’s relative decline against China became unmistakable to anyone watching. He frames Trump’s behavior as a familiar pattern from his interactions with dictators — sycophantic praise that the target does not reciprocate (“great leader,” “really a friend,” “from central casting”) — combined with newly explicit signaling that Taiwan is a tradeable asset (“we’re supposed to travel 9,500 miles to fight a war”; Taiwan “stole” semiconductor IP), in contrast to Biden’s clear deterrent assertion. The structural thesis: American decline since 2016 is not a pre-existing condition Trump inherited but a product of Trump’s actions — polarization, cuts to basic science, attacks on universities, an unnecessary Iran war that drained advanced-weapons stocks, alliance-system erosion (territorial threats against Denmark), and the administration’s open insistence that domestic Democrats are a bigger threat than China or Russia. Fukuyama closes with a Taiwan timing inference: Xi may indeed not invade while Trump is president because Trump’s self-harm to the U.S. is doing the work for him, but a successor seeking to reverse the trajectory could force Xi to act quickly.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Fukuyama is operating in his hawk-liberal-internationalist register here — the diagnosis is sharp and the optics-reading is well-observed (the podium chair-height detail and the sidelined State Department protocol officials are the kind of specific that elevates the column above the usual “Trump-is-weak-on-China” complaint). The Taiwan timing argument is the column’s most interesting load-bearing move: it converts what looks like a Trump dovishness (“China won’t invade while I’m president”) into a structural concession (“Xi doesn’t need to invade because the U.S. is self-immolating”), which is harder to refute than the standard hawk read and which generates a non-obvious prediction (Xi acts not when Trump leaves but when a reversing successor takes office). That’s a falsifiable claim with a clear 2028-2032 test horizon.

What’s weaker is the column’s near-total absence of any counterfactual discipline on the “Trump caused the decline” thesis. China’s manufacturing dominance, its high-voltage transmission buildout, its R&D scaling, and its rare-earth and shipbuilding leverage were trajectories well underway before 2016 and would have continued under any U.S. president; treating Trump as the unique causal agent of relative decline elides the structural realities Tooze, Smith, and Cowen have all chronicled. Fukuyama’s list of Trump-specific accelerants is real (the war in Iran, science cuts, alliance erosion), but mixing them with “polarization since 2016” — which has multiple authors across both parties — weakens the causal claim. The analysis would be more credible if it distinguished decline-accelerants Trump owns (Iran war, allied territorial threats, anti-university campaign) from background trends he inherited.

There’s also a genre mismatch worth flagging: this is a Fukuyama-the-pundit piece, not Fukuyama-the-theorist. The framework that made The End of History (and the more interesting Liberalism and Its Discontents he plugs in the bio line) intellectually generative — the distinction between regime-types and the question of what makes liberal democracy stable — gets only a passing nod via the Thucydides Trap reference Xi reportedly raised. The column would be more valuable if Fukuyama had stayed in that register and asked whether Trump’s behavior at the summit reflects a kind of regime-type convergence (personalist-leader-style behavior in a nominal democracy) rather than just a stylistic complaint about one bad president. As written, it’s a sharp op-ed but not the framework piece the byline could have generated.

↑ Back to TOC

Without a ‘Red Phone’: How is the new conflict between Washington and Beijing proceeding? (بدون “هاتف أحمر”.. كيف يمضي الصراع الجديد بين واشنطن وبكين؟)

Hassan Aourid (حسن أوريد) · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the May 13-14, 2026 US-China summit in Beijing, and the analytical debate over whether the bilateral relationship is best framed as a “New Cold War” (per British strategist Robin Niblett’s book of that title) or as a “Thucydides Trap” (per Harvard’s Graham Allison, the term Xi Jinping used in his summit remarks).

Hassan Aourid — Moroccan political scientist at Mohammed V University in Rabat, writing in Arabic for Al Jazeera — argues that the bipolarity organizing the post-unipolar world is structurally more dangerous than the Cold War because it lacks the Cold War’s stabilizing mechanisms. He recalls Raymond Aron’s formulation that the Cold War was “an impossible war and an improbable peace,” made tolerable by détente, arms reduction, peaceful coexistence, and the “Red Phone” (the 1963 Moscow-Washington direct hotline established after the Cuban Missile Crisis to prevent miscalculation); today’s US-China relationship has none of these, and the axis has shifted from “balance of terror” to “terror of imbalance” — a context with no operative rules, organized around the Thucydides Trap, in which “war is possible and peace is possible too.” Aourid distinguishes the Second Cold War from the First: economies are interlocked rather than ruptured (so decoupling is lose-lose, not zero-sum); China combines hard military power with economic, technological, and soft-power capacities the Soviets never possessed; and the conflict is read in the West as form-of-regime (democracy vs. authoritarianism) rather than as the genuinely ideological contest of the First Cold War. He reads the Beijing summit as asymmetric: the US delegation’s composition signaled a preference to stay in the economic/commercial/technical orbit and avoid strategic files, while China gave the summit a strategic frame and drew a red line on Taiwan against which Washington maintained “strategic silence.” Aourid pushes back on Paul Kennedy’s overextension thesis (it failed to predict America in the 1980s because it omitted soft power) and recommends instead the Chinese strategist Yan Xuetong on leadership-as-attractiveness and moral force. He likens Xi to Theodore Roosevelt (great-power stage-setter) and notes the Western comparison of Trump to Gorbachev wielding the sledgehammer of perestroika-style domestic restructuring. The likely outcome: extended competition that traces “the outlines of a new world” — neither stable Cold War nor Thucydidean inevitability, with Allison himself denying war is inevitable.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Aourid is doing something Anglophone IR commentary rarely does: he takes the Cold War seriously as a successful stabilizing architecture rather than as a regrettable confrontation that ended well only by accident. The “Red Phone” (formally, the Moscow-Washington Hotline Agreement of June 1963, established after the near-miss of the October 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis) is here not a piece of period color but the load-bearing example of what’s missing now. The implicit claim — that the absence of crisis-comms infrastructure between Washington and Beijing makes the present relationship structurally more dangerous than the Cold War rather than less — is not the dominant Anglophone read, which tends to oscillate between “this isn’t a real Cold War, calm down” (Niblett-adjacent) and “this is worse than the Cold War because ideologies are now civilizational” (Pottinger/Wittes-adjacent). Aourid’s “terror of imbalance” framing is sharper: it identifies the specific Cold-War feature whose loss is most consequential.

The Cold-War-comms-absence reading is partially sound but understated in one direction and overstated in another. Understated: the absence is even more thoroughgoing than Aourid emphasizes — the US and USSR had a layered architecture of arms-control treaties (SALT, START, INF, ABM) with verification regimes, observer programs, military-to-military channels, and a shared experience of having stared into the nuclear abyss together in October 1962 that created mutual elite-level recognition of the stakes. US-China has none of this, and the few mil-mil channels that existed (under the Defense Policy Coordination Talks framework) were suspended after Pelosi’s August 2022 Taiwan visit and only partially restored. Overstated: Aourid’s “no operative rules” framing slightly oversells the chaos; the US-China relationship does have implicit rules (decoupling-is-lose-lose, the Taiwan-status-quo ambiguity that has held since 1972, the technology-restriction-but-not-blockade pattern). What it lacks is the codified, dial-tested, redundant infrastructure that stabilized the First Cold War after 1963. The substantive point survives Aourid’s slight overstatement.

What an Arab-world observer sees that Anglophone analysts miss. Two things, both visible in Aourid’s framing. First, he reads the bipolarity through the lens of a region — the Arab world, more broadly the Global South — that is itself an object of the US-China competition rather than a participant in it. The British strategist Robin Niblett quote about “the Global South and whoever can win it over” being the theater of the battle is not throat-clearing for Aourid; it is the framing his readers actually experience. The Anglophone US-China commentariat typically treats the Global South as a passive board on which great-power moves are made; Aourid treats Beijing’s Belt and Road (the “Silk Road” in his phrasing) and the US alternatives as live competing offers that his readership weighs on the merits, with no a priori loyalty to either side. That perspective surfaces the “competitive coexistence” formulation (he attributes it to former Secretary of State Antony Blinken) more naturally than the Anglophone hawk-vs-dove split allows. Second, his invocation of Yan Xuetong — the Tsinghua IR theorist whose “moral realism” argues that great-power leadership depends on attractiveness and moral credibility rather than military or economic power — is unusual in Western commentary and gives Aourid an analytical vocabulary for what the US-China contest looks like to non-aligned states deciding whom to follow. Yan’s framework is one of the few non-Western IR theories that has actually shaped a great power’s elite discourse; that an Arab political scientist reaches for it before he reaches for Kennedy or Kissinger is itself the data.

The piece’s weakness is its slight self-undercutting closing: after building a strong case that the present is more dangerous than the Cold War (no Red Phone, no rules, Thucydides Trap is genuinely open in both directions), Aourid pivots to a soft “we are not in a new Cold War, and the wager remains: not to fall into the Thucydides Trap” that retreats from the sharper diagnosis. The article would be stronger if it followed its own framing through: if the structural-comms architecture is missing and the rules are implicit rather than codified, the wager-not-to-fall-into-the-trap is not a policy posture but a hope. Aourid knows this and the analyst’s caution in pulling the punch is itself worth naming.

The piece’s value to the roundup is as one of very few non-Anglophone reads on US-China bipolarity from a writer who is neither an Atlanticist transplant nor a Beijing client — a Moroccan academic reading the new bipolarity from a position with no automatic loyalty, reaching for both Aron and Yan Xuetong in the same paragraph and treating the absence of Cold-War crisis-comms infrastructure as the load-bearing structural problem.

↑ Back to TOC

An anti-liberal hidden thread runs through 250+ years of American history (一条反自由主义的暗线贯穿了美国 250 多年的历史)

Sun Liping (孙立平) · 2026-05-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Robert Kagan’s 2024 book Rebellion: How Antiliberalism Is Tearing America Apart — Again (Kagan is an American neoconservative historian, Brookings senior fellow, and co-founder of the Project for the New American Century, better known to Chinese readers for his 2003 Of Paradise and Power with its “Americans from Mars, Europeans from Venus” line).

Sun Liping — a retired Tsinghua University sociologist whose WeChat account 孙立平社会观察 is mirrored at szhgh.com — devotes a long expository essay to walking Chinese readers through Kagan’s central thesis: that behind America’s liberal-democratic surface there has always run “an anti-liberal hidden thread” tracing back to the founding, and that Trump and MAGA are this current’s contemporary expression rather than a historical accident. Sun lays out Kagan’s “two Americas” framework: the Declaration’s liberal America of natural-rights equality versus an anti-liberal America rooted in slaveholding southern resistance to liberalism’s “equalizing character,” institutionalized at the founding via the three-fifths compromise and embodied in the contradiction of Jefferson the equality-drafter who owned and sold slaves. He follows Kagan’s genealogy through the 1924 Immigration Act and government-backed eugenic sterilization, the recurrent conspiracy-pattern in which “ordinary whites” are imagined as victims of a Wall Street/Jewish-banker/cosmopolitan/Eastern-intellectual/Black-American elite cabal, into the rise of Christian nationalism. Sun emphasizes Kagan’s warning that liberal institutions “cannot survive when half the country no longer believes in the core principles that underpin the American system of government,” and applies James Burnham’s reading of McCarthy to Trump’s “crude charisma.” In a short closing section Sun notes critics’ two objections: that Kagan conflates anti-liberalism with racism and underexamines liberalism’s own failures, and that the book asks why an anti-liberal demagogue can attract tens of millions of voters but treats the answer as racism rather than as the costs of globalization, elite arrogance, and neglected working-class grievances. Sun endorses the book as useful background for Chinese readers trying to understand the American new right without strongly contesting Kagan’s frame.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The analytically interesting object here is not Kagan’s thesis — which Anglophone readers can find in dozens of New York Review of Books–style essays from 2017 onward — but Sun Liping’s choice to spend a long expository piece relaying it to a Chinese readership through a 2024 book, with only a brief gesture at criticism. The doubly-mediated structure (a Chinese sociologist’s reading of an American neoconservative’s book about America’s deep ideological structure) is itself the data point. Sun is one of mainland China’s most respected liberal-leaning sociologists, and his WeChat account survives because he writes carefully; the choice to use Kagan as a vehicle to discuss American political pathology — rather than, say, discussing PRC politics directly — fits the well-documented Chinese intellectual move of writing about elsewhere as a way of writing about home. The piece reads as exposition because Sun cannot, in this venue, do anything stronger; the work is in the selection of frame.

What’s distinctive in the selection. Sun chose Kagan rather than, say, Steven Levitsky, Yascha Mounk, or Anne Applebaum — all of whom offer similar diagnoses with different vocabularies. Kagan’s “anti-liberalism as parallel main current” thesis is structurally congenial to a Chinese readership that has been told for decades that American liberal-democratic claims are surface and that the deep structure is white-Christian-supremacist; Kagan, as a card-carrying neoconservative, validates this read without requiring Sun to make the argument in his own voice. The Burnham/McCarthy/Trump linkage — “crude charisma,” “the wreckers” — also lands easily with a Chinese audience trained to read American populism through the lens of mob politics. The two critiques Sun cites in closing (conflation of anti-liberalism with racism; failure to engage liberal-elite failure) are exactly the critiques that would be most legible to a Chinese reader skeptical of American moral universalism — they are not the deepest critiques of Kagan available in Anglophone discourse, but they are the most useful ones for the audience.

What the doubly-mediated frame reveals. The piece tells us that an educated Chinese reader following Sun Liping is now encountering Trump-and-MAGA as the surfacing of a 250-year structural feature of America rather than as either a contingent electoral event or a generic populist phenomenon. This is a stronger interpretive frame than the dominant Anglophone center-liberal one, which still treats Trump as anomaly. It is also a frame that makes “American liberalism” much less of a stable model to emulate or worry about — if liberalism was always shadowed by an equal-and-opposite tradition, then PRC critiques of American hypocrisy are not propaganda but description. Sun does not push this conclusion, and likely does not need to: laying out the Kagan thesis at length, with only mild qualification, accomplishes the work.

What Sun’s exposition leaves underexamined. The Kagan frame Sun relays has a real weakness Sun doesn’t probe: it conflates the persistence of an anti-egalitarian undercurrent (well-documented, uncontroversial) with the structural inevitability of Trumpism’s current form (a much stronger and contestable claim). The 2024 Trump coalition includes large blocs of Black, Hispanic, and Asian-American voters in numbers that complicate the “white Christian European civilization defending itself” frame Kagan reads as the throughline. A piece written for Chinese readers might have noted that the contemporary coalition’s demographics force at least a partial revision of the pure-racial-resentment reading — Sun cites the working-class-grievance critique but does not develop it. The result is that Chinese readers get a clean, narratively satisfying frame for understanding Trump that is sharper than the truth, and that conveniently aligns with prior Chinese diagnoses of American decay.

The piece’s analytical value to the roundup is precisely as a window on how Chinese liberal-academic discourse — not the propaganda apparatus, but the careful, professionally-cautious intellectual layer — is currently metabolizing the second Trump administration. The answer: through a 2024 American neoconservative’s structural-pessimism frame, lightly qualified, that confirms long-running PRC intuitions about the gap between American liberal self-presentation and American actually-existing political life.

↑ Back to TOC

Netizen Voices: Is Xi Jinping on Top of the World, or a Tortoise on a Utility Pole?

Samuel Wade · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a banned Douyin (TikTok’s mainland-China counterpart) video posted earlier this month in which a narrator argued that “if you see a tortoise perched on top of a utility pole, there’s no way it climbed up there itself” — universally read by Chinese netizens as a Xi Jinping allegory.

China Digital Times translator Samuel Wade compiles the video’s premise and a long selection of Douyin user comments before and after the account was banned. The tortoise figure carried multiple referents: Xi’s limited formal education (he is sometimes called “the junior high schooler,” a jab at the Cultural Revolution interrupting his schooling); his alleged elevation by Party elders rather than competence (“It must have struck a deal with the Immortals”); his dismantling of orderly leadership succession; and the tortoise’s longevity, which commenters cast as a “scary prospect” rather than as praise. The comments split into several registers — moralistic (“when rank exceeds virtue, calamity must follow”), structural (“its support base of second- and third-generation tortoises put it up there”), passive-fatalist (“it’ll wear itself out eventually, nod off against the high-voltage line, and get burned to a crisp”), and notably a cluster invoking foreign intervention by name, with users referencing “B2,” “F35,” “bald eagle,” “drone,” and “targeted U.S. strike” — translated by Wade explicitly “without endorsement” — alongside oblique nods to recent precedents in Venezuela and Iran (the latter a reference to the US-Israeli airstrike that killed Khamenei). A subsequent pig-on-utility-pole video followed; its poster was reportedly detained for 15 days. Coded language is everywhere: “yq” pinyin substitution for “pandemic” (疫情) to evade keyword scans, weapons emojis substituting for “guns.”

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The piece’s value is not the meme itself — pole-top-tortoise as ruler-allegory is a centuries-old trope, and Wade is documenting one Douyin instance, not making a sociological claim. The value is the comment-thread translation, which is the rare unfiltered window into mainland-China internet sentiment about Xi specifically in a week (mid-May 2026) when the Anglophone press is reading Xi as triumphant for hosting Trump and Putin in back-to-back state visits. The comments document a domestic register that contradicts the international-stagecraft frame: a meaningful slice of Douyin users in May 2026 read Xi as a placeholder elevated by elite networks (Wade’s translation: “the Immortals,” i.e., Party elders), incapable of governing on his own, and likely to fall — by attrition, by elite turn, or, in the most striking cluster, by external strike. That last cluster matters because it crosses what was until recently a redline in mainland online discourse: openly fantasizing about a US military strike on a Chinese leader, framed by reference to Iran (the Khamenei airstrike) and Venezuela. The references would have been unimaginable in Xi-era Chinese internet discourse five years ago.

The methodological caveat the piece earns: China Digital Times’ Wade is sampling comments on a video that itself was promoted because it was provocative, then preserved and ban-evaded by users who agreed with its premise. The selection is by definition not representative of Chinese internet sentiment as a whole; it represents the subset of users who (a) found the video, (b) agreed enough with it to comment, and (c) commented in registers that survived the platform’s moderation long enough to be screenshotted. Wade does not claim otherwise — the framing is “netizen voices,” a recurring CDT format documenting censored-but-spreading dissent. The question for a reader is not “what do Chinese people think of Xi” but “what views are getting through the censorship layer, and how have those views shifted?” On that narrower question, the spread documented here — from gentle ridicule to explicit calls for foreign intervention — is a meaningful update on Xi’s domestic standing as he enters his fourth term.

The piece also illustrates the live-fire vocabulary-evasion practice that any serious analysis of Chinese-internet discourse has to reckon with: “yq” for 疫情 to dodge keyword scans on pandemic-related content, weapons emojis substituting for the word “guns,” “Immortals” for “Party elders,” even “B2” / “F35” / “bald eagle” as compressed substitutions for “the United States will fix this.” The vocabulary is not folkloric — it is an adaptive response to the censorship architecture, and tracking which terms become unsayable and which workarounds emerge is itself a window into what the regime considers most threatening. That CDT translates “tortoise” and “pole” without much explanation while flagging “yq” as a censorship workaround tells you which layer of the discourse the regime is policing most aggressively.

↑ Back to TOC

Germany is Out of Options

Anthony J. Constantini · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the conventional-wisdom narrative that Europe and specifically Germany are rearming in response to Trump’s NATO pressure, anchored in von der Leyen’s €800B EU defense pledge and Merz’s 3.5%-of-GDP commitment via the Hague Summit Declaration. Constantini (a foreign-policy writer publishing in Commonplace/American Compass orbit) argues both pledges are stage props: von der Leyen’s €800B is really €150B, a third of which goes to Poland (already rearmed); Germany’s 3.5% was quietly walked back to 3.05%; and every meaningful deadline is set after Trump leaves office (2029, 2030, 2035).

The structural case has three legs. (1) Economy: German electricity costs 39.6¢/kWh versus 15¢ in the US (the consequence of cheap-Russian-gas dependence plus the mid-2010s decision to eliminate nuclear); the industrial base shed 160,000 jobs in 2025 alone with Volkswagen closing its first-ever home-country factory; Merz is now imposing health-system cuts and considering sin taxes, making him the world’s worst-polling democratic leader at 19% approval. (2) Society: Germans haven’t fought an offensive war since 1945; a 2024 poll found 57% would not fight to defend their country; the December 2025 military-questionnaire-on-turning-18 law (much milder than US selective service) drew thousands of student protesters. (3) Politics: the cordon sanitaire policy keeps the AfD (currently polling 29% versus CDU’s 22%) out of coalitions, leaving Merz’s CDU only the SPD/Greens/Linke as partners — none of whom will accept the welfare cuts rearmament requires. Constantini’s prescription: cut US troop presence by two-thirds from 30,000 to force German action, since “Germany’s promises are not worth the headlines they are printed on.”

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Constantini’s structural argument is the strongest section — the three legs do hang together, and the “every deadline is after Trump leaves” observation is genuinely load-bearing. Pledges are cheap if the political price comes due under your successor’s tenure, and the pattern (2029 for German 3.5%, 2030 for EU €800B, 2035 for the Hague 3.5%/3.5% split) is too consistent to dismiss as scheduling coincidence. The piece’s coordinate position is mechanism-first restraint — it pairs naturally with Sumantra Maitra’s case for European burden-shifting and (partially) with the Sternberg WSJ piece on European decline — and the analysis is strongest where it stays in that lane.

The weakest move is the “57% wouldn’t fight” Gallup data. Cross-national polling on hypothetical wartime willingness is notoriously poor at predicting actual behavior — pre-1939 Germans, pre-1941 Americans, and pre-2022 Ukrainians all polled lower than their eventual mobilization rates. The “Germans haven’t fought an offensive war since 1945” frame is also doing more work than its evidence supports: the relevant comparison is not 1945 Wehrmacht-style mass mobilization but the kind of defense-industrial capacity expansion the Bundeswehr is actually being asked to deliver. Constantini conflates “Germans will not stomach a draft” (probably true) with “Germany cannot rearm” (a different and weaker claim, since modern rearmament is mostly capital, not labor). The 1.35 birth-rate-as-rearmament-constraint argument has the same problem — a 250,000-person Bundeswehr does not require demographic surplus, it requires recruitment yield, which is policy-tunable.

The most interesting unaddressed wrinkle is the AfD. Constantini treats the cordon sanitaire as a structural constraint that traps the CDU, but the piece is silent on what happens if the cordon breaks — and the AfD’s foreign-policy posture (Russia-friendly) is precisely the opposite of what the piece wants. If Merz cannot rearm because the AfD is locked out, and the AfD cannot rearm because the AfD doesn’t want to, then “force Germany to act by withdrawing US troops” produces a German government that responds by hedging toward Moscow, not Warsaw. The piece’s policy prescription assumes the German political system can produce a rearmament coalition if pressure is applied; the analysis it just walked through suggests no such coalition exists at any pressure level. That tension is the structural finding that should be carried forward — it’s an important convergence with what Tooze and Setser have been arguing from very different priors, and it deserves engagement that the piece declines to offer.

↑ Back to TOC

Europe Can Be Poor if It Wants—but Does It?

Joseph C. Sternberg · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the backlash to Sternberg’s earlier WSJ column “What Happens When Europeans Find Out How Poor They Are?”, which drew unusually intense vitriol on X and a Substack debunking attempt from an American former newspaper columnist (unnamed but contextually identifiable as Krugman or similar), followed by a more rigorous response from Luis Garicano (co-author of last year’s European-competitiveness manifesto) and his son Pieter Garicano laying out the case that Europe is losing its mojo relative to America.

Sternberg (WSJ’s “Political Economics” columnist; the project’s center-right transatlantic political-economy specialist) uses the controversy as the entry point to a structural argument about why the European prosperity-versus-welfare-state debate is generating such disproportionate emotional heat. His core analytical move is to bracket the technical economic dispute (PPP-adjusted GDP versus nominal output; quality-of-life-versus-output metrics) and zoom in on what the heat is really about: the collapse of an economic theory European politicians have pursued for decades, in which a well-constructed welfare state was supposed to deliver the same material standard of living as American capitalism, or better. The thesis isn’t true, and the embarrassment falls on those Europeans (mainly left, but also some right) who built careers arguing it. Sternberg explicitly notes this isn’t a normative claim — European electorates can legitimately prioritize welfare over growth — but observes that this wasn’t the trade-off voters were told they were making, and “much of the Continent’s political turmoil of the past decade traces back in one form or another to voters’ insistence that politicians honor the old nonsense promise of simultaneously have-able and eatable cake.”

The aesthetic-cultural reading: it drives Europeans “of all stripes bananas” to discover that America, regarded as crass, keeps pulling ahead — especially under Trump. Sternberg’s structural contention is that America has built an economy resilient enough to survive tariffs, scattershot industrial policy, the Iran war, an ill-conceived immigration crackdown, and Trump’s Truth Social feed — any one of which could sink a European economy, with Iran-war energy-price spikes potentially doing exactly that. He concedes American vulnerabilities (a potential private-credit bubble, distributional fights over healthcare) but distinguishes them structurally: “America’s vulnerabilities tend to arise from our failure to constrain an excess of animal spirits, while Europe’s malaise arises from its struggle to summon any.” Sternberg’s closing read of European emotional hypersensitivity to economic-decline discussion is that it is itself a reason for optimism — “a genuinely contented polity would be indifferent to such criticism” — and that “more Europeans are wondering how contented they truly are.”

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Sternberg is operating in his recurring transatlantic-Political-Economics register here, and this is a sharper-than-average iteration because the column has a specific empirical referent (the Garicano response and the X backlash) rather than just the standard “Europe is stagnating” complaint. The “animal spirits” framing — America’s vulnerabilities from excess versus Europe’s from deficiency — is the column’s most useful analytical contribution because it actually does work in distinguishing the two diagnoses. It also pairs well with the Maitra cluster on Europe this same week (Maitra’s Hungarian Conservative piece on Europe in China’s shadow; Maitra’s Chronicles piece on the European force-posture shift under Trump) and with Constantini’s continued Italian-elite-failure register, building a multi-author convergence on the European-decline diagnosis that the synthesis composer should surface.

What Sternberg is doing well is bracketing the technical metrics dispute (PPP, quality-of-life adjustments) and reframing the debate around what European electorates were told they were getting versus what they have. The political-bargain framing is genuinely sharp: the welfare-state-and-prosperity bargain promised voters they would not have to choose, and the column’s strongest move is observing that the “political turmoil of the past decade” — Brexit, Le Pen, AfD, Meloni — traces in part to voters realizing they were lied to about the trade-off. That ties the European-economic-decline thread to the European-populist-rise thread in a non-trivial way and adds explanatory power that neither thread alone has.

Where Sternberg is weaker is in his sectoral framing of the American advantage. The column treats America’s resilience as a feature of structural dynamism, but most of the specific shocks it lists (tariffs, the Iran war, immigration crackdown, Truth Social) are not market-side phenomena but policy-side ones. The American economy has indeed absorbed them, but the cost of doing so — the inflation that produced 2026 cost-of-living rage Stoller covers, the fiscal trajectory Noahpinion has been warning about, the cumulative effect on alliance-network credibility Fukuyama analyzes elsewhere in this week’s roundup — is meaningful and not in Sternberg’s frame. A more careful version would have asked whether the American “animal spirits” advantage is being capitalized faster than it is being produced, with the post-Trump bill coming due later. The closing optimism about European emotional hypersensitivity is also a stretch — “they are upset, therefore they might reform” is weak evidence; the Brexit voters were also upset, and got Brexit not productivity-enhancing structural reform.

The piece also engages in a recurring Sternberg pattern that’s worth flagging: framing critics (“typically American liberals and America-skeptic Europeans”) as motivated by ideology rather than evidence while presenting his own position (“generally euroskeptic Americans and Europeans with experience of both places”) as the neutral observer’s view. This is a familiar move but it weakens the column’s analytical credibility — the Garicanos are not “neutral observers”; they have a specific competitiveness-agenda priors set, and the Krugman-side (or whoever the unnamed Substack debunker is) has its own priors. Acknowledging the symmetry would have strengthened, not weakened, the substantive case Sternberg is making. As a follow-up piece on a meaningful European-economic-trajectory question, it earns its slot in the roundup; as transatlantic political-economy analysis, it pairs productively with Maitra’s parallel European-decline framings to build a structural cluster the synthesis can engage.

↑ Back to TOC

Trump Is Correct About Shift in European Force Posture

Sumantra Maitra · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Pentagon’s recent decisions to cancel the rotation of the 2nd Armored Brigade Combat Team in Poland, reduce troop presence in Germany, and close the Norfolk NATO training base, shifting elements to France. Maitra — writing in his usual mechanism-first IR-realist register and from his “Dormant NATO” doctrine of structural rebalancing — endorses these moves as delayed but correct exercises of presidential prerogative, citing the historical baseline that US European troop strength fell from 300,000 at the Cold War’s end to ~60,000 by the late 1990s (the last period of US budget balance, he notes parenthetically). His core analytical move: only three pieces of European/peripheral real estate matter to American strategic interest — Greenland for the Arctic, Germany as continental logistical hub, and Turkey for the Mediterranean/Black Sea — while Spain, Italy, Poland, and Romania are tactically useful but strategically peripheral. The warning he wants restrainers to hear: if the drawdown becomes mere eastward relocation into Poland or the Baltics, it functions as an implicit American security guarantee that intensifies tensions with Russia while removing the European incentive to build independent defense capacity (he points approvingly to Germany’s new 45th Panzer Brigade in Lithuania as the right direction — Europeans deploying forward, not Americans). The piece closes with the Eisenhower 1956 quotation that NATO would have “failed” if US troops remained in Europe a decade on, framing burden-shift as the original transatlantic design.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Maitra in his canonical lane and at his clearest. The piece is a short, disciplined argument with a single mechanism: forward US deployment functions as an implicit subsidy that suppresses European defense investment, so retrenchment only works if it’s actually retrenchment rather than relocation. The “three pieces of real estate” framing (Greenland / Germany / Turkey) is sharp and falsifiable — readers who disagree have to identify which of Spain, Italy, Poland, or Romania holds long-run strategic value that Germany and Turkey don’t subsume, and that’s a productive disagreement to be invited into.

The piece’s strongest move is the warning against the bait-and-switch a hawkish Pentagon could easily execute: pull troops from Germany, redeploy them eastward, declare burden-shift accomplished, and watch European defense spending stall because the implicit guarantee survives in stronger form. This is exactly the policy substitution restrainers worry the second Trump administration’s military bureaucracy could deliver if not watched carefully, and Maitra is doing useful work flagging it inside a conservative venue (Chronicles) where the natural reader might otherwise interpret eastward movement as Trump being “tough on Russia.”

The weakness is what’s not in the piece: any engagement with what Eastern European partners actually think they’re getting from the forward deployments, or with the political economy of how Germany’s defense build-out (the 45th Panzer Brigade is real, but ~5,000 troops is a single brigade, not a continental defense force) is supposed to scale up to replace 80,000 Americans across realistic timelines. The Eisenhower quotation is doing rhetorical work that the historical record doesn’t fully support — Eisenhower’s NATO vision was more contingent on European recovery than the line suggests, and 70 years of subsequent commitment is not best explained as serial mistake. The piece is a clean restrainer-internal argument; it’s not a fully worked-out persuasion of the skeptical Atlanticist.

For the composition phase: pair with Sternberg (this week’s WSJ Political Economics piece on Europe, if surviving) and Tooze if any China-European-pivot pieces survive. The three Maitra pieces this week (this one + Hungarian Conservative on Trump-Xi summit + Engelsberg on Bengali liberalism) should NOT be themed together — the Bengali essay is a different register entirely; these two transatlantic pieces are the natural pair.

↑ Back to TOC

Britain’s Far Right Want Power

Luke Hallam · 2026-05-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the May 16, 2026 “Unite the Kingdom” march in central London organized by far-right activist Tommy Robinson (born Stephen Yaxley, EDL co-founder), which drew an estimated 60,000 attendees one week after Reform UK (Nigel Farage’s right-populist party) gained over a thousand council seats in local elections and Labour and the Conservatives both posted abysmal numbers — events Hallam frames as the likely toppling of Keir Starmer’s premiership.

Hallam, Persuasion’s senior editor, attends the march as a ground-level reporter and uses the texture of conversations with attendees to build a thesis about where British politics is going. The coalition he finds is surprisingly heterogeneous — an Iranian couple who hate Sharia law, an Indian student who doesn’t want Islam to spread in the UK, a Hindu German business owner with Afghan parents, working-class white Brexit voters from Luton and Birmingham, Christian evangelicals carrying crucifixes (“REPENT, TODAY WE ARE IN THE LAST DAYS”), British admirers of Trump (Robinson plays a Marco Rubio video and praises Charlie Kirk on stage), and Iranian monarchists carrying Reza Pahlavi imagery. The convergent view across the crowd is that radical Islam is the central civilizational threat — “Britain is broken, nobody is fixing it” — and the grievances are anchored to a specific chain of events: Brexit-era migration that increased rather than fell, post-COVID asylum-seeker hotels, the multi-region “grooming gangs” failures (police slow to investigate group abuse of young girls for fear of appearing racist), and the 2024 Southport stabbings of three girls that triggered a week of riots after Robinson amplified the false rumor that the killer was a Muslim asylum-seeker. Hallam acknowledges these concerns are not pure invention — religious intimidation, blasphemy flare-ups, hardline imam “sharia councils” arbitrating divorce, and attacks on British Jews are real — but argues the crowd has inflated them into “a vast narrative of terminal decline and elite betrayal,” in which any failure becomes a symptom of “civilizational collapse at the hands of one religion.” His structural reading: the movement was years in the making (Brexit, GB News as now-the-most-watched news channel, post-2008 stagnation, the small-boats crisis) and is not a fading street phenomenon but a coalescing electoral force whose voters will give Reform their ballots only because no party further right is viable. Hallam closes with Robinson’s “Are you ready for the Battle of Britain?” line and the question of how far the movement will go if it attains real power.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Hallam is doing the dispatch-as-argument move (sister to Goodhart-style civic reportage) in a register the Persuasion lineup is otherwise light on — ground-level texture from a rally, building toward a structural claim about an electorate. The reporting itself is the piece’s strongest asset: the intersectionality of the coalition (his word, slightly tongue-in-cheek) is genuinely the kind of detail that complicates the standard “white-working-class racism” frame, and the specific quotes (the “Stan Laurel” man, the Christian evangelical narrating Robinson’s prison conversion, the Tony-from-Birmingham complaint that Muslims “feast” rather than fast) read as authentic rather than cherry-picked. The Iranian-monarchist and Hindu-Afghan-business-owner presence is the kind of empirical detail that, properly weighed, ought to reshape the analytical frame for British populism — but Hallam doesn’t quite weigh it.
