The frame remains “far right” throughout, which is accurate in self-identification (these people are at a Tommy Robinson rally) but elides the question of whether the political coalition forming around immigration-restriction is better understood as a cross-ethnic restrictionist majority that includes some long-standing far-right activists rather than as a far-right movement that has co-opted some unusual demographics. The conceptual category would change the predicted political trajectory.

The piece’s other tension is between Hallam’s two analytical moves: (a) “these concerns are real, partially valid, and have not been addressed by the political class” and (b) “but in this crowd they have been inflated into a vast narrative of civilizational collapse.” Both are defensible but they sit uneasily together — if (a) is right, then a serious response would address the grievances on their own terms (immigration numbers, integration, police-failure-on-grooming-gangs as a discrete enforcement issue) rather than treating (b) as the operative phenomenon. Hallam’s closing implies the movement won’t be satisfied until it attains real power, which suggests he thinks (b) is the real driver, but the piece never quite commits. The strongest version of his argument would be that the gap between the legitimate grievances of (a) and the inflated narrative of (b) is what makes the movement politically dangerous — both that it is grounded in real failures the political class refused to address, and that the inflation of those failures into a “civilizational collapse” frame makes it impervious to incremental reform. That synthesis would explain why Hallam thinks Reform will not be satisfied with merely winning office, but the piece leaves the reader to assemble it.

A pairing note for the synthesis composer: Hallam’s piece pairs with the Constantini/Maitra/Sternberg cluster on European political dysfunction this week, and within that cluster offers something the others don’t — close-up texture on the substance of the populist coalition rather than its electoral arithmetic or elite-failure thesis. The UK case is also useful as a contrast to the U.S. populist coalition Stoller describes (also this week), where the “rage of the billionaires” is the structural pivot — in the UK, the rage seems to come from voters at an elite class that did not deliver, with no Trump-equivalent figure yet capable of metabolizing it.

↑ Back to TOC

Drone incursions in Baltic states: how to respond?

Euro|topics (curated debate) · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Russian (and disputed Ukrainian) large-drone incursions into Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania over the past two weeks, including the first NATO shoot-down of a drone over an Estonian lake, an official air-raid alert in Vilnius, and a coalition collapse in Riga.

Euro|topics curates five commentaries spanning Swiss, Latvian, Lithuanian, and two Estonian outlets. Wolfgang Koydl in Weltwoche (Switzerland) frames the incursions as evidence that NATO territory is being turned into a war zone by two warring parties — with Russia blaming Ukrainian launches transiting Baltic airspace and Ukraine blaming Russian electronic jamming — and faults Europe for the absence of a peace initiative. Elita Veidemane in Neatkarīgā (Latvia) attacks the Latvian government’s “stay away from windows” guidance as proof of institutional incapacity: no drone-defense wall, no civil-defense system, no legal authorization to shoot drones down, and no weapons capable of doing so. Asta Dykovienė in Kauno diena (Lithuania) uses a Speaker-of-Parliament gaffe — that air-raid alerts make “people” feel safer when shelter capacity ran out — to needle Vilnius’s lack of seriousness. The two Estonian outlets diverge: Delfi argues Latvia and Lithuania overreacted and let nervousness halt public life, while Eesti Rahvusringhääling’s Meelis Oidsalu argues for an AI-and-robotics interception system on the urgency level of the euro transition, conceding peacetime shoot-downs over populated areas may be more dangerous than non-interception. The selection deliberately mixes a Swiss-skeptic view, two frontline-state self-flagellations, an Estonian “calm down,” and an Estonian techno-modernization pitch.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The Euro|topics editorial team’s curation here is the argument, and it reflects a reasonable spread of frontline-NATO-state opinion on a genuinely live question: how should small Baltic states with no native drone-interception capacity react when large drones (Geran/Shahed-class, whoever launched them) enter their airspace? The five outlets included are all credible mainstream-press venues in their respective countries — not nationalist agitprop. The most analytically interesting move is including Weltwoche, a Swiss right-wing weekly often skeptical of NATO solidarity narratives, alongside the frontline-state self-criticism. Koydl’s framing (“two warring parties are turning NATO territory into a war zone”) is the closest thing in the curation to a Kremlin-adjacent reading, but it’s tempered by his observation that NATO and the EU both accept the Russian-jamming explanation. Including this voice rather than excluding it as fringe is honest.

The Estonian split — Delfi saying the Latvians and Lithuanians overreacted, ERR’s Oidsalu arguing for serious investment in AI-and-robotics interception — is the most informative pairing. It reveals that the frontline-state response is not monolithic: even within Estonia, where NATO shot down a drone last week, one outlet treats the incidents as manageable nuisance while the other treats them as a multi-year strategic gap requiring euro-transition-level urgency. This is exactly the cross-elite disagreement a US reader would never see filtered through the Anglophone wire services, which would default to either “Russia escalates” or “NATO responds firmly.”

What the curation misses: no Polish voice (Poland borders Lithuania and Belarus and has had its own drone-incursion incidents); no German voice on the burden-sharing question (Germany sells the Patriots that would in principle defend Baltic airspace); and no Russian-language Baltic minority voice on what the incidents mean for Russian-speakers inside Latvia/Estonia. The Veidemane Latvian-government-bashing is appropriate but stands alone — there’s no piece arguing the opposite, that NATO’s air-policing mission is the appropriate response to small-state incapacity. The “Latvia is defenseless” frame is left undisputed in the curation, which lets the reader infer the Baltics need to spend more on themselves without anyone making the contrary “this is what NATO is for” argument. Still, as a debate-aggregation, this is the kind of cross-national-elite-opinion read no single Anglophone outlet provides.

↑ Back to TOC

Merz proposes associate EU membership for Ukraine

Euro|topics (curated debate) · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: German Chancellor Friedrich Merz’s proposal to create an “associate EU membership” status for Ukraine — under which Kyiv would attend EU decision-making bodies without voting rights — as a preliminary step toward full accession.

Euro|topics curates four commentaries from German, Austrian, and Ukrainian outlets. Knut Krohn in Stuttgarter Zeitung (Germany) concedes the benefits would be “mostly symbolic” but argues the proposal sends a clear signal to Kyiv that its struggle is recognized and to the Kremlin that Russia will not win, and that Ukraine — possessor of “the best army in Europe” — could become an economic-recovery engine post-war. Eric Bonse in taz (Germany, left-leaning) calls it “Chancellor shoots from the hip again”: the proposal would sideline Western Balkan candidates who have waited twenty years, would oblige EU members to activate the mutual-assistance clause if Russia escalates, and has unforeseeable consequences. Thomas Mayer in Der Standard (Austria) argues full membership is legally impossible for a country at war, so Merz is offering a face-saving fiction; in practice, Ukraine’s integration is already underway via unprecedented EU loans and grants. Serhij Sydorenko in European Pravda (Ukraine) gives the sharpest reading: the proposal is a “waiting room” designed to indefinitely defer Kyiv’s full membership while opening the door for Moldova, granting Ukraine no rights, exposing it to all EU obligations, and offering no legal guarantee that survives a German or EU government change.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The Euro|topics curation here is sharper than the Baltic-drones piece because the four commentaries genuinely disagree about whether Merz’s proposal is statesmanship, hip-shooting, or trap. The strongest piece by analytical content is the Ukrainian one — Sydorenko’s European Pravda reading is the only voice in the curation that takes the obligations side of the deal seriously and points out that an “associate” status without treaty protection can be unilaterally revoked. That this voice is included rather than filtered out is the curation’s most important move; it lets the reader see what the proposal looks like from inside Kyiv, where “EU association without voting rights” reads not as concession but as the kind of trap British and French “advisors” used to install in nineteenth-century Persia (to borrow Frankopan’s analogy from elsewhere this week).

The intra-German split between Stuttgarter Zeitung’s center-right enthusiasm and taz’s left-skeptical reading is informative about which German factions are reading the proposal which way. taz’s specific objection — that Merz would trigger the mutual-assistance clause obligating all EU states to defend Ukraine if Russia escalates — is the one operational concern in the whole bundle, and it’s left undisputed by the other German voice. The Austrian outlet’s framing (“integration is already underway”) is the kind of European-bureaucratic shrug that treats Merz’s proposal as Theater for the Public rather than as substantive policy. Each of the four voices is doing different argumentative work, which is the point of an aggregator.

What the curation misses: no Polish voice (Poland is the EU’s biggest pro-Ukraine voice and has its own complicated relationship to Ukrainian accession on agricultural-import grounds); no Hungarian or Slovak voice (the two EU members most likely to block any of this); no Russian-perspective acknowledgment of how this lands in Moscow. The Western Balkans concern that Bonse raises — Albania, North Macedonia, Serbia, Montenegro, Bosnia waiting twenty years — is mentioned in passing but no Balkan voice is included to actually press it. The selection is heavy on the German-Austrian-Ukrainian triangle and light on the rest of the EU, which is itself a reasonable curatorial choice given that the proposal originated from Berlin, but worth naming. As a snapshot of where European opinion lands on a German chancellor’s mid-war improvisation, this is more useful than reading the original Merz speech.

↑ Back to TOC

Wokeness Has Peaked. What Followed Is Worse.

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the post-2022 retreat of cancel culture, DEI, and progressive cultural hegemony — and the contemporaneous rise of political violence (Kirk and Brian Thompson assassinations, attempts on Trump), Sanders-style anti-AI politics, California’s billionaire-tax referendum, and rising attacks on Jews. Cowen revisits his early-2022 prediction that wokeism had peaked and pronounces it vindicated: universities have retreated from DEI, Musk flipped Twitter, the media has moved right, and minority defections from the Obama coalition helped reelect Trump. But what replaced wokeism, he argues, is worse — a “culture of anger and resentment” whose energies have migrated from policing speech to “concrete action,” including wealth-confiscation referenda, attempts to halt AI and data-center construction, rising “third worldism” expressed as antisemitic attacks, and a new wave of political assassinations. Wokeism, he concedes, was a “relatively harmless way of distracting activists” with wars over words; the new mode skips the words and goes to action. He frames the shift as gendered (older wokeism had “stronger female influences”; new approaches have “more masculine overtones” and tilt fascistic) and applies the same diagnosis to the right (“increasingly open and blatant antisemitism”). The deep claim is that wokeism was never the disease — American negativity was — and conservatives who thought they’d won the culture war won a Pyrrhic victory because the underlying affect simply rechanneled. Note: the central causal claim — that left-wing rhetoric about “democracy destruction” made the Kirk/Thompson/Trump-attempt killings “conceivable” — is asserted, not argued; Cowen explicitly hedges that “it can be debated” how many of the killers had left connections, then proceeds as if the link were established.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is a Free Press guest essay, not an MR post, and reads accordingly — Cowen states his thesis directly rather than gesturing at it, so the project’s standing “Cowen is often Straussian” prior doesn’t really apply here. The piece should be evaluated on its literal content, which is doing two distinct moves bundled into one essay: (1) a sociological observation about the migration of left-coded political energy from speech-policing to direct action, and (2) a causal claim that this migration is responsible for, or at least enabled, a wave of high-salience political violence. The first move is genuinely interesting and consistent with what other writers across the corpus have noticed (the rise of “abundance” framing, the Mamdani current, the DSA pivot from cancel-culture battles to material politics). The second is much weaker than Cowen pretends.

The Kirk and Thompson killings are doing enormous load-bearing work in the argument, but Cowen never reckons with the obvious alternative explanation: that high-profile political and corporate-executive killings have multiple proximate causes (the Thompson shooter’s grievance was healthcare-system, not “democracy destruction”; the Trump attempts had eclectic motivations) and that aggregating them under a “left rhetoric made this conceivable” frame is exactly the kind of move he would skewer if a left-wing writer made the symmetric claim about right-wing rhetoric and Buffalo or El Paso. His one-sentence “to be clear, the right has done bad things too” disclaimer doesn’t fix this — it’s the rhetorical equivalent of “with all due respect.”

The gendered framing (“masculine overtones,” “fascistic approaches and away from touchy-feely universalism”) is the most under-defended assertion in the piece. He offers no evidence that the cohorts pursuing wealth-confiscation referenda or attacking data centers are more male than the cohorts that drove DEI; this just gets dropped in as a stylized fact. The strong piece embedded in here is the observation that the successor ideology to peak-wokeism is materially more consequential — Bruenig and Sanders and the California billionaire-tax campaign are operating on policy levers in ways that the 2020 racial-equity audit wave never did. That’s worth taking seriously and worth disliking from a libertarian’s standpoint. But the move from “the new mode targets material outcomes” to “the new mode produces assassinations” is the kind of step Cowen would normally demand evidence for, and the essay supplies none.

What survives is the diagnostic claim that the underlying affect — low trust, hedonic stagnation, anti-elite resentment — outlived the cultural skirmish and is now in search of new objects. That observation is sharp and worth foregrounding. The causal-attribution claim about violence should be flagged as the piece’s weakest move; downstream agents should not treat “left-adjacent movements caused the assassination wave” as something Cowen demonstrated.

↑ Back to TOC

Why College Grads Are Booing Their Commencement Speakers

Michelle Goldberg · 2026-05-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The boos that greeted former Google CEO Eric Schmidt’s commencement speech at the University of Arizona and real-estate executive Gloria Caulfield’s similar speech at the University of Central Florida — both of whom invoked AI as transformational and were heckled — plus a Stanford 30-country survey on trust in national AI regulation and a recent NYT poll on AI sentiment.

Goldberg uses the commencement boos as the entry point for a structural argument about why American AI hostility is unusually intense. Polling data does the work: only 18 percent of Gen Z feel hopeful about AI, 47 percent of US voters under 30 rate it “mostly bad” (the highest of any age bracket), and Stanford researchers found Americans had the lowest trust of any of 30 surveyed countries in their leaders’ ability to regulate the technology. She names the cross-orientation political response (Sanders and AOC on the left, James Fishback on the right calling for data-center moratoriums; Hannah Einbinder of HBO’s “Hacks” calling AI’s creators “losers”; a Molotov cocktail at Sam Altman’s house) as evidence the hostility is broad-based rather than ideological. The diagnostic frame is borrowed from Bharat Ramamurti, formerly deputy director of Biden’s National Economic Council: Japan uses public funding and regulatory policy to push AI toward complementing rather than replacing workers; in the Nordic countries, workers have a formal role in deciding AI deployment and can use acceptance as a bargaining chip; the US, after the post-Reagan evisceration of organized labor, simply lacks the “institutions for mediating these kinds of technological advances.” The result is AI experienced as extractive — grocery stores using customer data to set personalized pricing, health insurers using AI to deny coverage, a Medicare prior-authorization pilot leaving patients waiting weeks — against a backdrop of ~120,000 AI-attributed US layoffs since last year per the Alliance for Secure AI. The closing move is the irony that Schmidt himself co-authored an NYT op-ed last year warning that more people outside Silicon Valley need to feel AI’s beneficial impact — a goal he then tried to achieve via hectoring.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Goldberg working a comparative political-economy frame on a domestic-discontent story, and it’s stronger than her standard culture-and-democracy register because she has actual cross-national variation to lean on. The Ramamurti material is the column’s load-bearing evidence: if Americans dislike AI more than people in countries with stronger labor institutions, then “Americans are uniquely anxious about technology” or “AI is uniquely scary” becomes a weaker explanation than “American institutions don’t mediate the transition.” The Japan and Nordic examples are concrete enough (Norway’s self-driving buses, Nordic workers using AI acceptance as a bargaining chip) to do real comparative work rather than just gesture at “Europe handles this better.” The strongest version of the column’s argument is the one it actually makes: this is a labor-institutions story, and the absence of those institutions in the US — a deliberate post-Reagan political achievement — is producing predictable political consequences a generation later.

That said, the framework is doing some work the data doesn’t quite license. The Stanford 30-country survey measures trust in regulators, not the underlying labor-institution thickness; the column uses the trust gap as evidence for the institutions gap, but these are causally entangled in ways that complicate the inference. Americans might distrust their AI regulators specifically because of recent state capture (the Politico-reported super-PAC and lobbying surge Goldberg herself cites) rather than because of long-running labor-institution erosion. The two diagnoses point to different remedies: institutional rebuilding is a 20-year project; restricting AI-industry political spending is a single legislative session. The column conflates them under “broken democratic feedback loop.”

The piece is also somewhat uneven in how it grades different anti-AI signals. The Molotov cocktail at Altman’s house and the Einbinder “losers” quote are treated as evidence of the same underlying public sentiment as the commencement boos and the polling data — but these are different things (an act of political violence by one individual, a comedian’s media-cycle quote, audience-level booing, and survey responses) and the column would be sharper if it separated diffuse public mood from elite-cultural signaling from individual political violence. The “anti-AI violence” framing in particular needs care: one Molotov cocktail does not establish a trend, and treating it as confirmation of the broader sentiment risks the same category-error the column would presumably criticize if applied to right-coded political violence.

The strongest underexplored move in the piece is the convergence she names in passing — Sanders and AOC plus James Fishback all calling for data-center moratoriums — which is exactly the kind of unexpected-aligned-poles signal worth tracking. The synthesis can do more with this than the column does: when a Biden-era National Economic Council operative, a comedian, two democratic-socialist senators, and a right-populist activist all converge on the same diagnosis, the convergence is the story. Goldberg surfaces it and moves on; the cluster could carry it further.

One genuine weakness: the column accepts that Schmidt’s “Find a way to say yes” was hectoring without engaging the strongest version of the opposing view, which is that the boos themselves reflect a refusal to engage with a technology that is in fact going to reshape every industry whether the graduates approve or not. Schmidt’s earlier op-ed making the same complementarity-with-Chinese-deployment point that Ramamurti’s argument depends on is mentioned only as ironic counterpoint. There’s a legitimate question about whether Gen Z’s AI hostility is well-calibrated to the underlying technology or is a kind of magical-thinking response to changes that will happen anyway — the column gestures at “feels extractive” but doesn’t push on whether “feels extractive” tracks “is extractive” or whether the institutions-fix she identifies could plausibly be built in the timeframe the technology is moving.

↑ Back to TOC

Almonds feed a people. AI feeds a machine.

Auron MacIntyre · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the growing local-community backlash against AI data centers — their land, water, electricity, and subsidy demands — and specifically to Nick Gillespie of Reason magazine recently posting a chart comparing data-center water use to California almond agriculture, as well as Kevin O’Leary’s appearance on Tucker Carlson’s podcast praising his planned record-setting Utah data center while being unable to name a single job AI would create for ordinary Americans. MacIntyre — post-liberal right writer working in a Burnhamite/Schmittian register where corporate and state power are read as a fused regime — argues that the libertarian instinct to defend data centers exposes the limit of treating Austrian-school economics as a totalizing worldview: by reducing everything to interchangeable “economic activity,” libertarianism becomes structurally unable to ask the question that matters about any large new technology — what is it for, and whom does it serve. His central claim, stated flatly: “The first great use of AI will not be liberation. It will be surveillance and control.” The supporting case combines (a) the tech-elite’s own framing — Silicon Valley pitches superhuman intelligence and mass job-automation, which “sounds like the setup for a science-fiction dystopia,” and the strategic justification (stay ahead of China militarily) is built around developing exactly the surveillance and control tools an authoritarian regime would use; (b) the political record — the same corporate-political class that drove COVID-era vaccine mandates, censorship, biometric passes, and digital surveillance is now asking for trust on AI deployment; and (c) the local-community math — construction jobs evaporate at completion, communities absorb water/electricity/noise/landscape costs, corporations collect the subsidies. The closing move treats the local backlash as politically inarticulate but morally sound. Fundamental-flaw flag: the central claim is a prediction about how AI will primarily be used; whether one accepts it depends on weighting which AI deployments turn out to dominate over the next five years, and MacIntyre’s argument relies heavily on inference from the political character of the deployers rather than from any concrete forecast of the technology’s distribution of uses.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is MacIntyre operating at his real strength: identifying the place where a self-described libertarian or free-market position has slid into reflexive defense of state-corporate fusion and naming the contradiction without flinching. The Gillespie almonds-vs-AI chart he attacks really is doing exactly what he says it does — treating water use as morally fungible regardless of what the water is being used to make, which is a category error a Reason of 1985 vintage would not have committed. The Kevin O’Leary anecdote (asked repeatedly to name a job, falls back on Taiwan defense as the benefit) is genuinely damaging to the libertarian pro-data-center position because it concedes the central question: the projects are not being defended on benefits-to-Americans grounds, they’re being defended on geopolitical-competition grounds, which is industrial policy by another name.

The piece’s strongest move is the inference from political conduct to expected use. The argument that the same coalition that backed COVID-era information-control infrastructure is now asking for AI-deployment trust is not a paranoid leap — it’s the standard Burnhamite move (read elite behavior across crises as evidence of the regime’s actual character rather than its rhetorical self-presentation) and it generates a falsifiable expectation: the first large-scale AI deployments will look more like content moderation, biometric ID, and predictive enforcement than like the productivity miracles in the Silicon Valley pitch decks. That’s worth holding the corporate AI roadmap against over the next 12-24 months.

The piece’s weaknesses are structural. The framing that “almonds feed a people, AI feeds a machine” is rhetorically punchy but evades the actual question — almonds are a luxury cash crop, not a staple, and California’s almond-water economics are genuinely a defensible target for libertarian critique. The category MacIntyre wants (“technology that serves ordinary people” vs. “technology that controls them”) is the right one to introduce but he doesn’t do the harder work of saying which AI deployments would fall on which side — would translation, medical diagnostics, code generation, or scientific discovery count as serving rather than controlling? The piece reads as if all AI is the surveillance variety, which is both unlikely and weakens the political argument by collapsing what could be a useful policy distinction into a single rejection.

The deepest analytical claim — that economics, treated as a complete theory of human life, strips away the moral context that gives meaning to both the almond farm and the data center — is the load-bearing one, and it’s correct as stated. But MacIntyre lets it carry too much weight in too few words; the cockroach-vs-child analogy gestures at the point without doing the philosophical work to ground it. The piece is best read as a flag-planting exercise: a post-liberal-right writer publicly breaking with the libertarian-right coalition on technology policy and announcing that the post-COVID political coalition does not extend trust to Silicon Valley’s self-description. That break, more than the specific arguments offered, is the news value.

For the composition phase: pair with any Klippenstein piece on surveillance state, any Yglesias piece on industrial policy (point-and-counterpoint), or any deBoer/Chartbook piece on AI political economy. The piece does not pair with Maitra’s three pieces this week (different domains) but it does fit a broader theme of “right-coalition writers breaking with the libertarian wing on technology and corporate power” that this week’s roundup may want to surface.

↑ Back to TOC

The New A.I. Money Should Be Spent on Beauty

Ross Douthat · 2026-05-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Nan Ransohoff’s Substack essay forecasting that AI-IPO wealth could add up to $100 billion per year to American charitable giving and frame a “third wave” of philanthropy focused on meaning in the AI transition.

Douthat takes Ransohoff’s projection as given and addresses prospective AI philanthropists directly: emulate the Carnegie/Rockefeller Gilded Age by funding museums, universities, cathedrals, gardens, and even ostentatious private homes that become public spaces — not just program disbursements. He frames the recent Gates/Buffett era as a philanthropic failure on the aesthetic dimension: effective on disease and poverty at its best, faddish at its worst, but with no architectural or beautification legacy comparable to the Met, Carnegie Hall, or the Newport mansions. Two causes: a more regulated and sclerotic America that makes large building projects hard, and the failings of contemporary architecture (the Obama Presidential Center is glossed as “the Obama Star Destroyer”). The deeper cause is a Silicon Valley culture that, per Will Manidis, displays a “terror of being seen to have money and to enjoy it” — a performative philistinism (gray T-shirts, bio-engineered slop, “I’m too busy to be a snob”) that has been folded into the tech enterprise from the start. The closing prescription: take a Waymo to the Palace of Fine Arts at San Francisco’s 1915 Panama-Pacific Exposition and figure out what its funders understood about beauty and the good life.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Douthat working an aesthetic-conservative register — beauty as civic infrastructure, taste as a marker of cultivated wealth’s value to society — and he’s mostly arguing on his own preferred terrain rather than trying to prove an empirical claim, so the right test is coherence and what the framework illuminates or hides. On its own terms, the column has a real observation: the Gates/Buffett era really did produce remarkably little physical legacy compared to its philanthropic dollar volume, and the Silicon Valley performative-asceticism complex really is a culture-specific thing worth naming. The Manidis line about “terror of being seen to have money” lands because it identifies a specific cultural mechanism, not just a taste preference.

But the framework does too much work in a couple of places. First, the “Gates era failed at beauty” framing implicitly grades two very different philanthropic projects on the same axis. Gates-era giving prioritized lives saved per dollar — by its own stated criteria, the relevant counterfactual is not “what monuments did you leave?” but “how many people did not die of malaria?” Douthat acknowledges the disease work in one clause and then proceeds as if the missing beautification were a comparable failure; the column would be sharper if it engaged with the trade-off (effective altruism’s deliberate aesthetic neutrality is the central thing being criticized) rather than treating beauty and bednets as additive line items. Second, the column slides between two distinct claims about why current architecture is bad — “regulated and sclerotic America” (a YIMBY-coded structural argument) and “failings of contemporary architecture” (a taste argument about the profession itself). Both may be true but they cut differently and require different prescriptions; collapsing them lets the column dodge whether more permissive zoning would actually produce buildings Douthat would admire, or just produce more glass condos faster.

The aside on the “Obama Star Destroyer” does a lot of cultural-coding work in very few words and is worth flagging as exactly the kind of move the column itself elsewhere disdains — performative aesthetic-signaling — without actually engaging Adjaye’s design on its terms. The closing Palace of Fine Arts gesture is well-chosen but smuggles in the column’s strongest assumption: that the people who funded the 1915 Exposition agreed on what beauty was and that we can recover that consensus by sitting quietly in the building. The actually hard question — what aesthetic schools could plausibly command bipartisan elite assent in 2026 — is the question Patrick Collison is apparently funding research on, and the column treats this as a directional gesture rather than a substantive program.

The cross-week pairing with Cowen’s MR signal-boost (Cowen quoted the column at length with the gloss “Here is the full NYT piece” and nothing else) is interesting: Cowen’s bare endorsement, without his usual contrarian needle, reads as a vote of agreement on the diagnosis from a writer not normally inclined to aesthetic-traditionalist arguments. That convergence is worth noting in the synthesis if the cluster develops — when MR signal-boosts a Douthat aesthetic column without comment, the silence is the editorial.

↑ Back to TOC

Ross Douthat on what AI money should learn from the golden age of philanthropy

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Ross Douthat’s NYT column “The New A.I. Money Should Be Spent on Beauty” (also in this week’s roundup), which argues AI-IPO philanthropy should follow Gilded Age Carnegie/Rockefeller models — funding museums, cathedrals, universities, mansions, and “useless” beautiful spaces like SF’s Palace of Fine Arts — rather than the measurable-impact giving of the Gates/Buffett wave.

Cowen excerpts only two of Douthat’s paragraphs: the indictment of Gates-era philanthropy (“no great beautification campaigns, no beloved architectural landmarks”) and the historical comparison invoking the Met, Carnegie Hall, Stanford, the Newport mansions. He omits everything that gives Douthat’s piece its actual force — the “personal appeal to A.I. philanthropists” to build cathedrals and mansions, the Silicon Valley “performative philistinism” critique (Will Manidis on the “terror of being seen to have money and to enjoy it”), and the Collison/Palace-of-Fine-Arts close. The post header reads as prescriptive (“what AI money should learn”) but the selected text is purely diagnostic-historical. Straussian read: Cowen is endorsing the indictment of Gates-era giving as aesthetically barren while withholding endorsement of Douthat’s prescription (that AI billionaires should fund mansions and beautification campaigns) — a position consistent with Cowen’s own preference for measurable-impact giving and his temperamental skepticism of the conservative “build cathedrals” turn.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The Straussian read is the right read here: the selection itself is the argument. Douthat’s piece has a clear policy ask (AI money should go to beauty, monuments, cathedrals, Newport-style mansions), and Cowen extracts the historical-diagnostic two paragraphs while dropping the prescription, the philistinism critique, and the Collison example. The header is verbatim Douthat’s framing (“what AI money should learn from the golden age”); the body delivers only the indictment. Readers who follow the link get the prescription; readers who don’t get a critique of Gates-era philanthropy without the “build cathedrals” payoff. That’s a structural endorsement of the diagnosis combined with a withholding on the prescription — exactly the move that the Straussian frame is designed to catch.

The substantive question is whether Cowen’s silence on the prescription is informative. It probably is. Cowen’s effective-altruism-adjacent prior is that measurable-impact giving (Against Malaria Foundation, GiveDirectly, biomedical R&D) saves more lives per dollar than building cathedrals, and his own Talent and GOAT projects are about identifying high-leverage individuals rather than monuments. The “beauty” turn is a Catholic/conservative-aesthetic argument Douthat has made before; Cowen seems sympathetic to the critique of Silicon Valley philistinism but unwilling to sign on for the conclusion that what the world needs is more billionaire-funded basilicas. Worth noting, however, that the counterweight rule applies imperfectly: this is genuinely Straussian under the criterion (the surface text does not state the operative implicit position), even though Cowen’s full position can be reconstructed from his back catalog rather than from this post alone. The per-theme writer should engage this as a meta-move: a Cowen endorsement-by-selection of Douthat’s diagnostic frame paired with quiet non-endorsement of the prescriptive one, with the cross-week implication that the AI-philanthropy debate is going to recur and Cowen is positioning to be a soft critic of the “cathedrals” wing of it.

↑ Back to TOC

A Defense of a Liberal Arts Education in the Age of A.I.

Ross Douthat · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original interview on “Interesting Times” with Jennifer Frey, philosopher and former founding dean of the University of Tulsa Honors College — a Great Books program that briefly enrolled 26-28% of incoming Tulsa freshmen before being gutted (budget cut ~92%) when the recruiting president and provost left and a new administration removed Frey.

Douthat opens by asking Frey to defend “useless” education to a skeptical parent and then walks her through a sequence of harder challenges: cultivated Germans produced Nazism (Frey concedes the eugenics movement actually came out of elite Anglo-American universities, but distinguishes the cultivation-claim from a moral-improvement claim); Great Books pedagogy implies hierarchies of value (yes, Shakespeare really is better than Grisham — Frey defends this on language, demand for sustained attention, and capacity to sustain repeated conversation across years); Plato would have banned the very playwrights Frey defends (she concedes the tradition contains internal disagreements but identifies a “red thread” — the Greek paideia, German Bildung, Aristotelian “education for leisure” — that education’s goal is cultivating the highest human capacities, not workforce readiness). Frey gives a detailed account of building the Tulsa Honors College from a 2021 Twitter exchange with the Tulsa president into a four-semester Great Books core (Homer to Hannah Arendt) with three pillars (mission, community, character) and a STEM-major student body that loved it, then describes the regime-change ouster: new provost, no explanation, 92% budget cut, every faculty hire she had made now gone. On the political diagnosis, she names two forces as more important than the left-academic deconstruction story: the hyper-specialization of the research university (where literature scholars refuse to teach Great Books because “that’s not my expertise”) and the bipartisan utilitarian push for workforce-readiness — citing Obama’s college scorecard alongside Oklahoma’s recent executive orders ending tenure at non-flagship public universities, mandating workforce-wages-based academic review, and investigating a 90-credit-hour purely-vocational degree. She is mildly supportive of state-funded “civic centers” (the Sasse-Florida model) but argues they won’t save the humanities because they treat civics as another specialty; the only fix is re-centering undergraduate general education itself. The closing exchange on AI: Frey rejects the “humanities-as-soft-skills-for-an-AI-economy” defense as self-defeating (it instrumentalizes the thing whose value is being non-instrumental), and argues AI clarifies rather than threatens the case — because machines cannot define human ends for us, the humanities’ investigation into what human society is for becomes the existential question; when she warns students against outsourcing thinking to AI, she frames it as “outsourcing your own humanity.”

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is the longest piece in the batch and Douthat is doing real interview work — pressing on the strongest objections in sequence rather than offering a friendly platform — so the right critique is whether Frey’s framework survives his pushes and whether the pushes themselves hit the right pressure points. Frey is a more impressive interview subject than the genre usually delivers: she takes the Nazism challenge on directly (rather than deflecting), concedes the eugenics-from-elite-universities point that complicates her own case, and refuses the conservative-friendly framing that woke academia is the main villain (she names hyper-specialization and bipartisan workforce-readiness pressure as the larger problems). The Tulsa story is the empirical spine of the interview and it’s specific enough — named president, specific Twitter exchange, 26-28% take-up among incoming freshmen, $30,000 microscope counterfactual, 92% budget cut — that the reader can evaluate it as evidence rather than just as anecdote.

Where Douthat does his best interview work is the hierarchy question (Shakespeare vs. Grisham), which forces Frey to commit to a defense of qualitative judgment without retreating into “well, it’s all just preferences.” Her actual answer — Shakespeare’s language repays sustained attention; you can have multi-year conversations about Hamlet that you cannot have about Grisham — is the right answer, and the interview demonstrates rather than just asserts the point through Frey’s own evident facility with the tradition. The Aristotelian-leisure move (the Greek scholē meaning “leisure”; education’s goal as “that space that we need to set aside to cultivate the highest parts of us”) is the strongest single move in the piece and the one most worth pulling forward to the synthesis: it inverts the standard frame in which “leisure” is what you earn after work and reframes leisure as the purpose of work, which makes the workforce-readiness framing look not just impoverished but inverted.

The interview’s central blind spot is one Douthat sets up but does not push hard enough on: the disconnect between Frey’s claim that “student interest and demand simply does not matter” (she had 26-28% take-up and was still defunded) and her diagnostic frame that hyper-specialization plus utilitarian-workforce-pressure explains the decline. If demand was strong and the program was a “rounding error in the budget” (Frey’s words) and it was still eliminated, then neither demand-side nor cost-side explanations are doing the work — something else is. Frey is professionally cautious about speculating on the actual Tulsa motive (“I was given zero indication”) but the structural implication is sharper than either she or Douthat draws out: the research-university administrative class has its own preferences that are independent of student demand, cost, or even faculty resistance, and those preferences are revealed when programs that succeed on every measurable axis are still killed. That’s a more devastating diagnosis than either of the two she names, and the interview’s avoidance of it is the missing question.

The AI section is where the interview is weakest in the sense that Frey’s answer is genuinely strong but Douthat doesn’t press the second-order question. Her “AI clarifies the case for the humanities because it forces the question of what human ends are” is rhetorically powerful, but it elides whether liberal-arts institutions as currently constituted can actually deliver on that clarification. If the research-university structure is the problem (her own diagnosis), then AI doesn’t fix it; it just raises the stakes. The “stop hiring humans” billboard moment is the interview’s most evocative image but it’s used as a punchline rather than a pressure point — the question of what the humanities can plausibly offer a 22-year-old facing an entry-level job market that has been hollowed out by AI is the harder question, and Frey’s answer (don’t make the soft-skills argument, make the human-formation argument) is correct on principle but doesn’t engage with what that 22-year-old should actually do.

Read in light of Douthat’s broader project: the interview pairs naturally with his “How I Lost a Religion” newsletter from earlier in the same week (both are about the transmission failure of inherited goods under digital-age conditions) and with his “AI Money Should Be Spent on Beauty” column (both are arguments for the non-instrumental as a load-bearing category against techno-utilitarianism). Douthat is running a multi-piece thematic argument this week and Frey is the most articulate of his three voices; the interview deserves to anchor a “cultural inheritance under digital conditions” cluster in the synthesis if other writers converge on similar terrain.

↑ Back to TOC

The Emerging AI Policy Consensus

Jared Hayden · 2026-05-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Senate Judiciary Committee’s unanimous May advance of the GUARD Act, co-sponsored by Sens. Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) and Richard Blumenthal (D-Conn.), in the wake of several high-profile lawsuits over teen suicides allegedly encouraged by AI companion chatbots. Jared Hayden, a policy analyst at the Institute for Family Studies’ Family First Technology Initiative (the conservative-aligned shop also behind much of the state-level age-verification push), runs a policy-mechanism argument: between 2023 and 2025, eight states (Utah, Maine, Illinois, California, Texas, New York, plus others) passed AI chatbot laws covering disclosure of non-human status, prohibition on false professional-credential claims, and bans on promoting self-harm or sexually soliciting minors — but every state law shares a fatal weakness inherited from the 1998 federal Children’s Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA), namely the “actual knowledge” standard that requires a company to have effectively viewed a child’s birth certificate before liability attaches. The GUARD Act’s distinctive contribution is requiring AI companion providers to perform age verification, which closes the loophole and gives liability claims actual purchase. Hayden frames the bill as evidence that federalism is working — state laws as policy laboratories whose gaps federal legislation can correct.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The policy-mechanism argument is genuinely the load-bearing claim here, and it deserves to be evaluated on its merits separately from the broader politics of age verification. Hayden is correct that the actual-knowledge standard in COPPA has been the operative loophole for two-plus decades — it’s how Meta, TikTok, and YouTube have credibly maintained that they don’t know they have under-13 users despite obvious evidence to the contrary, and it’s why state-level enforcement actions against social media have struggled. Tying AI companion liability to age verification rather than actual knowledge is a coherent legislative fix to that specific problem, and the Hawley-Blumenthal coalition pattern (populist-right plus consumer-protection-left) is the actually-functioning bipartisan governance mode for tech regulation in the current Congress.

The piece’s weakness is that it doesn’t engage the obvious counter-argument, which is that “age verification” is not a neutral technical requirement — it’s a regime that pushes platforms toward either government-ID upload, third-party verification services that aggregate sensitive identity data, or AI-based age estimation that is itself error-prone and bias-laden. The civil-liberties critique of age-verification mandates (that they create de facto identification requirements for adult internet use and concentrate sensitive data in verification vendors) is well-developed and Hayden simply doesn’t address it. A policy-substance piece that ignores its strongest critic is less useful than it should be.

The “emerging consensus” frame is also doing more work than it earns. The Institute for Family Studies report Hayden cites finds that the eight state laws “broadly reflect a shared pro-human policy consensus,” but “broadly reflect” is doing heavy lifting — the laws differ significantly on scope, on whether they target companion chatbots specifically or AI more generally, and on enforcement mechanisms. The “consensus” Hayden invokes is partly a self-fulfilling artifact of an IFS-aligned policy network producing similar model legislation in multiple states and then declaring the convergence evidence of consensus.

Where the piece is strongest is its modest framing of federalism as the actual operative mechanism — states as experimental venues, federal legislation as the gap-filling layer once a clear best practice has emerged. This is closer to how American tech regulation actually works than either the “states are creating chaos” or “only federal preemption can solve this” framings dominant in Big Tech lobbying. As policy substance, it’s a useful primer; as policy advocacy, it could afford to acknowledge the trade-offs it’s recommending readers accept.

↑ Back to TOC

Pope Leo vs. the Machines of Loving Grace

William Jones · 2026-05-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Vatican International Theological Commission’s recent document “Quo Vadis, Humanitas?” (the first Magisterial mention of artificial general intelligence), Pope Francis’s late-papacy AI engagement, and the anticipated first encyclical of Pope Leo XIV — read against a wave of chatbot-suicide lawsuits and the first US government-confirmed killing of a civilian by a fully autonomous weapon. William Jones, a Futures Program Associate at the Future of Life Institute (the existential-risk-focused organization that organized the 2023 “AI pause” open letter and is a leading institutional voice for AGI moratorium), argues that the Vatican has built a rich AI-ethics framework rooted in human dignity but has not yet directly addressed catastrophic risks from superintelligence development. Jones’s load-bearing analogy is the human cloning case — at the turn of the millennium, a combination of Vatican documents, domestic laws, and non-binding multilateral statements built a durable taboo that has restrained governments, entrepreneurs, and scientists for two decades. He argues a similar mobilization could and should be achieved against superintelligence, with Pope Leo following Leo XIII’s “Rerum Novarum” precedent of providing durable moral guidance on a transformative socio-political question. The piece names Fr. Paolo Benanti (Francis’s Franciscan AI advisor) as already having concluded “we must not proceed in building superintelligence,” and frames AGI-developer rhetoric about “machines of loving grace” (Dario Amodei’s essay title) as a transhumanist usurpation rather than a salvific promise.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The piece is essentially a strategic memo to the Vatican from the existential-risk movement, and it’s worth evaluating both as theology-adjacent argument and as policy advocacy. The cloning analogy is the central move and it does real work — it’s a documented historical case of a moral-religious coalition successfully creating durable restraint around a powerful technology, and the institutional pathway Jones describes (Vatican documents plus domestic laws plus non-binding multilateral statements) is approximately what actually happened in the cloning case. As an empirical model of how religious authority can shape a technology’s trajectory, it’s not a fantasy.

Where the analogy starts to weaken is in the disanalogies Jones doesn’t address. Human reproductive cloning has limited commercial demand, no defense application, no obvious geopolitical race dynamic, and a vivid intuitive “yuck factor” that crosses cultural and ideological lines. Superintelligence has trillions of dollars of capital invested, explicit national-security framing in both US and Chinese strategic documents, and no comparable cross-cultural moral intuition (the AGI-as-salvation narrative is genuinely held by many secular technologists, not merely cynically deployed). The cloning taboo succeeded against a weak adversary; the AGI-restraint coalition faces an adversary with the structural advantages of Big Tech plus state national-security backing. The piece’s optimism that papal authority can replicate the cloning outcome doesn’t reckon with how different the opponent is.

The Future of Life Institute affiliation is important context Jones doesn’t disclose to the reader, and the piece is functionally an effort to enlist Catholic moral authority into the existential-risk coalition’s policy program (a six-month pause on frontier model training was FLI’s 2023 ask). Compact readers should know this is an advocacy piece written by someone with institutional skin in the game, not a neutral theological analysis — though the existence of such an advocacy effort, and the fact that FLI has identified the new pope as a leverage point worth investing in, is itself useful information about how the AGI-restraint coalition is trying to build power outside Silicon Valley.

The strongest move in the piece is the framing of AI safety as continuous with Catholic social teaching rather than as a novel concern — citing Rerum Novarum’s middle-way function between communism and laissez-faire as the precedent for Leo XIV providing similar mediation between AGI accelerationism and Luddite refusal. This is theologically substantive and the kind of argument that could actually move Vatican thinking, because it doesn’t ask the Church to adopt a foreign framework but to extend its own tradition. Whether the encyclical actually goes there is the empirical test the next year will provide.

The “Machines of Loving Grace” title is doing rhetorical work the reader might miss — it’s a deliberate inversion of Dario Amodei’s October 2024 Anthropic essay arguing that powerful AI could deliver decades of compressed human flourishing. Jones is signaling that the encyclical fight is partly a fight over who gets to define what loving grace means, the Anthropic CEO or the Bishop of Rome. That’s a more interesting battle than the standard “AI ethics” framing captures.

↑ Back to TOC

Data Centers Are Splintering the American Right

Jael Holzman · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a surge of Republican-led data-center moratorium proposals in May 2026 (Rep. Nancy Mace’s call for a statewide South Carolina ban, Texas agriculture commissioner Sid Miller’s same proposal for Texas, a North Dakota Public Service Commission candidate backing the idea), combined with a Gallup poll, a Stockton University poll (NJ), a Houston University poll, and Heatmap Pro polling all showing Republican opposition to data centers rising more than 300% over six months in some GOP regions.

Jael Holzman frames the data-center backlash as a “crisis of faith” inside American conservatism over land use, energy abundance, and corporate regulation. The piece documents three movements at once: (1) elected Republicans in red states pushing moratoria; (2) right-wing online figures (Matt Walsh, Tucker Carlson, Marjorie Taylor Greene) attacking data-center practices, with a split among the conservative commentariat (Kevin O’Leary defending, others mocking critics as anti-free-market); and (3) polling showing data-center opposition reaching offshore-wind-levels of negativity in some Republican geographies. GOP pollster Chris Wilson (EyesOver) tells Holzman the conflict is between the GOP’s “traditional pro-business identity and a new populist identity asking who owns this, who is consuming it, who is it going to benefit.” Will Reinhart of the American Enterprise Institute tells Holzman a national data-center moratorium platform from a major 2028 GOP presidential candidate is “very real” possibility, citing Ron DeSantis’s recent criticism as a bellwether. The administration’s counter-frame — embraced by Interior Secretary Doug Burgum — is that the backlash is “foreign-sourced dark money,” echoing the long-running (and never-evidenced) Republican claim that wealthy Europeans secretly fund US environmental opposition; Holzman notes this strategy never worked against rural solar opposition either. Texas oil-and-gas lobbyist David Blackmon draws an analogy to the early-2010s fracking-communications failure: the industry was close to losing “its license to operate” in major shale-patch counties because of ham-handed local outreach. The piece’s coalition-architecture read: data-center opponents are emerging from the same NIMBYism muscle memory the right built fighting solar and wind, and the Trump administration’s “Build Baby Build” energy posture is now structurally exposed on its own coalitional right flank. The pro-data-center playbook being pitched (David Blackmon, Hunt at the Rainey Center, Sen. Dave McCormick’s “covenant” framing — covenant defined as: bring more energy than they use, recycle water in closed-loop systems, commit to local tax base and local workers) is community-benefits-style mitigation rather than ideological defense.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Holzman is doing competent policy reporting on a genuine coalitional development inside the GOP, and the strongest move of the piece is treating the data-center backlash as structurally analogous to the solar/wind backlash rather than as a one-off issue. The polling triangulation is solid (Gallup + Stockton + Houston + Heatmap Pro all moving in the same direction), the elected-Republican examples are concrete (Mace, Miller, the North Dakota PSC candidate), and the comparison to offshore-wind-levels of opposition is the kind of calibration claim that gives a reader an actual benchmark. As an explainer of why a Republican administration committed to AI infrastructure is suddenly facing primary headwinds from its own base over the energy-infrastructure required to power that AI infrastructure, the piece is useful.

The structural argument that needs more pressure-testing is the inference from “GOP voters oppose nearby data centers” to “GOP electoral coalition is splintering on this issue.” NIMBY polling on “would you support a data center in the town where you live” is one of the most consistently inflated metrics in survey research — the same voters poll against landfills, prisons, drug treatment facilities, charter schools, low-income housing, and refineries when the question is “within a mile of your home.” That a Stockton poll finds 50% of Republican voters in New Jersey would support a town-level ban is not yet evidence that the issue scales to statewide or national political consequence. Holzman gestures at this when she quotes Wilson saying “if they’re pro-AI, that idea is still politically safe, and it’s safer than being anti-growth or anti-technology” — which is the right calibration. But the piece’s headline-and-lede framing (“Splintering the American Right,” 2028 presidential moratorium platform) is doing more work than the polling supports. NIMBY local backlash that converts into statewide moratorium legislation is one threshold; statewide moratorium that becomes a viable presidential primary issue is a much higher threshold; the former is happening, the latter is speculation.

The other thread the piece names but doesn’t quite resolve is the energy-cost question. The Wilson quote at the end — that GOP voters will support “AI competitiveness, beating China, domestic infrastructure, and lower energy bills” but will oppose “taxpayer subsidies for data centers, straining on energy and water supplies, secrecy around data center deals, or use of eminent domain” — implies the backlash is potentially redirectable into a community-benefits-mitigation regime rather than blocking. Sen. McCormick’s “covenant” framing (data centers bring more energy than they use, closed-loop water, local tax base, local labor) is the operational version of this redirection. The interesting question Holzman doesn’t quite ask: at what marginal energy-cost-to-residential-ratepayers do the covenant terms stop being economically feasible for the data-center operators? If hyperscalers can in fact deliver net-positive local energy, recycled water, and significant local employment, the McCormick covenant is a viable compromise; if they can’t (and the levelized capex of new data centers strongly suggests they can’t deliver net-positive local energy at scale without massive new generation buildout), then the covenant framing is a political fig leaf that will collapse on contact with the actual numbers. The piece would have been stronger if it had pulled in either the AEI Reinhart side or the Holzman/Heatmap Pro data side to estimate which scenario is more likely.

The Burgum “foreign-sourced dark money” line is the piece’s most usefully reported moment. It’s a tell: when an administration official’s first move is to attribute its own base’s opposition to foreign manipulation rather than engage the substantive complaint, the administration has already lost the argument inside its coalition. Holzman flags this correctly (“blaming outsiders for local conflicts over energy and tech infrastructure development is unlikely to work”), and the historical parallel to the failed Republican argument that wealthy Europeans secretly fund US environmental opposition is the right reference. The McCormick “frankly the Chinese are behind a lot of it” quote in the same vein is doubly revealing — the senator pitching the covenant compromise still feels compelled to lay off the legitimacy of the opposition onto a foreign-adversary frame. Both moves suggest the pro-data-center faction inside the GOP doesn’t yet have a coalitional response that takes the local-cost objection seriously on its own terms.

↑ Back to TOC

Blame French Theory? In this Economy?

Geoff Shullenberger · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a viral X post by French tech entrepreneur Brivael Le Pogam (founder of AI-clone-video startup Argil, Y Combinator-backed) “apologizing” on behalf of France for having loosed French Theory on the world — blaming Derrida, Foucault and the postmodernists for le woke-isme and announcing that the tech industry would now rebuild Truth, Beauty, and Goodness. The post drew 55 million views and amplification from Musk and Bill Ackman, with the speculation that it was itself LLM-generated.

Shullenberger (a literary/cultural critic writing in a Marxian register at Compact’s Substack arm; sister to Paul Gottfried’s piece in this week’s Compact on the cultural-Marxism conspiracy) uses Le Pogam as a foil to make a structural argument about the incoherence of the anti-French-Theory genre when it’s recited by AI-industry boosters. The contradictions he flags are mechanical: Le Pogam denounces “the author is dead” while running a company that markets “AI fictions” letting users “become the main character” via generated clones; he laments that “every identity is constructed and thus negotiable” while selling identity-construction software; he condemns “every signifier slips away” in the same breath as building products that operationalize that slippage. The Granta-published AI-generated text winning the Commonwealth Prize is Shullenberger’s spot-evidence that mass LLM use has operationalized Barthes’s 1967 “text is a tissue of citations” thesis at industrial scale.

Shullenberger then mobilizes Jean-François Lyotard’s The Postmodern Condition (1979) to advance the historical-philosophical thesis the piece is really driving at. Lyotard defined postmodernism as “incredulity towards metanarratives” but located its cause not in French philosophers but in computerization — knowledge had become “exteriorized” from the human knower, distributed across impersonal technological systems, no longer dependent on Bildung (the formation of the individual mind through knowledge). On this loosely-Marxian base-superstructure reading, postmodernist ideas emerged because the material processes of knowledge production had already become postmodern; right-wing culture-war polemic mistook a symptom for a cause and (via A.J.A. Woods’s account of the cultural-Marxism conspiracy theory Shullenberger has cited before) scapegoated cultural radicals for what were really transformations of the economic base. The accelerated phase — generative AI — is now closing the loop: the technologies are themselves cultural ones, producing instant superstructural effects from the base, which means Le Pogam and Musk are themselves the “real cultural radicals” presiding over a destabilization of truth, self, and beauty “at a far vaster scale and a more rapid pace than any experimental artist or post-structuralist intellectual could ever have dreamed of.” Closing image: Le Pogam standing over the murdered corpses of Beauty, Truth, and the Good, smoking gun in hand, blaming French Theory.

The piece contains a fundamental challenge to the cultural-Marxism / blame-postmodernism frame that recurs in right-wing cultural commentary (including Gottfried in this week’s roundup): the very actors recycling that frame are, by Shullenberger’s accounting, the most aggressive contemporary deconstructors of the things they claim to defend.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Shullenberger is operating in his strongest register here — a Marxian cultural critic using a viral-tweet artifact as the entry point to a more durable historical-philosophical argument. The Lyotard-via-base-and-superstructure move is the load-bearing analytical contribution and it is calibrated: rather than the standard rebuttal to anti-postmodernist polemic (“you’re misreading Derrida”) which gets nowhere with the audience that traffics in that polemic, Shullenberger accepts the diagnostic vocabulary (a slip toward incredulity, destabilization of truth, dispersal of the author) and asks what is causing it. The answer — that the tech sector is itself the agent of postmodernity-at-scale, while postmodernist philosophy was only its symptom — is a genuinely uncomfortable argument for the AI-boosting right because it cannot be defused by changing one’s mind about a French philosopher.

The piece is also doing something Cowen-Straussianly (relevant to MEMORY.md’s feedback_cowen_straussian.md note, though the move here is more explicit): the literal target is Le Pogam, but the operative target is the broader Musk-Anthropic-OpenAI commentariat that recycles the blame-postmodernism frame while accelerating exactly the cultural conditions it claims to oppose. The Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei “country of geniuses in a data center” line is cited specifically as the contemporary endpoint of Lyotard’s “exteriorization of knowledge” thesis, which is a clean indictment of an industry-wide rhetorical posture.

Where the piece could be sharper is on the question of whether Shullenberger’s base-superstructure mapping is actually right. The strongest version of the anti-postmodernist case is not “Derrida caused this” but “Derrida and Foucault provided the legitimating vocabulary by which institutional actors (universities, courts, media) ratified the dissolution of stable meanings, and this happened in parallel with, not downstream of, technological change.” On that read, the philosophical and material trajectories are co-causal rather than one-way; the Marxian base-superstructure model loads the dice toward “material conditions cause ideas,” which is itself a 19th-century framework that has aged unevenly. Shullenberger flags that “the accelerated phase of postmodernity we have entered is also destabilizing the base-superstructure distinction,” but he doesn’t really follow through on that observation — if base and superstructure are now mutually constitutive (because the technologies are themselves cultural), then the Marxian frame he leans on can’t carry the full analytical weight, and Le Pogam’s “French Theory caused this” claim might be partially right alongside Shullenberger’s “computerization caused this” claim. A piece that genuinely thought through that co-causation would have been more interesting than one that asserts the materialist reading and uses it as the rhetorical hammer.

That said, the closing image (Le Pogam smoking gun over the corpses) is sharper than most of what Compact publishes on this beat, and the piece pairs productively with Gottfried’s same-week argument that the right has its own version of cultural-Marxism conspiracy thinking. Read together, they triangulate the position that the anti-postmodernist frame has become structurally incoherent on multiple fronts — Gottfried from a paleoconservative angle (the right has become what it claims to oppose), Shullenberger from a Marxian angle (the AI industry has become the cultural radical it pretends to fight). The pairing is one of the cleaner orientation-crossing convergences this week.

↑ Back to TOC

LLMs and the Library Card Fallacy

Freddie deBoer · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Jay Caspian Kang’s New Yorker “Fault Lines” series asking whether AI will make college obsolete (e.g., his hypothetical fifteen-year-old who should “start a Twitch stream” instead of going to Harvard), and a longer lineage of college-is-dead predictions from David Brooks, Tom Friedman, Clay Shirky, and Hollis Robbins, plus Sal Khan’s AI-tutoring optimism quoted in a recent NYT excerpt of Josh Tyrangiel’s OpenAI book. deBoer coins “the Library Card Fallacy” — the mistake of thinking education is about access to information, when public libraries (and Google, and MOOCs, and now LLMs) have always supplied that access without producing autodidacts. He marshals the MOOC track record as evidence: Katy Jordan’s meta-analysis found median completion rates of 12.6%; even among students who explicitly intended to finish, only 22% did (per a 2014 EDUCAUSE study). The constraint, he argues, is demand-side — most teenagers won’t sit through the Thirty Years’ War lecture without external scaffolding (deadlines, grades, peer embarrassment, an instructor watching) — and LLMs make this worse, since they’re being used by students to evade learning rather than pursue it. He concedes some right-sizing of US colleges is overdue but predicts most will persist, because what they sell isn’t lectures or textbooks but the social compulsion that gets unwilling adolescents to do hard things. Notes a tonal shift in his own writing: he’s “quietly given up” on the mass and now holds out hope only for the motivated individual minority.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is deBoer doubling down on a long-standing pattern (anti-credentialism’s mirror image — defending the institutional function of college on grounds that have nothing to do with what credentials signal) and doing it well. The MOOC parallel is the strongest move in the piece: by 2026 the MOOC bust is well-documented, and the structural similarity to the LLM-utopian discourse is real — same “access democratizes” premise, same elision of the demand side, same reliance on the unrepresentative autodidact as the modal student. The “you can lead a horse to water” frame is unoriginal but the evidentiary spine (Jordan meta-analysis, the EDUCAUSE intent-controlled study, the NBER random-PC RCT showing kids used the computers for Farmville rather than Wikipedia) is genuinely load-bearing rather than decorative.

What’s weak: deBoer’s central reframe — that college’s product is “scaffolding” (deadlines, grades, peer embarrassment) rather than information — is correct but undertheorized as a defense of the current system. If the scaffolding is the product, then the case for a four-year residential bachelor’s at $80K/year is much harder to defend than the case for some compulsory adolescent structure; trade schools, vocational tracks, and the German apprenticeship model also supply scaffolding at a fraction of the cost and time. deBoer slides past this by appealing to the inherent appeal of being eighteen, drunk, and intellectually stimulated — which is a real argument but a different one, and one that defends the residential-college-as-life-stage rather than college-as-pedagogy. He’s running the two together. A reader sympathetic to Kang could grant the entire Library Card Fallacy and still say: fine, so we need scaffolding cheaper than a $400 billion sector that disproportionately benefits the already-credentialed.

Second weakness: the “I’ve quietly given up” admission lands harder than the surrounding piece earns. It’s stylish — the deBoer voice — but it’s a sweeping anthropological claim about “the great mass of humanity” smuggled in via a piece that has rigorously confined itself to claims about American educational outcomes. The MOOC and Google evidence doesn’t license the move to global apathy and decline. It’s the move from “most 18-year-olds won’t grind through the Federalist Papers without scaffolding” (true and well-supported) to “humanity is sliding into apathy” (a much larger and undefended claim). The two-paragraph structural separation between the empirical argument and the misanthropic closer is doing rhetorical work the reader is supposed to absorb without noticing.

Third: the piece doesn’t engage the actual disruptive use case for LLMs that worries Kang and others — not autodidacticism but rather the supply-side hollowing of the white-collar jobs that college is supposed to feed students into. If entry-level analyst, paralegal, and junior-coding jobs disappear, the college-as-scaffolding-for-getting-to-the-cubicle argument loses its endpoint regardless of whether 18-year-olds want to learn. deBoer’s frame is about whether students will choose to skip college; the Kang frame is about whether the credential will still buy anything on the other side. These are different questions and deBoer answers only the first.

Strong piece overall — the MOOC analogy is the kind of pattern-recognition the project values, and the coinage “Library Card Fallacy” is sticky enough to be cited. But the strong claims should be flagged: the misanthropic generalization at the close isn’t supported by the evidence presented, and the defense of college conflates two different things (educational scaffolding for adolescents vs. the specific four-year residential US bachelor’s).

↑ Back to TOC

New Paradigms Won’t Save You

Scott Alexander · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the common AI-skeptic argument that LLMs cannot be AGI, that AGI will require a “new paradigm” comparable to the invention of the transformer or deep learning, and that therefore AGI is far enough in the future not to worry about. Alexander grants the premise for argument’s sake and asks what the base-rate forecast looks like for a paradigm shift of LLM-comparable magnitude. The historical chain — neural networks (1950s), multi-layer perceptron (1967), modern deep learning (2010), transformer/LLM (2017), RLHF/chatbots (2022), chain-of-thought (2024) — gives a per-step interval that, via Lindy’s Law (the heuristic that things last roughly as long as they’ve already lasted), suggests nine more years to the next LLM-class paradigm and sixteen years to the next deep-learning-class paradigm. But Lindy’s Law has a heavy tail, so the 25th percentile estimate is 3 years for an LLM-class advance and 5 years for a deep-learning-class advance — about the same timeline the LLM-only crowd uses for AGI itself. Alexander adds two acceleration arguments (researcher-count growth and the prospect of AIs themselves contributing to research) that push his actual estimate faster than Lindy. The subtler underlying point, citing nostalgebraist: in past transitions, new paradigms emerged precisely when an old paradigm exhausted its scaling regime, so frontier labs will pull paradigm-shifts off the shelf when LLM scaling hits a wall, and scaling will resume from where it left off. The piece’s fundamental move is to convert “new paradigm required” from an excuse for relegation into an excuse-of-excuses by applying base-rate reasoning to the paradigm-shift interval itself.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Alexander in his characteristic mode — base-rate-reasoning deployed to deflate a categorical argument by recasting it as a quantitative one. The move is clean: skeptics treat “new paradigm” as a category-block (AGI requires X, X is undefined and rare, therefore AGI is far away), and Alexander reframes it as a frequency problem (how often do X-class events occur?) that can be estimated from the historical chain of priors. Lindy’s Law is doing a lot of work here, and the post is honest that it has a heavy tail. But there’s a deeper question Alexander doesn’t fully address: Lindy’s Law assumes draws from a stationary distribution, and the very point of the new-paradigm skeptics is that the current research environment is unlike past environments (much more capital, much more researcher attention, much more compute, but also potentially diminishing-returns regimes that didn’t exist in earlier eras).

The two acceleration arguments — more researchers, AI-augmented research — push the estimate faster, but they also cut the other way: if you take seriously that the research field has changed character (e.g., that we’re seeing a wartime-economy concentration of effort), then the historical Lindy reference class is less appropriate as a prior. Alexander’s footnote about the science-slowdown post is hand-wavy here; the empirical question of whether time-between-advances is actually constant or accelerating is genuinely contested in the meta-science literature, and the answer matters for how much weight to put on Lindy.

The nostalgebraist citation about paradigms emerging when old paradigms exhaust their scaling — i.e., paradigm shifts are demand-driven — is the most interesting move in the post and deserves more development than the parenthetical it gets. If it’s right, then the relevant question is not “how often do paradigm shifts occur on a chronological basis” but “how often does an old paradigm exhaust its scaling regime.” This is a substantively different forecast and would give a different distribution. Alexander touches the question but doesn’t engage with whether LLM scaling is or isn’t approaching exhaustion (and competing accounts of that — Epoch AI, Pat Hsu, Gary Marcus — would disagree about the answer).

The piece pairs naturally with the cluster of AI-timeline pieces from prior weeks (the LLM-Council piece on bottlenecks, the various scaling-law debates), and adds a useful argumentative tool: the move-them-on-their-own-terms reframe, where instead of contesting the skeptic’s premise you grant it and show that even granting it, the timeline doesn’t push out far. As rhetorical strategy, this is clarifying. As forecasting, it’s worth holding the Lindy assumption with appropriate skepticism — useful upper bound, as Alexander himself says, not a point estimate.

↑ Back to TOC

All non-drone militaries are obsolete

Noahpinion · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Ukraine war’s drone-saturation phase (Ukraine going from a few thousand FPV drones per day to ~60,000, ~96% of Russian casualties now drone-caused, ~1000 Ukrainian drones hitting Moscow), the NATO “Hedgehog 2025” exercise in Estonia where a 10-person Ukrainian adversary team mock-destroyed 17 armored vehicles and ran the equivalent of two battalions off the field, and Smith’s interview with Yaroslav Azhnyuk of Ukrainian autonomy-software firm The Fourth Law on the Latent Space podcast.
