Smith argues the cost asymmetry is decisive: a $400-500 FPV drone, an iPhone-of-warfare in Azhnyuk’s coinage, can be produced in numbers that overwhelm any system three orders of magnitude more expensive (a Rheinmetall tank ~$5M, an artillery shell ~$4,000, a 10kW counter-drone laser ~$3M for 600-6,000 drones’ worth of intercepts). The shotgun-defense excuse and the laser-defense excuse both fail on cost-exchange math. The structural argument: drones run on lithium-ion batteries and rare-earth electric motors, technologies the US and allies have ceded to China, which manufactures DJI, dominates EV production, and could produce 4 billion FPV drones to Ukraine’s 4 million per year — and could mount millions of them on shipping containers showing up at any coastline. The piece’s fundamental move is to elevate Smith’s longstanding “Electric Tech Stack” thesis into a hard military claim: not just that the West is behind in green-tech industrial policy but that this gap is the supreme determinant of modern warfighting capacity. The closer quotes a NATO commander from the Hedgehog 2025 exercise: “We are f—.”

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Noah Smith at full deployment of his pro-industry, materialist-economic, China-as-industrial-base register. The argument has real teeth: the cost-asymmetry data is sourced from Azhnyuk, an operator who founded one of Ukraine’s leading drone-autonomy companies and has commercial incentives to know the numbers; the NATO Hedgehog 2025 exercise, sourced from Wall Street Journal reporting earlier this year, is a genuinely revealing piece of evidence that the institutional militaries are not adapting fast enough; and the lithium-ion / rare-earth / electric-motor supply chain chokepoint is a structural argument Smith has been building for two years across his “Electric Tech Stack” thread.

The empirical reasoning is more contestable than Smith presents it. The 96% of Russian casualties from drones figure comes from Ukrainian operational sources during a phase where Russia has been advancing in regions of dense drone saturation (Pokrovsk axis, etc.); generalizing it to “all warfare is drone warfare” extrapolates beyond what the data supports. The cost-exchange calculations are correct for the current attritional Ukraine theater but assume the defending side cannot adapt at the same rate (mass shotgun/net/EW deployment, hard-kill systems, large autonomous drone-on-drone interceptors — work in active development) and that the attacking side’s drone-production cost stays constant as countermeasures force complexity upward. Smith’s “every non-drone military is obsolete” headline claim is a maximalist version of a more defensible “drones have become a dominant supplement and current Western forces are not configured to fight at scale in a saturated drone environment.” The maximalist version drops the qualifier and is more shareable but less defensible.

The China-industrial-base argument is the strongest leg. Smith correctly notes that the same industrial capacity that produced DJI’s drone dominance, BYD’s EV dominance, and the global lithium-ion and rare-earth refining stack would, in a wartime mobilization, give China a drone-production advantage measured in orders of magnitude. This is the same “China-as-industrial-civilization” thread that Tooze, Aurelien, Sumantra Maitra, and others have been pursuing from different angles. Smith’s contribution is to make the military-application case explicit and specific, which most of those writers either don’t or do at higher altitude. The piece pairs naturally in synthesis with the Tooze and Maitra Chinamaxxing/industrial-base pieces if those are in this week’s slate.

The weakness Smith doesn’t address: the maximalist drone-paradigm thesis is precisely the kind of single-domain extrapolation that tends to be overstated in the months immediately following a high-salience demonstration war. The “tank-is-obsolete” thesis was made after the Yom Kippur War (Sagger missiles), after the 1991 Gulf War (precision-guided munitions), and after the 2003 Iraq War (network-centric warfare); in each case the maximal version was wrong but the moderate version (qualitative change in the operating environment) was right. The base-rate-aware reading of this piece is to take the structural China-industrial-base argument seriously, take the cost-asymmetry argument seriously as a binding constraint going forward, and discount the “all non-drone militaries are obsolete” headline by the appropriate factor for novelty-overclaim.

↑ Back to TOC

AI and the New Machinery of African Repression

Khalid Bencherif · 2026-05-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a March 2026 study by the Institute of Development Studies (IDS) and the African Digital Rights Network (ADRN) finding that 11 named African governments (Algeria, Egypt, Kenya, Mauritius, Mozambique, Nigeria, Rwanda, Senegal, Uganda, Zambia, Zimbabwe) have spent more than $2 billion on AI-enabled smart-city surveillance systems, with Nigeria alone accounting for $470 million.

Khalid Bencherif argues the relevant question for Africa is not whether AI flips a democracy into a dictatorship but whether AI strengthens habits of rule already present in many African states — executive impunity, politicized security services, weak courts, opaque procurement, treatment of dissent as a security problem. The infrastructure is concrete: CCTV with automatic number-plate recognition, facial recognition, biometric layers, analytics platforms, and command centers — much supplied or financed by Chinese firms and banks (Huawei is named for confirmed Uganda safe-city deployments and Zambian opposition spying; CloudWalk is named for Zimbabwe’s 2018 facial-recognition partnership that potentially feeds African faces into training data exported elsewhere), with spyware layers from NSO Group (Israel), Cellebrite (Israel), and Intellexa-linked firms. The Kenya case is the central illustration: the 2024–2025 Gen Z-led protests, organized through phones and hashtags, were met with state surveillance and online intimidation that Amnesty International links to 128 deaths, 3,000+ arrests, and 83+ enforced disappearances. The Atlantic Council/Paradigm Initiative 2025 report finds 49 African countries have at least one biometric system and 35 use biometrics in elections, while only 38 percent of sampled citizens know their governments have these systems. Bencherif’s structural argument: the pre-emptive effect is what changes the protest calculation — a planned protest identified Saturday through metadata, social-media monitoring, and face-matching may not need police because the organizers know they have been seen, employers can be called, passports delayed, tax files reopened, and the protest simply doesn’t form. He invokes Ethiopian-born AI scholar Abeba Birhane’s framing of “algorithmic colonialism” — African bodies as training data for technologies designed, owned, and governed elsewhere — and Kenyan writer Nanjala Nyabola’s argument that digital politics cannot be separated from analogue politics. The demand: not that citizens “out-compute” the state but that states justify and limit what they buy with law, warrants, independent oversight, procurement transparency, deletion rules, and remedies.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Bencherif is doing materialist-leftist reporting on a real and well-documented phenomenon: the AI-surveillance procurement build-out across African states, the vendor ecosystem that supplies it (Chinese hardware financing, Israeli spyware tooling), and the institutional environment that lets it be operationalized for political-opposition tracking rather than crime control. The piece is well-sourced — the IDS/ADRN $2B figure, the 11-country list, the Nigerian $470M and Mauritian $456M and Kenyan $219M numbers all come from a March 2026 study with named authors, and the Wall Street Journal 2019 Huawei reporting on Uganda and Zambia is independently confirmed. The Amnesty International casualty figures from the 2024–2025 Kenyan Gen Z protests (128 deaths, 3,000+ arrests, 83+ enforced disappearances) are linked and citable. As a primer on the surveillance-procurement layer of contemporary African state-building, this is competent and informative reporting.

The structural argument is also strong: the pre-emptive-detection mechanism (Saturday metadata flag → Sunday protest doesn’t form → no police mobilization needed) is the right mechanism to identify, and it captures something the cruder “AI surveillance enables crackdowns” framing misses. The cost-of-authoritarianism argument is the genuine analytical move. The historical comparison Bencherif builds — colonial counting and pass systems → postcolonial national IDs and voter rolls → AI-enabled retrieval that joins face, plate, transaction, and contact network in seconds — places the current procurement build-out in a continuous lineage rather than treating it as a discrete novelty. This is the right framing.

Where the piece could be pressure-tested is on the selection-bias question for the vendor-pipeline framing. Bencherif names Chinese firms (Huawei, CloudWalk) and Israeli firms (NSO Group, Cellebrite, Intellexa) as the dominant vendors, which is empirically accurate, but doesn’t engage the counterfactual: in the absence of Chinese and Israeli vendors, would African states procure equivalent capability from Western firms (Palantir, NEC, Thales, Axon) at higher prices and with slightly different oversight regimes? The 38-percent-citizen-awareness figure suggests the procurement-opacity problem is upstream of vendor selection — that is, even if all the cameras were French or American instead of Chinese, the underlying weak-procurement, weak-courts, weak-data-protection problem would persist. Naming the Chinese-and-Israeli vendor dominance is descriptively correct, but the implication that the vendor identity is the structural cause (rather than a downstream consequence of African state procurement choices and Western-firm pricing) is doing more argumentative work than the evidence supports. The Birhane “algorithmic colonialism” framing names a real asymmetry — that African data trains models that serve other markets — but as a causal account of why African states surveil their populations, it’s a partial answer at best.

The piece’s strongest move is the procurement-vs-oversight asymmetry: “the control room is funded before the oversight body, and cameras are installed before the law defines search, storage or redress.” This is a specific, testable institutional claim — that the budget process privileges acquisition over governance — and it identifies a concrete reform lever (procurement-transparency law, data-protection authority funding, human-rights impact assessments as procurement requirements) that activists in the named countries could actually press for. The closing distinction — that the democratic demand is not citizens out-computing the state but states justifying what they buy — is the operationally useful frame, and it lands cleanly.

The Pegasus-and-NSO line carries some of the piece’s analytical risk. The Israeli spyware vendor ecosystem has been documented extensively (Citizen Lab, Forbidden Stories), but linking it to African political-case usage requires care — the cited Citizen Lab reports on Cellebrite use against Kenyan activist Boniface Mwangi and on Pegasus targeting in Africa are credible, but the larger inference that “Israeli spyware is structurally driving African repression” leans on a smaller evidence base than the Chinese-camera-financing claim does. Bencherif handles this responsibly by citing specific Citizen Lab and Amnesty cases rather than making sweeping vendor-aggregate claims, but the piece would be stronger if it explicitly named the evidence-density gradient (more confirmed Chinese-hardware deployments than confirmed Israeli-spyware operations) rather than treating both vendor pipelines as equally well-documented.

↑ Back to TOC

Three Signs of the Chamber Apocalypse

Oren Cass · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a single week in which the GOP-controlled House moved on three bills the party’s donor-class enforcers (the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, Americans for Tax Reform’s Grover Norquist, the Wall Street Journal editorial board) had long held off — the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act restricting institutional investors who own 350+ single-family homes (passed 396-13, with the White House calling the provision Trump’s “signature priority”), the Faster Labor Contracts Act (FLCA) reaching 218 signatures on a discharge petition forcing a floor vote (seven House Republicans broke with leadership to sign), and the Railway Safety Act (RSA, introduced post-East Palestine derailment) being adopted 54-11 by the House Transportation Committee over the chair’s objection.

Kishi (writing in the lead “Daniel” voice; Daniel Kishi is American Compass’s deputy director) reads the three votes as evidence of a single political fact: the Chamber/Norquist/WSJ “intellectual machinery” that disciplined Republicans against pro-worker, anti-Wall-Street positions remains operational but no longer reliably produces votes. Cass (American Compass founder and Commonplace’s major-domo) then pivots in a sprawling second half — the regular Understanding America weekly format — through the macroeconomic picture under newly-installed Fed chair Kevin Warsh (gas at $4.50/gal, real wages down, mortgage rates at nine-month highs), the AI-stock-concentration question (top-five market cap is now Google/Apple/Microsoft/Nvidia/Amazon, with Berkshire sitting on $400B in cash because it can’t find investments), a debunking of the viral “entry-level jobs down 35%” stat (it’s postings down 35%, and an LSE study finds work-from-home, not AI, drives the decline), a critique of “Universal Basic Capital” as repackaged UBI that privatizes redistribution and locks in Silicon Valley’s permanent winners, and a long China-industrial-policy section endorsing Greg Ip’s (WSJ) framing that the pervasiveness of Chinese industrial policy makes Trump’s tariff/rules-of-origin response the only plausible answer. Closing notes on rare-earth defense magnets (the Pentagon must hold the line on the January 2027 ban over contractors begging for extensions) and Stephen Moore’s pro-chip-export-to-China Fox News op-ed (dismissed as “generic market fundamentalism with the word ‘chips’ inserted in the ‘[put topic here]’ boxes”).

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The lead Kishi piece is the strongest argument the project has seen this week for the post-liberal-right’s institutional thesis: that the GOP donor-enforcement apparatus is structurally weakened, not just episodically defied. The three-bills-in-one-week framing is genuinely load-bearing — a single defiant vote could be noise, but the combination of (a) a near-unanimous housing vote with the White House naming the institutional-investor restriction Trump’s “signature priority,” (b) a successful discharge petition (a procedural mechanism that’s “very rare” precisely because majority members must override their own Speaker), and (c) a committee vote against the chair, the rail industry, and a coalition of free-market groups suggests the enforcement infrastructure is being routed around rather than placated. Kishi’s anchor — naming the seven House Republicans by district — gives the claim verifiability rather than vibes.

The harder question Kishi doesn’t engage: is this a coalition realignment or just Trump’s personal coattails? The White House SAP endorsement on housing reads as Trump using the GOP base against the party’s donor class, not as the donor class losing its grip on the broader conference. If Trump went, would Hawley-Booker labor coalitions or Vance-Brown rail coalitions survive a generic Republican Senate? Kishi treats the votes as evidence of a working majority that has internalized the new posture; the alternative reading is that a small coalition is exploiting Trump-era leadership weakness to force votes that would not have happened under a stronger Speaker. Both are consistent with the evidence. The piece’s claim would be sharper if it engaged the counterfactual.

The Cass-authored back half is the regular Understanding America weekly grab-bag — five distinct arguments, each modest individually, that together do real work. The UBC/UBD critique is the most pointed: Cass correctly identifies that “give workers equity in AI companies” privatizes the redistribution decision in a way that locks in current concentration, and his observation that the corporate tax system already exists to do this work cuts through a lot of Silicon Valley self-congratulation. (Note Cass doesn’t take the next step — that the corporate tax rate has been cut substantially since 2017, and that the policy implication is restoration rather than the UBC alternative. The piece leaves the reader to draw that.) The entry-level-jobs-down-35% debunking is real public service — that stat has been repeated everywhere without scrutiny, and the WEF→Revelio Labs→postings-not-jobs chain of attribution failure is exactly the kind of mistake the rest of the commentariat is making. The LSE WFH-vs-AI study is the kind of finding that should be widely circulated and probably won’t be, because it cuts against everyone’s preferred narrative.

The Stephen Moore takedown is the weakest section — it’s right on the merits but reads as Cass enjoying himself rather than engaging the strongest version of the case for chip exports. (The strongest version is not “more profit for U.S. firms” but “denying China the political pretext for accelerated indigenization” — Moore doesn’t make that argument, but Cass doesn’t acknowledge it exists either.) The China-industrial-policy section’s heavy lean on Greg Ip’s WSJ column to do the analytical work is a tell: Cass has been arguing the same thesis for years and now treats it as obvious enough to outsource to an establishment columnist. That’s a meaningful shift in the discourse, though not an argument the piece itself makes.

↑ Back to TOC

High Home Values, Low Housing Prices, Choose One

Oren Cass · 2026-05-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the political deadlock in which housing affordability requires lower prices but homeowner-voters (nearly four homeowner votes per renter vote in 2024) treat home-value appreciation as wealth — and specifically to Marc Short’s Washington Examiner op-ed opposing the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act’s institutional-investor restrictions on the grounds that Wall Street capital is needed for new construction. Cass (American Compass founder) opens with the “you are born short housing” frame: just as a stock short-seller is exposed to price increases without owning shares, every non-homeowner is exposed to housing-market appreciation whether they’re trying to buy or rent.

The piece does two things. First, it skewers Short’s argument as “downright acrobatic” market fundamentalism — Short initially claimed institutional investors don’t drive up prices, then pivoted under pressure to claiming the bill is bad because it would lower prices for home sellers, exposing that the underlying commitment is to market outcomes regardless of distributional effects. Second, it offers a reframing: when a person buys a home, they close their short position rather than create a long one, becoming roughly market-neutral. The growing-family homeowner contemplating a larger house should care as much about a low price on the next home as a high price on the current one — they are “seller-buyers” who are closer to indifferent than they realize. The genuine high-home-value beneficiaries are late-in-life downsizers and the never-buyers’ inheritance lottery; the political-economy implication is that most homeowner self-interest is misaligned with most homeowners’ rhetorical position, and a politics that named this could redirect the median voter from windfall-protection to young-family-pathway concerns.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The “born short housing” frame is the rare conceptual contribution that actually does load-bearing analytical work. It cuts through a confusion that has hobbled YIMBY discourse for a decade — the argument that “housing should be cheaper” loses to the argument that “my house should be worth more” in any direct rhetorical contest, but Cass’s framing dissolves the conflict by showing that for the median homeowner, the two preferences should be approximately equivalent. The growing-family-seller-buyer case is the cleanest application: a family selling a 3BR to buy a 4BR in the same metro is mechanically better off when both prices fall (the differential is smaller in absolute terms, plus property taxes and insurance scale down). The Boomer-downsizer counterexample Cass concedes — they want the maximum absolute differential between current and next home — is real but demographically narrowing.

The Short takedown is the piece’s most enjoyable section but also where Cass overreaches slightly. Short’s actual two-step (we need investor capital for construction → restricting investors lowers sale prices) is consistent under a single coherent position: that institutional capital both supplies construction finance and supports current home values, and that the bill harms both groups (renters by reducing supply, sellers by reducing demand) simultaneously. That’s a bad argument on the merits — the empirical question of whether institutional investors net add or subtract from housing supply is contested, and Cass would be right that the construction-finance story is weaker than the price-pressure story — but it’s not the rhetorical contradiction Cass paints it as. Cass’s frame (“acrobatic display of market fundamentalism”) wins by making Short look incoherent rather than by engaging the strongest version of the case. Worth noting because it’s the same move the piece accuses Short of: rhetorical framing substituting for analytic engagement.

The deeper question Cass doesn’t engage: if the seller-buyer reframing is correct, why doesn’t the median homeowner already think this way? The answer is probably that consumption-housing and asset-housing have been collapsed in the American mortgage-backed-securities financial architecture in ways that make the two functions mechanically inseparable — home equity is the primary retirement asset for most middle-class Americans, the primary collateral for HELOC-financed consumption, and the primary inheritance vehicle for intergenerational wealth transfer. Cass’s reframing is philosophically right but the institutional infrastructure that would let households act on the reframing (a robust rental market with stability for families, alternative retirement vehicles, decoupled mortgage finance) does not exist. A piece that named this would point to the policy work the post-liberal-right’s housing agenda actually has to do — and that’s harder than the Marc Short takedown. Pairs naturally this week with Yglesias’s “We can fix the Trump-Warren build” piece on the construction-finance side and with Kishi’s lead in the Chamber Apocalypse roundup on the institutional-investor restriction itself.

↑ Back to TOC

Work Will Always Matter

Chris Griswold · 2026-05-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the AI-driven post-work scenario broadcast by Anthropic’s Dario Amodei (“white-collar bloodbath” with 10-20% unemployment), Nvidia’s Jensen Huang (humanoid robots “a few years away”), and Elon Musk (“there will come a point where no job is needed”) — and the implicit question of whether such a future would be a liberation or a catastrophe. Griswold (American Compass policy director) argues it would be the latter, and grounds that conclusion in a philosophical case that work is intrinsically rather than instrumentally good.

The piece sets up two competing theories. The first — held jointly by Marxism and neoclassical economics — treats work as unpleasant necessity, captured in the Genesis 3 itstsabon (“painful toil”) framing and the labor-leisure tradeoff model U=f(C,L); from this view, AI-enabled escape from work is unambiguous progress, the path to either Marx’s “realm of freedom” or Aaron Bastani’s “Fully Automated Luxury Communism.” The second — drawn from Genesis 2 (where the Hebrew term for work, avad, evokes agency and worship rather than toil), Plato’s Republic (work as the rare class of goods that are both instrumentally and intrinsically valuable, like knowing or seeing), Tolkien’s “sub-creator” framework, and Laborem Exercens (work makes man “more a human being”) — treats work as constitutive of human thriving. Griswold marshals Matthew Crawford (Shop Class as Soulcraft), Fukuyama on tacit knowledge from struggling with material reality, and Musa al-Gharbi’s review of Orwell’s Road to Wigan Pier to argue that workless populations are reliably miserable, and that Marc Andreesen’s “OxyContin and video games to keep those people quiet” represents the actual telos of post-work society. Closes with a call to “embed Option Two in our societal source code” so that what shape the AI transition takes is governed by the right beliefs about human nature, not the wrong ones.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Griswold’s strongest move is collapsing the apparent Marx/neoclassical opposition into a single position — they share the disutility-of-labor premise, they only disagree about who’s stealing the surplus. That’s a genuinely useful reframing: it explains why both Yang-style UBI advocacy and Bastani-style luxury-communism visions converge on “automate it all and distribute the proceeds,” and why the post-liberal-right’s pro-work register is doing different philosophical work than either left-wing populism or right-wing labor-market intervention typically does. The Plato-via-Hanson distinction (goods instrumentally valuable, goods intrinsically valuable, and the rare class of goods that are both) is the load-bearing philosophical anchor, and Griswold deploys it cleanly rather than gesturing at it.

The weaker section is the empirical claim that workless populations are reliably miserable. The al-Gharbi quote does the work, but the underlying evidence is thinner than the rhetorical confidence suggests — “workers who are unable to work but have their material needs provided for” is a population shaped by selection (disability, long-term unemployment, etc.) that’s not generalizable to a post-work society where the prior probability of work has dropped for everyone. Retirees, trust-fund children, and the leisured rich are also “workless populations with material needs met,” and the evidence on their well-being is mixed at best — many retire and find meaning in volunteer work, hobbies, family caregiving, or creative pursuits the piece would presumably classify as “work” without paid employment status. That suggests the philosophical case needs to define work more carefully than “paid employment in the labor market.” Without that definitional work, the argument risks proving too much: if a Tolkien-style sub-creator impulse is what matters, then a post-employment society in which everyone is a sub-creator (writers, gardeners, carpenters, parents) is precisely the eudaimonic outcome, not its opposite.

The Andreesen “OxyContin and video games” quote is doing rhetorical work the piece doesn’t fully cash. It’s clearly the most viscerally damning frame in the essay, but it’s also a third-hand reconstruction of a private dinner (“said to have made”), and Andreesen’s actual public position is closer to “AI will make abundance possible if we let it” than to deliberate sedation of the deplorable masses. Griswold knows enough to keep the attribution conditional but uses it as the essay’s emotional center anyway. That’s a rhetorical choice rather than a flaw, but the piece would be stronger if the case rested on the philosophical argument alone — the Plato/Tolkien/JPII triangulation is sufficient without the personal-villain anchor.

Pairs naturally this week with the broader corpus on AI labor effects (LSE work-from-home finding in Cass’s Understanding America; Anthropic-Amodei-Klein discourse on the white-collar bloodbath claim), and with the Crawford-Fukuyama-tacit-knowledge thread that runs through several of this year’s roster pieces. The position is recognizable in the post-liberal-right’s coordinate region but not reducible to it; the philosophical lineage Griswold draws on (Plato, Tolkien, Wojtyła) is one the secular left has largely abandoned, which is itself part of the political-economy story he’s telling.

↑ Back to TOC

We can fix the Trump/Warren build-to-rent ban

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The pending Senate-passed ROAD to Housing Act and the ongoing House-Senate fight over whether to keep the build-to-rent restriction language inserted at the urging of Trump and Senator Elizabeth Warren.

Yglesias walks through the legislative arc: the ROAD to Housing Act (Senator Warren’s housing-supply package co-led with Senator Tim Scott, with assists from the bipartisan Build America Caucus in the House) had emerged from the Senate Banking Committee as a rare “Secret Congress” win — outcomes-oriented federal land-use provisions, chassis reform for manufactured housing, the works. That win is being derailed by language Trump championed in his State of the Union (and Warren-aligned populists welcomed) that severely restricts institutional ownership of single-family homes, including a requirement that new build-to-rent projects be sold off within seven years — a window short enough to kill the sector. Yglesias’s empirical hinge: the private-equity-buying-up-single-family-homes panic is largely a fake problem (the post-2008 wave has long since waned; small landlords dominate single-family rentals; owner-occupiers vastly outnumber renters), while build-to-rent has emerged in Sun Belt growth markets as a real substitute for the cheap starter homes that can’t be financed since the 2008 mortgage tightening and can’t be built as apartments because of zoning. The constructive alternatives are easier mortgages for weaker-credit borrowers or legalizing apartment construction — not banning new rental supply. He sides with House ranking member Maxine Waters and Senator Brian Schatz against Warren, Trump, JD Vance, and the populist-left commentariat (Matt Stoller’s American Economic Liberties Project, More Perfect Union, the American Prospect), and notes the structural perversity that Trump is sabotaging a bill whose passage would help his own affordability politics — broadly parallel to his sabotage of permitting reform via the wind-and-solar crusade.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Yglesias in his most characteristic mode — supply-side wonk doing legislative play-by-play with a clear preferred outcome and a willingness to name names across both parties’ commentariats. The piece does several things well. The “fake problem / real problem” framing is sharp and the data point (small landlords and owner-occupiers vastly outnumber institutional single-family owners) is the load-bearing one — most coverage of the private-equity-buying-homes panic doesn’t engage with the share question at all, and Yglesias is right that the political intensity around this issue has scaled inversely to its empirical significance. The observation that build-to-rent has become a real substitute for the missing starter-home-for-sale market — because both mortgage access and zoning bind on the alternatives — is the kind of mechanism-first analysis his project is built around, and it’s the framing that lets him explain why the seven-year-sell-off requirement is functionally a build-to-rent ban rather than the moderate compromise it can sound like.

The piece’s weakest move is the symmetric blame allocation. Yglesias treats Trump and Warren as roughly co-responsible for the derailment (“Trump, with some critical assists from Warren”), but his own narrative concedes that the Warren-aligned populists were running a parallel track for months before Trump’s State of the Union turned the private-equity language into a must-include. The proximate cause is Trump’s intervention; Warren’s role is opportunistic alignment after the bipartisan pressure shifted. Whether the framing matters depends on what one is grading — Warren’s craft as a legislator (she did get to yes on the broader package) versus her political judgment (she let the populist provision in when she could have held the line). Yglesias doesn’t quite distinguish these.

The bigger analytical question the piece sidesteps: why has the institutional-investor narrative grown more politically intense as the underlying phenomenon has waned? Yglesias notes the puzzle (“for reasons that remain somewhat unclear to me”) and then moves on. But this is the genuinely interesting structural question — the gap between a quantitatively-small phenomenon and its enormous discursive footprint is what made Warren’s package vulnerable in the first place, and it’s a pattern the populist-left commentariat has reproduced across multiple issues (consolidation, surge pricing, shrinkflation). A piece that engaged that question — rather than treating the bogeyman as a fixed feature of the political landscape to be routed around — would be doing harder work. The structural irony is that Yglesias is essentially defending the legislative craft of the policy class against the demands of its own activist base, which is a recurring Slow Boring move; the asymmetric symmetry here (blame both sides but trust the technocrats) is more visible than usual.

One strong subsidiary observation worth flagging for the synthesis: the “good bill passing is good for the incumbent” point — and the implication that this creates a structural impediment to bipartisan reform under any administration — is a generalizable mechanism Yglesias has been developing across pieces, and it ties to his read on the permitting-reform impasse. The fact that Trump is the one currently sabotaging a bill that would help him is the genuine puzzle, and Yglesias is right to name it as perverse rather than rationalize it as 4D chess.

↑ Back to TOC

The dumb policy making groceries more expensive

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary, prompted by recent marketing texts urging permanent year-round legalization of E15 (15-percent-ethanol gasoline) and the ongoing Biden- and Trump-era frustration over the lack of fast-acting tools to bring down grocery prices.

Yglesias argues that the US Renewable Fuel Standard — the Bush-era policy (Energy Policy Act of 2005, expanded by the Energy Independence and Security Act of 2007) requiring oil refiners to blend ethanol into gasoline — is a “dumb policy” that raises both gas and grocery prices for no real environmental benefit, and that ending it is a fast-acting affordability lever both administrations have ignored. The original rationale was twofold (energy independence in an era of fears about peak oil, and emissions reduction) and both have collapsed: the US found vastly more oil, and the cleanest carbon accounting (the Argonne National Laboratory team of Lee/Kwon/Wu/Wang) still pegs ethanol at $160-$190 per ton of CO2 abated, while less generous accounting (Lark/Hendricks/Smith/Gibbs at Wisconsin/Kansas State/UC Davis) finds carbon intensity at least 24 percent worse than gasoline because of land-use change. Yglesias cites a 2013 paper by Michael Roberts and Wolfram Schlenker showing that diverting corn to ethanol raises the global price of all four major grain staples (corn, wheat, rice, soybeans) by 20-30 percent because of substitution. The political case for ethanol — Iowa as quintessential swing state — has lapsed because Iowa is no longer competitive, and Yglesias argues a coalition of southern Republicans and coastal Democrats could roll the corn-state bloc. He notes his epistemic shortcut on the technical E15 ozone question (“Claude tells me”) and declines to engage it because the underlying mandate is the policy he wants to end.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Yglesias playing to his strongest hand — taking a well-studied policy that almost every serious economist has called wasteful, locating the political logic that keeps it alive (Iowa’s caucus role), and arguing the political logic has expired. The policy economics is solid: the Roberts-Schlenker 20-30 percent global-grain-price effect is a finding most affordability discourse simply never engages with, and the cost-per-ton-CO2-abated figure he cites is genuinely a damning number even on the friendliest carbon accounting (mainstream carbon-pricing arguments target costs an order of magnitude lower). The piece’s central contribution is the explicit affordability framing — ethanol policy as a “fast-acting tool” both administrations could deploy — which routes around the usual environmental-policy framing where ethanol gets defended on emissions grounds it cannot actually justify.

The weakest move is the political prediction. “Iowa is no longer a swing state, so the corn lobby can be rolled by southern Republicans and coastal Democrats” treats Iowa’s caucus status as the only thing holding the Renewable Fuel Standard in place, but the durable constituency is the broader Farm Belt (Illinois, Nebraska, Indiana, Minnesota, Missouri, Kansas — most of which Yglesias acknowledges in passing), the entire farm-equipment and grain-processing industry (ADM, Cargill, Bunge), and the ethanol refining sector itself, much of it now owned by oil majors who’ve made peace with the mandate via the credit-trading system. The Iowa-as-king-of-corn framing makes the politics sound thinner than they are. A Yglesias piece serious about how to roll this coalition would name the specific veto points (Senate farm-state Republicans, House Ag Committee), the budgetary and trade-policy compensations the farm lobby would demand, and the executive-action route (the EPA’s discretion over Renewable Volume Obligations) versus the legislative route. Without that, the piece reads as “this is dumb and Iowa is no longer special, ergo it should end,” which is closer to a tweet-thread than a policy argument — and Yglesias normally does better.

The “Claude tells me” tic deserves a separate flag, and not only for this piece. Outsourcing a sub-question to an LLM in the middle of a paid policy column is an epistemic move that ought to be explained or defended, not glossed over with a parenthetical. The specific E15 ozone question is not central to the piece’s argument, but the move itself — citing an LLM as a research assistant whose work the writer has not audited — sets a pattern where readers can’t tell which sentences carry Yglesias’s own confidence and which are AI-laundered. It’s the kind of thing that, if it became a Slow Boring habit, would erode the column’s signal quality.

The strongest secondary observation: the piece is a clear example of the abundance-liberalism playbook applied to a non-housing case, and the framing (“Stop forcing oil companies to turn cropland into gasoline!”) would generalize to other supply-side affordability moves — tariff cuts, occupational licensing, the Jones Act — that both administrations have similarly declined to use. There’s a synthesis-level observation here about the gap between “we have no fast-acting tools” rhetoric and the menu of fast-acting tools sitting unused for institutional-political reasons.

↑ Back to TOC

Liberal Economists Score an Own Goal Against Bezos

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-05-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Jeff Bezos’s tweet that the U.S. tax system is the world’s most progressive (top 1% pay 40%, bottom 50% pay 3%) and his proposal to zero out income tax on the bottom half — and to Gabriel Zucman’s and Justin Wolfers’s replies, which countered that the system isn’t progressive once payroll and consumption taxes are included.

Alex Tabarrok at Marginal Revolution charges the Zucman/Wolfers response with two errors. First, it’s a rhetorical own goal: Bezos was proposing more progressivity, and the redistributionists rushed to dispute the baseline he was building from. Second and more substantively, treating FICA (the federal payroll tax funding Social Security and Medicare) as a “regressive tax” abandons eighty years of liberal doctrine, traceable to FDR’s 1941 line to Luther Gulick that payroll contributions exist precisely so “no damn politician can ever scrap my social security program” — the “earned right” framing that Arthur Altmeyer, Wilbur Cohen, Robert Ball, and Nancy Altman’s Social Security Works! built into the program’s structural defense against means-testing and privatization. Tabarrok offers the Buchanan/Summers test (a mandated payment is a tax only to the extent it doesn’t deliver an offsetting marginal benefit) and concludes that Medicare’s Hospital Insurance portion is unambiguously a progressive tax, while Social Security’s OASDI is a contribution at the bottom (where the 90/32/15 PIA bend points deliver above-market returns) and a tax only at the top — so the net OASDI rate is progressive too. He closes by warning that if the left wants to win the “regressive payroll” argument it will hand AEI and Cato the rhetorical opening to privatize.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The structural argument here is strong: Tabarrok correctly notices that Zucman and Wolfers are simultaneously holding two incompatible doctrinal positions — FICA-as-premium when defending the program, FICA-as-regressive-tax when attacking the rich — and that this incoherence has a real cost, because the “earned right” framing is what kept Social Security off the chopping block through six decades of conservative attack. The Larry Summers paper Some Simple Economics of Mandated Benefits is the right citation: the wedge between marginal product and the worker’s reservation wage isn’t the statutory rate, it’s the gap between mandated payment and the worker’s marginal benefit. Catherine’s point that OASDI offers higher rates of return at the bottom than at the top is empirically correct given the bend-point structure.

But the rhetorical-tradeoff framing is more contestable than Tabarrok lets on. The “earned right” framing was a political construction designed for a specific era when means-testing was the live threat. In an era when the live threat to Social Security is fiscal insolvency under a Trump administration that’s already breaking taboos on benefit cuts, it’s not obvious the old framing is still load-bearing — and the modern left’s reframing as redistribution may be a recognition that “you earned it” no longer has the political weight it had in 1941. Tabarrok treats the framing as inert protective capital that the left is foolishly liquidating; an alternative read is that it was already spent by Trump’s first-term tax cuts and by the Biden-era reframing of Social Security as anti-poverty insurance. Tabarrok doesn’t engage that argument; he assumes the framing still has its 1980s political force.

The piece is also notably one-sided about who’s holding two positions. Tabarrok’s own preferred system — “a forced savings program with a closer connection between marginal payments and benefits” — is structurally a privatization plan dressed in actuarial language. The Catherine-Summers test he invokes (mandated payment minus marginal benefit equals the tax wedge) is correct as economics but is also exactly the framework AEI and Cato use to argue that all of OASDI is a tax for high earners and therefore a transfer that should be privatized away. He’s not wrong about the analytics; he’s just not naming that the analytics deliver his preferred policy too. That’s a fair move in a polemic, but the synthesis composer should notice that “the left is being incoherent” and “and conveniently the principled framework points to my preferred reform” are doing more work together than separately.

↑ Back to TOC

Monopoly Round-Up: The Rage of the Billionaires Is Coming

Matt Stoller · 2026-05-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a cluster of recent signals — students booing commencement speakers (including former Google CEO Eric Schmidt) for promoting AI, NERC’s data-center grid-reliability alert, billionaire Ken Griffin’s organized harassment campaign against NYC mayor Zohran Mamdani after Mamdani filmed a vacation-home-tax video outside Griffin’s $238M apartment, Steve Roth comparing “tax the rich” to racial slurs and “from the river to the sea,” CEO comments from Anthropic’s Dario Amodei (half of white-collar jobs gone), OpenAI’s Sam Altman (AI “will probably most likely lead to the end of the world”), and Palantir’s Alex Karp (“some people can get their heads cut off”), and Kevin O’Leary accusing Utah data-center critics of working for China.

Stoller (BIG; the project’s anti-monopoly mechanism-first writer) uses these signals to build a structural prediction: the next six months will see the superrich get more defensive and embittered as the public rejects their values. The mechanism he identifies is the “Lina Khan Derangement Syndrome” pattern from Khan’s FTC tenure — the Wall Street Journal editorial page attacked Khan every eleven days for her entire run, in a coordinated framing of her as a secret Marxist with disregard for the rule of law — which Stoller predicts will now generalize to a broader set of populist Democratic targets. The political-economy thesis: since 2006, the U.S. has had “change elections” in every cycle (House majorities have flipped four times — soon five — over 20 years, unprecedented since 1874-1894), Democratic voters now distrust their leadership for the first time, and a next-generation Bernie Sanders-style populist tendency is emerging (Mamdani in NYC; Graham Platner defeating Janet Mills for the Maine Senate nomination; Abdul Al-Sayed in Michigan) that combines pre-Trump populism with Trump-style norm-ignoring.

Stoller’s structural-power-of-elites observation is that the superrich have every visible advantage — Supreme Court, money, press ownership, captured Republicans, meek Democrats, corporate control — but a single critical weakness: “they don’t just want to be rich, they demand to be loved.” Griffin’s organized backlash to mild criticism by Mamdani is the visible expression of that weakness. The international parallel: Macron, Merz, and Starmer are all loathed, suggesting Western politics is durably out-of-kilter with voters in a way one or two elections won’t correct. Stoller predicts the coming period will look like an oligarchic backlash that will seem scary, then weird, then dumb — “such is how an oligarchy ends, not with a bang, but with a bunch of whiny billionaires crying about taxes.”

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Stoller in his Monopoly Round-Up format — looser and more polemical than the structural-mechanism pieces (compare “Wires to Nowhere” earlier this week), but doing real synthesis work that’s hard to find elsewhere on the roster. The single most analytically valuable move is the cataloging of the “Lina Khan Derangement Syndrome” pattern as a generalizable mechanism — not a one-off attack on a single regulator but a template the superrich-coalition will use against any populist Democrat who threatens them. The WSJ-attack-every-eleven-days data point is the kind of structural-pattern observation that turns episodic criticism (oh, the WSJ hates that mayor) into legible mechanism (this is how oligarchic pushback works, and we have a calibration baseline for it).

The change-election thesis is also load-bearing and pairs productively with Klein/Drutman’s gerrymandering piece this same week. Klein and Drutman emphasize structural disenfranchisement by gerrymandering as the cause of voter dissatisfaction; Stoller emphasizes substantive policy failures by both parties as the cause. Both can be true and reinforcing — gerrymandering makes the major-party duopoly more insulated from voter preference, which produces the change-election cycle as voters use the one cycle-to-cycle lever available to them. Reading the two pieces together generates a more durable thesis than either alone.

Where the piece is weakest is on the prediction’s calibration. Stoller has been calling the imminent populist-Democratic moment for several years now, and the on-the-ground evidence is mixed — Mamdani won the NYC primary, but the broader Democratic establishment is hardly in retreat, and the Platner-defeating-Mills nomination is a single Senate primary in a small state. The Al-Sayed claim is even shakier — Stoller says he is running a “surprisingly strong race” but offers no polling data. A more careful version of the piece would have distinguished signals (booing of Schmidt) from confirmed wins (Mamdani primary, Platner nomination) from speculative trend extrapolation (the next 2-4 years), and would have been honest that the dominant Democratic primary pattern in 2026 has been establishment candidates beating populist insurgents in most contested races, not the reverse. The Klein-Drutman analysis cited above is actually a partial corrective to Stoller’s reading — the structural barriers to populist Democratic success may be higher than Stoller’s polemic acknowledges.

The Ken Griffin episode is the piece’s strongest mid-level evidence: a billionaire mobilizing the Governor, the NYC press, and Donald Trump in response to a politician filming a video outside his apartment is empirically extreme and supports Stoller’s “they demand to be loved” structural diagnosis. The Steve Roth “tax the rich is like racial slurs” quote is genuinely shocking and is the kind of public statement that should make a careful analyst update toward Stoller’s thesis. The pairing of these elite-overreaction signals with the structural-power-of-elites cluster elsewhere in this week’s roundup (Klippenstein on IRS-as-tax-haven; NR’s McCarthy on the Trump slush fund; Stoller’s own “Wires to Nowhere” on utility capture) is the analytically valuable convergence to surface. Both Stoller pieces, read together, are doing more analytical work than the average Monopoly Round-Up.

↑ Back to TOC

Thomas Massie’s Dead-End Libertarianism

Daniel McCarthy · 2026-05-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Thomas Massie’s loss in his Kentucky GOP primary to challenger Ed Gallrein, and the broader libertarian-right post-mortem about whether Trump’s party can tolerate principled dissenters. Daniel McCarthy, Heritage Foundation conservative-thought fellow and former editor of The American Conservative, argues that the Ron Paul-Massie strain of liberty Republicanism was historically necessary during the post-Bush wilderness years (2008-2015) when the GOP needed an oppositionist conscience, but that Trump’s takeover changed the terms — the party now has a coherent worldview (anti-globalism, immigration restriction, post-Reagan economics) and a working majority, so “strictly oppositionist ideology” is obsolete coalition behavior. McCarthy contends that Massie made the strategic error of becoming the poster boy for budget-balancing and Epstein-file disclosure (issues the base does not prioritize) while antagonizing the pro-Israel coalition on Iran, forfeiting any chance to shape MAGA’s foreign-policy posture from inside. The piece’s load-bearing move is the analogy between the Tea Party era’s “leaderless protest movement” and today’s libertarian holdouts: McCarthy frames Trump’s discipline-imposition as fulfilling what 2008-era critics said the GOP needed, and casts Massie’s defeat as self-inflicted by refusing to compromise in the near term for long-term restraint gains.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The coalitional logic McCarthy lays out is the strongest part of the piece, and it deserves to be engaged on its own terms rather than dismissed as Heritage-Foundation discipline-enforcement. He is correct that Ron Paul’s appeal in 2008 was structural — the GOP had no answer to its own Bush-era failures, and uncompromising critique was the only way to force confrontation. He is also correct that this function expires once a party has actually reorganized around a new vision; pure oppositionism inside a coherent coalition becomes a free-rider problem. The empirical claim that Massie made himself synonymous with the wrong issues (budget, Epstein) rather than the issues the base cares about (immigration, anti-elite) is testable and largely supported by primary-election post-mortems.

Where the argument weakens is in its definition of what “being part of the coalition” would have looked like for liberty Republicans on the Iran question specifically. McCarthy elides the fact that Massie’s anti-Iran-war position was not a vanity stance but the entire substantive content of “non-interventionism” — there is no version of liberty Republicanism that supports a Middle East war and remains liberty Republicanism. The piece implicitly demands that Massie sacrifice his core foreign-policy commitment to retain coalition influence on… foreign policy. That is closer to dissolution than compromise, and McCarthy’s gesture toward “some libertarian-leaning Republicans in the administration are doing that” without naming them or what restraint they have actually delivered is the weak spot.

The deeper unstated assumption is that MAGA’s foreign policy is shapeable from inside through patient coalition work rather than locked in by Trump’s personal commitments and the donor-class realignment around Israel. If the second is closer to true, then Massie’s “permanent protest” posture may be the rational response to a coalition that genuinely has no room for his core position — not a failure of coalition imagination. McCarthy’s frame requires the first to be true, but he never argues for it, only assumes it.

The closing observation that anti-Zionism is finding a home in Democratic primaries while MAGA’s libertarian-leaning wing has no exit option is shrewd realignment-watching and points to the genuine bind facing Massie’s coalition: they have nowhere to go, and so the threat of defection that gives oppositionist factions leverage in normal politics doesn’t apply. This is McCarthy’s actual diagnosis dressed as advice.

↑ Back to TOC

How Trump and the neocons beat Thomas Massie

Ross Barkan · 2026-05-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Rep. Thomas Massie’s (R-KY) decisive loss in a Kentucky Republican primary to Ed Gallrein, a former Navy SEAL personally recruited by Trump. Gallrein’s campaign was backed by more than $34 million in outside spending, much of it from pro-Israel groups — making it the most expensive House primary in US history.

Ross Barkan, a left-leaning New York political columnist, reads the result as confirming two intertwined truths about today’s GOP: Trump’s personalist dominance is total (“the rest of us get the privilege of living in it,” in Rep. Randy Fine’s phrase), and that dominance is now fused with pro-Israel hawkishness as the operative ideological filter. Massie’s offenses were stacked: he held up a 2020 Covid relief bill, voted against the “Big, Beautiful Bill” tax package, voted against a House resolution condemning the Boycott Divestment and Sanctions (BDS) movement, opposed Iron Dome funding, criticized Netanyahu, and — Barkan argues was the breaking point for Trump personally — co-led the bipartisan push (with Rep. Ro Khanna, D-CA) to force release of the Epstein Files. Barkan’s structural read: AIPAC and Republican Jewish groups poured money in because the Republican Party is now their “last stand,” with Democrats under 50 turning against the Jewish state and the Mamdani New York mayoral win demonstrating that pro-Palestine candidates can now beat the Israel lobby in major Democratic primaries. The hawks bought Kentucky, but they have not converted young conservatives: Tucker Carlson, Candace Owens, and Megyn Kelly are ascendant on the anti-Israel/restrainer right, Ben Shapiro’s Daily Wire is laying off staff, and recent NYT/Siena polling shows half of Republicans want the US to pay less attention to overseas problems. JD Vance, formerly an isolationist, has been forced to swallow an Iran war he might not have backed as president. Barkan’s closer: Massie’s worldview may be the future of the GOP, but not yet — and not while Trump lives.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Barkan is a left-coalition writer (formerly New York Magazine, recent Mamdani profile linked in the suggested-reading rail) covering a right-coalition story, which is the analytical interest of the piece. The Massie primary is being covered this week by multiple roster writers — Andrew McCarthy and the National Review bench from inside the Republican coalition — so what matters is what Barkan adds that intra-GOP writers won’t. The answer is the AIPAC-as-last-stand frame and the explicit linkage between the New York Mamdani mayoral race and the Kentucky Massie primary as a single cross-coalition story about Israel-lobby exposure. Mamdani won the Democratic nomination as an openly pro-BDS candidate in the most Jewish city in America; AIPAC then spent $34 million to defeat a Republican incumbent in Kentucky over the same fault line. Barkan is the only commentator this week framing these as one story, and the framing earns its place.

The piece is well-calibrated on Trump-personalism. The Randy Fine quote (“This is Donald Trump’s Republican Party. The rest of us get the privilege of living in it”) is doing genuine evidentiary work — Fine, a freshman from Florida, combines unfiltered Trump-fealty with anti-Muslim rhetoric (“the choice between dogs and Muslims is not a difficult one”), and his being elevated to validator status by Trump is exactly the kind of operational signal that tells you what the median Trump-coalition Republican is allowed to say in 2026. Gallrein, the Massie challenger, was personally recruited by Trump as a “warm body to beat Massie” (Trump’s words) and declined nearly all debates and interviews. This is not a coalition fight between two plausible candidates; it is a loyalty test administered with a $34 million budget.

Where the analysis is least calibrated is on the AIPAC framing. Barkan accepts the implicit claim that the $34 million was decisive — and at the level of the primary it almost certainly was — but he doesn’t sit with the question of whether anti-AIPAC sentiment is actually shifting elite Republican opinion in the way the youth-poll data suggests. The Republican-under-50 polling on Israel is real (the INSS data and NYT/Siena are credible sources), but the question of whether those preferences translate into Republican primary outcomes when AIPAC can write a $34 million check is exactly the question the Massie defeat answers in the negative. Barkan acknowledges this (“It’s not that regular voters care all that much — it’s the money”), but then pivots to youth-trend optimism without quite engaging the implication: a movement with strong youth preferences but no countervailing money apparatus can lose every primary it contests against an organized donor class indefinitely. The Bernie-Sanders-AIPAC arc in the Democratic Party took roughly 15 years from Sanders’s 2016 refusal to attend the AIPAC conference to a credible primary alternative, and the Democrats had a denser progressive-donor infrastructure to absorb the transition than today’s restrainer-right has. Barkan’s “2036, 2046” timeline is closer to the right scale than the implied near-term shift.

The piece’s strongest analytical move is naming the Epstein Files vote as Trump’s actual breaking point with Massie, distinct from the BDS or Iron Dome votes. This reframes the primary from “Israel-lobby fight” to “Trump-personalism fight with Israel-lobby money attached,” which fits the evidence better and explains why Trump personally recruited the challenger rather than letting AIPAC find one. The version of the piece that led with the Epstein-vote analysis and used the AIPAC money as the operational mechanism, rather than treating both as parallel causal stories, would have been sharper.

↑ Back to TOC

Iran War vs. Epstein Files

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-05-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Kentucky GOP primary in which Trump-backed retired Navy captain Ed Gallrein is challenging Rep. Thomas Massie — one of the few congressional opponents of the Iran War and a co-sponsor (with Rep. Ro Khanna) of the push to release the Epstein files — and to Gallrein’s campaign strategy of refusing debates and citing his “classified” SEAL Team 6 background as the entire warrant for his fitness for office. Klippenstein documents Gallrein’s verbal-tic pattern of refusing to discuss specifics (“I can’t speak to any classified operations I may or may not have ever been involved in”), notes terminology errors that suggest exaggeration of operator status (Gallrein’s actual roles were apparently educational/training positions like head of instruction at the Joint Special Operations University, not direct-action), and surfaces a 2013 episode in which Gallrein was fired from the Y-12 nuclear facility and lost a whistleblower complaint on a procedural error. Klippenstein names the underlying frame as “Shut the Fuck Up and Salute” — a worldview in which civilians are sheep, special operators are sheepdogs, and elected oversight is the problem rather than the solution. He pairs Gallrein with the Democratic “national security moms” (Spanberger, Sherrill, Slotkin) to argue this is bipartisan: the national security state as its own source of political legitimacy, floating above democratic accountability. The piece’s fundamental move is to expose the credential-as-argument structure — “the president has seen the classified file, therefore you don’t need to evaluate me” — as an inversion of the principal-agent relationship.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Klippenstein in his strongest register: a single-candidate exposé that opens onto a structural argument about the national-security state’s metastasis into electoral politics. The investigative work is solid — the “security activities” vs. “special activities” terminology slip, the “secret compartmented” vs. “sensitive compartmented” error, the JSOU instruction-head detail, and the Y-12 whistleblower dismissal collectively suggest a candidate whose operator credentials are inflated. The Trump-binder anecdote (“He knows everything about me … I’ll be your champion”) is genuinely revealing of the principal-agent inversion Klippenstein names: the candidate’s appeal is not “trust me because of what you can verify” but “trust me because the commander-in-chief has verified, and that’s enough.” This is the credential-as-secret structure carried to its logical endpoint.

The piece’s strongest move is the symmetrical pairing of Gallrein with the “national security moms” (Spanberger/Sherrill/Slotkin). It refuses the temptation to make this a partisan story; the diagnosis is that “national security” has become a bipartisan rhetorical formation that licenses elected officials to stand above public accountability. The framing here — “the national security state as its own source of political legitimacy, floating above democratic accountability” — is a recurring Klippenstein thread that the project has tracked across multiple weeks. The misleading article title (“Iran War vs. Epstein Files”) gestures at Massie’s positions (anti-Iran-War, pro-Epstein-files-release) rather than the piece’s actual focus, which is a portrait of the challenger.

The analysis-weakness is that Klippenstein doesn’t fully reckon with the possibility that Gallrein’s strategy is rational on its own terms in the current Trump-era Republican primary environment — i.e., that voters in the relevant median may genuinely prefer the credentialed-but-opaque candidate to the visible-and-accountable one. The piece treats the strategy as obviously creepy and insulting (which it is), but doesn’t engage with why it might also be electorally effective in the way that, say, the Herschel Walker-Walker-style Trump endorsements have been. The piece would be stronger if it admitted that the structural problem isn’t just Gallrein, it’s the electorate that finds “the president has seen the file” persuasive — which would push the diagnosis toward the deeper question of why the credential-as-secret structure has demand-side appeal rather than only mapping the supply side.

↑ Back to TOC

How to End the Gerrymandering Doom Loop Forever (The Ezra Klein Show)

Ezra Klein · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the cascading mid-decade redistricting wars (Texas → California → Florida → Louisiana / Mississippi / Alabama / South Carolina / Tennessee), the Supreme Court’s Louisiana v. Callais decision gutting Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, and the Virginia state court invalidating Newsom-style counter-gerrymandered maps — events that, per Drutman, have cost House Democrats roughly seven to ten seats and reduced the universe of competitive House races to about 15 of 435.

Klein hosts Lee Drutman (New America Political Reform program, author of Breaking the Two-Party Doom Loop) to ask how the gerrymandering arms race can be ended structurally rather than escalated indefinitely. Drutman frames the past summer’s events as the collapse of the last guardrails: Rucho (2019) cleared partisan gerrymandering, Callais has now cleared racial gerrymandering by setting a near-impossible intentionality bar, and the norm against mid-decade redistricting has been broken by Trump’s pressure on Texas — which California and Virginia then mirrored, with under-noticed cost to majority-minority districts in blue states as Democrats split them for partisan advantage. Drutman’s diagnosis is that single-member-district winner-take-all is the structural cause: it produces both the gerrymandering incentive and the two-party lock-in that turns every election into an existential binary (“maximum warfare, everywhere, all the time” in Hakeem Jeffries’ phrase), and it disenfranchises minority-party voters in every safe district. His proposed fix is open-list proportional representation in multi-member districts (e.g., Louisiana’s six seats as one six-member district; New York’s 26 split into three ~8-9 member districts), under which a 40% Republican vote share yields ~40% of seats and gerrymandering becomes mechanically inert. The maximalist case extends beyond gerrymandering: PR would allow new parties at the 20% threshold, restore cross-pressure within coalitions (a conservative non-Trump party, a rural-Democratic faction, a progressive party distinct from establishment Democrats), and break the binary psychology that lets voters tolerate norm-breaking from their own side because the alternative is “fascists” or “communists.” Klein presses on counterexamples — UK Reform-surge, Israeli Knesset fragmentation, German AfD, Italian Meloni — and Drutman parries by distinguishing first-past-the-post multipartyism (UK) from PR, citing the Israeli 3.25% threshold as bad design rather than indictment of PR, and pointing to Brazil’s multiparty coalition holding Bolsonaro accountable as the contrast case to the US Republican Party’s inability to break with Trump. On feasibility, Drutman notes the Uniform Congressional District Act of 1967 mandates single-member districts but Congress can amend it under the Elections Clause; the political bind that incumbents would be voting away their seats can be eased by expanding the House by ~150 seats (per his American Academy of Arts and Sciences proposal), restoring the pre-1911 norm of House size scaling with population.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The conversation is doing two distinct things, and the second is stronger than the first. The first half is a competent and accurate inventory of the gerrymandering collapse — Rucho, Callais, the Texas-California-Virginia ping-pong, the dummymander concept, the convergence of structural gerrymandering and partisan sorting (Drutman’s note that “competitive” House seats fell from ~50 to ~15 over twenty years is mostly sorting, not just line-drawing). None of this is novel for a regular reader of either Klein or Drutman, but the framing is crisp and the empirical claims hold up. The second half — the PR pitch — is where Drutman is actually trying to move the reader, and Klein, to his credit, does the work of pressing on the strongest counterexamples (UK, Israel, Germany, Italy) rather than letting Drutman cruise on the friendly version of the argument.

Where the analysis is strong: Drutman’s distinction between PR and merely “multiparty” systems is correct and load-bearing. The UK is genuinely a single-member-district system that happens to have multiple parties, and that is the worst-of-both-worlds configuration (33% of votes → two-thirds of seats for Labour). The Brazil-vs-US Bolsonaro/Trump comparison is the most concrete and best evidence in the conversation for the structural-coalition-discipline claim: a multiparty coalition could exit Bolsonaro when the cost of staying became too high; the GOP could not exit Trump because exit meant Democratic victory. That is a real mechanism, not just an intuition. The House-expansion-as-political-grease argument is also genuinely clever and under-discussed: it converts the “incumbents will never vote to lose their seats” objection from a fatal flaw into a tractable design problem, and the historical claim (House grew with population until 1911, then froze) is accurate.

Where the analysis is weaker: Drutman repeatedly responds to counterexamples by retreating to “every country is unique” — fine as far as it goes, but it dissolves the comparative leverage he had been using to argue for PR a moment earlier. If Israeli fragmentation is “specific design failures plus enemies on all sides” and German AfD-rise is “they had to compromise,” then the international evidence has been pre-filtered for cases that confirm rather than test the theory. The deeper unaddressed problem is one Klein gestures at but doesn’t pursue: PR’s coalition-discipline mechanism depends on parties being willing to form coalitions across the polarization axis, and there is no obvious reason why a US in which the existing Democrat-Republican animosity has hardened into mutual-illegitimacy would suddenly produce, say, a center-right willing to coalition with a center-left to lock out a MAGA-successor party. The Israeli case Drutman waves away is exactly the case where polarized coalitions don’t form across the dividing axis — the religious-Zionist right coalitions with the further-right rather than with the center.

The “increase party power, weaken the candidate-centered primary” thread also deserves more scrutiny than it gets. Drutman concedes (“It is”) that open-list PR moves substantial power to party structures and conventions, then immediately pivots to the benefit (good team players, diverse candidates) without engaging Klein’s specific worry — that the same Democratic Party leadership that produced the 2024 Biden-loyalty disaster and the Cuomo/Mills-type institutional candidates is the body that would now control nomination lists. If party institutions are currently failing at exactly the function Drutman wants to delegate more of to them, the case for empowering them further is not self-evident; it depends on a theory of why party institutions are failing now (Drutman gestures at “anti-institutional moment” without much more) and whether PR mechanically corrects that or just locks it in.

Finally, the synthesis question Klein doesn’t quite ask: Drutman’s diagnosis (structural rather than cultural) and his fix (a single statutory change plus House expansion) are at very different elevations. The diagnosis says the disease is binary psychology, mutual delegitimization, and zero-sum framing — cultural conditions whose roots predate Rucho and Callais. The fix is a procedural mechanism. The implicit theory of change — that procedure shapes culture downstream, breaking the doom loop by making third-party coalitions possible — is contestable: the same cultural conditions that produce gerrymandering escalation would also presumably condition how PR is implemented, gamed, and metabolized. Drutman has the better of the argument on PR’s mechanical superiority for gerrymandering specifically. He has the harder argument to make on PR as a solvent for the broader two-party-doom-loop pathology, and the conversation lets him slide on the harder version.

↑ Back to TOC

Martin Gurri: Trump’s Endgame in Cuba

Martin Gurri · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Trump administration’s escalating Cuba pressure campaign — January’s “Cuba’s next” declaration after the Delta Force snatch of Venezuelan strongman Nicolás Maduro, the naval blockade enforcing the oil embargo, new sanctions on Grupo de Administración Empresarial S.A. (GAESA, the Cuban-military-run conglomerate), the Raúl Castro indictment (announced on Cuban independence day), and the May 14 unannounced visit by CIA director John Ratcliffe to Havana while Trump was meeting Xi in Beijing. Gurri’s frame: the Cuban revolution has become “a theme park of 20th-century Marxism-Leninism,” genuinely doomed but at the pace of historic time rather than Trump time. The Maduro removal eliminated Cuba’s subsidized Venezuelan oil supply; the electric grid has crashed (most of day in blackouts), water is faltering even in Havana, food is scarce, protesters burn garbage and bang pots at night. Without subsidies, the regime is “circling the drain.” But Trump misread the dynamics — he expected weakness would force change; Gurri argues weakness instead deprived Havana of “strong leadership, institutional adaptability, tactical intelligence” needed to depart from the old script, so negotiations went nowhere. Ratcliffe’s secret visit delivered an ultimatum dressed as a $100M humanitarian-aid offer conditional on “fundamental changes” (opening economy, releasing political prisoners, eliminating Russian/Chinese listening posts). The Cubans leaked the meeting themselves, signaling willingness to deal with “the devil.” Gurri identifies the administration’s profound ambivalence: Trump expects “fundamental change” without “regime change”; Cuba is dying of incompetence yet allegedly a “growing threat” (per a suspiciously-Ratcliffe-sounding Axios leak claiming 300 Iranian-advised drones at Guantánamo); economic sanctions have failed but military intervention is “a last resort.” Castro’s prosecution would mostly boost Trump with Cuban Americans; the disappearance of the 94-year-old was already priced into Havana’s calculations. Gurri’s prediction: nothing dramatic until the Iran war wraps; the regime will collapse from entropy on its own timetable, not Trump’s.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Gurri is the right writer for this piece — his core thesis across the last decade has been that the public’s loss of faith in central authority outpaces any state’s ability to manage the transition, and the Cuban revolution-as-theme-park frame is a clean application of that lens to a regime that has been waiting to die for years. The “robotic ruling class lacked the wherewithal… to depart from the old narrative” observation is the most distinctively Gurri-shaped move and is the reason this piece is worth reading even alongside González’s same-week Castro-indictment essay: González treats the indictment as historical accountability; Gurri treats it as one of multiple ambivalence-driven moves the administration is making because it doesn’t actually know what it wants.

The Ratcliffe-leak material is the most original reporting in the piece — the detail that Cubans themselves leaked the CIA director’s visit (signaling willingness to deal) is the kind of small fact that materially changes the read of the situation. The Axios “300 Iranian-advised drones at Guantánamo” leak, with Gurri’s parenthetical that the “senior U.S. official” source “sounded suspiciously like CIA chief Ratcliffe,” is good bounded-distrust analysis — the leak is doing pretextual work that may or may not survive contact with the underlying intelligence, and Gurri correctly flags both the leak’s structure and Axios’s own caveat that “U.S. officials don’t believe Cuba is an imminent threat.”

The piece’s load-bearing claim — the administration’s “profound ambivalence” between four incompatible positions (regime change without regime change, growing threat from a dying state, sanctions-don’t-work but military-intervention-is-last-resort, expect-fundamental-change without specifying mechanisms) — is the analytic contribution. This is the contradiction the foreign-policy press has not been naming clearly, and Gurri’s framing of it as either “psychological warfare or… indecision and contradiction—or possibly both” is honest about the indeterminacy. Compare with Maitra’s TAC piece earlier this month on the foreign-policy restrainer/hawk split inside the administration: Gurri is reading the Cuba portfolio as a specific case of the more general factional incoherence Maitra has been tracking.

One weakness worth naming. Gurri treats “Cuban communism will unravel from sheer incompetence” as a settled fact rather than a prediction with a confidence interval. He cites the material indicators (electric grid, water, food, protests) and the diplomatic signals (Ratcliffe-meeting-leak), but the regime has been “circling the drain” by analogous metrics for at least a decade and has not collapsed. The piece would be sharper if it engaged the survival-mechanisms question — why has this regime, more than the Eastern European ones in 1989, demonstrated such durability under material stress? — rather than treating collapse as the default and Trump’s impatience as the only variable. That said, the central observation about administration ambivalence stands on its own and is the most useful diagnostic of the Cuba policy this week.

Pair with González in the synthesis: same Castro-indictment story, opposite interpretive moves.

↑ Back to TOC

Thirty Years Late, Justice Comes Knocking on Raúl Castro’s Door

Roberto González · 2026-05-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Justice Department’s unsealing of a superseding indictment charging Raúl Castro and five Cuban co-defendants with conspiracy to kill U.S. nationals, destruction of aircraft, and four counts of murder — 30 years after the February 1996 Cuban MiG shoot-down over international waters of two unarmed Cessnas operated by Brothers to the Rescue, killing Carlos Costa, Armando Alejandre Jr., Mario de la Peña, and Pablo Morales. González pushes back on press coverage that has read the indictment primarily as a Trump-administration pretext for future Cuba action. The piece reconstructs the underlying crime in detail: the Wasp Network (La Red Avispa) was a Cuban intelligence operation in 1990s South Florida that penetrated exile groups and surveilled Southern Command, Homestead Air Reserve Base, and the Turkey Point Nuclear Generating Station; intelligence it gathered helped enable the ambush, in which Cuban MiGs fired air-to-air missiles at slow-moving civilian aircraft outside Cuban airspace while pilots celebrated with “¡Patria o Muerte!” on cockpit recordings. The ICAO confirmed the planes were over international waters with no interception protocols attempted; the OAS condemned it as an international-law violation. The 2003 indictment stopped at the pilots and an operational officer; the new filing reaches up the chain of command to Castro himself.
