González names Netflix’s 2020 Wasp Network as having reframed the Cuban spy ring into “patriotic underdogs” and the murdered men as a subplot, “cement[ing] a misleading public memory” especially for younger viewers. His core claim: even if 94-year-old Castro never enters a Miami courtroom (extradition is unlikely), the indictment has independent value as a public, durable, citable legal account that corrects the mythology — “it creates a public, testable, durable account of what happened.” He explicitly separates this from the Trump-foreign-policy reading: the substance of the indictment matters apart from whatever instrumental use the administration makes of it.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is a focused piece doing one rhetorical move well. González bracket-quotes the geopolitical-pretext reading at the top, sets it aside, and devotes the body to the historical reconstruction — and the move works because the historical material is genuinely under-remembered and the Netflix-mythology correction is doing real cultural work. The named victims, the ICAO and OAS findings, the cockpit recordings, the surveillance targets (Turkey Point Nuclear is the surprising detail) — this is the kind of material a thirty-year-old crime needs to surface again and the piece’s strongest contribution.

The Wasp Network / “Cuban Five” / Netflix triangulation is the interesting analytic claim. González is asserting that the Obama-era 2014 prisoner exchange (which released the remaining Cuban Five as part of the Alan Gross deal) was the move that gave Havana the narrative space to mythologize the operation, and that Netflix’s 2020 film was the cultural ratification of that mythology. Whether or not one accepts the implicit critique of the Obama exchange, the observation that prosecutorial accountability stopped at the operational level in 2003 and only this week reached the political principals is a real point about how international-law enforcement against state actors actually works (or doesn’t).

Two weaknesses. First, the explicit “this indictment matters on its own terms apart from Trump’s foreign-policy use of it” frame is the piece’s main rhetorical move, but it’s also slightly disingenuous — the timing of the indictment (announced on Cuban independence day, alongside escalating sanctions on GAESA, after the Maduro snatch, during Trump’s “Cuba’s next” pressure campaign) is obviously not coincidental. González knows this; he just declines to engage with the implication that the prosecutorial decision is itself politically timed. A stronger piece would have argued that the case’s merit and the timing’s political utility can both be true.

Second, the piece doesn’t reckon with the conceptual question of what a U.S. court indictment of a foreign head of state actually accomplishes when extradition is unavailable. González’s “creates a public, testable, durable account” answer is the strongest version of the case, but it begs the question of whether U.S. federal court is the right venue for that documentary-record function (versus, say, an international tribunal or a truth-commission-style process). The Pinochet/Milošević precedents he doesn’t cite would have either bolstered or complicated the claim.

For roundup purposes: pair this with Gurri’s “Trump’s Endgame in Cuba” (same week, same publication, contrasting framings — Gurri reads the indictment as decapitation tactics; González reads it as historical accountability). The two pieces share a topic but make incompatible claims about what the move is, which is exactly the kind of intra-publication cross-pressure worth surfacing.

↑ Back to TOC

India fertility facts of the day

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: India’s new Sample Registration System (SRS) Statistical Report 2024, summarized in ten facts by economist Jesús Fernández-Villaverde.

India’s total fertility rate (TFR) fell to 1.88 in 2024 from 1.92 in 2023, continuing the ~0.06/year decline that has held since 1985 (when TFR was 4.3). The smartphone-as-cause hypothesis gets no support from the Indian data: no acceleration after smartphone introduction. The sex ratio at birth is normalizing slowly (918 girls per 1,000 boys in 2022-24, up from 907 in 2018-20, vs. a natural ~952), which pushes India’s effective replacement rate to 2.15 rather than 2.10 — meaning India is already 0.27 children below replacement and widening. Regional spread is enormous: Kerala 1.3 (near Italy/Spain), Delhi 1.2, Bihar 2.9; rural 2.1, urban 1.5. Projected to hit 1.57 (current U.S. level) around 2031. Cowen’s framing line — “No Indian fertility Sonderweg” — refuses the recent commentariat hope that India might be an exception to the developed-world fertility collapse.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

The facts as presented are clean and the Fernández-Villaverde reading is well-anchored. The two contributions worth noting are (1) the empirical rebuttal of the smartphone-causation story (which Jonathan Haidt and others have pushed as part of the broader “phones are eating childhood” frame) and (2) the framing line about Sonderweg, which is doing more work than it looks. The Sonderweg reference (German for “special path,” historically used to describe Germany’s distinctive route to modernity) is Cowen’s signal that the cohort of writers — including Lyman Stone, Tooze in his demography registers, and various pro-natalist conservatives — who have been hoping that high-religiosity, family-structured cultures like India’s Hindu-majority interior might resist the demographic transition are wrong. The Kerala-vs-Bihar gap (1.3 vs 2.9) is consistent with the standard female-literacy-and-urbanization story, which has zero distinctively Indian content; it’s the same story that explains the Italian or South Korean curves.

The data point worth flagging for cross-week tracking: the 2031 convergence-to-U.S.-levels projection. If India is at 1.57 in 2031 and the U.S. has by then continued its own decline, the “India will inherit the demographic dividend” frame that has structured a lot of geopolitical commentary (Indian-century-vs-Chinese-decline framings, Ray Dalio’s long-cycle work, etc.) needs to be priced down. India’s working-age peak now comes earlier and the decline that follows is steeper than the optimistic 2010s projections. Pair with the companion fertility-and-financial-risk-taking post: Cowen is running two fertility data points in three days, both pointing the same direction — fertility is falling, the fall has real downstream effects (here on regional convergence; in the companion post on stock-market participation). The pairing suggests a quiet preoccupation rather than a coincidence.

↑ Back to TOC

Fertility and financial risk-taking

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Judith Bohnenkamp, Ville Rantala, and Melina Murren Vosse, “Fertility and Financial Risk-Taking,” using nationally representative panel data from three countries.

The headline finding: childless adults who do not expect children are 21-36% more likely to invest in stocks than those who do expect children, controlling for personal characteristics, and the effect survives a medical-infertility instrument (i.e., it isn’t reverse-causal). The effect is specific to stocks — no similar shift in other savings categories or self-reported risk tolerance. The authors propose financial planning horizon as the mechanism: households expecting children report shorter horizons, which depresses risk-bearing. The implication Cowen flags through citation choice: declining fertility may causally increase young adults’ stock market participation through the childbearing-expectations channel.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

A clean short link-post; the analysis warrant matches the data warrant. The empirical move (using medical infertility as an instrument for child expectations) is the standard way to address the reverse-causation worry that risk-averse people might both avoid stocks and avoid having children, and the authors apparently get the same result instrumented as uninstrumented. The planning-horizon mechanism is plausible and consistent with the lifecycle-savings literature: expecting children compresses the time between “I have money” and “I need to spend it on a person,” which mechanically lowers the optimal equity share.

The piece is most interesting paired with the India fertility post Cowen ran two days later: he is running two fertility data points in three days, both pointing at the same downstream conclusion that the demographic shift is causally rewiring economic behavior in ways the standard models haven’t priced. The downstream cross-currents implication — rising equity participation among the childless-by-choice cohort means a structural support under stock valuations as fertility falls — is the kind of fact that Wall Street strategists are likely to internalize before policy commentary does. Worth flagging only because the pairing matters; on its own this is a tidy academic-finance result without surprises.

↑ Back to TOC

How I Lost a Religion

Ross Douthat · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original newsletter column; the upstream prompt is Douthat’s own experience watching his childhood baseball fandom fail to pass to his children, treated as a small-scale model for the 21st-century transmission problem facing religion, literature, and other inherited practices.

Douthat opens with his anticipated Red Sox game with his son and the guilt he feels that the team that defined his first two decades no longer means much to his kids. He frames sports fandom as a place where he is “the weak link in the chain of intergenerational transmission” — analogous to the Christmas-and-Easter Catholic who can’t pass on the faith — and uses it to extract three general lessons about how traditions fail. First, eccentricities and distinctives matter: his Red Sox fandom was bound to a specific identity (tragic underdogs haunted by Buckner’s 1986 error), and once they became Yankees-level winners after 2004, the binding power dissolved — explicitly analogous to liberal religious modernizers who wanted a church more in step with the times and found the modernized version lost its hold. Second, cultural transmission depends on leisure and “constructive boredom,” which is now claimed from both sides by meritocratic over-scheduling and infinite on-demand entertainment that offers guaranteed dramatic payoff a quotidian 5-2 baseball game cannot match. Third, traditions need material and chronological anchors — the morning sports section, scheduled SportsCenter highlight reels — and the digital era’s “always available” replacements paradoxically thin engagement by removing the ritualized appointment. The piece closes by refusing the elegy mode (sports will survive without his dollars; his older son still identifies as a Red Sox fan even if he doesn’t practice the religion) but accepts the personal failure: the annual Fenway pilgrimage is now participation in the spirit of the age, an inheritance fading into digital static.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Douthat in an unusual register — memoir-essay rather than column — so the appropriate critical mode is whether the personal frame illuminates or obscures the structural argument, not whether the argument has the data to support a causal claim. As memoir-essay, the piece works: the Buckner game, the Roger Angell shelves, the wife who is “extremely bored by baseball,” the candid acknowledgement that he self-deceived about the post-curse Red Sox — these are concrete and unflattering enough that the essay earns the analogical move to religious transmission rather than asserting it. The three-lessons structure is the piece’s load-bearing move and it is a good structure: distinctiveness, leisure, and material anchors are genuinely separable mechanisms, and naming them separately lets the reader see which of them might apply to other transmission problems (religion, novel-reading, civic ritual) without forcing the analogy.

That said, the central analogy is doing more work than the essay acknowledges. The 2004 Red Sox-as-liberalized-religion line — “I was like the religious liberalizers in the 1960s and 1970s insisting that all they wanted was a church more in step with the times” — is the strongest move in the piece, but it loads the dice on which direction the religion analogy runs. The Red Sox didn’t liberalize; they won. The thing that changed was their position in the cosmology of fandom (perennial sufferers became winners), not their doctrine. If the analogy holds, the religious parallel isn’t liberal-modernization theology; it’s prosperity-gospel — what happens when a faith built on persecution and outsider identity becomes culturally dominant. That’s a more interesting and less self-flattering reading (it’s what happened to American Catholicism in the second half of the 20th century), and the column would be sharper if Douthat had pulled on this thread instead of reflexively reaching for the standard conservative-Catholic complaint about Vatican II-style modernization.

The “constructive boredom” lesson is the most generalizable of the three and the one where the essay does its best structural work. The point that streaming offers “guaranteed dramatic payoff” against which a 5-2 baseball game cannot compete is genuinely a different mechanism from prior accounts of attention erosion — it’s not just that attention is scarcer, it’s that the comparative supply curve of guaranteed-payoff entertainment has shifted, making low-payoff committed engagement structurally weaker. This deserves to be load-bearing in the synthesis if the cluster develops. The “material anchors” lesson is more contestable: the morning paper and the 6am SportsCenter were artifacts of the broadcast era’s supply constraints, not of any intrinsic property of ritual, and there’s a reading in which the loss-of-anchor problem is less “too much availability” and more “no shared schedule produces no shared experience.” Douthat gestures at this but doesn’t quite separate the appointment-with-yourself problem (his framing) from the appointment-with-others problem.

The closing refusal of elegy is the right move — the essay would have collapsed into self-pity without it — and the line about his older son who “identifies as a Red Sox fan even if he doesn’t really practice the religion” is the essay’s most honest sentence. It’s also a tell: the religion analogy isn’t an analogy. The piece is about Catholicism with sports providing low-stakes cover.

↑ Back to TOC

A diverse “Odyssey” is the triumph of Western civilization

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Elon Musk’s week-long X complaints about Christopher Nolan casting Lupita Nyong’o as Helen of Troy in the upcoming “Odyssey” film, which Musk attributed to Academy Award diversity rules.

Yglesias uses the Musk meltdown to argue for a positively-inflected, civic case for “Western civilization” as an inherited cultural patrimony — and contends that inclusive casting of foundational European myths is what keeps the canon load-bearing rather than dismantled. He concedes the case against race-swapping when stories are about race (Mandela in “Invictus,” Shaw in “Glory”), and acknowledges a real asymmetry in Black actors’ filmographies — they are disproportionately routed into Movies About Race, a complaint Black creatives also voice. But Homer’s “Odyssey” is myth, not history; the dramatic Western tradition (Shakespeare’s Denmark, Italy) has always been insouciant about period accuracy, and Nolan’s deliberate anachronistic armor signals he’s making a Batman-style mythic film, not “Dunkirk.” A canon limited to actors of European ancestry would face overwhelming demand to be torn down; a canon that lets Denzel play Macbeth and Nyong’o play Helen is “re-inscribing” the canon. The piece also previews a future Yglesias post making a more conservative-inflected Western-civ case, including endorsing the Trump administration’s neo-classical civic-architecture push.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Yglesias operating in his “fusion centrist” mode — taking what reads as a culture-war flashpoint and re-routing it through a structural argument designed to give both sides something to nod at. The structural argument is genuinely good: the distinction between Movies About Race (where casting is constitutive of the story) and Myth Adaptations (where casting is convention) is the clarifying move the surrounding discourse usually muddles, and the directionality observation — that race-neutral roles flow toward Black stars more than the reverse because the stock of legacy IP skews white — is the kind of small economic point that dissolves a lot of nominally-political heat. He also gets credit for the “Hamilton” example (an opposite-direction case that proves the principle).

What’s weaker is the piece’s central pivot, where Yglesias tries to land the inclusive-Odyssey case as a conservative-friendly argument. The “open your heart to the more optimistic interpretation” appeal assumes the right-wing complaint is fundamentally aesthetic rather than political — that Musk and his retweet-army are reachable by an argument that frames diverse casting as canon-preservation. But the actual conservative objection isn’t to ahistorical casting per se (no one complains about anachronistic armor); it’s to the perceived directionality Yglesias himself names. The piece thus has the structure of a left-coded olive branch dressed as a right-friendly take, and it’s worth asking whether the addressee is real or whether the piece is mostly aimed at center-left readers who want intellectual cover for liking the casting choice.

The Whiggish “Western civ as cultural patrimony” framing is also doing more work than Yglesias acknowledges. The footnote tracing US → English → French → German Reformation → Italian Renaissance → Rome → Greece is a coherent narrative, but it’s a choice — one that, e.g., consistently routes around the African and Islamic transmission of Greek texts during the medieval period (Aristotle reached Aquinas via Ibn Rushd / Averroes), the Atlantic slave economy that helped fund early-modern Europe, and the Mexican/Indigenous components of actual North American history. A genuinely inclusive canon-defense would probably need to argue for the expansion of the canon, not just inclusive casting within an unchanged canon — and Yglesias punts on whether his canon defense requires the curriculum reforms (or counter-reforms) it implies. The “endorse the Trump neo-classical architecture push” preview is the tell here: it suggests his future “more conservative” Western-civ post will be the one where the actual stakes show up.

A minor footnote: the piece’s use of “Claude tells me” framing (deployed in the ethanol post, not this one) is becoming a tic across Slow Boring this week — Yglesias is showing his epistemic work, but the implicit appeal to an LLM as authority is one the readership should probably push back on harder than it has.

↑ Back to TOC

The death of Bengali liberalism

Sumantra Maitra · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the BJP’s recent electoral victory in West Bengal and the symbolic renaming of a Kolkata park from Siraj Uddyan (commemorating the last pre-British Nawab) to Shivaji Uddyan (honoring the 17th-century Maratha king whose armies historically raided Bengal). Maitra — Bengali by birth, mechanism-first realist by training, here writing in cultural-historical rather than IR register — uses the renaming as entry point to a sweeping obituary for “Bhadralok” cosmopolitan liberalism, the 200-year tradition built by the 1820s Bengal Renaissance under reformers like Rammohan Roy (founder of the Brahmo Samaj reform movement) and Ishwarchandra Vidyasagar (widow-remarriage advocate), in collaboration with British officials like Lord Bentinck (who abolished sati) and orientalist scholars like William Jones. Maitra’s structural claim: Bengal’s history shows a recurring pattern of “Hindu reactionary cycles” against any universalizing/liberalizing force — the 7th-century king Śaśāṅka against Buddhism, the 15th-century Chaitanya movement against political Islam, late-19th-century Vivekananda against Brahmo unitarianism — and the BJP’s win represents the latest iteration, now finally hollowing out the British legacy of common law, secular rationalism, and meritocracy. He treats the absence of Western academic attention to Bengali liberalism as an “epistemological blackhole” deliberately maintained because honest study would force concession that the British Empire functioned as a “progressive and liberalising force” — a taboo modern Anglo-American historiography refuses. The essay closes by treating the surviving Bengali liberal type as a diaspora phenomenon (scattered competent administrators across Europe, Asia, America) while the homeland’s surface markers (tea, cricket, Hamlet, Victoria Memorial Hall) become “empty skin suits,” and floats — quietly — that India may be strategically inclined toward a “short and successful” territorial war against Bangladesh to fortify the “chicken neck” corridor against China. Fundamental-flaw flag: the essay reads Bengal’s entire political history through a single Whig narrative (British liberalism = civilization; reaction = recurring Hindu pathology) and the structural claim — that elite-led liberalism is the only true civilizing force, with mass democracy as inherent threat — is asserted rather than argued; the piece is openly anti-democratic-mass-politics and pro-Empire in a register most readers will not encounter elsewhere this week.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is the most ideologically distinctive piece in this week’s batch, and worth reading less for its policy bearing than for what it reveals about Maitra as a writer when he steps off the IR-realist beat. The historical sweep is genuinely impressive — the recurring-Hindu-reaction motif (Śaśāṅka → Chaitanya → Vivekananda → BJP) is a real pattern in Bengali religious history and Maitra deserves credit for compressing it into a single legible arc. The grievance against Western academy’s silence on Bengali liberalism is also pointed: there genuinely is no major English-language one-volume history of the Bhadralok, and Maitra correctly identifies why — admitting that the East India Company and Macaulay-system English education produced a flourishing native intelligentsia would complicate the postcolonial framework most Western South Asia departments operate within.

But the essay’s central frame is doing more work than its evidence carries. Maitra’s structural argument is that elite-led liberalism is the only true civilizing force, and that mass democracy is inherently a reactionary threat — this is the Isaiah Berlin / Edmund Burke note he closes on. The frame is openly anti-democratic (“most of the masses in any society are not elite, and thereby not enlightened”), openly pro-British-Empire as a “liberalising force,” and it elides the question of why the Bhadralok project failed to extend beyond a thin elite stratum across 150 years. If the project required mass disenfranchisement to survive, that’s worth saying outright; if it failed because the elite stratum lost cultural confidence, the BJP-as-natural-recurrence framing under-explains the role of 60 years of post-Independence Congress mismanagement Maitra mentions only in passing.

The piece also runs uncomfortably close to advocating ethnic-cleansing-adjacent territorial revision near the end. The throwaway reference to a possible Indian “short and successful” war against Bangladesh modeled on “Crimea, Lebanon or Armenia” — to “permanently sever Bangladesh’s jugular” and reconnect the chicken-neck corridor — is presented as strategic logic rather than evaluated as policy. Coming from a Senior Research Fellow at an institute connected to the second Trump administration’s policy ecosystem, this is the kind of paragraph that would draw immediate scrutiny if it appeared in TAC under the Maitra byline most American readers know. Engelsberg Ideas is a venue with smaller US footprint, which may be why the line is allowed to pass without elaboration.

For the synthesis composer: this piece does NOT pair with Maitra’s other two pieces this week (Chronicles on Europe force posture, Hungarian Conservative on the Trump-Xi summit) — those are restrainer IR pieces in his usual register. The Bengali essay is a different Maitra, working a cultural-historical frame that surfaces priors his IR writing keeps tacit (anti-mass-democracy, pro-elite-Whig, comfortable with revisionist territorial reasoning). Worth holding all three together when assessing where Maitra’s commitments actually sit.

↑ Back to TOC

What the Right Can Learn from the Frankfurt School

Paul Gottfried · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A.J.A. Woods’s new book “The Cultural Marxism Conspiracy: Why the Right Blames the Frankfurt School for the Decline of the West” (recently reviewed by Geoff Shullenberger in Compact) and Gabriel Rockhill’s countervailing left claim that the Frankfurt School was a CIA-funded counter-Soviet cutout. Paul Gottfried, Chronicles editor-in-chief, former graduate student of Herbert Marcuse at Yale in 1964, and longstanding paleoconservative critic of mainstream conservatism, makes a cross-pollination argument: conservative animus toward Adorno, Horkheimer, and Marcuse is partly justified (their antifascist mania produced the Authoritarian Personality F-scale and the “neofascist West” framing) but rests heavily on hearsay and the popular Boomer-conservative impulse to blame “the German invasion” (Allan Bloom’s Closing of the American Mind being the canonical text) for homegrown American cultural shifts. Against William Lind’s view that the Frankfurt School was “truly Marxist,” Gottfried argues they prefigured cultural-radicalism and therapeutic politics in ways orthodox Marxists rejected, and that contemporary “cultural Marxism” has morphed into something more repressive than anything Adorno or Marcuse envisaged. The piece’s load-bearing move is the recommendation that today’s right adopt the Frankfurt School’s method — focus on dominant social and cultural forces and the power of ideology — over what Gottfried calls “abstract value talk,” with Adorno’s “culture industry” concept (commercial-technological degradation of high culture) singled out as still-relevant. His personal anecdote about Marcuse asking a student which edition of Maistre’s Soirées de Saint Pétersbourg he had consulted serves as a closing contrast between mid-century left intellectual seriousness and the present’s media-driven cliché.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Gottfried in his familiar mode — paleoconservative settling accounts with both the mainstream right’s hearsay culture and the contemporary woke left’s intellectual sloppiness — and the central distinction he draws (that the Frankfurt School was something other than orthodox Marxism, and that “cultural Marxism” as polemical category obscures more than it reveals) is genuinely important and largely correct. Adorno and Horkheimer’s relationship to Marx’s actual political economy was attenuated by the 1940s, and treating the F-scale or One-Dimensional Man as straightforward Marxist texts collapses real intellectual distinctions. Anyone who has actually read Dialectic of Enlightenment knows it is much closer to Weber-with-Nietzsche-overtones than to anything recognizable as historical materialism.

The interesting question is whether Gottfried’s positive recommendation — that the right appropriate the Frankfurt School’s method without its commitments — is coherent. The Frankfurt School’s analysis of mass culture, instrumental reason, and the administered society was inseparable from their political horizon (some form of emancipation from capitalism). Stripped of that horizon, the “method” is just sociology with attitude — a description of pathologies with no diagnostic about what to do next. Christopher Lasch, whom Gottfried correctly invokes as a kindred Telos-circle figure, managed this synthesis by importing populist republicanism as the alternative horizon, but Gottfried himself rarely names what positive vision is meant to replace the bourgeois order he agrees with Adorno is disintegrating. The “abstract value talk” he mocks in respectable conservatives is partly placeholder for the absence of any specific account of what comes next.

The hearsay charge against the Mark Levin-style “Franklin School” right is well-taken but the piece undercounts how much that hearsay has done political work — for two decades it gave the populist right a unified-field theory of cultural change that mobilized voters and donors. Whether or not Adorno belongs in the indictment, the indictment delivered electoral results. Gottfried’s preference for accuracy over rhetorical utility is admirable but he doesn’t really grapple with the trade-off he is asking the movement to accept.

The strongest single observation is the throwaway about Horkheimer’s late-life conservative turn, his break with Adorno over Habermas, and his Schopenhauer-reading pessimism. This is the kind of intellectual-biographical detail that does more to defamiliarize the “Frankfurt School as monolithic left bloc” trope than any amount of polemical correction — it shows that the school’s internal trajectory was always toward fragmentation, with each thinker carrying its critical apparatus in different political directions. The Telos-circle synthesis Gottfried himself participated in (1991 Elizabethtown populism conference with Lasch and Samuel Francis) is the historical proof-of-concept for what he’s recommending; the question is whether anything comparable is possible inside today’s polarized intellectual culture.

↑ Back to TOC

American Empire Is Here to Stay

Ralph Leonard · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The cross-orientation consensus emerging in recent weeks that the Iran war is the “American Suez” — a hinge moment confirming irreversible US decline and the arrival of multipolarity. Ralph Leonard, a London-based left-leaning writer who regularly contributes heterodox materialist arguments to Compact, runs a deliberately cross-cutting move: a writer from the left defending the durability of American empire against a chorus that includes both left declinists and right restrainers. His material argument is straightforward — the dollar remains the global reserve currency with no credible alternative, the US navy is still the sole guarantor of shipping lanes, the post-2000s shale revolution made America the world’s largest liquid-fuels producer (inverting the 1970s vulnerability), and previous decline predictions (Paul Kennedy’s 1987 “Rise and Fall of the Great Powers,” Giovanni Arrighi’s Japan-then-China hegemonic-succession thesis) all failed. The deeper argument is that American power rests on “social power” rooted in two world-historical revolutions (1776 and 1861-65) that give the polity multiple sources of renewal China and Iran lack. Leonard’s tragic twist: this durability is also a curse on the American republic itself — imperialism necessitates the overbearing bureaucratic state (“the fourth branch”) William Graham Sumner warned against in 1899’s “The Conquest of the United States by Spain.” The emerging order Leonard sees is not multipolar collapse but a neo-developmental Pax Americana that weaponizes interdependence and adopts civilizational rather than globalist legitimation.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Leonard’s piece is a genuinely useful corrective to a roundup-week consensus that almost certainly is over-extrapolating from a single conflict. The material reasons he names — dollar reserve status without alternative, naval primacy without contender, energy independence inverting the 1970s setup — are the right empirical facts to anchor a decline-skeptic argument, and the historical analogy to the 1970s/Sputnik/Arrighi-on-Japan cycle of declinist predictions that proved wrong is doing real work. Anyone arguing that this time is different needs to engage these specific facts, not just gesture at multipolarity.

That said, the argument has a structural soft spot in its load-bearing “social power” claim. Leonard reaches for revolutionary heritage (1776, the Civil War amendments) as the deep source of American adaptive capacity, but this is metaphysics doing the work that material argument should do — and it conveniently exempts the United States from the same kind of analysis Leonard would surely apply to any other empire’s foundational mythology. Either revolutionary social power is something measurable (capacity to absorb migration, capacity to renew elites, capacity to integrate immigrants into civic identity) or it is a just-so story, and Leonard doesn’t do the measurement. The Civil War he invokes happened 160 years ago; whatever social power it generated has had to be replenished by intervening events, and the project of replenishment is precisely what’s contested in the current American moment.

Where Leonard’s piece is strongest is its diagnosis of the adaptation, not the persistence. The shift from neoliberal Pax Americana to neo-developmental Pax Americana — industrial policy, weaponized interdependence, civilizational rather than globalist legitimation — is the actual phenomenon recent commentary has been describing, just mislabeled as decline. This reframing is useful because it forces the question: is what we’re watching the end of American hegemony, or the end of a particular ideological packaging of it? Leonard is right that those are different questions and that conflating them produces bad analysis.

The Iran-specific empirical premise the piece opens with — that “Tehran’s asymmetrical hybrid warfare has rendered most US bases in the region inoperable and effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz” — is also doing rhetorical setup work and is worth verifying against the actual military reporting rather than accepting at the level of premise. Leonard accepts it to grant his opponents their strongest version of the decline claim before refuting it, which is honest argumentative form, but readers should not absorb it as established fact from this piece alone.

The closing question — “what would it take to create a world that no longer needs a cop to police it” — is genuine and the piece’s most interesting move, because it refuses both the imperialist defense and the declinist celebration. Leonard leaves the answer open, which is appropriate given that no left or right tradition currently has a credible one.

↑ Back to TOC

The end of Japanese pacifism

Christopher Harding · 2026-05-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s April 2026 cabinet decision to abolish the long-standing restriction limiting Japanese military equipment transfers to five non-lethal categories (rescue, transport, warning, surveillance, mine-sweeping), opening exports of missiles, drones, frigates, and eventually railguns and laser weapons to 17 partner nations including the US, UK, and Australia (but not Taiwan).

Christopher Harding, a Japan historian, argues this is the structural end of Japan’s 80-year pacifist posture, accelerated by uncertainty over US guarantees for Taiwan after Trump’s Beijing visit (during which Trump told reporters he had been schooled on Taiwan by Xi Jinping and was reluctant to “travel 9,500 miles to fight a war”). Takaichi’s broader program: a fast-tracked review of Japan’s three non-nuclear principles, expanded defense-sector capacity (Toshiba, Mitsubishi Electric hiring), proposed sales to the Philippines (three retired Abukuma-class destroyer escorts), and capture of market share from a US arms industry stretched thin by Ukraine and the Middle East. Japan has working prototype technologies — railguns, lasers tested aboard the experimental vessel Asuka — that the US has abandoned. Harding argues the alarming historical analogy is not unreconstructed samurai mentality (which he treats as Western propaganda residue) but the 1930s pattern: a perceived foreign threat combined with capture of politics by industrialists, military men, and unaccountable security services, with parliament progressively silenced. He notes Takaichi’s policy was made at cabinet level without parliamentary vote, that her mentor Shinzo Abe passed a State Secrecy Law in 2013, and that Takaichi now plans a new national intelligence bureau and a “Japanese CIA.” The risk Harding flags is not remilitarization but the erosion of democratic checks needed to govern remilitarization responsibly — and the secondary risk that Japan becomes a “merchant of death” trading on the global reputation it built as a peaceful country.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Harding is doing two things at once: writing a competent strategic-policy explainer on what Takaichi has actually done (the export-restriction lift, the cabinet-not-parliament method, the new intelligence architecture), and offering a historian’s analogy back to the Meiji-to-Showa transition that produced Japanese militarism. The strategic-policy half is reliable: the April 2026 export-restriction change is real, the partner list of 17 nations is correct, the Philippines destroyer-escort transaction is in motion, and Takaichi’s broader fast-track review of the three non-nuclear principles is the operative move. As a reader-friendly primer on what Japan just did, this is the most thorough piece in the UnHerd batch this week.

The historical analogy is where the piece earns its analytical weight and where it should be pressure-tested. Harding’s argument is that the 1930s Japanese-militarism slide was not a permanent feature of Japanese culture (he explicitly rejects the “unreconstructed samurai” frame as Allied wartime propaganda residue) but a contingent product of specific civil-society failures: industrialist-military capture of politics, an unaccountable secret police, intimidation of newspapers, and progressive silencing of parliament. By that argument, the relevant 2026 risk indicators are not Japanese cultural attributes but institutional ones — specifically the 2013 State Secrecy Law (no judicial oversight on what counts as a secret), the new national intelligence bureau, and a defense industry suddenly dependent on export contracts and therefore on foreign-policy outcomes. This is a defensible reading and it cleanly avoids the trap of “Japanese remilitarization” being read as cultural reawakening rather than as institutional drift.

Where the piece is weakest is on whether the analogy actually holds beyond the institutional-mechanism level. Pre-1937 Japan had a uniquely weak parliament (the Diet had limited constitutional power, parliamentarians were “given precious little real power” in Harding’s own words), genuine extrajudicial assassinations of political figures by military officers (the 1932 May 15 incident, the 1936 February 26 incident), and a public broadcasting and newspaper environment under wartime-build mobilization. 2026 Japan has none of these conditions: the Diet is the constitutional sovereign, Japan has a robust civil society and NGO network (Harding mentions Nihon Hidankyo’s 2024 Nobel), independent media (Asahi, Mainichi, Tokyo Shimbun all routinely criticize the LDP), and a court system that does engage on civil liberties when pressed. The analogy is useful as a checklist of warning signs, but the gap between 1930s Japan and 2026 Japan on the underlying preconditions is wider than Harding acknowledges. Naming Takaichi’s cabinet-not-parliament method as “creeping toward the 30s” requires also naming what would be needed for the analogy to actually fire: military insubordination, extrajudicial political violence, an actual press crackdown. None of these is in evidence.

The most underexplored angle is the geopolitical one. If the Trump-era US guarantee is genuinely weakening (which the Trump-Xi Taiwan comments suggest), then Japan’s choice is not “rearm and risk the 30s slide” vs. “stay pacifist and risk Chinese coercion” but rather “rearm with strong democratic checks” vs. “rearm with weak checks” — and the strategic environment forces the rearmament regardless. Harding gestures at this near the end but doesn’t quite commit. The strongest version of his argument would be: Japan has no choice about rearming; the choice is whether civil society and the courts succeed in domesticating the rearmament. That’s the right framing, and it would land harder if explicitly distinguished from the strawman “should Japan rearm at all” debate.

↑ Back to TOC

Lessons From Singapore: The Welfare Paradox

Eric Feigenbaum · 2026-05-24 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the long-running comparative-governance puzzle of why Singapore — which spends 18.4% of GDP on total government, less than the US’s 20% on social spending alone — achieves a 0.05% poverty rate against the US’s 10–12%, with effectively zero homelessness.

Feigenbaum opens with two quotes from Lee Kuan Yew, Singapore’s founding prime minister, that read as opposite political camps in the West (compassion for street poverty vs. attack on welfare-as-rioters-in-waiting) but were Lee’s simultaneous positions throughout his career. The piece is a tour of the Singaporean mechanisms that translate that dual conviction into policy: regulated cheap public transport (often under one Sing dollar), 80-bed open fan-cooled hospital wards that strip creature comforts to lower the bill, encouragement of family insurance-fund transfers, and — most importantly — a 90% homeownership rate produced by government-built affordable housing financed via mandated payroll-tax savings (the Central Provident Fund). The closest analogue to Western welfare is the Workfare Income Supplement, which “tops up” wages for low earners aged 30–60 (up to S$4,200/year for employees, S$3,267 for self-employed, S$4,900 for seniors 60+), with payments split between cash and CPF deposits used for healthcare, retirement, and housing down payments. Feigenbaum’s framing: Singapore’s model is to lower the cost of necessities and require participation, rather than redistribute cash and accept dependency. He quotes Lee’s own theory — Singapore subordinates individual to societal interest while keeping markets competitive, and provides equal educational opportunity so children of poor families can rise — and closes by noting that no American president of either party has actually built a workfare program at this scale despite decades of speeches.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Feigenbaum is making a comparative-governance argument: Singapore’s CPF-plus-workfare model achieves redistributive outcomes through input subsidies and forced savings rather than cash transfers, and the West should pay more attention. The argument is genuinely informative on the descriptive layer — the institutional mechanics of CPF (Central Provident Fund), the open-ward hospital regulation, the workfare income supplement structure — and these are details an American or European reader genuinely won’t know in detail. As a primer on how Singapore actually works, the piece is competent.

The structural problem is the inferential leap. Feigenbaum treats Singapore’s poverty and homelessness statistics as evidence the model works, then implicitly invites the West to copy it. But the comparison controls almost nothing. Singapore is a city-state of 5.9 million with no rural hinterland, no internal migration, and a population it can effectively select via immigration policy (the state’s tolerance for “no homelessness” is sustained partly by deporting failed guest workers, who are not counted in the denominator). It runs a 90% homeownership rate on a public-housing system (HDB) built on land the state owns via the Land Acquisition Act of 1966, which let the government compulsorily purchase land at below-market rates — a policy lever the United States lacks at any plausible political price. The CPF works partly because Singapore has had near-continuous GDP growth since 1965 and almost no entitlement legacy to refinance; a forced-savings system imposed on the existing US workforce would face a transition problem (today’s workers must simultaneously fund their own CPF accounts and pay for current retirees) that wrecked the Bush 2005 partial-privatization proposal. None of this is in Feigenbaum’s piece.

The Lee Kuan Yew quote that closes the piece — about how “the human being is motivated by instincts that go deep down into the basic genes of life” — should also have been engaged more critically. Lee was working with a sociobiological frame (and a specifically anti-welfare-dependency theory of incentives) that contemporary economists of poverty would not endorse without significant caveats. Reading off the Singaporean poverty rate as confirmation of Lee’s instinct-driven theory is exactly the kind of post-hoc reasoning the empirical poverty literature has spent thirty years dismantling.

The piece would be stronger if it engaged the scope-conditions question directly: which features of the Singaporean model are transferable, which depend on city-state scale and land-ownership control, which depend on developmental-state path-dependence, and which depend on political authoritarianism (Lee’s People’s Action Party has governed continuously since 1959, with limited tolerance for opposition organizing). Workfare-style wage subsidies and EITC-like expansions are the answer to “what’s transferable”; the housing model is the answer to “what isn’t, without restructuring land tenure.” A Western reader needs that triage. Without it, “Singapore does it better, why don’t we?” is a frame more political than analytical — and in a US context it tends to land on the right because it implicitly attacks cash transfers while not requiring the right to also endorse the Singaporean state’s level of intervention in housing, labor, and family savings.

↑ Back to TOC

The Rise of Build-to-Rent Housing

Brian Potter · 2026-05-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Senate’s 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, whose section 901 would require institutional investors (owners of 350+ single-family homes) to sell their build-to-rent (BTR) inventory to individual homeowners after seven years — a provision that has halted new BTR project funding pending the bill’s passage and drawn coordinated opposition from 100+ pro-housing groups including Berkeley’s Terner Center, NAHB, and Potter’s own Institute for Progress.

Potter (a structural engineer and the most empirically rigorous of the roster’s housing writers) traces BTR’s growth from sub-2% of new housing starts in the 1990s to 7%+ today — at least 68,000 single-family starts built specifically to rent in 2025, possibly 100,000+. He walks the institutional origins: post-2008 foreclosure crisis dropped the homeownership rate from 69% to 63%, banks tightened credit (sub-620 credit-score mortgages went from 7% to ~0% of originations from 2003-2011), creating both a pool of cheap distressed homes and a wave of new renters. Treehouse Group (founded 2010 in Phoenix, acquired by Blackstone in 2012 and turned into Invitation Homes, now 86,000 homes across 12 states), American Homes 4 Rent (founded 2012 by Public Storage’s Wayne Hughes, now 61,000 homes across 24 states), and Tricon all built portfolios buying distressed properties; the FHFA’s 2012 REO-to-Rental pilot and Fannie Mae’s 2017 billion-dollar Invitation Homes loan provided federal scaffolding. By 2012-2015 a handful of Phoenix-based companies (NexMetro, BB Living, then AHV) pivoted from acquiring to building rental homes directly, drawn by lower maintenance, design optimization, and easier management of clustered properties. Today the four largest home-rental companies own 200,000+ homes, BTR has reached ~350,000 units (~1.5% of the single-family rental market), and major homebuilders (Lennar’s Quarterra, DR Horton, Taylor Morrison’s Yardly) have entered the segment. Potter breaks BTR into three sub-types — detached single-family (Tricon’s Palomino Ranch), single-family attached (BB Living’s townhomes), and “horizontal multifamily” (NexMetro’s Avilla Homes, essentially apartment complexes spread out into low-rise rental homes that rent for 15-20% more per square foot than comparable apartments).

On the demand side, Potter identifies post-2012 home-price appreciation outrunning income, post-COVID interest-rate hikes, COVID-driven demand for more living space due to work-from-home, and a structural pool of renters who can’t qualify for mortgages (Amherst Group reports 85% of its renters wouldn’t qualify due to low credit and incomes) — plus a “renters by choice” segment of retirees and others who don’t want home maintenance. On the institutional-ownership controversy (banning institutional investors is now a bipartisan cause — Trump’s January executive order; Warren’s framing), Potter walks the academic literature: most studies find institutional buy-to-rent investors raise house prices (Lambie-Hanson 2019 attributes more than half of 2006-2014 price appreciation to them), but the rent effects are more contested — Lee/Wylie and Gurun find investors leverage market power to raise rents on entry, while Coven, Wang/Zhai, and Barbieri/Dobbels find that added rental supply dominates the market-power effect and produces net consumer surplus. Crucially, Potter notes all this literature studies buy-to-rent (which reallocates existing supply), not build-to-rent (which creates new supply); rigorous evidence on BTR’s specific market effects is thin. His conclusion frames BTR as a product of housing dysfunction rather than its cause — shutting it down is a poor response to underlying supply constraints, and the policy lever should be making it cheaper and easier to build, not banning a category of builder.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

Potter is operating at his usual register here — empirically grounded, generous with primary-source citation, careful to distinguish what the evidence does and does not show. The piece’s most valuable analytical contribution is the explicit distinction between buy-to-rent and build-to-rent, which the political debate (including section 901) collapses. The literature he walks through is genuinely contested on rents — Hanson 2024 disputes the price-causation finding and the most recent papers (Coven 2025, Wang/Zhai 2026, Barbieri/Dobbels 2026) systematically find that supply effects dominate market-power effects — and Potter’s willingness to surface the Coven-Wang-Barbieri converging finding rather than picking a side is exactly the kind of move that makes Construction Physics worth reading.

Where the piece could be sharper is on the political economy of why section 901 made it into the bill in the first place. Potter notes the bipartisan coalition against institutional ownership — Trump’s executive order and Warren’s framing — but doesn’t really engage with what the political coalition is for. The “ban institutional landlords” frame is electorally potent (Stoller’s piece this week, also in the roundup, makes the structural case for why) because it offers a visible villain for housing dysfunction in a way that “abolish single-family zoning and reform building codes” does not. Potter’s policy recommendation — fix the underlying supply constraints — is correct on the merits but politically inert. A stronger version of the piece would have engaged the question: given that voters will not, in the foreseeable future, vote to abolish zoning, what is the case for or against using anti-institutional-investor rules as a second-best lever? Section 901 might still be bad policy, but the piece doesn’t quite engage the second-best argument; it just observes that supply expansion would be better.

A secondary tension: Potter’s pro-BTR conclusion rests partly on the “more housing options is good” framing, but the empirical case for BTR’s social value is meaningfully weaker than the case for new for-sale construction or new apartment construction. The CBRE 350,000-unit estimate is 1.5% of single-family rentals, and Potter’s own data suggest BTR has been driven less by a structural shift in demand than by an unaffordable for-sale market plus federal subsidy (REO-to-Rental, the Invitation Homes Fannie Mae loan) — which is to say, by housing dysfunction. If for-sale housing became affordable again, would BTR demand collapse? Potter’s case for keeping BTR as a long-run feature of the market rests on the “renters by choice” segment (retirees, the maintenance-averse), which the piece concedes is a fraction of demand. The honest version of his argument might be: section 901 is the wrong tool now because BTR-built supply is real new supply at the margin, but BTR’s long-run social value depends on whether for-sale housing affordability is fixed — and if it is, BTR will shrink naturally. That’s a more defensible version of the pro-BTR case than the “more options is good” framing the piece reaches for.

A pairing note: Potter’s piece sits in a small but coherent housing cluster this week (Commonplace’s “high home values, low housing prices” piece is the analytical sibling) and should be read alongside Stoller’s “Wires to Nowhere” utility piece as a parallel case study in regulatory capture — different sector, similar structure (a federally-blessed cartel arrangement where rate-setting mechanisms produce systematic over-investment in profitable projects and under-investment in socially valuable ones). Potter doesn’t draw that parallel explicitly but the structural similarity is striking.

↑ Back to TOC

Disney erased FiveThirtyEight

Nate Silver · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Disney/ABC’s quiet decision in May 2026 to auto-redirect the FiveThirtyEight archive — text articles from Silver’s near-decade tenure under ESPN/Disney/ABC — to the generic ABC News home page, completing the erasure of a site Disney had formally shut down in March 2025. Silver narrates the history from his own vantage point: founding FiveThirtyEight.com in 2008 under the pseudonym “poblano” as a Daily Kos spin-off (funded by his prior online-poker income, ended by the UIGEA), partnering with the New York Times 2010-2013, signing with ESPN in 2013 over rival offers from NBC, Bloomberg, and the Times (a choice he now calls “I chose poorly” given ESPN/NBC’s TV-rather-than-subscription business models), the 2014 ESPN relaunch he characterizes as a “disaster,” the 2017 attempted exit to The Athletic that died on a Disney deadline when ABC News emerged as an internal bidder, and the long lame-duck period under ABC until his 2023 contract expired and ABC News later shipped a “literally broken” replacement election model under G. Elliott Morris. The structural argument: Disney never invested in FiveThirtyEight even while spending on it; senior staff begged for a paywall around 2018-2019 and were told Disney couldn’t be bothered with the operational mechanics during the Hulu acquisition; Silver estimates a paywall would have produced “comfortably in the seven figures annually,” call it $5M — a sum trivially uneconomic to a company with $69B in 2019 revenue, which is precisely the problem. The closer: when Silver approached Disney through his agent about acquiring the remaining IP to restore the archive, he was rebuffed; Disney sent him a Mickey Mouse plaque after 10 years that misspelled his first name as “Nataniel.” The piece’s fundamental move is to convert Silver’s private grievance into a business-school case study of large-corporation neglect toward acquired niche brands — Disney as the structural type of company that “only understands things that operate at very large scales to truly mass-market audiences.” Silver names the underlying frame: “It would be nice if that work could be preserved for the public record.”

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Silver in a register the project hasn’t seen from him before — he is the protagonist of the piece, the wronged party, and the analyst all at once. The first analytical question is whether his account is credible. On balance it is: the structural claims (Disney’s failure to monetize FiveThirtyEight via paywall, the 2017 Athletic deal that died on a Disney deadline, the broken 2024 election model under his replacement, the eventual full shutdown) are externally documented through linked NYT, Semafor, and contemporaneous reporting. The internal claims (begging for a paywall and being refused, the Hulu-acquisition excuse, the lack of dedicated business staff) are uncorroborated but consistent in pattern with broader documented Disney editorial-property handling (Grantland, the various ESPN-non-sports experiments). Silver’s restraint is notable — he explicitly declines to talk about “competing editorial visions or personality clashes,” doesn’t extend the Morris feud beyond what’s necessary, and concedes his own mistakes (the 2014 launch problems, the staff over-hiring, the 2016 Trump-dismissal pre-general). This is the calibrated honesty signature he’s tried to brand around for fifteen years.

The structural argument — that Disney is the wrong owner for any property that operates at a scale below the conglomerate’s “rounding error” threshold — has implications beyond FiveThirtyEight. Silver is implicitly making a case that the conglomerate-media-owns-everything model that produced ESPN-Grantland, ABC-FiveThirtyEight, etc. cannot retain talent or build subscription businesses at the niche-but-substantial scale ($5M-50M ARR) that Substack-era independent operators can capture for themselves. This is the structural argument that justifies Substack’s existence as an ecosystem; it’s also the reason that Silver, Bari Weiss (whose Free Press recently sold for $150M, a figure Silver cites as a benchmark), and others have left institutional media for independent platforms. The deeper structural claim is that the “premium content for a large but not mass audience” market segment is fundamentally underserved by conglomerates and is being structurally captured by Substack-format independents. The implication for the discourse: institutional media’s content-archive is being progressively erased while the independent-newsletter content-archive is being preserved by the platform layer (Substack, third-party Internet Archive captures, etc.). This is a real and underdiscussed change in the topology of public-record memory.

The brand-erasure argument deserves engagement on its own terms. Silver’s frame — “200,000 person-hours of work that ABC News just deleted” — is rhetorically strong but elides that the archive isn’t actually deleted (Internet Archive captures persist, late-stage abcnews.com URLs survive), it’s just delisted from the canonical address space. The harm is to discoverability and to the canonical-pointer status that gave FiveThirtyEight content its citation weight. This is a smaller harm than literal deletion but a real one: most casual link-followers won’t find Internet Archive copies, and even academic researchers face the broken-link problem.

What Silver doesn’t engage with is the Hanlon’s-razor question he raises and then ducks. The Disney redirect could be malice (annoyance at his public criticism); it could be stupidity (no one bothered to maintain the redirect rules); it could be a contractual issue (ABC may be liability-concerned about old articles that contain claims Disney’s lawyers don’t want to defend). Silver flags Hanlon’s razor and then writes “I don’t know which explanation is better suited.” The honest answer is probably that the malice and the stupidity coexist — the people who would have prevented the silent redirect were laid off in earlier cuts. This pairs with the broader structural-media theme several roster writers have been hitting: the institutional layer of the media has lost the staffing density required to maintain legacy editorial properties, which compounds the talent-flight to independent platforms that produces the staffing-density loss in the first place. As contribution to the synthesis, this is the most synthesis-relevant beat in the piece — it bears on the same media-fragmentation argument that, e.g., Maitra-on-Substack-as-distribution-substrate and the various essays on legacy-media decline have been making this season.

↑ Back to TOC

Is Trump trying to turn Texas blue?

Eli McKown-Dawson · 2026-05-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the May 26 Texas Senate GOP runoff between AG Ken Paxton and incumbent John Cornyn, the question of whether the general-election seat is now genuinely in play for Democratic nominee James Talarico (a Presbyterian seminarian and state House member), and the broader pattern of Trump’s recent primary-intervention “wins” (Cassidy in Louisiana, Massie in Kentucky, Indiana state senators on mid-decade redistricting). McKown-Dawson argues this Texas episode is the inverse of a political-operation win. Trump’s initial co-endorsement of all three Republican primary candidates defeated the informational purpose of an endorsement; the late single-candidate endorsement of Paxton came in the final week of the runoff after letting both campaigns burn over $20M in primary ad spend that could have gone to Maine, Iowa, Florida, or other genuinely contested Republican-defended seats; and the choice of Paxton (in a Herschel Walker-2022 parallel) selects the loyal-but-scandal-plagued candidate over the safer “establishment” alternative at a moment of Trump’s worst approval (-19.3 net, the worst of any president at this point in a nonconsecutive term). The piece quantifies the candidate-quality effect: Silver Bulletin’s modeled state partisanship would put a generic Republican at R+4 in Texas given the current ~D+7 generic ballot, but scandal-affected candidates underperform by roughly 5 points historically, which moves Paxton-Talarico to genuine toss-up (matching prediction-market consensus of ~50%). The author notes counterweights: Texas has not elected a Democratic senator since Lloyd Bentsen in 1988, Talarico’s highest office is the state House (the lowest tier of Silver Bulletin’s three-rung candidate-experience scale), his “God is nonbinary” 2021 comment offers Republican attack surface, and an Obama-2008-style information-flow asymmetry could favor Paxton once he pivots from intra-party bashing to general-election campaigning. The piece’s fundamental move is to argue that a Senate seat that “should” be R+4 has been moved to a coin flip by an avoidable strategic sequence — Trump’s late, ill-timed endorsement of the inferior general-election candidate — and that even if Paxton wins, the resource-misallocation cost across the broader Senate map is the real story.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

McKown-Dawson is operating in standard Silver Bulletin mode here — data-anchored political analysis with explicit base-rate framing, modeled toplines, and explicit acknowledgment of where the model’s uncertainty lies. The argument has clean structure: it disaggregates the question into (1) the general-election competitiveness given candidate identity, (2) the resource-allocation cost of Trump’s late endorsement, and (3) the asymmetric information-flow effect that could rescue Paxton once the Cornyn campaign winds down. The 5-point scandal-effect estimate is the load-bearing empirical claim; if it’s roughly right, the piece’s conclusion follows.

The data interpretation seems calibrated. The author explicitly flags that early-cycle polls deserve discount relative to fundamentals (the modeled R+4 baseline), that polls during contested primaries are noisy because of one-sided information flow, that prediction markets’ ~50% Paxton-Talarico line is “probably working off reasonable assumptions” rather than mispricing, and that Talarico’s primary-completion bump may revert toward a tighter R+1 baseline as the information flow normalizes. The framing of Texas as “the proverbial case of a rock meeting a hard place” — strong Democratic generic-ballot environment colliding with deep structural Republican fundamentals — is honest about the model’s tension rather than collapsing to one side.

The argument has two genuine weaknesses worth flagging. First, the Texas-suburban-shift / Hispanic-shift mechanism (the “Beto map on steroids” Talarico would need) is described but not really argued: the structural question of whether 2026 looks more like 2018 (high Trump backlash, partial Beto-style coalition) or more like 2024 (continued Hispanic shift toward Republicans even in a Trump-fatigue environment) is exactly what would resolve the toss-up, and the piece doesn’t engage with which direction the recent Texas data points. Second, the “Trump endorsed badly” frame, while empirically defensible, treats Trump’s endorsement decisions as if they were ordinary strategic optimization problems with knowable correct answers. An alternative reading is that Trump’s loyalty-tax / Cornyn-cannot-be-allowed-to-survive-as-an-incumbent goal-function might mean the endorsement was correct from his vantage even if costly to the GOP Senate caucus. The piece nods at this with the Pelosi-Freaky-Friday rhetorical move but doesn’t develop it; the deeper structural argument here is that Trump’s incentive structure is increasingly orthogonal to the Republican Party’s collective interest, which is a frame the project has tracked in other roster pieces.

The piece pairs naturally with: any Klein/Drutman-on-gerrymandering content this week (the Texas-Senate angle and the redistricting wave are part of the same midterm-map story); any DNC-autopsy pieces (the Talarico-as-Democratic-recruit theme); and the broader cluster of roster writers arguing that Trump’s electoral instincts have peaked and his late-second-term political operation is degrading in capability. As contribution to the synthesis, McKown-Dawson’s clean numerical framing is useful as a calibration anchor: when other writers gesture vaguely at “Trump’s standing is bad” or “the GOP is in trouble,” this piece provides specific magnitudes (the -19.3 approval floor, the R+4 vs. toss-up swing on candidate quality, the $20M+ runoff ad burn that could have funded other races).

↑ Back to TOC

Nithya Raman Was Supposed to Be LA’s Mamdani. Then Spencer Pratt Showed Up.

Peter Savodnik · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the LA mayoral jungle primary (June 2, 2026), where Emerson polling has Mayor Karen Bass at 30%, former reality-TV figure Spencer Pratt (a registered Republican) at 22%, and progressive city councilmember Nithya Raman at 19% — with mail-in voting already underway. Savodnik builds the piece around Raman’s hour-long interview with leftist streamer Hasan Piker, in which she affirmed (cautiously) that Israel had committed “a genocide” and was “an apartheid state” while adding she believed Israel had a right to exist. The interview becomes the frame for Savodnik’s larger argument that Raman is a technocratic good-government liberal — MIT urban-planning credentials, “old-fashioned bleeding heart,” authentic but practiced — who entered the race expecting to play Mamdani to Bass’s Cuomo and has been blindsided by Pratt’s populist appropriation of the anti-establishment lane. Raman’s policy answers (unarmed homelessness “ambassadors” plus some LAPD presence) are quoted directly and unflatteringly, with an LA Assistant DA’s blistering response printed verbatim (“that will last until some good-natured female ‘ambassador’ gets raped and stabbed 50 times… it’s a zombie show”). West-side Jewish voters who would have written off Bass after the Palisades-fire response are now drifting back to her or contemplating Pratt; the LA DSA, which once propelled Raman, declined to endorse her this cycle (over Israel, per her own supporters). The deeper diagnosis: Raman keeps responding to Pratt with policy attacks and MAGA labels in a moment when voters are “looking for a mayor who could… look around at the sprawling mess that Los Angeles has become and say enough’s enough,” and Pratt — like Trump in 2016 — wraps himself in the accusations and laughs. Raman’s October 7 condemnation of Hamas and subsequent insistence on naming antisemitism alongside Islamophobia is the through-line: she was politically naive enough to do the human thing on 10/7, and the post-10/7 DSA radicalization left her without a base she’d built on.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is a well-reported FP piece that does two things at once and the seam shows. As LA political reporting, it’s strong — Savodnik did the legwork (Olvera Street, the small-business meeting, the DA quote, the west-side fundraiser dialogue, the polling number, the DSA’s non-endorsement explanation), and the piece is informationally dense in the way that good local reporting is. The Raman-as-blindsided-by-Pratt frame is plausible and supported by the on-record material; the policy excerpts (especially the homelessness-ambassador answer) are damning in the specific way only direct quotation can be.

But the piece is also doing analytic work it doesn’t fully earn. The implicit thesis is that Raman represents the failure mode of a particular kind of progressive politics — the credentialed-technocrat liberalism that has policy answers but no affective register for civilizational decay — and that Pratt’s rise is the diagnostic. That thesis is interesting and probably partly right, but it’s adjacent to the FP house view in a way that should make the reader check the framing. Notably, Savodnik never asks whether Pratt’s polling (22%) is durable or whether jungle-primary dynamics will translate to a general-election win against Bass; the piece treats Pratt’s surge as a settled fact about LA’s mood when it might be a momentary novelty effect that 30-day polling can’t distinguish. The Schnur quote (“imagine if Joe Rogan had run in the New York City mayor’s race”) is doing the analytic work the body of the piece doesn’t.

The Israel material is the most rhetorically loaded section. Savodnik’s parenthetical — “(does a ‘genocidal’ regime really have a right to exist?)” — is a fair shot at the incoherence of Raman’s Piker-interview position, but the framing of Piker as “Chinese Communist Party–apologist” and the catalog of his prior offenses is doing work that crowds out the harder question of what a credible progressive position on Israel-Gaza actually looks like at this moment in LA. The piece is more interested in the political costs to Raman than in the substantive question. That’s a defensible choice for a local political dispatch but the reader should notice that Savodnik is not asking it.

What’s distinctive about this piece for the roundup: it’s the only place this week where a non-NY mayoral race is being read as a stress test on the “Mamdani model” — the question of whether the Mamdani-style insurgent-against-the-machine framing generalizes outside NYC’s particular electorate. Savodnik’s implicit answer (no, because Mamdani’s economic populism was the substance and Raman’s identitarian-technocratic blend doesn’t deliver the same affect) is a real claim worth tracking. Pair in the synthesis with whatever Mamdani-direct coverage shows up this week — the cross-coastal comparison is the interesting question, not Pratt-as-novelty.

↑ Back to TOC

America is heading for a debtpocalypse

Noahpinion · 2026-05-23 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the US fiscal trajectory under both Biden and Trump, recent rises in long-term Treasury yields, Jamie Dimon’s April warning of a coming bond-market crisis, and the political question of whether AI growth or Fed action can avert a debt reckoning. Smith argues we are now in the self-reinforcing debt-service phase: the average maturity of US debt is under 6 years, interest rates started rising in 2022, and the rollover is now hitting at materially higher rates than the debt was originally issued at; debt is back above 100% of GDP (similar to post-WW2) but at nearly twice the post-WW2 interest rate (1.5-2% then vs. ~4-5% now). Trump has roughly matched Biden’s deficit-to-GDP ratio but conditions are worse because new debt is rolling at higher rates without inflation-erosion. Fed rate cuts are blocked: headline inflation has spiked (Iran-war oil-price feed-through), inflation expectations risk becoming self-fulfilling, Sargent’s hyperinflation literature shows the path runs through a captive central bank, and forcing the Fed could trigger any of three failure modes — inflation breakout, long-term-rate blowout (Brazil pattern), or currency crisis with loss of reserve-currency exorbitant privilege (Choi et al. 2024 estimate this provides ~30% additional debt capacity). AI-growth-as-rescue fails on Chow et al. 2025 grounds: higher long-run-growth expectations push long-term real rates up, not down. Japan-comparison fails because Japan ran its government like a hedge fund (currency-trade and equity profits offset debt-service) and ultimately had to face the music post-pandemic as the BoJ raised rates, private investors pulled out, and the yen fell. The proposed remedy: revive the bipartisan deficit-fear of the early 1990s with two competing party-tailored plans (heavier on spending cuts for Republicans, heavier on tax hikes for Democrats), citing the work of Marc Goldwein (CRFB) and Jessica Riedl (Manhattan Institute) on compromise frameworks. The piece’s fundamental move is to stake out a position that the debt arithmetic has crossed into self-reinforcing territory and that both parties’ “a little more deficit-splurging is fine” stance is now actively dangerous.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Smith deploying his pro-economic-orthodoxy register against both parties at once — the kind of piece that establishes his “not a partisan” credibility by criticizing his own coalition’s preferences (Democrats’ tax cuts for upper-middle-class voters, the MMT camp’s “fiscal arsonists” framing). The core mechanism — that the rollover of cheap-era debt at expensive-era rates produces a compounding interest-service problem when combined with primary deficits — is sound macroeconomics and consistent with what Matt Klein, Brad Setser, and other bond-market observers have been writing. The Brazil parallel is genuinely concerning and underdiscussed in US fiscal commentary; emerging-market debt-spiral dynamics are usually treated as inapplicable to reserve-currency issuers, but the Choi et al. (2024) finding that reserve-currency status provides only ~30% extra debt capacity is exactly the kind of bound that bites when capacity is already strained.

The argument’s weak points are the political-strategy section and the “scaring people” theory of change. The 1992 analogy is the obvious reference, but the contextual differences matter: in 1992, Ross Perot operated as a third-party deficit hawk who externalized the issue from intra-party politics, and bond markets were actually moving against Bush in real time (the bond-market vigilantes were real). Today there is no Perot-equivalent, no third-party pressure-relief valve, and the partisan-coalition incentives run in the opposite direction. Smith’s prescription that the parties produce two competing deficit-reduction plans and negotiate a compromise is the kind of pre-2010 institutional-process imaginary that the post-2010 Republican party (and increasingly the post-2020 Democratic party) has shown no appetite for. Smith doesn’t engage with whether the political bind itself is the binding constraint — i.e., whether the problem is technical-economic (we don’t know what to do) or political-coalitional (we know what to do but neither party can bear the electoral cost of doing it).

The AI-growth-as-rescue argument is the most interesting analytical move in the piece and the one most likely to be contested. Smith cites Chow et al. (2025) showing that higher long-term growth expectations correlate with higher long-term real rates, and concludes AI growth makes the debt situation worse rather than better. This is a sharp claim that runs against the prevailing “AI will outgrow the debt problem” intuition shared by both AI-optimist VC commentary and some Treasury voices. The mechanism (consumption-smoothing pushes real rates up as future growth becomes more expected) is theoretically standard but the empirical magnitude is the question; Smith doesn’t engage with the counter-argument that AI-driven productivity growth, if it shows up in measured TFP, could lower nominal rates via lower inflation even as real rates rise. This is the most synthesis-worthy beat in the piece because it cuts against the soft optimism several other roster writers (including Smith himself in adjacent pieces) hold about the AI-productivity-growth dividend.

The piece pairs naturally in the synthesis with Tooze on debt dynamics, Aurelien on the broader collapse of Western financial hegemony, and possibly Dalio if a Dalio piece surfaces in this window. As a free-standing argument, it’s a useful corrective to roster pieces that treat the debt arithmetic as a chronic-but-manageable condition rather than as a phase-transition risk.

↑ Back to TOC

Is space warfare offense-dominant or defense-dominant?

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A 2025 long-form essay at Beren’s Blog (theoretical-AI and rationalist-adjacent writer Beren Millidge) on the offense-defense balance in interstellar warfare between Kardashev Type 3 civilizations.

The excerpted argument: invasion ships (the standard sci-fi trope) are strictly dominated by relativistic kinetic kill vehicles (RKVs) — kinetic-weapon projectiles accelerated to a substantial fraction of light speed and aimed at a target system. Two reasons. First, the “tyranny of the rocket equation”: invasion ships must carry their own braking fuel for deceleration at the target (no infrastructure to assume there), and that fuel must itself be accelerated, compounding the energy cost. Second, deceleration is unhidable — the exhaust plume points at the target during the braking burn, making a thousands-of-years-long beacon screaming “I am here, come kill me.” Even magnetic-sail deceleration radiates friction heat omnidirectionally. The structural conclusion (Cowen lets the excerpt carry it without further comment): interstellar space warfare is strongly offense-dominant for one-shot kinetic strikes and effectively defense-impossible for any attempt at occupation.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is a Cowen link-post with one of his lighter framings — he picks an interesting essay, excerpts the core mechanical argument, and lets the reader follow the link. The physics is correct: the rocket equation (Tsiolkovsky’s logarithmic relation between fuel mass, exhaust velocity, and achievable delta-v) really does make round-trip relativistic missions essentially impossible without infrastructure at both ends, and the unhideability of a high-thrust deceleration burn is a well-known result in the Fermi-paradox-and-game-theory literature (it’s roughly the “dark forest” intuition Liu Cixin made famous).

The implicit Cowen point is the one worth pulling out: offense-defense balance is a structural rather than a technological variable, and when it’s heavily offense-dominant, the equilibrium is either (a) preemptive first-strike or (b) absolute silence (the dark-forest equilibrium). This is the same logic Cowen uses in earthbound contexts when he writes about nuclear deterrence or AI-arms-race dynamics — the question is always whether the defense can be made to survive long enough for retaliation to be credible.
