The implication that goes unsaid: if the interstellar analog runs offense-dominant, then by the same structural logic some terrestrial domains (cyber, drone swarms, hypersonic missile saturation) may be moving the same direction, and the implications for great-power competition are not friendly to defensive postures. This isn’t quite Straussian — the excerpt is what it says it is, a piece on space warfare — but the choice to surface it the week of Iran/Israel/space-asset commentary is consistent with Cowen’s ongoing pattern of using sci-fi-coded posts to flag structural-strategic worries he isn’t ready to argue directly.

↑ Back to TOC

The Types Of Candidate You Find In The California Gubernatorial Race

Scott Alexander · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: California’s open 2026 gubernatorial primary, in which 60 candidates have filed — too many for Alexander’s traditional one-by-one coverage from prior cycles. Instead of profiling each candidate, he sorts the field into a typology: Top-Tier Democrats (one will win, otherwise indistinguishable), Top-Tier Republicans (cowboy-hat-and-flag-pin pose, will lose), Conflict Theorists (small businesspeople running on “make fraud illegal, make the business climate good”), Mistake Theorists (the clever idea from the shower last night), Media Getters (candidates buying fake magazine cover features and inventing AI-generated polls placing themselves ahead), This-Time-It’s-Personal (vague platform plus one extraordinarily specific issue revealing the family-court case driving them), AI Natives (one candidate’s policy is openly written by a chatbot, converging on neoliberal-YIMBY positions Alexander prefers to most rivals), Nominative Determinists (people who change their name to “LovesAmerica Smith” and lose, though Alexander notes the “John Smith over Mohammed Kryzscyzbski” variant works at sub-municipal level), College Students (a UCLA senior’s protest-resume website on one side, an American Solidarity Party Catholic-philosopher candidate on the other), Anti-Semites (whose ballot statement Alexander uses as a springboard for a 5-paragraph close reading of Gittin 57a and the Talmud’s treatment of “Yeshu the Nazarene”), On A Mission From God (Brent Maupin’s tent-revival vision and 0-percent God-endorsement track record in CA), Entrepreneurs (the Undercover Billionaire alumna and Gary Kidgell selling $1,000 copies of his platform), The Ones Who Are Just Having Fun (Sam Sandak’s single-issue pro-film campaign), and the unclassifiable. The piece’s fundamental move is to treat the long-tail of vanity gubernatorial candidacies as a folk-taxonomy worth observing rather than dismissing — making the argument anthropologically that the bottom of the candidate distribution is where the most genuine variation lives.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is Alexander in entertainer mode — closer to his “Acquittal Roundup” and earlier candidate-tour pieces than to his analytical posts. There’s no central thesis to evaluate, just observation, and the observation is genuinely funny and structurally interesting. The piece’s argumentative move (insofar as it has one) is implicit: the lower-tier candidate field is a richer empirical sample of political-psychology types than the top-tier field, where talent-pool selection compresses everyone into the same archetype. The “Top-Tier Democrats” and “Top-Tier Republicans” sections — both essentially one-line dismissals — are doing work, gesturing at the same compression-into-archetype point that Yglesias, deBoer, and others have made from different angles about contemporary professional-class candidate selection.

The Talmud digression in the Anti-Semites section is the strangest and most characteristic Alexander move — turning a candidate’s antisemitic conspiracy ballot statement into a five-paragraph close reading of Gittin 57a, the Onkelos-necromancy story, and the question of whether “Yeshu the Nazarene” is actually meant to be Jesus of Nazareth. This is the genre signature: a willingness to follow the rabbit-hole regardless of where it leads, treating the source-text on its own terms even when the proximate occasion is contemptible. Reasonable readers will disagree about whether this is principled engagement or whimsical aesthetic; the project has tracked this same tic in his “Acquittal” and “Cardiologists” pieces.

The “AI Natives” section is the most substantively interesting beat in the piece — Alexander’s comment that Gary Kidgell’s AI-written platform “converges on the same neoliberal YIMBY technocratic positions” as the major AIs, and his statement that he’d prefer to be governed by Kidgell (conditional on continued AI consultation) than by most of the human field. This is a subtle policy claim disguised as a joke. It rhymes with the running ACT thread that AIs trained on broad human discourse arrive at recognizably reasonable centrist positions, and that the human filter (selection effects in who-runs-for-office, who-becomes-staffer) actively degrades that reasonableness. It’s worth flagging as a real argument under the comic veneer; in a different ACT post it would be the central thesis.

The piece is best read as periodically useful relief and as a slice-of-life record of what the California ballot actually looks like at the bottom of the distribution. As contribution to the roundup’s working themes, it’s lightweight — perhaps usable in the synthesis only as a counterpoint or anchor for the “candidate-selection-as-elite-compression” thread if one of the other writers this week is making that case more substantively.

↑ Back to TOC

One More Time: The Average American K-12 Student is Doing Fine Relative to the International Baseline

Freddie deBoer · 2026-05-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the bipartisan crisis-narrative in mainstream education commentary (he names Matt Yglesias, Jon Chait, the American Enterprise Institute, The Atlantic, the NYT Upshot, and “Chartbeat guy” as recurring offenders), and to commenters on his own prior post who insist the system fails their gifted kids. deBoer pulls the 2022 PISA (Program for International Student Assessment) data to show US students scored 504 in reading (higher than 68 of 80 systems, beaten meaningfully by only Ireland, Japan, Korea, Estonia, Singapore), 499 in science (higher than 56), and 465 in math (still beating 43 of 80). Top-end US performance is stronger still: 14% of US students scored at PISA Level 5+ in reading vs. 7% OECD average, and Team USA has medaled in the International Math/Physics/Chemistry/Biology/Informatics/Linguistics Olympiads at world-leading rates over the past decade. Post-COVID test-score declines, deBoer argues, are a worldwide phenomenon flagged as “unprecedented” by the OECD itself — US rankings have actually risen since 2018 (29th to 26th in math, 8th to 6th in reading) because peer nations fell further. The genuine crisis is concentrated in a small number of high-poverty districts — Detroit (66% chronic absenteeism in 2023-24), Cleveland (only 8% of fourth-graders proficient in reading in 2024), Baltimore — which share extreme child poverty, segregation, and disinvestment rather than common pedagogy or union arrangements. The Gallup gap (parents grade their own kids’ schools much higher than “American schools” generally; the gap has held since 1985) deBoer reads as parents being correct based on direct experience, not as cognitive bias. Policy upshot: concentrated problems demand concentrated interventions (community investment, poverty reduction in the worst districts) rather than systemic reform like vouchers or mass teacher firings.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is one of deBoer’s strongest modes — empirically-anchored heterodoxy on education policy, which is a topic he’s spent fifteen years on and knows the data well. The PISA stats and Olympiad results are accurate as cited, and the international-comparison frame is the right one for the question he’s actually asking. The argument’s weight rests on three pillars and each holds: (1) the US ranks well in reading and science and not-as-badly-as-claimed in math; (2) recent declines are a global PISA pattern rather than a uniquely American failure; (3) within-US variance is enormous and concentrated, so national averages mislead.

The Detroit/Cleveland/Baltimore comparison is the most quietly powerful part of the piece. The implicit experiment — what would happen if Detroit students went to Bloomfield Hills schools, and vice versa — is the cleanest way to show that the variance in outcomes is driven by student-population characteristics (poverty, family stability, neighborhood) rather than school-level variables (curriculum, pedagogy, union contracts). The OECD bottom-decile stat (US bottom-SES math performance ranks 6th of 64 comparable nations) is genuinely surprising and load-bearing — it means even the kids we most poorly serve outperform the world’s most disadvantaged.

What’s weak: deBoer’s strong materialist read — that the problem is “concentrated poverty” full stop and the schools are essentially blameless — is a stronger version of his real claim. The real claim is “poverty explains the bulk of the variance and reformer-flavored interventions are a category error.” That’s well-supported. But the rhetorical move “blaming the schools is like blaming thermometers for global warming” overshoots: schools at the margin do vary in their effects, and the question of how much marginal-school-quality matters at a given level of student deprivation is separate from the question of whether systemic-reform narratives misdiagnose the problem. He’s running them together. A more conservative reader could grant the second and still want answers on the first.

Second weakness: the framing leans heavily on a tribal-enemies list (Yglesias, Chait, AEI, the Upshot) as a marker of bad-faith reasoning. Some of that is earned — the crisis narrative is indeed bipartisan and durable, and reformist coverage often is selective in the ways deBoer describes. But naming Yglesias and Chait as “propagandists” without engaging their specific arguments substitutes signaling for engagement. Yglesias in particular has written more carefully on PISA international comparisons than deBoer credits here, and the piece would be sharper if it engaged the strongest version of the reformist case (e.g., the within-district teacher-quality variance literature, or the charter-school RCT results in places like Boston and New Orleans) and showed why that case still misdiagnoses.

Third: the dismissal of the “they’re all Asian” commenter response (“number two… so what if they were? All of those kids are American citizens”) is rhetorically correct but ducks an interesting empirical point — if a disproportionate share of top-end US Olympiad performers come from immigrant families with selection-on-talent and intensive parental investment, that does say something specific about the US system (it’s good at amplifying motivated talent under any starting condition, including immigrant-second-generation), which would actually strengthen deBoer’s broader case but he chooses to wave it off as a gotcha rather than incorporate it.

Overall: a strong piece that materially advances the empirical claim against the standard education-crisis narrative. The case for concentrated rather than systemic intervention is well-made and is the kind of argument the project should propagate. The weakness is rhetorical rather than evidential — the schools-are-blameless overshoot and the named-enemy list make it easier for skeptics to dismiss what is actually a careful empirical argument.

↑ Back to TOC

You Can’t Defend a Policy By Getting Angry at the Suggestion That It’s Benefitted People

Freddie deBoer · 2026-05-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the DOJ’s recent Title VII (Civil Rights Act of 1964) lawsuits against Yale School of Medicine and the New York Times over hiring and admissions practices, and a 2024-election-cycle context in which “DEI candidate” was hurled at Kamala Harris; deBoer cites Andrew Sullivan’s framing that the NYT must now legally argue it “never once” used race or sex as a basis for hiring or promotion despite a 2021 corporate report bragging about exactly that shift. deBoer reconstructs a 2024 essay pitch — repeatedly rejected by editors — that took the “DEI candidate” accusation seriously: if Harris had benefited from diversity programs in her career, that would be an argument for such programs, evidence they work as intended. The pitch was unpublishable because progressive culture treats pointing to any individual beneficiary as offensive (it implies the person was “undeserving”). DeBoer frames this as a hall-of-mirrors: progressives spent years demanding institutions diversify (publishing houses, NYT, elite colleges), institutions complied and announced metrics, and then defenders react with rage when anyone notices the change happened or asks who lost the corresponding slots. The NYT’s 2021 self-report (48% of new hires people of color; people-of-color leadership up from 17% to 23%; women in leadership up from 40% to 52%) is the legal exhibit; the rhetorical posture that “nothing happened” is now the legal defense. DeBoer’s college-affirmative-action thought experiment makes the same point: students who support affirmative action will refuse to affirm “there are Black students here who wouldn’t have gotten in without it,” but if that’s not what affirmative action does, then it does nothing. He remains “supportive of race-based affirmative action in theory” but says trickle-down racial-justice politics (more BIPOC at Harvard / Goldman / the Senate) has clearly failed to improve life for the median Black American.

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is deBoer doubling down on a long-running pattern — anti-progressive-elite-consensus, defending working-class commonsense, contempt for editorial gatekeeping — but the specific argument is sharper than usual because it identifies a genuine logical contradiction rather than a stylistic one. The “you can’t have it both ways” frame names a real problem: a program defined as redistributive cannot be defended as having had no redistributive effects, because if it had no effects it didn’t run. That logical structure is correct and is what makes the Title VII suits hard to defend on the merits as currently posed. The NYT 2021 report excerpt is well-chosen — it’s a direct admission in the institutional voice that becomes legally inconvenient when the institutional voice is later asked to deny what was admitted.

The pitch-and-rejection anecdote is doing double duty as evidence: it’s both the example and the proof that the example is unpublishable. This is a standard deBoer rhetorical move (he positions himself as the lone honest voice rejected by cowardly editors) and the reader should be alert to it, but it’s not load-bearing for the central argument — even discounting the pitch story entirely, the NYT 2021/2026 contradiction stands on its own.

What’s weak: deBoer’s central frame assumes the progressive position must defend the programs as having had real selection effects. But there’s a coherent if uncomfortable middle position he doesn’t engage — that the programs primarily lowered barriers at the application/recruitment stage rather than at the selection stage, expanding the pool from which qualified candidates were drawn without necessarily displacing specific other candidates one-for-one. That position is harder to articulate than either deBoer’s strong “they obviously displaced people” or the institutional “we never used race or sex” position, but it’s the actual mechanism a careful defender would point to. By treating the question as binary (displacement vs. nothing happened) deBoer collapses the strongest version of the defense before engaging it. He’s right that most institutional defenders aren’t articulating the middle position — but his argument would be sharper if he conceded its possibility and then showed why the explicit hiring goals (48% of new hires) cannot be reconciled with a pure pool-expansion story.

Second weakness: the slide from “progressives can’t defend their programs coherently” to “trickle-down racial justice has clearly failed” is doing a lot of work in one sentence. Whether elite-tier diversity policies improve life for the median Black or Hispanic American is an interesting empirical question (and one the project’s roster — including Bruenig and Klein — engages from different angles), but deBoer asserts the answer rather than arguing it. The piece would be more disciplined if it stayed on the rhetorical-coherence problem and didn’t drift into the (separate and harder) empirical-efficacy question in the closing paragraphs.

Third: there’s no engagement with the strongest legal defense — that the Title VII text and the colorblind reading of it that the DOJ is now wielding is itself the product of a specific Supreme Court interpretation history (Bakke through SFFA) that progressives historically opposed precisely because it constrained the kind of explicit race-conscious remediation that 1964-era civil rights advocates had not anticipated being read to forbid. DeBoer notes that “colorblindness is now seen as tantamount to white supremacy among many liberals, but inconveniently it’s written into many of the most important civil rights victories” — which is a fair observation about progressive rhetorical drift but skates past the substantive jurisprudential debate about whether disparate-impact theory under Title VII actually does permit the kinds of programs the NYT ran.

Strong piece overall — the central logical claim is correct and identifies a real coalition-management problem on the left. The weaknesses are at the edges (the binary frame, the trickle-down claim, the missing engagement with the strongest defenses) rather than at the core. Worth flagging as a coordinate-cell argument the project should track — deBoer’s mode here is “Bernie-left writer notices the Civil Rights Act actually says what it says” which is a productive heterodoxy.

↑ Back to TOC

Ideas are still big but books got small

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-22 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Reader questions submitted to Slow Boring’s mailbag, including one (Tiger Lava Lamp) referencing an Argument podcast episode in which Jerusalem Demsas argued there are no “big idea” nonfiction books anymore on the Silent Spring / Feminine Mystique scale.

The lead segment argues that the “big idea book” hasn’t disappeared so much as the book has become incidental to the influence — podcasts and columns now do the work books once did, and even authors of influential books (Klein-and-Thompson’s Abundance; Yglesias’s own 2012 The Rent Is Too Damn High) get their influence through derivative media rather than the book itself. He also pushes back on the framing in two ways: the 1960s monoculture (CBS, Time/Newsweek) made Silent Spring-scale ubiquity possible in a way no current medium can replicate; and the causal story about Silent Spring itself driving the environmental movement is partly reconstructed ex post. A second substantial answer revisits One Billion Americans (his 2020 pronatalist book) and argues two things have changed: low interest rates can no longer be assumed to make family-subsidy spending fiscally easy, and John Burn-Murdoch’s recent data suggests the fertility decline is now mostly about couple-formation rather than married-couples-having-fewer-children — placing it inside Derek Thompson’s “anti-social century” pattern. The mailbag also includes: an argument that majority-minority districts are politically obsolete because parties have sorted on racial attitudes and Black politicians like Joe Neguse now routinely win majority-white seats; a defense of the Harris 2024 campaign as tactically underrated given the structural hand she was dealt; a skeptical take on Vienna-style social housing as a “solution in search of a problem” for U.S. progressives reluctant to admit they actually need deregulation; an alternate history in which a Powell 2000 candidacy would have lost the GOP primary to Bush but would have squeezed out McCain’s New Hampshire moment; and a brief on Ernest May’s Strange Victory arguing the French defeat in 1940 turned on German operational risk-taking through the Ardennes plus French intelligence failure rather than on Third Republic dysfunction. Recurring tic: Yglesias self-cites repeatedly (Rent Is Too Damn High, One Billion Americans, his own SF BARF / YIMBY timeline claims).

↑ Back to TOC

Claude’s Response:

This is the kind of piece where the format does most of the analytical work — eight loosely-connected mailbag answers, each one a quick directional take, none building to a thesis. The most useful one for the roundup is the One Billion Americans revisitation, where Yglesias explicitly updates against his own prior framework. The two changes he names — the post-2022 interest-rate environment killing the “structurally cheap to borrow for family subsidies” assumption, and the John Burn-Murdoch couple-formation finding shifting the policy lever from married-fertility incentives to a much harder-to-target “people are doing less of everything” social-formation problem — are both real and consequential. The interesting tell is what he doesn’t do with them: he names the AI-and-robotics counterfactual (“are we 10 years away from humanoid robot nannies?”) as a reason for personal writer’s block, but doesn’t engage what the couple-formation collapse means for his own abundance liberalism program, which has always assumed a growing population was the demographic input. Pronatalism as policy targets parents; if the bottleneck is now upstream of pair-formation, the policy menu has to be different and Yglesias hasn’t worked out what that menu is.

The “big ideas” answer is more interesting for what it reveals about Yglesias’s self-conception than for what it argues. The case that Silent Spring’s causal role in the environmental movement is partly reconstructed ex post is fair but does not establish his stronger implicit claim that his own 2012 Rent Is Too Damn High belongs in roughly the same conversation as Silent Spring and Abundance. The “I was there first on housing” move — twice in three paragraphs — is the kind of horn-tooting Yglesias usually gets right by being funny about it; here it lands as half-funny because he’s serious. The genuinely-new point is the medium-shift observation: that books now function as flags-in-the-ground that podcasters and columnists then translate into actual reach, which inverts the 20th-century relationship in which the book was the medium and everything else was promotion. That’s worth holding onto as a structural claim about how ideas move in 2026.

The majority-minority-districts answer is short and confident and has a real argument inside it — partisan sorting on racial attitudes has made the older tradeoff between “elect more Democrats” and “elect more Black Democrats” largely obsolete, evidenced by the rising number of Black senators (Obama, Booker, Scott, Harris, Alsobrooks, Blunt Rochester, soon Stratton) from constituencies that contain no majority-Black seats. The argument is correct as far as it goes but elides the question raised by the original Joseph America 2028 prompt about “the incumbent protection racket that is the Congressional Black Caucus,” which Yglesias never engages. The CBC question is a real one about how a redistricting reform that’s individually rational at the partisan level interacts with specific incumbents’ interests in keeping their districts as drawn; Yglesias’s silence on that is conspicuous because it’s exactly the kind of intra-Democratic-coalition fight he usually walks toward.

The Harris-2024 defense is the most characteristically Yglesias take in the piece — flip-flopping works better than not flip-flopping; she should have flip-flopped harder on Biden; she ran a tactically good race in a structurally impossible context — and is a doubling-down on his pre-election position rather than an update. Worth flagging in synthesis as a contrast to the Compact / Damage / Persuasion writers who treat the 2024 result as a referendum on Democratic substance rather than tactics. The Vienna-social-housing answer is on-brand (the Rent Is Too Damn High / abundance / deregulation prior pulling through) and the Strange Victory / Powell-2000 answers are pleasant historical-counterfactual chat that does not bear synthesis weight.

↑ Back to TOC

Full Articles

The Trump Slush Fund Is an Abuse of Political Power, Not a Legal Wrong

Andrew C. McCarthy · 2026-05-23 · Original →

By ANDREW C. MCCARTHY May 23, 2026

President Donald Trump delivers remarks in the East Room of the White House in Washington, D.C., May 22, 2026. (Evelyn Hockstein/Reuters)

Once again, Trump is doing what Democrats did, just far more ostentatiously.

I heartily endorse our editorial on President Trump’s appalling “anti-weaponization fund.” As I explained earlier this week (but after this column was substantially written), this fund is just part of a settlement that has proven all the more unseemly with the revelation that acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, previously Trump’s personal lawyer, quietly tucked in a provision granting his former client immunity against being further audited or potentially prosecuted for tax violations — notwithstanding Justice Department and government-wide conflict of interest rules.

Ostensibly, all of this self-dealing arises out of a lawsuit: a farcical “case” in which the president and his elder sons sued executive agencies that answer to him so that he might — with the blessing of the judiciary — pay himself $10 billion in taxpayer funds. No one, however, should be fooled by the legal trappings. This is a political controversy. I mean “political” in the constitutional sense of political power, which may be used or abused. Far from a legal matter, this episode powerfully demonstrates that law is inadequate to the task of political accountability.

To be sure, there may well have been a legal controversy in the technical, jurisdictional sense. On that score, I was underwhelmed by the Democratic lawyers’ amicus briefs that Dan McLaughlin addressed in his stellar article. Judge Kathleen Williams, the Obama appointee to the federal district court in South Florida who was presiding, certainly seemed poised to conclude that the president’s lawsuit was collusive. After all, it was collusive, as a matter of fact. As a matter of law, however, there is enough wiggle room in the relevant jurisprudence for a judge to come out either way on the key question: whether one actor has such dominant control over both the plaintiff and the defendant that the matter is not sufficiently contested to warrant judicial intervention.

It’s good that Trump withdrew the lawsuit. Not because the outcome will now be better but because the courts shouldn’t be drawn into another branch’s corrupt machinations.

As things played out, the Trumps will not be getting billions of dollars in damages over what was undeniably a violation of their legal rights. Yes, the government leaked nonpublic taxpayer information about Trump that Democrats long craved, and the politicized Biden Justice Department predictably gave a slap on the wrist to the perp, IRS contractor Charles Littlejohn. But the wrong isn’t worth anywhere near $10 billion in damages. It’s reminiscent of New York Attorney General Letitia James’s fraud suit against Trump: The allegations were based on what really were misrepresentations by the Trump organization regarding the value of its assets. But with no proof that anyone was actually defrauded (and with James’s invocation of a consumer fraud statute that was inapplicable to arm’s-length transactions between sophisticated financial players), a New York appeals court rightly found that the alleged wrongs were not worth anything close to the half billion in damages that James sought and an elected progressive Democratic judge awarded.

Nevertheless, what’s contemplated in the Trump DOJ–approved settlement of Trump’s claims against the Trump IRS is a slush fund — with Trumpian schmaltz, it’s $1.776 billion in public funds — to be paid out to the president’s fellow travelers for sundry political grievances in order to put a government imprimatur on the claim that Democratic policy and enforcement choices were not merely wrongheaded but illegal. In that, it’s just the flip side of Trump’s lawfare against his political enemies — we’re to see them not just as abusive but as criminals. It’s another step in the government’s bipartisan descent into banana republic depths.

There’s no original thinking here, just the familiar audacity. Naturally, Democratic critics of Trump’s collusive lawsuit — and of his Justice Department’s recent payouts of $1.25 million to Michael Flynn (in connection with his Russiagate prosecution) and $5 million to the family of Ashli Babbitt (a pro-Trump rioter shot to death by a Capitol Police officer on January 6, 2021) — make no mention of the Biden administration’s similarly generous settlements. These include those with Andrew McCabe (the FBI’s former deputy director in charge of the Russiagate fiasco, who was compensated despite the Obama-appointed DOJ inspector general’s richly supported findings that he lied to investigators on multiple occasions, including when under oath), and with Peter Strzok and Lisa Page (the FBI Russiagate culprits who were paid $1.2 million and $800,000, respectively, for the publication of texts that demonstrated their rabid anti-Trump bias and kid-glove approach to the Clinton emails caper). And Democrats were delighted by the Obama DOJ’s targeting of municipal police departments, a campaign in which the departments were “defended” by big-city Democratic officials who were only too happy to agree with the DOJ that the police were systemically racist and in desperate need of Obama-prescribed “reforms” that fueled Democratic political narratives.

Once again, as with Trump’s cryptocurrency schemes with foreign benefactors, compared with which the Biden family’s $24 million influence-peddling haul isn’t even a rounding error, the president is doing what Democrats did, just far more ostentatiously.

None of this is illegal. Over a decade ago, I wrote a book called Faithless Execution about impeachment. One of the major points was that, while more serious than criminal offenses, impeachable acts need not be and often are not criminal offenses. Rather than private wrongs (which is how most actionable criminal and civil misconduct can be described), impeachable offenses are public wrongs — malfeasance that Hamilton (in Federalist No. 65) aptly described as “POLITICAL” (there’s that word again, all caps in the original) because they are offenses involving “abuse or violation of some public trust.”

On The Remnant podcast last week, in one of his solo “Ruminant” episodes, our pal Jonah Goldberg addressed a rhetorical tactic of Trump defense, in which it is contended that some grotesque act or other must be appropriate because it’s perfectly legal. It’s a peculiar line of argument. Often, the official behavior that threatens a society is most perilous precisely because it is legal.

It is not practicable for a well-functioning society to criminalize sundry forms of abusing power. If the judgment calls that must be made by top officials — e.g., cabinet officers, agency bureaucrats, judges, prosecutors, and police — could be grist for ex post facto criminal prosecution or civil liability, these officials would refrain from taking the actions that must be taken for society to be protected and to prosper. The Framers therefore insisted that executive and judicial officers be subjected to vetting by the Senate prior to assuming office; we need to weed corrupt people out before they wield power, because once they’re wielding it, there’s not much we can do other than strip them of it.

This was obvious to the Framers. It was obvious, with the benefit of over two centuries’ experience, when I wrote about impeachment. And it has never been more obvious than it is today, after the Supreme Court held in Trump v. United States (2024) that presidents have absolute immunity for core executive actions, no matter how potentially corrupt, and may have such immunity even for actions at the broad margins of executive authority. (The Court’s majority did not settle how far immunity goes, but it implied that the presidency cannot function as designed without it.) It’s no longer enough to say that most serious abuses of power may not be crimes; even if presidential crimes could be proved, they probably could not be prosecuted.

A corrupt pardon is not a crime, but it’s a classic impeachable offense. Exploiting an office of public trust for personal gain, especially financial gain, will frequently not be prosecutable (bribery is impossible to prove if the public official can show some plausible public purpose — the president has to have an airplane, right?), but it is clearly an impeachable offense. The president’s foreign affairs authority is so sweeping that courts will almost never entertain criminal or civil suits, but a president’s exercise of foreign affairs authority for the benefit of a foreign power and to the detriment of the United States may have been the potential impeachable conduct that most animated the Framers.

Madison, who led the crafting of our fundamental law, concluded that a congressional impeachment power would be “indispensable” for purposes of “defending the Community [against] the incapacity, negligence or perfidy of the chief Magistrate.” Still, the Constitution does not call for Congress to leap instantly to impeachment as the remedy for executive abuses of power. The idea was that impeachment would be a rare occurrence (probably not as rare as it was until 1998, but rare nonetheless). Most of the time, Congress would use less drastic but quite effective ways to rein in a wayward executive — the power to deny funding, to deny legislative support, to refuse to confirm appointees, to conduct oversight in public hearings, etc.

That said, to have the democratic republic that the Framers contemplated, Congress has to be an active participant. As Madison put it (in Federalist No. 51), “In republican government, the legislative authority necessarily predominates.” If Congress is passive, if it does not jealously defend its institutional prerogatives, if it is derelict in its duty to check executive excess, then we no longer have the government formed by the Constitution. We are at the mercy of presidents, who might be good if we’re lucky but might just as easily be disastrous out of malevolence or incompetence or both.

The risibly labeled “Anti-Weaponization” slush fund that the president wants and that his Justice Department has dutifully concocted is a profound abuse of political power. So is the DOJ’s conferral of tax immunity on Trump in his private capacity. These are not legal problems. They are executive challenges that the Constitution empowers Congress alone to address.

ANDREW C. MCCARTHY is a senior fellow at National Review Institute, an NR contributing editor, and author of BALL OF COLLUSION: THE PLOT TO RIG AN ELECTION AND DESTROY A PRESIDENCY. @andrewcmccarthy

↑ Back to article summary · ↑ See Claude’s response

Trump’s Egregious Abuse of an Egregious Delegation

John R. Puri · 2026-05-22 · Original →

By JOHN R. PURI May 22, 2026

Left: President Donald Trump delivers his State of the Union address at the U.S. Capitol in Washington, D.C., February 24, 2026. Right: The U.S. Capitol Building. (Kenny Holston/Pool via Reuters, Annabelle Gordon/Reuters)

Congress never should have given away the power to appropriate unlimited settlements.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche must think Americans are stupid, because he keeps saying things that no intelligent person could take seriously.

When announcing his office’s naked slush fund for friends of the president, Blanche righteously proclaimed that “the machinery of government should never be weaponized against any American.” Says the man prosecuting James Comey over seashells.

He reports that the dollar figure “of the Anti-Weaponization Fund’s funding does not represent the value of any claim by Plaintiffs, but rather is based on the projected valuation of future claimants’ claims.” And — what a coincidence — this rigorous projection came out to exactly $1.776 billion.

To defend the fund, Blanche cites the Keepseagle settlement agreed to by the Obama administration, which distributed $300 million to Native American nonprofits and philanthropic organizations. But that settlement was approved by a federal judge. Blanche’s fund, by contrast, was devised to avert impending judicial scrutiny.

Also, was the Keepseagle settlement not an abuse of power? Here, Obama’s past misdeeds are an excuse masquerading as a moral justification. Unjust acts are unjust, regardless of who did it first. Having two agencies that you control contrive a pretextual “deal” to reward political taxpayers with taxpayer funds is a betrayal of the presidential oath.

Trump’s slush fund is not, however, an abuse of presidential power. It is an abuse of legislative power — the Article I power to appropriate money — that the nominally legislative branch unduly delegated to the executive. Appropriations have always included disbursements of legal judgments and settlements owed by the federal government. Congress surrendered its authority to oversee such disbursements relatively recently.

Until the mid-20th century, Congress approved or denied every monetary judgment against the United States through annual appropriations. It almost never withheld payments, though the due diligence required was apparently too much to handle. To relieve itself, Congress in 1956 created the Judgment Fund — the account used to finance Trump’s slush fund — as a permanent, indefinite appropriation to pay judgments under $100,000.

Fulfilling what remained of its constitutional duty still proved too burdensome for Congress. So it expanded the Judgment Fund over the next two decades to remove any substantive limit: first allowing it to pay “compromise settlements” in the same manner as court-ordered judgments, then removing the $100,000 cap on payments. Nowadays, the Judgment Fund spends billions of dollars per year, all without a whit of involvement by the one branch empowered to spend public money.

Many in Congress are upset at how the president is misusing the authority they gave him. Too bad. Because the statutes they enacted impose no limit on how much the Judgment Fund can spend, Trump’s “settlement” may not violate the law. If Congress could muster enough courage to amend the law or expressly prohibit the slush fund, the president would veto their reprisal. This is how the policymaking process, once abdicated, stays inverted: The president initiates major acts under broad statutory powers, and the legislature fails to respond.

Perhaps, however, the Judgment Fund’s very structure is unconstitutional, inviting the judiciary to step in. The fund’s creation is not, in fact, an appropriation — defined as an act or instance of assigning money — but, rather, a delegation of the power to appropriate, unbounded by any intelligible principle. The Supreme Court once held that such delegations are unlawful under the Constitution’s careful separation of powers. It could do so again.

As with most of Trump’s offenses, he started small to test what he could get away with, then scaled up. His Justice Department paid $5 million to Ashli Babbitt’s family from the Judgment Fund to settle a wrongful death suit, $1.25 million to Michael Flynn for a lawsuit that had been dismissed by a judge, and another million to Carter Page for a dismissed case. All compensation for personal servility, paid for by Americans.

Some Democrats want to salvage the Judgment Fund by barring Trump’s particular misuses while keeping the account open for a future administration. Instead, what if Congress did its job? It won’t, of course, unless the Court affirms that the Framers meant what they wrote: “No Money shall be drawn from the Treasury, but in Consequence of Appropriations made by Law.”

JOHN R. PURI is the Thomas L. Rhodes Fellow at National Review.

↑ Back to article summary · ↑ See Claude’s response

Trump’s Victims Fund Is a Scandal. But It May Be Legal.

Jed Rubenfeld · 2026-05-20 · Original →

Trump’s Victims Fund Is a Scandal. But It May Be Legal.

The administration should cancel its plan to compensate lawfare victims of its own choosing, even if the courts can’t block it. “The Trump administration’s hands aren’t exactly clean when it comes to lawfare and weaponization,” writes Jed Rubenfeld. (Kevin Dietsch via Getty Images)

The $1.8 billion “Anti-Weaponization Fund” just announced by the Department of Justice may not be 100 percent illegal, but it’s still a gross embarrassment that the Trump administration should rescind.

Here’s what we know so far. Back in January, President Donald Trump, along with his eldest sons and the Trump Organization, sued the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) for $10 billion over their tax returns leaking between 2018 and 2020. That suit was tricky, to put it mildly, because Trump is now essentially in charge of the IRS, and it’s a long-settled maxim of the law that a man “cannot sue himself.” After all, what would prevent President Trump, as the defendant, from agreeing to pay $10 billion of taxpayer money to Trump the plaintiff, even if the lawsuit had no legal merit?

The presiding judge in the case called for a hearing on this issue, suggesting she might have to dismiss the case because of it. But this past Monday, possibly to avoid that hearing, the “parties” to the case announced a “settlement” in which Trump and the other plaintiffs agreed to drop their claims and, in exchange, the Justice Department—of which Trump is also essentially in charge—agreed to create an “Anti-Weaponization Fund.”

This fund, according to the published settlement agreement, will compensate people who were victims of “lawfare” and “weaponization,” which are defined as the “use of the levers of government power by Democrat elected officials, political and career federal employees, contractors, and agents to target individuals, groups, and entities for improper and unlawful political, personal, and/or ideological reasons.”

Trump is now essentially in charge of the IRS, and it’s a long-settled maxim of the law that a man “cannot sue himself.”

Moving quickly, the DOJ announced that $1.8 billion would be deposited into the new fund. At the same time, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche made another move even more astonishing than the fund itself. The United States government, he declared, would permanently drop any claims it might have had against Trump, his sons Don Jr. and Eric, and the Trump Organization—not only as to their taxes, but seemingly as to “any matters currently pending or that could be pending before” any government department.

That’s like a blanket pardon, of which no mention was made in the settlement agreement. In fact it’s better than a pardon, because pardons cover only criminal offenses, whereas this declaration purports to block the United States from bringing not only criminal prosecutions but civil actions as well.

Under the agreement, the fund will be administered by five officials who President Trump can fire without cause at any time. The names of people who receive money from the fund may be kept private, as will the amount of money they receive. And the fund will disband in December 2028.

The fund has provoked sharp criticism from both sides of the aisle. “This all seems to be an obvious abuse of power by the Department of Justice, by the president,” said Democratic senator Jack Reed. “He negotiated essentially with himself.” Republican senator John Thune, the majority leader, said he was “not a big fan” of the fund. And Senator Bill Cassidy, a Republican and Trump critic who recently lost his bid for reelection, called it “a slush fund.” In fact, on the day the fund was announced, the Treasury Department’s general counsel resigned, possibly in protest.

If the fund goes forward, who is likely to rake in some cash?

This article is featured in U.S. Politics. Sign up here to get an update every time a new piece is published.

Testifying before a Senate committee on Tuesday, Blanche said that the fund would be open to anyone who felt they had been the victim of lawfare, “whether you’re Hunter Biden” or “another individual who believed they were a victim of weaponization.” News reports, however, say that January 6 defendants are the most likely recipients. Many Trump allies who believe they were targets of Biden-era lawfare are sure to put in hefty claims as well.

What about the president and his family? Numerous media reports claim that Trump and his sons will not be eligible to receive money from the fund, but I’m not so sure. The settlement agreement is a little ambiguous on this score.

It says that the plaintiffs in the case—Trump, Don Jr., Eric, and the Trump Organization—will receive no money to compensate them for the claims that are being settled, but conceivably they might be eligible for payment on other claims. For example, could Trump collect money for damages he allegedly suffered from the Russia investigation, or from the suits brought against him by Jack Smith, Fani Willis, Letitia James, and Alvin Bragg? If James were ultimately to succeed in collecting the $500 million judgment initially issued against Trump in the civil fraud case she brought against him (an intermediate appellate court has correctly thrown out that judgment, but it’s on appeal to New York’s highest court), could Trump be reimbursed by federal taxpayers through the Anti-Weaponization Fund? We don’t exactly know.

In fact, there’s a decent argument that Trump and certain Trump allies deserve to be reimbursed for the Democrats’ unconstitutional and politically motivated lawfare against them in 2024. Some had to pay enormous legal fees out of their own pockets. Many other people suffered millions of dollars in business damages because the Biden administration unconstitutionally induced social media companies to deplatform them. Trump collected a $25 million settlement from Meta for such deplatforming, but most other victims have never received any compensation.

Still, if some victims of Biden-era abuses deserve compensation, the Anti-Weaponization Fund is not the way to do it, for two reasons.

First, without transparency or independent decision-makers, the fund will be awash in the appearance—if not the reality—of corrupt self-dealing. Five people, all of whom Trump can fire at will, will be in charge of doling out $1.8 billion of taxpayer money without the public knowing who’s getting the money, how much they’re getting, or on what basis. In theory, the people handing out money from the fund could agree to give millions of dollars to themselves.

Second, the Trump administration’s hands aren’t exactly clean when it comes to lawfare and weaponization. Trump’s executive orders targeting law firms are a good example. Several of these orders were blatantly unconstitutional and directed at the president’s perceived enemies. Trump did serious damage to some of these firms, but I think we all know they won’t be collecting payments from the Anti-Weaponization Fund. That will be a very bad look for the administration. It might also be illegal.

Blanche has cited an Obama-era $680 million settlement fund as precedent, but in the Obama fund, there was no obvious conflict of interest. Obama was certainly not the plaintiff in the case being settled. The recipients were not Obama allies but Native American farmers who had been discriminated against by the Department of Agriculture. Moreover, the fund was disbursed publicly by a judge, not doled out secretly by handpicked officials.

In theory, the people handing out money from the fund could agree to give millions of dollars to themselves.

Could a court throw out the Anti-Weaponization Fund? It’s not obvious who would have standing to bring suit against it. The most serious opportunity for judicial oversight might have been up to Judge Kathleen Williams, the presiding judge in Trump’s suit against the IRS.

Normally courts rubber-stamp settlement agreements, but in appropriate cases judges have the power—indeed sometimes the obligation—to probe and review them before signing off. One circumstance warranting such a review is when the plaintiff and defendant may not really be adverse to one another, and where they might not be serving the interests of people they are supposed to represent.

This case fits that bill, but it looks like Judge Williams missed the moment.

Hours after the DOJ announced the creation of the Anti-Weaponization Fund, Williams issued an order formally closing the case. Her order says that Trump filed a dismissal of the suit without referencing or attaching any settlement, so “there is no settlement of record.” Because the DOJ had never technically answered the lawsuit, the plaintiffs had a right to dismiss it, and while the judge expressed concern about the “public’s strong interest in knowing about the conduct of its government and expenditure of its resources,” she said her authority over the case was finished.

Blanche has already declared that the Anti-Weaponization Fund does not need judicial approval to go forward, and he may be right.

On the other hand, the arrangement is so fishy I could imagine a later Democratic administration declaring the whole thing unlawful and coming after the fund administrators or trying to claw back the money. I could also imagine a later administration declaring Blanche’s blanket civil and criminal pardon null and void. And for sure the fund is going to be a haymaker for the Democrats, with lawsuits being filed, people making FOIA requests, and lawmakers investigating—especially if the Democrats take control of the House or Senate after the midterms.

Even if the fund is beyond the reach of the law, the administration would be well advised to reconsider. The Free Press is for free thinkers. Send this to someone who values open debate and curiosity.

↑ Back to article summary · ↑ See Claude’s response

The End of the SEC Gag Rule

Dan McLaughlin · 2026-05-22 · Original →

By DAN MCLAUGHLIN May 22, 2026

Outside the Securities and Exchange Commission headquarters in Washington, D.C., May 12, 2021 (Andrew Kelly/Reuters)

After 54 years, the commission will no longer bar settling defendants from speaking.

Since 1972, the Securities and Exchange Commission has imposed a gag order as a condition of civil settlements, sometimes referred to as a “no admit/no deny policy.” Under that approach, the SEC files charges accusing a defendant of some misconduct; the defendant settles the case without admitting it; but the SEC then gets to trumpet the accusation publicly without having proven it in court, and the defendant is barred from publicly contesting or denying the SEC’s account.

On Monday, the SEC rescinded the policy and announced as well that it would no longer seek to enforce it as a condition of prior settlements. This is a good move by the commission (all three of whose current members are Trump appointees) and a victory for legal challengers to the rule, led by the New Civil Liberties Alliance. NCLA has been pursuing free speech challenges to the policy since 2018, building on the work of critics of the gag-order policy going back decades.

As Chairman Paul Atkins observed, “Speech critical of the government is an important part of the American tradition. This rescission ends the policy prohibiting such criticism by settling defendants.” Commissioner Hester Peirce, a vocal critic of the policy, added: “Settlements shrouded in forced silence by the non-governmental party do not serve either the markets or the Commission’s investor-protection mission. To the contrary, people’s freedom to speak against the government contributes to its ability to govern well.” As Pierce argued in 2024 when arguing to end the policy then:

The requirement that defendants must either admit or at least promise not to deny the government’s allegations of wrongdoing as a condition of settlement has not been widely adopted by federal agencies. Some agencies even explicitly allow settling defendants to deny the allegations of wrongdoing. As the Federal Trade Commission noted when approving one such settlement in 2012, it was confident in the work of its staff . . . “it is the evidentiary record developed by FTC staff during the course of its investigation, not any ensuing settlement agreement, that forms the basis for the action by Commission.”

The SEC gag order policy was set out in its Rule 202.5(e) (codified at 17 CFR § 202. 5(e)), which is now rescinded:

The Commission has adopted the policy that in any civil lawsuit brought by it or in any administrative proceeding of an accusatory nature pending before it, it is important to avoid creating, or permitting to be created, an impression that a decree is being entered or a sanction imposed, when the conduct alleged did not, in fact, occur. Accordingly, it hereby announces its policy not to permit a defendant or respondent to consent to a judgment or order that imposes a sanction while denying the allegations in the complaint or order for proceedings. In this regard, the Commission believes that a refusal to admit the allegations is equivalent to a denial, unless the defendant or respondent states that he neither admits nor denies the allegations.

The ostensible theory of the policy was to prevent miscreants from settling charges — which are usually linked in some way to taking advantage of investors — and then turning around and reassuring their customers or shareholders that they did nothing wrong. When the SEC has been feeling bolder, it has argued as well that the policy promotes public confidence in the enforcement process — which is really just another way of saying that it insulates the commission from public criticism.

The no admit/no deny policy has a more complicated relationship with private damages lawsuits. The inability to publicly contest liability complicates the defense of such suits (which often tout settlements in the allegations of a complaint), but it doesn’t apply to bar legal filings or testimony denying liability. The plaintiffs’ bar, for its part, has long been frustrated that the SEC, unlike criminal prosecutors, typically doesn’t extract sworn admissions of guilt from settling defendants that can be used against them in court.

Unlike barring later out-of-court speech, there’s no free-speech issue in demanding allocutions of guilt under oath as a condition of either a guilty plea or a civil settlement. In some civil contexts, such as defamation cases, a public apology or retraction by the defendant is often just as important as the money. Indeed, outside of the rarer case of so-called Alford pleas, where defendants agree to plead guilty without confessing guilt, sentencing judges in criminal cases generally see a verbal allocution of guilt as necessary to protect the integrity of the process and to assess the defendant’s remorse for sentencing purposes.

By contrast, no admit/no deny greases the skids for settlements in cases where either the SEC doesn’t have a very strong case but can force a settlement on regulated parties who don’t want a fight and will accept a nuisance settlement (perhaps because they’re in the middle of a merger, public offering, or some other event requiring regulatory approval), or the opposite is true: The SEC, which has many irons in the fire, lacks the resources for a protracted battle with a deep-pocketed corporation or Wall Street firm over a single case. The gag order helped preserve the fiction that these settlements were all about the merits rather than the business reasons why settlements often happen.

In any of these situations, the government’s asserted interest in permanently restricting denials is hard to square with the burden on free speech. The SEC admitted, when retracting the rule, “We are not aware of any instance where the Commission has sought to reopen a district court action or administrative adjudication following a violation of a no-deny provision, and there are no reported opinions where a court has ruled upon such a motion.” That doesn’t mean that the policy had no effect; indeed, it shows how regularly it has stifled speech. But it also reflects the SEC’s lack of confidence that its policy would stand up in court if put to a strict test. That is, frankly, the commission’s modus operandi going back decades: It frequently prefers to test expansive legal theories in settlements, or against poorly funded bad actors, and it often loses in court when it actually has to defend its positions against good lawyers.

NCLA’s challenges have been an uphill battle. The Second and Ninth Circuits have upheld the policy under the First Amendment, but the Ninth Circuit warned that surviving a facial challenge might not mean that the policy would stand against a challenge as applied, and at least two Fifth Circuit judges have warned that it could fall. The SEC had until Tuesday, May 26, to file a response in the Supreme Court to an NCLA cert petition in Powell v. SEC, so rescinding the policy likely enables the commission to avoid a final reckoning on the issue. Given the current Supreme Court’s robust free-speech rulings in recent years, many of which have been unanimous, that was a real risk. Nothing in the rescission of the policy prevents a new Democratic administration from appointing commissioners who press to bring it back, but it will be harder to justify that than to simply rely on the inertia of a longstanding policy.

Of course, as the defense bar has warned, this isn’t all necessarily good news for defendants: The SEC staff may feel compelled to work harder to make their case in charging documents, and companies that could just blandly tell the press that they were legally barred from discussing a settlement won’t be able to do that anymore. But forcing the agency to defend its work is a good thing. And insulating the commission from criticism that it has settled cases for reasons other than their merits is a bad thing. That’s apt to be particularly important when the SEC is locking horns, as it has in the past, with outspoken tycoons such as Mark Cuban and Elon Musk. For an agency whose entire existence is supposed to be justified by the theory that markets require transparency above all, the last thing it ought to have been doing is restricting speech.

DAN MCLAUGHLIN is a senior writer at National Review Online and a fellow at National Review Institute. @baseballcrank
