Jerusalem Demsas: But the assembly stuff I actually, like, is that really the thing that’s important here? Like, I think the battery production and manufacturing, that’s important. But assembling cars at the end, well, I mean, who cares?

Matthew Yglesias: Well,it’s all part of a thing, right? I mean…

Jerusalem Demsas: But is it? I think this is the thing, that everything gets conflated in this very weird way. Like, is end-of-the-line assembly of an already manufactured car helping our national security? In what way? Like, people just say this. I’m just…

Matthew Yglesias: Well, what do you mean? No, no, no. So, I mean, so there’s specifics of, like, developing a battery industry and like, what’s the hard part, what’s the easy part? But there’s also the reality, right? That, in wartime, car factories get converted to manufacturing military equipment, right? Like the…

Jerusalem Demsas: But so could battery factories.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, yes, yes, yes, but, I mean, all kinds of things, right? Like the ability to have a manufacturing base is relevant.

Jerusalem Demsas: But then the question is like, what should we… We both agree that it’s not maximally economically efficient to do any of this stuff that we’re talking about: subsidies, tariffs, whatever. So we are taking that cost because there are other things that matter than maximal economic efficiency. And then it’s like, OK, but economic efficiency does matter, so, what is the thing that we could do here that would generally not undermine price signals in the market, reduce the competition for new companies to come in, whatever it is? And that’s, to me, clearly like, let’s be narrowly targeted here about what we’re actually trying to do.

Like, you’re trying to do you’re trying to an end run around, like maybe when there’s a hot war, we’ll like need… Well, I’m like, I don’t know, maybe AI will fight all our wars and like…

Matthew Yglesias: No, no, no. I mean, I do think this is why going back to the international relations point is important. Like, I think — beyond the cars, right — we should be trying to lower trade barriers, dramatically lower trade barriers with Europe, with Japan, with South Korea, with Latin America, and with a mix of, like, there’s true allies, right? And then there’s a lot of countries out there that are kind of a mixed bag in terms of human rights and geopolitical alignment, but they’re not hostile to the United States in a geopolitical sense. And they’re also not that large, right?

Like, you raised the UAE, which is, you know, that’s an important country because of their oil wealth. But there’s no universe in which the UAE becomes like the dominant global power. And they know that, right? Like, they’re just trying to sell oil to people and make money, right? And I don’t know why they’re screwing around in Sudan the way they are.

Jerusalem Demsas: Gold.

Matthew Yglesias: But they’re not going to conquer the world. And so we want that. And I think that we should be trying to not, like, reshore 100% of the manufacturing that has drifted to China, but to decouple, substantially, so that we are both manufacturing more stuff in the United States and importing manufactured goods from, you know, from Mexico, from Colombia, from Venezuela, from Jamaica, from Spain, rather than from China.

And we should be trying to get a world in which friendly Asian countries — you know, Japan, India, Korea, etc. — are like in a U.S.-centric trading system rather than a China-centric trading system. It’s through a little bit of happenstance that cars has become the hard, hard nub of this, but I think we want to build on that nub. Like Apple shifting more and more of its assembly to India, like, that’s very good that new…

Jerusalem Demsas: So, I totally agree that the number one thing that I wish people — more people who cared about this, people who are more concerned about China and are certain about it — would do is they would care a lot more about “friend-shoring” instead of using this issue as an attempt to return us to the economy of the 1950s.

Like, I agree with this. I think it’d be great. I don’t think it’s great that 90% of the world’s rare earths that go into lithium-ion batteries are coming from China. It’s clearly not good that any supply chain would be so unbelievably undiversified, but I think — and again, maybe we’re actually just saying the same thing.

If it was like 30% coming from China, I don’t really care that much. Do you know what I mean? I actually think that that’s substantially different than what we’re trying to do right now. We’re trying to, like, eliminate China as a trading partner. And that seems — I mean, I don’t know…

Matthew Yglesias: We are trying to do that. I mean, you know, sadly, the Trump administration is not super coherent in its policy approach, right? I mean, they have relaxed export controls on Nvidia chips and stuff like that. I don’t think that Trump has a principled approach to geopolitics. I think the Biden administration…

Jerusalem Demsas: They have sort of a principled approach to deal making with China on this, though, actually.

Matthew Yglesias: Sort of. But, I mean, Trump is very interested in the stock market. You know what I mean? And so, you know, he has exempted from tariffs stuff that is needed to build data centers because that’s been a big element of GDP. He has allowed Nvidia to export a lot of chips because that’s good for one of the biggest S&P 500 components.

It’s a little incoherent, right? His approach to that leading edge technology, with his approach to the auto industry, where those companies are now small enough that, like, it’s not as big a deal for the stock market, but it still plays a big role in the kind of American mythos, etc.

The Biden administration, I thought, had a more coherent view of what its China policy was, but then its manufacturing policy was much more invested in narrow labor union politics, which was also bad.

You know, a big challenge for American geopolitical competition with China is that: You said, like, national security stuff is the only thing that can get through Congress on a bipartisan basis. But there’s, on a practical level, very little buy-in for the idea that there’s geopolitical competition with China.

Jerusalem Demsas: What do you mean?

Matthew Yglesias: Like, nobody wants to say, ”I am doing X and it is going to be moderately economically costly. But the reason that I’m doing it is geopolitical competition.”

Jerusalem Demsas: That’s because it’s a very bad message. You can’t put that on a press release.

Matthew Yglesias: Right, but I mean, I think at the height of the Cold War, if the USSR, if Brezhnev had come and been like, “I want to buy all of your most advanced microchips,” and the Nixon administration were like, “No,” people wouldn’t have been like, “What’s going on?”

You know what I mean? Like people believed in the Cold War.

Jerusalem Demsas: But, I mean, I think right now, the debate over chips is very much like having a similar flavor. The problem is that Trump seems not to be that concerned about the potential for China to…

Matthew Yglesias: Sure, but that’s part of what I mean though. There isn’t real… Like, for all that….

Jerusalem Demsas: But there are lots of people in Congress who are worried about it. Lots of people in D.C. I mean, I don’t know.

Matthew Yglesias: Right, I mean, it’s just, it’s thin. You know, it’s like…

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, we haven’t been in a hot war, where lots of Americans were fighting, in a long time. I think it’s made people way more complacent about the potential threat of war on people’s lives.

I mean, I just think that, even what’s going on in Iran right now, like it’s obviously super unpopular, but most Americans are not actually exposed to this. Most people do not have family members that are fighting in Iran and are at risk of dying. Like, we’re just bombing this other country or allowing Israel to bomb them. And again, I just think it’s been a while. Like even Iraq, it was a very much smaller proportion of people fighting than earlier conflicts.

Matthew Yglesias: I mean, yes, Iraq was relatively disconnected. But the idea, right, if you go back in time, circa 2002, 2003, 2004, the idea that the United States was in some sense involved in a “War on Terror” was quite widespread. Like, it had mass penetration.

And so if somebody said like, “Why are these kids dying in Iraq, Mr. President?” He said that was to advance our goals in the war on terror. And then his opponents said, like, “No, it’s not. That’s not a good way to pursue that goal.” But the idea that some kind of sacrifice in pursuit of fighting Al-Qaeda or something was very mainstream. I think the whole idea of geopolitical competition with China is a very elite project that nobody wants to articulate to a mass public, and that that therefore makes it…

Jerusalem Demsas: Do you think that’s different than the way that Japan was talked about in the ‘80s?

Matthew Yglesias: Well, the Japan thing was different because, you know…

Jerusalem Demsas: Because you look back — I mean, I wasn’t alive at the time — but you look back at these…

Matthew Yglesias: Oooh.

Jerusalem Demsas: Were you? Yeah, I guess you were.

Matthew Yglesias: I was alive in the 80s. Jesus.

Jerusalem Demsas: I guess when you look at the coverage at the time, it seems very, you know, Yellow Peril. Like Walter Mondale, what does he say? He says something on, pre-campaign, about how, you know, “Do we want our kids mopping the floors of factories around Japanese computers? ”[What do we want our kids to do? Sweep up around Japanese computers?”] Like, that just kind of sounds like how people talk now.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. But, so the difference is, I think it was almost the opposite, right? Like before the Japanese stock market crash, there was an incredible amount of authentic grassroots anxiety about rising Japanese clout in the world. About things like Japanese companies had all these sort of surplus — for, essentially for currency manipulation reasons, they kept recycling dollar profits into purchasing American investments, right? So Sony bought an American movie studio.

I mean, now it’s just one of the big five American movie studios. But in the ‘80s, that was a big deal. They [Mitsubishi] bought Rockefeller Center in New York. And so it was all this, “Oh my God, like Japan is buying everything up.” The Michael Crichton book Rising Sun and the associated movie, great text of this era. But on an elite level, at that time, people were saying “Japan is a treaty ally of the United States. Japan’s a democracy. Like we don’t actually have…”

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, I mean, some people were saying that.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, yeah, but I mean, it was an elite-focused…

Jerusalem Demsas: I dunno if I buy this. I mean, even people who are concerned about currency manipulation and who bought Rockefeller Center aren’t elites?

Matthew Yglesias: No, no, no, no, no, no, I mean, they didn’t understand the currency manipulation. They just saw hard American assets coming into the ownership of Japanese companies and were freaking out. “The Japanese are taking over.” People were also way more racist.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think you’re too New York City pilled. Like, are you…

Matthew Yglesias: I am very New York City pilled.But also, people were more racist then.

Jerusalem Demsas: That’s true.

Matthew Yglesias: You know, overtly, I think actually. But it was a populist criticism that “the elites”…

Jerusalem Demsas: Isn’t the China one, too? I mean, right now, so much of the “authentic energy,” I mean, again, obviously the term elite here is getting a little confusing. But when you think about people who are pushing for a revival of manufacturing, people who are situating themselves as members of the heartland, people who keep citing the same David Autour paper over and over. I mean, these people are considered populists. So there’s…

Matthew Yglesias: So, there is a brain dead populist argument about propping up American manufacturing jobs, right? These are the people who would say that the worst thing that could happen would be NAFTA, right? I’m saying there is a different argument, which is about geopolitical competition with China, right? China. Just China, right? So, from a China-focused view, if we manufacture things in Mexico, that’s good.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes, but those people don’t exist.

Matthew Yglesias: That is not a mass constituency, right? There is no… This is why the friend-shoring struggles to take off. If people who were upset about “jobs going to China” were worried about China, then you would have a mass constituency for friend-shoring. You’d be saying, “Look, we can get our cheap consumer goods and also decouple from China by working blah blah blah.”

Jerusalem Demsas: But, instead, friend-shoring is like a checkmate debate point that me and you pull out to prove that people don’t actually care about China.

Matthew Yglesias: Or something like that. Or it’s like we have good think tank papers about it, you know, and people have these kinds of discussions, but there is not a… And it’s also why exports to China are very appealing to Trump. Whereas from a competition framework, you gotta be careful about that. So, you know, I think it’s a problem.

Jerusalem Demsas: So maybe more people… Maybe I should chill out then. People aren’t that worried about a hot war with China. We’re not actually…

Matthew Yglesias: I think we should be fomenting more panic about this because it’s really bad.

Jerusalem Demsas: OK, well, we’ve come full circle. I still am not going to get my BYD, but maybe I will get a different electric vehicle. I’m just balking at these prices. Adam Ozimek, who is an economist, has been, for years now, pointing out that it’s insane that people spend like $40,000 on a car. And while…

Matthew Yglesias: Buthe said that like six years ago when everything was cheaper.

Jerusalem Demsas: He’s still saying that now. He’s still saying it now, maybe it’s a little bit higher, but still, when you look at the median cost of a car, I think it’s above $50,000. Now our fact checker will let me know if that’s correct [yes]. But that’s like, that’s actually crazy. Median cost of a new car. Fifty-thousand dollars.

Matthew Yglesias: I only ever bought one car, so I don’t know. It was very expensive.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, and plug-in hybrid, man. Wow.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. Not a good idea; I would actually just go electric. I made a mistake.

Jerusalem Demsas: But I don’t own a house.

Matthew Yglesias: That’s tough.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, this is a great time to transition to Peer Review, I think.

Matthew Yglesias: All right, let’s do it.

Jerusalem Demsas: So Peer Review is our section where Matt locks back in to his love of social science research. He has a paper for us today about smartphones.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes. My paper today is “The Effects of School Phone Bans: National Evidence from Lockable Pouches,” by Hunt Allcott, E. Jason Baron, Thomas Dee, Angela Duckworth, Matthew Gentzkow, and Brian Jacob. So, this is looking at: There’s been a lot of discussion about smartphones in schools over the past few years. There’s been a big trend to requiring kids to not have phones, to lock them down.

So, they looked at the use of pouches, right? So, they were able to verify that this pouching is occurring, and then they showed that the pouch rules substantially reduces phone use as measured by GPS pings and teacher reports. They show that disciplinary incidents increase in the short term, I think because teachers have to enforce the rule. Student well-being goes down because kids like to have their phones

And then they find that, in later years, this levels out — that students end up better off subjectively, that the disciplinary issues go away because kids are just kind of following the rules. Unfortunately, the academic benefits that I think people were hoping to see from this don’t really materialize.

They say that maybe some scores go up and maybe some scores go down, but on average there is zero effect. Little evidence of effects on school attendance, on classroom retention, or on perceived online bullying.

This was widely reported in the press as a disappointing result for the phone ban movement, and it generated much of the pushback that you normally see when people get unwelcome empirical social science findings: “The statistical power of this experiment is not that high.” “It’s looking at a relatively short time span,” blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.

But, basically, making the kids be less distracted by phones all day did not make them better at reading and math.

Jerusalem Demsas: So, when I was first reading this paper, I saw that the tested intervention were these Yondr pouches, as you mentioned. So, there are lots of different ways that teachers or schools can ban phones. They can ban them just during class time, or you have to put them in your pocket or you’ll get in trouble. So teachers are constantly policing, like, “Hey, you put your phone away.” You know what I mean?

You can also have these like pouches which are — if you’ve ever… I’ve been to a comedy show once that forced me to put my phone in one of these pouches. And so you get to keep the pouch with you and you just can’t unseal it. Like, you need the person to unseal the pouch.

And then there’s other versions where I know teachers will have, in the front of the room, like baskets or places where kids can put it with their names on it or whatever. And there’s lots of different things. And so they’re testing this specific one.

I think this intervention is not as bad as the teacher monitoring constantly and saying, “Put your phone away.” But I also don’t think it’s anywhere near my ideal, which is literally, like, you cannot have it in the school. Maybe you can have it in your locker, but you are not allowed to have your phone on your person at all over the course of the day.

And part of this is there’s significant research now about… Like, when I had this Yondr pouch at this comedy show, I was thinking about, like, “Oh man, I wish I could have my phone sometimes.” Like, I was constantly distracted by the fact that I couldn’t get it.

And there’s all these reports of students figuring out how to break into these pouches, and they do verify in the paper that GPS pings went down. So, it’s not, like, mass jailbreaking of their phones, but, just, there’s like a cognitive load of just having the possibility of having your phone.

Like just imagine other things: Like if there was just a television next to you or your best friend next to you at the office. Just, even if you’re supposed to be working, these things… Taking the effort to not do something that is distracting is actually pulling you out of working or engaging with the classroom or whatever. So, I’m a little…

Matthew Yglesias: You know, I thought we were friends. We’re here at the office.

Jerusalem Demsas: We are, and we get distracted constantly.

Matthew Yglesias: Is it so bad?

Jerusalem Demsas: I have to, like, leave the office to work.

Matthew Yglesias: It’s all my fault. You gotta put me in a pouch.

Jerusalem Demsas: Exactly. We gotta Yondr Pouch Matt.

But I just think that that’s one thing. Second thing is, people were really down on this paper and, as someone who is not a Smartphone Theory of Everything person, like, whatever. But I just think that: Eight hours of the day, you don’t have your phone, but the other 16 hours of the day, you do have your phone and you’re like TikTok-maxxing AI slop.

Like, I don’t know if your test grades are gonna go up that much. But it seems good that the school is not this impossible-to-manage rage cage, you know what I mean?

Matthew Yglesias: I mean…

Jerusalem Demsas: Like, it would have been good if the scores went up, but, I wasn’t super shocked by that, I guess.

Matthew Yglesias: Look, I’m still comfortable with it as a policy, since at the end of the day, the scores didn’t go down, and the subjective well-being actually went up, which I think is sort of actually what is motivating parents around this kind of stuff.

I mean, I’ll just say, in the morning, you know, I’ve got a fifth-grader, and we live really close to the school, so we always get there early, and the kids hang out and play. And most of the time they’re running around, or they’re climbing trees, or other kinds of stuff like that.

This morning, I was talking to another mom at drop-off, and I said to her, I was like, “What’s going on? Everybody’s too quiet.” And we looked over, and what happened is: There was a kid there who’s not usually there early, but who got there early today, and he had his phone with him, because he was one of the fifth-graders who has a phone. And the kids were all huddled around watching a video or watching him play a game or something like that.

And I’m a huge hypocrite about this, because you will constantly find me staring at my phone, and, you know, Jose thinks this is like the dumbest shit ever, that I get on this high horse, because I’m always on my tweets and stuff. But it’s like, it seems like it’s better for the children to interact with each other and play than to be on their phones all the time.

That being said, I am actually surprised that it didn’t have more academic benefits than this. It just seems to me like it would be a huge distraction that would hurt people’s learning. I’m putting this in the bucket of, like, I’m not trying to be a total social science denialist, but I don’t really believe it. I’ll be curious to see what happens.

Jerusalem Demsas: But it was a pretty short time span. So it was…

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. I mean, it was relatively brief. The other thing I will say, I mean, not just in the spirit of, like, total skepticism, but the limits of this kind of experimental evidence is that, you know, what we’re really talking about with phones.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, we’re talking about between April 2025 to February 2026, like, yes, I mean, there’s summer in that, in which you have your phone the whole time, there’s like five months of instruction, much of which…

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. No, no, I agree, I agree. I mean, it might be better over a longer term. But, also, I think we’re really talking about the dynamic interplay of rules and norms, right? And, just, all schools everywhere saying that while school is in session, you can’t have a phone isn’t just a school policy. It’s like a statement of society about, “What is the purpose of school? What is the role of phones in our lives?” that I think has implications for…

Because, I mean, one thing is like, “Well, shouldn’t just everything be up to parents, right? But, as a parent, we live in a society, right? And it’s like, I will never be the first parent in my peer group to get my kid some new kind of device. And I probably also won’t be the last. You know what I mean? Like, I’m going to be somewhere in the middle.

And I’m comfortable being below average, but there’s only so far below average I’m gonna go, and things that shift where the midpoint is then impact how parents parent, and how parents parent is probably a bigger deal than literally “What’s the rule in the school?” But the school is where we make collective decisions about stuff. But you’re not gonna find this in a difference-in-differences study of the rollout of the policy.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, I think over a longer time period ,you could probably find this. But I think that, you know, I have a friend who works as an occupational therapist in a public school system, in the U.S., in a major American city. And she, you know, she talks all the time about how a lot of her work is she’s evaluating young children for developmental delays, and that, increasingly, largely what’s happening is that there are lots of kids who would not have developmental delays if not for the fact that they were just literally plopped in front of an iPad for 24 hours.

And once that intervention happens, and they’re not doing that, these people can now enter Gen Ed and they’re not actually necessary — don’t need her services anymore. And what she finds is she’s having these conversations constantly with parents. Like, all the time about like, “Hey, tell me about screen time use and it’s really like —and this is just in agreement with what you’re saying — but it’s very, very difficult to have this conversation without parents feeling like they’re being shamed for it. And so, they get really hostile with her if she even brings up screen time.

And so, given that context, given that we’re still in this equilibrium where parents are clearly themselves — like, not all parents but — somewhat addicted to their phones, just like Matt on Twitter, can’t get off. And me on short-form video: I just can’t get off of it.

It’s not surprising that, like, yes, OK, they’re now in their Yondr Pouches all day in school, but then they go home, and they just immediately mainline. And maybe it’s even worse now. Like, they’ve been feeling withdrawal all day from their AI fruit slop videos. And they’re now watching even more, like, I don’t know.

I think a really great study would be seeing screen time use. I don’t know if Apple shares that with researchers, but does screen time use change? Does the number of hours they’re watching change? Are they sleeping less? There’s just so many things that could be going on here.

Because until we achieve sort of a society-wide view that it is largely not good for minors to be spending hours and hours a day on their phones doing nonacademic stuff, I just don’t think we’re going to have some massive change in test scores.

Matthew Yglesias: But what if they’re listening to podcasts?

Jerusalem Demsas: Then it’s fine.

Matthew Yglesias: Or if they’re watching podcast videos,

Jerusalem Demsas: If they subscribe to The Argument Mag.

Matthew Yglesias: Like and subscribe. I mean, we’re all part of the same hypocrisy, unfortunately.

Jerusalem Demsas: Not me. I’m weaned off my smartphone.

Matthew Yglesias: What do you mean?

Jerusalem Demsas: I got a dumb phone and now my smartphone is locked down.

Matthew Yglesias: Wow. OK.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah. You’re not following my dumb phone journey?

Matthew Yglesias: I don’t know, I guess, I get some green texts.

Jerusalem Demsas: Some green texts for me.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah. It’s gross.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, I think that’s a great place to end. Everyone, thank you so much for listening. If you enjoyed the show, subscribe, like, tell your friends about it.

We’re still early days and we’re growing. We want to make sure to reach more people. So, subscribe to The Argument.com, subscribe to Slow Boring. We’re on YouTube, everywhere you get your podcasts.

Matthew Yglesias: Bye.

Jerusalem Demsas: Bye.

↑ Back to article summary · ↑ See Claude’s response

Europe in the Shadow of Beijing: What the Trump–Xi Summit Means for the European Union

Sumantra Maitra · 2026-05-20 · Original →

When Trump and Xi shook hands in Beijing on 15 May, they were not speaking to Europe. That omission was not accidental—it was structural. The summit’s deeper importance lies in what it revealed about the world now taking shape: one in which Europe’s security, prosperity, and values are being shaped by forces it neither chose nor can meaningfully influence.

The EU finds itself squeezed between two superpowers whose interests diverge from Europe’s in different ways—Washington’s growing indifference and Beijing’s patient strategic ambition. That double pressure has brought Europe to a reckoning it can no longer postpone.

The reckoning is this: the world being constructed around Europe is a world of empires, in the Schmittian sense—vast, self-willed blocs that control what others need and use that control to write the rules of global order on their own terms.

Carl Schmitt’s nomos—the spatial ordering of political power—is being redrawn, and Europe is not among the drawers. Europe must now choose whether to become a pole within this order or be governed by those who are.

For years, European foreign policy thinkers have identified the G2 scenario as their deepest strategic fear: Washington and Beijing managing their rivalry bilaterally, distributing costs and benefits between themselves while the rest of the world adapts. The Beijing summit has not formalized such an arrangement, but it has moved unmistakably in that direction.

Beijing’s post-summit statement spoke of a framework for ‘strategic stability’ to govern the relationship ‘over the next three years and beyond’. Washington’s readouts focused on trade wins and personal rapport.

‘Both logics converge on the same outcome…a bilateral order in which European interests are neither represented nor particularly relevant’

The divergence is itself revealing: China thinks institutionally, in terms of long-term equilibrium; America thinks transactionally. But both logics converge on the same outcome from Europe’s perspective—a bilateral order in which European interests are neither represented nor particularly relevant.

This is the uncomfortable truth that European capitals are only beginning to voice. The damage to trans-Atlantic solidarity has come as much from Washington as from Beijing. Tariffs on allied exports. Conditional security guarantees. A Ukraine policy that subordinated European interests to American negotiating tactics.

China has been patient and strategic in undermining Western cohesion. America has been impatient and blunt—but the effect has been remarkably similar. What the summit crystallized is not merely a diplomatic slight but what Thucydides understood as the brute grammar of power: great states act in their interest, and the rest accommodate.

Europe’s strategic anxiety predates Trump’s second term, but the second administration stripped away the diplomatic packaging and exposed the structural shift beneath. The shocks have arrived with rhythmic regularity: tariffs on European industrial goods, ambiguity over Article 5, Ukraine treated as a bargaining chip, and a military intervention in Iran with severe consequences for European energy markets—all undertaken without meaningful consultation with allies.

Taken together, these episodes force a question onto the agenda of every serious European government: is the trans-Atlantic alliance, as understood since 1949, still a reliable foundation? This is not a question anyone in Berlin, Paris, or Warsaw asks with pleasure.

But what the Beijing summit also clarified is that the United States remains entirely capable of sustained, strategically serious diplomacy—when the partner is China. Europe was not so much excluded as irrelevant. That is a different and more troubling message than neglect. It suggests a strategic reorientation, not a temporary lapse.

‘Europe was not so much excluded as irrelevant…It suggests a strategic reorientation, not a temporary lapse’

Kant imagined a federation of free states bound by law rather than power as the path to perpetual peace. The post-war Atlantic order was, imperfectly, an attempt to build something in that spirit. What is now being dismantled—not conspiratorially but structurally—is the Kantian wager, replaced by the Hobbesian logic that both Washington and Beijing are increasingly comfortable with: influence flows from control over necessities, not from normative standing.

The Beijing summit offered a masterclass in how supply chain dominance translates into geopolitical leverage. China’s control over the processing of rare earth minerals—inputs on which modern defence, clean energy, and digital technology all depend—was not a background condition of the summit. It was one of Beijing’s primary sources of confidence.

The precedent was established during the 2025 tariff confrontation, when China threatened to restrict the flow of rare earths and Washington backed down. The lesson: whoever controls the materials controls the negotiation. Europe is as exposed as the United States, and more so given its simultaneous commitments in three materials-intensive domains—rearmament, energy transition, and digital infrastructure.

This is not merely an industrial policy problem. In the terms Fernand Braudel used to describe long-cycle economic dominance, it is a structural problem of dependency. A bloc that depends on a rival for the materials its defence and economy require is not sovereign in any meaningful sense—it is a client, however prosperous.

Rome understood that supply line security was existential. So did the British Empire. Beijing understands it with exceptional clarity, which is why it has spent two decades building processing dominance far beyond its natural resource endowment.

Relief is not available from Washington either. American ‘friend-shoring’ rhetoric masks straightforward industrial competition. The only viable European response is collective investment at a scale individual member states cannot sustain—a European critical materials strategy with genuine funding and genuine political will. That it has not yet materialized is a measure of how much work the current moment of strategic clarification still has to do.

Europe’s relationship with Chinese clean energy technology sits at the intersection of its climate commitments, its industrial interests, and its strategic anxieties. Chinese solar panels, batteries, and electric vehicles are substantially cheaper than European alternatives. The climate transition cannot wait for geopolitical conditions to improve, and Chinese manufacturers are currently its most cost-effective suppliers.

Yet the structural warning is equally real. Europe’s dependence on Russian gas was not the product of carelessness—it was rational economic calculation, repeated across decades, that consistently underweighted political risk. The calculation looked sound every year until the year it proved catastrophic.

What is changing is the calculation terms. For much of the past decade, European China policy was shaped substantially by alignment with Washington. That alignment rested on American leadership providing both the strategic analysis and the political cover for positions that often had domestic economic costs. As American leadership becomes less coherent and more self-interested, the consensus has weakened.

A Europe building genuine strategic autonomy will need to make its own risk assessments. In practice, this might mean a more differentiated approach: harder lines on defence-adjacent supply chains; more pragmatic engagement on climate cooperation, and scientific exchange where dependency risks can be managed.

Beijing’s post-summit communications were unambiguous in treating Taiwan as the central issue in the bilateral relationship—the variable on which the entire architecture of US–China relations ultimately depends. Trump, asked directly about Taiwan during his time in Beijing, said nothing.

That silence is its own message. A Washington that declines to reaffirm its commitments under direct pressure is a Washington whose security guarantees elsewhere have become harder to value at face value. For European NATO members on the alliance’s eastern edge, American ambiguity under Chinese pressure carries an obvious and uncomfortable implication.

‘A more strategically autonomous Europe will need to develop a genuine position on Taiwan’

Europe’s traditional posture—defer to Washington, issue hedged statements, avoid commitment—is no longer tenable on its own terms if Washington itself is becoming an unreliable anchor. A more strategically autonomous Europe will need to develop a genuine position on Taiwan. Not a military commitment it cannot sustain, but a coherent view of what kind of Indo-Pacific order serves European interests.

Europe is paying significantly more for energy as a result of a war in which no European government had a meaningful voice. Roughly a fifth of global oil and LNG supplies previously moved through the Strait of Hormuz before the conflict disrupted that flow. The Beijing summit resolved none of this.

The political dimension deserves to be stated as clearly as the economic one. A major military intervention with severe and foreseeable consequences for European energy security was undertaken without any meaningful consultation with European allies. The decision was made in Washington and Tel Aviv. The costs have been distributed across European households and businesses.

This asymmetry—between who decides and who pays—is precisely what subordination looks like in practice, and it illustrates the core insight of dependency theory, applied not to postcolonial peripheries but to a prosperous continent: structural powerlessness is not about poverty but about the inability to participate in decisions that determine your condition.

Iran is therefore a test case for the broader argument. An energy shock absorbed because Europe had no voice in the decision that caused it is not an argument for accepting that condition permanently. It is an argument for changing it.

The summit’s failure to produce any meaningful agreement on artificial intelligence—no binding safety framework, no resolution on chip exports—leaves the world’s two most capable AI powers in an unmanaged competition. For Europe, this is doubly uncomfortable.

The EU has invested enormous political capital in positioning itself as the global leader in AI governance. The AI Act is a genuine achievement. But regulatory leadership without technological capability is a structurally weak position—what might be called, borrowing from political theory, auctoritas without potestas: the authority to pronounce without the power to enforce. Regulation applied to technologies produced entirely elsewhere is a form of borrowed sovereignty. It functions as long as producers value European market access enough to comply with it. It fails the moment they decide otherwise.

‘Regulation applied to technologies produced entirely elsewhere is a form of borrowed sovereignty’

The only serious response is a major, collectively funded European effort to build an indigenous AI capability. Not as a substitute for international cooperation, but as its prerequisite. A Europe that produces world-class AI systems can negotiate the terms of AI governance from a position of genuine standing. A Europe that regulates other people’s AI systems is a spectator dressed as a referee.

The deepest irony of the current moment is that US pressure designed to keep Europe divided may be producing the opposite result. External shocks of sufficient severity have historically been the most powerful drivers of European integration—more powerful than any internal political argument. The series of shocks delivered by the second Trump administration, compounded by challenges from China and the costs of the Iran conflict, has created precisely the conditions under which integration arguments become irresistible: when the alternative to collective capacity is repeatedly and expensively demonstrated to be collective vulnerability.

Defence spending across the EU is rising at rates not seen since the Cold War. The European Defence Union has moved from aspirational language to serious institutional design. Mario Draghi’s call for investment at a scale only possible through common European borrowing has shifted the scope of what is considered politically feasible.

The analogy that suggests itself is not the Coal and Steel Community, born of the desire to make war between France and Germany structurally impossible. It is closer to what Tocqueville observed in the American founding: that separate sovereignties, each insufficient to the challenges they faced, were compelled by the logic of circumstance toward a union that none had initially willed. The structural logic is similar. Scale is the precondition of sovereignty in a world of imperial blocs.

Trump’s legacy in Europe, if current trajectories continue, may be the precise opposite of his intention, as laid out by the 2025 National Security Strategy. The president most hostile to European institutions may go down in history as the most effective catalyst for European unity since the Marshall Plan.

There was no European chair at the Beijing summit. There was no European voice in the conversations about trade architecture, technology governance, Taiwan’s future, the Strait of Hormuz, or the framework that will structure the world’s most consequential bilateral relationship for the next three years. This absence was not a diplomatic oversight. It was a structural condition.

The world being assembled in Beijing and Washington does not resemble the world Europe spent the post-war decades constructing. That world—of multilateral rules, of institutions that constrained the strong and gave voice to the less powerful — was the most ambitious political project of the 20th century, and Europe was its primary architect and primary beneficiary. It is being dismantled by the accumulated weight of choices made by powers that never fully shared its premises.

‘The world being assembled in Beijing and Washington does not resemble the world Europe spent the post-war decades constructing’

In its place, a world of empires is taking shape. Europe’s potential power is immense; its actual power remains constrained by the unwillingness to act collectively. The conversion from the world’s largest economic unit into a coherent geopolitical actor is what European integration, at its most ambitious, has always been about. The Beijing summit has made the cost of failing to complete that project impossible to ignore.

The question Europeans must now answer is one the architects of the post-war order hoped they would never face: in a world that runs on imperial power, does Europe have the will to become a force within it—or will it remain, however prosperous and principled, a subject of history rather than its author? The empty chair in Beijing was a symptom. The answer to that question will determine whether it becomes a permanent condition.

↑ Back to article summary · ↑ See Claude’s response

What Xi Knows That Trump Doesn’t

Francis Fukuyama · 2026-05-19 · Original →

In an hour we will host a book club with Freya India discussing her book GIRLS: Gen Z and the Commodification of Everything. Please join us on Substack Live!

Trump and Xi in Beijing on May 15, 2026. (Photo by Yan Yan/Xinhua via Getty Images.)

It was both painful and humiliating to watch media coverage of Donald Trump’s recent visit to Beijing, because it amply demonstrated America’s decline as a great power relative to China. Prior to the summit, expectations were very low: Trump was in a weakened position, beset by inflation and declining popularity, while seeking Chinese help in getting out of the Iran trap he has created for himself. Xi, on the other hand, had forced Trump to back down in his trade war the year before, with China showing strong export growth in the face of Washington’s weak response.

And so it was. Trump returned to Washington with little to show for his visit: only two agreements on opening Chinese markets to U.S. products, and no political help in the Middle East. China did agree to buy 200 Boeing aircraft (fewer than expected), but it has failed to follow through on similar announcements in the past. The White House also claimed that China has agreed to purchase $17 billion of agricultural products, but China has not confirmed this. It did not prevent Trump from claiming that they “did great trade deals” and that the meeting was “a great success.”

It was the optics of the meeting that demonstrated how far Trump has fallen in Chinese eyes. Trump was not met at the airport by Xi. He was seated on the podium in a chair that made him look smaller than Xi, a slight that could have been avoided had Trump’s State Department not sidelined the protocol officials whose job it is to look after these things. The worst part of the visit was Trump’s constant sycophancy, exclaiming that Xi was a “great leader,” “really a friend,” someone “from central casting”; he effused time and again about how beautiful and impressive China is. As in previous interactions with various dictators, Trump seems to have thought that they would be impressed by the same kind of praise and flattery that he himself revels in. Xi, for his part, failed to reciprocate any of these assertions of friendship, saying merely that the United States and China “should be partners and not rivals.”

The most significant issue arising out of the summit was Taiwan. Trump had held up a $14 billion arms package voted by Congress in advance of the summit, and there is no indication delivery will resume anytime soon. Xi told Trump that future relations with Washington would be conditioned on the level of U.S. support for the island. A light went on in Trump’s head that Taiwan would be a “very good negotiating chip” in trade negotiations with Beijing. Trump made other dismissive remarks about the island, noting that “we’re supposed to travel 9,500 miles to fight a war,” and repeating his assertion that Taiwan has stolen semiconductor chip technology from the United States.

His failure to say anything about Taiwan’s security stood in sharp contrast to Joe Biden’s clear assertion that the United States would act in its defense.

Donald Trump is a politician who is unable to see the world in anything but personal and self-interested terms. He was furious after his return at the suggestion that Obama was treated with more respect than he was, using the occasion to assert that “nobody respects Obama,” who was in any case “a divider.” The Chinese media has been talking for some time about the United States as a “declining power”; Xi brought it up with Trump by expressing hope that their countries could avoid the “Thucydides trap” if a declining America ceded power gracefully to a rising China. Trump immediately interpreted this as Xi agreeing with him that America was in decline under Joe Biden, but that it was great again now that he was president. As usual, Trump reserves his greatest anger and hostility for his domestic opponents, and not the leaders of the world’s great dictatorships.

The truth of the matter, which the Chinese understand very well, is the opposite: American decline is a direct product of Trump’s rise since 2016. It is as if Trump has decided to do everything in his power to weaken the United States vis-à-vis China. He has polarized an already polarized country like no previous president; he has cut funding for basic scientific research and attacked American universities which were the best in the world; he has gotten the United States involved in an unnecessary war in the Middle East that has depleted stocks of advanced American weapons; he and his colleagues have openly stated that their domestic opponents, the Democrats, pose a far bigger challenge to the future of the United States than either China or Russia.

Subscribe now

Trump has also systematically sought to undermine the U.S. alliance system, disparaging allies while heaping tariffs on even the closest traditional friends, and threatening to grab territory from Denmark, a loyal NATO ally. He claims that the United States under his leadership is now respected as never before, when something close to the opposite is true: both friends and rivals agree that the United States has become something of a rogue state that is contributing to global instability and disorder, as well as something of a laughing stock.

Trump has made Xi Jinping’s life enormously easy in a way that was reflected in his behavior during the summit. America under Trump is engaged in such a determined process of self-harm that China does not really have to do much but sit back and watch it unfold. Trump predicted that China would not attack Taiwan while he was president. He may be right about this: Xi does not want to get in the way of a declining United States. But he may be forced to act quickly if America finally gets a president who wants to reverse that trajectory.

Francis Fukuyama is the Olivier Nomellini Senior Fellow at Stanford University. His latest book is Liberalism and Its Discontents. He is also the author of the “Frankly Fukuyama” column, carried forward from American Purpose, at Persuasion.

Follow Persuasion on X, Instagram, LinkedIn, and YouTube to keep up with our latest articles, podcasts, and events, as well as updates from excellent writers across our network.

And, to receive pieces like this in your inbox and support our work, subscribe below:

Subscribe now
